# 🎯 Project Charter: Build Your Own SQLite

## What You Are Building
You are building a fully functional, file-based relational database engine that mirrors the internal architecture of SQLite. This includes a SQL frontend (lexer, recursive-descent parser, and bytecode compiler), a register-based Virtual Machine (VDBE), and a sophisticated storage backend featuring a page-based buffer pool, self-balancing B-tree/B+tree structures, and ACID-compliant transaction managers. By the end, your engine will store data in a custom binary format on disk, support secondary indexes, and recover automatically from simulated system crashes.

## Why This Project Exists
Most developers treat databases as high-level black boxes, but the abstractions they provide—atomicity, durability, and query optimization—rely on specific, low-level engineering choices. Building a database from scratch is the most effective way to master systems programming, as it forces you to reconcile the speed of in-memory data structures with the latency and unreliability of physical disk I/O.

## What You Will Be Able to Do When Done
- **Implement a SQL Compiler:** Build a lexer and parser that converts raw SQL strings into Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) and then into executable bytecode.
- **Design a Virtual Machine:** Create a register-based VM that executes database operations using custom opcodes.
- **Manage a Buffer Pool:** Write a page cache with LRU eviction and pin/unpin logic to minimize disk head movement.
- **Construct Persistent B-trees:** Implement on-disk B-trees for table storage and B+trees for secondary indexes using a slotted-page binary format.
- **Guarantee ACID Transactions:** Implement both a Rollback Journal and Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) to ensure data integrity during power failures or crashes.
- **Build a Query Optimizer:** Code a cost-based planner that uses data statistics to decide between full table scans and index lookups.

## Final Deliverable
A complete database library (approximately 4,000–6,000 lines of C, Rust, or Go) and a CLI tool. The system will handle a binary database file that persists across restarts, supports `CREATE TABLE`, `INSERT`, `SELECT` (with `WHERE`, `JOIN`, and `GROUP BY`), `UPDATE`, and `DELETE`. It includes an `EXPLAIN` command to view the generated bytecode and a verifiable recovery mechanism for "hot" journals.

## Is This Project For You?
**You should start this if you:**
- Have a strong grasp of a systems language (C, Rust, or Go).
- Are comfortable with pointer arithmetic and manual memory management.
- Understand basic data structures like linked lists, hash maps, and binary trees.
- Want to move from "using" tools to "building" infrastructure.

**Come back after you've learned:**
- **Manual Memory Management:** If you don't understand `malloc/free` or Rust's ownership model, this will be overwhelming.
- **Binary File I/O:** Practice reading and writing structs to files using `fread/fwrite` or `pread/pwrite`.

## Estimated Effort
| Phase | Time |
|-------|------|
| SQL Tokenizer & Parser | ~11 hours |
| Bytecode Compiler (VDBE) | ~10 hours |
| Buffer Pool Manager | ~8 hours |
| B-tree Page Format & Table Storage | ~12 hours |
| SELECT Execution & DML | ~10 hours |
| Secondary Indexes | ~8 hours |
| Query Planner & Statistics | ~10 hours |
| Transactions (Rollback Journal) | ~10 hours |
| WAL Mode Concurrency | ~12 hours |
| Aggregate Functions & JOIN | ~14 hours |
| **Total** | **~105 hours** |

## Definition of Done
The project is complete when:
- **Query Correctness:** A test suite of 50+ complex SQL statements (including JOINs and Aggregates) produces bit-identical results compared to a reference SQLite instance.
- **Crash Consistency:** The database file survives a `SIGKILL` or simulated power failure mid-INSERT and successfully recovers to a consistent state upon restart.
- **Performance:** A query on an indexed column in a 100,000-row table completes in under 10ms, verified by counting page reads in the buffer pool.
- **Persistence:** Data inserted in one session is retrievable in another session after the process has fully exited.

---

# 📚 Before You Read This: Prerequisites & Further Reading

> **Read these first.** The Atlas assumes you are familiar with the foundations below.
> Resources are ordered by when you should encounter them — some before you start, some at specific milestones.

## Database System Architecture
### 1. SQLite Architecture Overview
- **Code**: [sqlite/src/main.c](https://github.com/sqlite/sqlite/blob/master/src/main.c) (The entry point and subsystem orchestration).
- **Best Explanation**: [SQLite.org: Architecture](https://www.sqlite.org/arch.html) — The entire page.
- **Why**: It is the official structural blueprint for the project you are about to build.
- **Timing**: Read BEFORE starting the project — provides the "Big Picture" mental map.

## The Frontend: Tokenizing & Parsing
### 2. Recursive Descent & Pratt Parsing
- **Paper**: Vaughan Pratt (1973), "Top Down Operator Precedence."
- **Code**: [clox/compiler.c](https://github.com/munificent/craftinginterpreters/blob/master/c/compiler.c) — `parsePrecedence` function.
- **Best Explanation**: *Crafting Interpreters* (Robert Nystrom), Chapter 17: "Compiling Expressions."
- **Why**: This is the industry-standard way to handle operator precedence without the complexity of Yacc/Bison.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 2 (SQL Parser) — critical for building the expression engine.

### 3. Finite State Machines for Lexing
- **Code**: [sqlite/src/tokenize.c](https://github.com/sqlite/sqlite/blob/master/src/tokenize.c).
- **Best Explanation**: *Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools* (Dragon Book), Section 3.6: "Finite Automata."
- **Why**: Provides the formal mathematical framework for converting a stream of characters into a stream of tokens.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 1 (Tokenizer) — you’ll understand the transition logic.

## The Virtual Machine: VDBE
### 4. Register-Based Virtual Machines
- **Paper**: Ierusalimschy et al. (2005), "The Implementation of Lua 5.0."
- **Code**: [sqlite/src/vdbe.c](https://github.com/sqlite/sqlite/blob/master/src/vdbe.c) (The opcode dispatch loop).
- **Best Explanation**: [SQLite.org: The Virtual Database Engine](https://www.sqlite.org/vdbe.html).
- **Why**: Explains why SQLite uses registers instead of a stack to minimize instruction count and overhead.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 3 (Bytecode Compiler) — justifies the instruction set design.

## Storage & Memory Management
### 5. Slotted Page Layout
- **Code**: [postgres/src/include/storage/bufpage.h](https://github.com/postgres/postgres/blob/master/src/include/storage/bufpage.h).
- **Best Explanation**: *Database Internals* (Alex Petrov), Chapter 3: "Database Storage."
- **Why**: It is the universal standard for packing variable-length rows into fixed-size disk blocks.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 5 (B-tree) — required to understand how to write bytes to pages.

### 6. B-Tree Algorithms
- **Paper**: Bayer & McCreight (1970), "Organization and Maintenance of Large Ordered Indices."
- **Code**: [sqlite/src/btree.c](https://github.com/sqlite/sqlite/blob/master/src/btree.c).
- **Best Explanation**: *The Art of Computer Programming* (Donald Knuth), Volume 3, Section 6.2.4: "Multiway Trees."
- **Why**: The primary data structure for on-disk sorted storage in nearly every RDBMS.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 5 (B-tree) — helps with the complex split/merge logic.

### 7. Buffer Pool Management (LRU & Clock)
- **Paper**: Effelsberg & Haerder (1984), "Principles of Database Buffer Management."
- **Code**: [postgres/src/backend/storage/buffer/freelist.c](https://github.com/postgres/postgres/blob/master/src/backend/storage/buffer/freelist.c) (Clock algorithm).
- **Best Explanation**: *Database Internals* (Alex Petrov), Chapter 5: "Buffer Management."
- **Why**: Teaches how to manage memory as a cache for disk, avoiding OOMs while maintaining high hit rates.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 4 (Buffer Pool) — essential for the eviction strategy.

## Query Optimization
### 8. Cost-Based Join Ordering
- **Paper**: Selinger et al. (1979), "Access Path Selection in a Relational Database Management System."
- **Code**: [sqlite/src/where.c](https://github.com/sqlite/sqlite/blob/master/src/where.c) (The core of the query optimizer).
- **Best Explanation**: [Architecture of a Database System](https://dsf.berkeley.edu/papers/fntdb07-architecture.pdf) (Hellerstein et al.), Section 4.2.
- **Why**: The foundational paper that defined the dynamic programming approach to SQL optimization.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 8 (Query Planner) — explains why statistics like NDV matter.

## Durability & Transactions
### 9. ARIES (Write-Ahead Logging)
- **Paper**: C. Mohan et al. (1992), "ARIES: A Transaction Recovery Method..."
- **Spec**: [SQLite File Format: The Write-Ahead Log](https://www.sqlite.org/fileformat2.html#wal_format).
- **Best Explanation**: *Database System Concepts* (Silberschatz), Chapter 19: "Recovery System."
- **Why**: The "gold standard" algorithm for ensuring ACID properties and high-performance concurrency.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 10 (WAL) — clarifies how redo/undo logging differs.

### 10. fsync and the Storage Hierarchy
- **Best Explanation**: [Everything You Always Wanted to Know About fsync()](https://danluu.com/file-consistency/) by Dan Luu.
- **Why**: A masterclass in why standard `write()` calls aren't durable and how power loss corrupts data.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 9 (Transactions) — you'll understand why write ordering is mandatory.

## Formal Logic
### 11. Three-Valued Logic (NULLs)
- **Best Explanation**: *SQL and Relational Theory* (C.J. Date), Chapter 4: "Missing Information."
- **Why**: Explains why `NULL = NULL` is not true, a common source of catastrophic query bugs.
- **Timing**: Read during Milestone 6 (DML) — vital for implementing the expression evaluator.

---

# Build Your Own SQLite

This project constructs a fully functional embedded SQL database engine from first principles — spanning the entire pipeline from raw SQL text to durable, crash-recoverable on-disk storage. You will build a tokenizer and recursive-descent parser that produce an AST, compile that AST into bytecode instructions executed by a register-based virtual machine (the VDBE), layer a buffer pool manager over page-based disk I/O, implement B-tree and B+tree storage for tables and indexes, build a cost-based query planner, and finally implement ACID transactions via both rollback journal and write-ahead logging.

The architecture mirrors real SQLite's layered design: a frontend (tokenizer → parser → compiler), a virtual machine execution engine, and a backend (buffer pool → B-tree → pager → OS interface). Each layer communicates through narrow, well-defined interfaces — the VM never touches disk directly, and the B-tree never parses SQL. This separation of concerns is what makes SQLite's ~150K lines of C manageable, and what makes this project teachable.

By the end, you will have internalized the fundamental tension of all storage systems — speed vs. durability — and understood why every database in existence makes the same handful of architectural choices, just with different tradeoffs.



<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m1 -->
# Milestone 1: SQL Tokenizer
## Where We Are in the System
Before you write a single line of code, orient yourself. You are building an entire database engine, and the tokenizer is the front door — the first component that touches raw SQL text from the user.

![SQLite Engine — Full System Architecture Map](./diagrams/diag-satellite-system-map.svg)

The tokenizer sits at the absolute boundary between the human world (SQL strings) and the machine world (structured data your engine can process). Everything downstream — the parser, the bytecode compiler, the virtual machine, the B-tree storage engine — depends on the tokenizer producing a clean, unambiguous stream of classified tokens. If the tokenizer misclassifies a single character, the entire pipeline produces garbage.

![Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page](./diagrams/diag-data-flow-sql-to-disk.svg)

Your job in this milestone: take a raw `const char*` of SQL text and produce an array of `Token` structs, each carrying a type, a value, and a source position (line and column). That's it. No parsing, no tree-building, no execution. Just classification.
---
## The Revelation: Why "Just Split on Whitespace" Fails
You might think tokenization is trivial. SQL has keywords, identifiers, numbers, and a few operators. Split on whitespace, check each piece against a keyword list, done. This intuition works for maybe 60% of SQL statements — and catastrophically fails on the rest.
Consider this statement:
```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'it''s complicated' AND id >= -42 -- this is a comment
```
Try splitting on whitespace. You get:
```
["SELECT", "*", "FROM", "users", "WHERE", "name", "=", "'it''s", "complicated'", "AND", "id", ">=", "-42", "--", "this", "is", "a", "comment"]
```
The string literal `'it''s complicated'` is torn apart. The comment `-- this is a comment` becomes four separate "tokens." The `>=` operator might or might not survive depending on your split logic. And `-42` — is that a negative number or the subtraction operator followed by `42`?
The problem is that **meaning depends on context**. A single quote inside a string literal means something entirely different from a single quote that terminates one. A minus sign after `>=` is probably the start of a negative number; a minus sign after `id` is a subtraction operator; two minus signs together are a comment. You cannot determine what a character *means* without knowing what *state* you are currently in.
This is why tokenization requires a **finite state machine** (FSM).

> **🔑 Foundation: Finite state machines as the formal model for lexical analysis**
> 
> ## Finite State Machines as the Engine Behind Lexical Analysis
A **finite state machine** (FSM) is a computational model that exists in exactly one of a finite number of *states* at any given time. It reads input one symbol at a time, and each symbol triggers a *transition* to another state (or the same state) according to a fixed set of rules. When the machine lands in a designated *accepting state*, it signals that the input it just consumed forms a valid token.
In lexical analysis, the FSM is the formal model that answers: *"How does a lexer decide where one token ends and the next begins?"*
### How it works concretely
Imagine your lexer is scanning the input `if (x >= 42)`. The FSM starts in a **start state**. It reads `i` — transitions to a state meaning "inside an identifier." It reads `f` — stays in "inside an identifier." It reads a space — the space doesn't belong in an identifier, so the machine hits an accepting state for `IDENTIFIER`, emits the token `if` (which a later stage may reclassify as a keyword), and resets.
Here's a minimal FSM for recognizing integer literals:
```
State 0 (start) --[digit]--> State 1 (accepting: INTEGER)
State 1         --[digit]--> State 1 (accepting: INTEGER)
State 1         --[other]--> emit token, return to State 0
```
That's it. Two states, two transition rules, and you can recognize every integer in your source code.
### Why you need this right now
You're building a lexer, and you have a choice: write a tangle of nested `if/else` branches that get harder to maintain with every new token type, or structure your lexer around an FSM where each token type corresponds to a clear set of states and transitions. The FSM approach gives you:
- **Correctness by design** — each token type maps to a well-defined path through states, so you can reason about (and test) them independently.
- **A direct path from specification to code** — regular expressions formally convert to FSMs, so if you can write a regex for a token pattern, you can mechanically derive the FSM that recognizes it.
- **Longest-match semantics** — by staying in accepting states as long as valid transitions exist and only emitting when no transition is possible, you naturally implement the "maximal munch" rule (e.g., `>=` is one token, not `>` followed by `=`).
### The key insight
**Your lexer already *is* a finite state machine — the question is whether you make that structure explicit or hide it in spaghetti code.** Every `if` branch that checks the current character and decides what to do next is an informal state transition. When you make the states and transitions explicit (whether as a table, an enum, or a `switch` statement organized by state), your lexer becomes easier to extend, debug, and verify. Adding a new token type means adding new states and transitions rather than inserting fragile logic into existing branches.
A practical mental model: think of your lexer as a cursor sliding across the source text with a **mode dial** (the current state). Each character it reads clicks the dial to a new position. When the dial reaches a position marked "done," you snip off the text you've traversed and label it as a token. Turn the dial back to start. Repeat.

---
## Designing the Token Types
Before building the state machine, define what it produces. Every token your lexer emits needs a type. Here is the complete set for a SQLite-compatible tokenizer:
```c
typedef enum {
    // Literals
    TOK_INTEGER,        // 42, 0, 999
    TOK_FLOAT,          // 3.14, 0.5, 1e10
    TOK_STRING,         // 'hello', 'it''s'
    // Identifiers and keywords
    TOK_IDENTIFIER,     // table_name, col1
    TOK_KEYWORD,        // SELECT, INSERT, CREATE, ...
    // Operators
    TOK_EQ,             // =
    TOK_NE,             // != or <>
    TOK_LT,             // <
    TOK_GT,             // >
    TOK_LE,             // <=
    TOK_GE,             // >=
    TOK_PLUS,           // +
    TOK_MINUS,          // -
    TOK_STAR,           // *
    TOK_SLASH,          // /
    // Punctuation
    TOK_LPAREN,         // (
    TOK_RPAREN,         // )
    TOK_COMMA,          // ,
    TOK_SEMICOLON,      // ;
    TOK_DOT,            // .
    // Special
    TOK_EOF,            // end of input
    TOK_ERROR           // unrecognized character
} TokenType;
```
And the token struct itself:
```c
typedef struct {
    TokenType type;
    const char *start;   // pointer into original SQL string
    int length;          // length of the lexeme
    int line;            // 1-based line number
    int col;             // 1-based column number
} Token;
```
Two design decisions deserve attention here.
**Why store `start` + `length` instead of a copied string?** Because copying every token's text into a heap-allocated string is wasteful. The original SQL string already contains the text. Your token just points into it. This is called a *string slice* or *string view* — the token borrows the original string's memory. The parser can copy the text later if it needs to own it, but during tokenization, zero-copy is both faster and simpler.
**Why separate `TOK_INTEGER` and `TOK_FLOAT`?** Because the parser and compiler need to know the difference. An integer literal `42` becomes a 64-bit signed integer; a float `3.14` becomes a double-precision floating point. If you merge them into a single `TOK_NUMBER`, you push the integer-vs-float distinction into the parser, which shouldn't care about character-level details.
---
## The Tokenizer State Machine
The core of your lexer is a loop that examines one character at a time, decides what state it's in, and dispatches to the appropriate handler. Here is the state machine:

![Tokenizer Finite State Machine](./diagrams/diag-tokenizer-fsm.svg)

The states are:
| State | Entered When | Exits When |
|-------|-------------|------------|
| **START** | Beginning of each new token | Character dispatches to another state |
| **IN_IDENTIFIER** | Letter or `_` seen | Non-alphanumeric/underscore seen |
| **IN_NUMBER** | Digit seen | Non-digit, non-dot seen (with float transition) |
| **IN_STRING** | Single quote `'` seen | Unescaped closing `'` seen |
| **IN_QUOTED_ID** | Double quote `"` seen | Closing `"` seen |
| **IN_OPERATOR** | Operator character seen | Complete operator determined (possibly with lookahead) |
| **IN_COMMENT** | `--` seen | Newline or EOF |
The driver loop:
```c
typedef struct {
    const char *source;   // full SQL string
    int current;          // current position in source
    int line;             // current line (1-based)
    int col;              // current column (1-based)
    int length;           // strlen(source)
} Lexer;
void lexer_init(Lexer *lex, const char *sql) {
    lex->source = sql;
    lex->current = 0;
    lex->line = 1;
    lex->col = 1;
    lex->length = (int)strlen(sql);
}
```
The central function peeks at the current character and dispatches:
```c
Token lexer_next_token(Lexer *lex) {
    skip_whitespace_and_comments(lex);
    if (lex->current >= lex->length) {
        return make_token(lex, TOK_EOF, lex->current, 0);
    }
    char c = lex->source[lex->current];
    if (is_alpha(c) || c == '_')  return lex_identifier_or_keyword(lex);
    if (is_digit(c))              return lex_number(lex);
    if (c == '\'')                return lex_string(lex);
    if (c == '"')                 return lex_quoted_identifier(lex);
    if (is_operator_start(c))     return lex_operator(lex);
    if (is_punctuation(c))        return lex_punctuation(lex);
    // Unrecognized character
    Token err = make_token(lex, TOK_ERROR, lex->current, 1);
    advance(lex);
    return err;
}
```
This is the **dispatch-on-first-character** pattern. The first character of every token unambiguously tells you which handler to call — with one exception we'll address shortly (the minus sign). Let's implement each handler.
---
## Lookahead and Buffering
Before diving into handlers, you need two utility functions that embody a critical pattern: **lookahead**. The tokenizer must sometimes peek at the next character without consuming it to decide what to do with the current character.
```c
static char peek(Lexer *lex) {
    if (lex->current >= lex->length) return '\0';
    return lex->source[lex->current];
}
static char peek_next(Lexer *lex) {
    if (lex->current + 1 >= lex->length) return '\0';
    return lex->source[lex->current + 1];
}
static char advance(Lexer *lex) {
    char c = lex->source[lex->current++];
    if (c == '\n') {
        lex->line++;
        lex->col = 1;
    } else {
        lex->col++;
    }
    return c;
}
```
The `peek` function looks without moving. The `advance` function consumes and updates position tracking. This peek-then-advance pattern appears everywhere in stream processing — network protocol parsers use the same logic when deciding whether a byte is a header or payload, and file format parsers use it when reading variable-length fields.
---
## Handler: Identifiers and Keywords
An identifier starts with a letter or underscore and continues with letters, digits, or underscores. After collecting the full lexeme, you check it against the keyword table. If it matches, it's a keyword; otherwise, it's an identifier.
```c
static Token lex_identifier_or_keyword(Lexer *lex) {
    int start = lex->current;
    int start_col = lex->col;
    int start_line = lex->line;
    while (lex->current < lex->length && 
           (is_alpha(peek(lex)) || is_digit(peek(lex)) || peek(lex) == '_')) {
        advance(lex);
    }
    int len = lex->current - start;
    // Check if this identifier is a keyword (case-insensitive)
    TokenType type = check_keyword(lex->source + start, len);
    Token tok;
    tok.type = type;  // TOK_KEYWORD or TOK_IDENTIFIER
    tok.start = lex->source + start;
    tok.length = len;
    tok.line = start_line;
    tok.col = start_col;
    return tok;
}
```
The keyword check must be **case-insensitive**. SQL requires `SELECT`, `select`, and `SeLeCt` to mean the same thing. The simplest approach: convert the lexeme to uppercase (or lowercase) before comparison.
```c
static const char *keywords[] = {
    "SELECT", "FROM", "WHERE", "INSERT", "INTO", "VALUES",
    "CREATE", "TABLE", "DROP", "DELETE", "UPDATE", "SET",
    "AND", "OR", "NOT", "NULL", "INTEGER", "TEXT", "REAL",
    "BLOB", "PRIMARY", "KEY", "UNIQUE", "INDEX", "ON",
    "JOIN", "INNER", "LEFT", "RIGHT", "ORDER", "BY",
    "ASC", "DESC", "LIMIT", "GROUP", "HAVING", "AS",
    "BEGIN", "COMMIT", "ROLLBACK", "EXPLAIN", "ANALYZE",
    "PRAGMA", "IF", "EXISTS", "IN", "BETWEEN", "LIKE",
    "IS", "COUNT", "SUM", "AVG", "MIN", "MAX",
    NULL
};
static TokenType check_keyword(const char *text, int len) {
    // Temporary buffer for case-insensitive comparison
    char upper[64];
    if (len >= 64) return TOK_IDENTIFIER; // no keyword is 64+ chars
    for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
        upper[i] = (char)toupper((unsigned char)text[i]);
    }
    upper[len] = '\0';
    for (int i = 0; keywords[i] != NULL; i++) {
        if (strlen(keywords[i]) == (size_t)len && 
            memcmp(upper, keywords[i], len) == 0) {
            return TOK_KEYWORD;
        }
    }
    return TOK_IDENTIFIER;
}
```
> **Performance note**: A linear scan through ~50 keywords is fine for a learning project. Production tokenizers use a perfect hash function or a trie for O(1) keyword lookup. SQLite's own tokenizer uses a hash table generated by a custom tool (`mkkeywordhash.c`). For your build, the linear scan is perfectly adequate — a 50-element comparison completes in nanoseconds, and the tokenizer is never the bottleneck.
### Design Decision: Keyword Tokens
| Option | Pros | Cons | Used By |
|--------|------|------|---------|
| **Single `TOK_KEYWORD` type ✓** | Simple token enum, keyword identity from text | Parser must string-compare to dispatch | SQLite (internally uses hash codes) |
| Separate type per keyword (`TOK_SELECT`, `TOK_FROM`, ...) | Parser dispatch is a switch on type | Enum grows large, keyword set changes break it | Many compiler textbooks |
For this project, use `TOK_KEYWORD` and let the parser compare the text. It keeps the tokenizer simpler and means adding a new keyword only requires updating the keyword table, not the enum.
---
## Handler: Numeric Literals
Numbers start with a digit. The tricky part: distinguishing integers from floats. An integer is a sequence of digits. A float has a decimal point, an exponent (`e` or `E`), or both.
```c
static Token lex_number(Lexer *lex) {
    int start = lex->current;
    int start_col = lex->col;
    int start_line = lex->line;
    TokenType type = TOK_INTEGER;
    // Consume digits before decimal point
    while (lex->current < lex->length && is_digit(peek(lex))) {
        advance(lex);
    }
    // Check for decimal point followed by digit
    if (peek(lex) == '.' && is_digit(peek_next(lex))) {
        type = TOK_FLOAT;
        advance(lex);  // consume '.'
        while (lex->current < lex->length && is_digit(peek(lex))) {
            advance(lex);
        }
    }
    // Check for exponent
    if (peek(lex) == 'e' || peek(lex) == 'E') {
        type = TOK_FLOAT;
        advance(lex);  // consume 'e'/'E'
        if (peek(lex) == '+' || peek(lex) == '-') {
            advance(lex);  // consume sign
        }
        while (lex->current < lex->length && is_digit(peek(lex))) {
            advance(lex);
        }
    }
    int len = lex->current - start;
    Token tok = { type, lex->source + start, len, start_line, start_col };
    return tok;
}
```
**Critical pitfall: negative numbers.** The tokenizer should NOT handle negative numbers. When you see `-42` in SQL, the tokenizer emits `TOK_MINUS` followed by `TOK_INTEGER(42)`. The parser (or the expression evaluator) applies the unary minus. Why? Because in `WHERE id > -42`, the minus is a unary operator. But in `SELECT 10 - 42`, it's binary subtraction. The tokenizer cannot tell the difference — that requires syntactic context, which is the parser's job. If the tokenizer consumed `-42` as a single negative number token, it would incorrectly tokenize `10-42` as `10` followed by `-42` instead of `10`, `-`, `42`.

![Tokenizer Ambiguity Cases](./diagrams/diag-tokenizer-ambiguity.svg)

---
## Handler: String Literals
String literals in SQL are enclosed in single quotes. The complication: SQL escapes a single quote by doubling it. The string `it's` is written as `'it''s'` in SQL. Your tokenizer must recognize that `''` inside a string is an escaped quote, not two empty strings.
```c
static Token lex_string(Lexer *lex) {
    int start = lex->current;
    int start_col = lex->col;
    int start_line = lex->line;
    advance(lex);  // consume opening '
    while (lex->current < lex->length) {
        if (peek(lex) == '\'') {
            advance(lex);  // consume this quote
            // Check if it's an escaped quote ('')
            if (peek(lex) == '\'') {
                advance(lex);  // consume second quote, stay in string
            } else {
                // End of string
                int len = lex->current - start;
                Token tok = { TOK_STRING, lex->source + start, len, 
                              start_line, start_col };
                return tok;
            }
        } else {
            advance(lex);
        }
    }
    // Unterminated string - reached end of input
    Token tok = { TOK_ERROR, lex->source + start, lex->current - start,
                  start_line, start_col };
    return tok;
}
```
The state machine for string lexing has exactly two states: **IN_STRING** and **MAYBE_END**. When you see a quote in IN_STRING, you transition to MAYBE_END. In MAYBE_END, if the next character is another quote, you go back to IN_STRING (it was an escape). If it's anything else, the string is terminated.
Note that the token includes the surrounding quotes (`'it''s'`). The parser or a utility function will strip quotes and unescape `''` to `'` when it needs the actual string value. Keeping the raw lexeme in the token preserves the original source text, which is useful for error messages and EXPLAIN output.
---
## Handler: Quoted Identifiers
SQL uses double quotes for identifiers that contain spaces or are reserved words. `"Group"` is an identifier (column name), not the keyword GROUP. `"First Name"` is a single identifier, not two tokens.
```c
static Token lex_quoted_identifier(Lexer *lex) {
    int start = lex->current;
    int start_col = lex->col;
    int start_line = lex->line;
    advance(lex);  // consume opening "
    while (lex->current < lex->length && peek(lex) != '"') {
        advance(lex);
    }
    if (lex->current >= lex->length) {
        // Unterminated quoted identifier
        Token tok = { TOK_ERROR, lex->source + start, lex->current - start,
                      start_line, start_col };
        return tok;
    }
    advance(lex);  // consume closing "
    int len = lex->current - start;
    Token tok = { TOK_IDENTIFIER, lex->source + start, len, 
                  start_line, start_col };
    return tok;
}
```
The distinction between single quotes (strings) and double quotes (identifiers) is a common source of bugs. In many programming languages, both delimiters mean "string." In SQL, they mean completely different things. MySQL blurs this line by allowing double quotes for strings in some modes, but standard SQL and SQLite are strict: single quotes for string literals, double quotes for identifiers.
---
## Handler: Operators
Operators require lookahead because some are two characters long. When you see `<`, it could be `<` (less-than), `<=` (less-than-or-equal), or `<>` (not-equal). You must peek at the next character before deciding.
```c
static Token lex_operator(Lexer *lex) {
    int start = lex->current;
    int start_col = lex->col;
    int start_line = lex->line;
    char c = advance(lex);
    switch (c) {
        case '=': return (Token){ TOK_EQ, lex->source + start, 1, 
                                  start_line, start_col };
        case '+': return (Token){ TOK_PLUS, lex->source + start, 1, 
                                  start_line, start_col };
        case '-': return (Token){ TOK_MINUS, lex->source + start, 1, 
                                  start_line, start_col };
        case '*': return (Token){ TOK_STAR, lex->source + start, 1, 
                                  start_line, start_col };
        case '/': return (Token){ TOK_SLASH, lex->source + start, 1, 
                                  start_line, start_col };
        case '<':
            if (peek(lex) == '=') {
                advance(lex);
                return (Token){ TOK_LE, lex->source + start, 2, 
                                start_line, start_col };
            }
            if (peek(lex) == '>') {
                advance(lex);
                return (Token){ TOK_NE, lex->source + start, 2, 
                                start_line, start_col };
            }
            return (Token){ TOK_LT, lex->source + start, 1, 
                            start_line, start_col };
        case '>':
            if (peek(lex) == '=') {
                advance(lex);
                return (Token){ TOK_GE, lex->source + start, 2, 
                                start_line, start_col };
            }
            return (Token){ TOK_GT, lex->source + start, 1, 
                            start_line, start_col };
        case '!':
            if (peek(lex) == '=') {
                advance(lex);
                return (Token){ TOK_NE, lex->source + start, 2, 
                                start_line, start_col };
            }
            // '!' alone is not valid SQL
            return (Token){ TOK_ERROR, lex->source + start, 1, 
                            start_line, start_col };
        default:
            return (Token){ TOK_ERROR, lex->source + start, 1, 
                            start_line, start_col };
    }
}
```
Notice that `!=` and `<>` both produce `TOK_NE`. SQL supports both syntaxes for "not equal." By normalizing them to the same token type, the parser doesn't need to care which one was written.
---
## Whitespace and Comments
Whitespace and comments are skipped entirely — they produce no tokens. But they must still update the line and column counters.
```c
static void skip_whitespace_and_comments(Lexer *lex) {
    while (lex->current < lex->length) {
        char c = peek(lex);
        // Whitespace
        if (c == ' ' || c == '\t' || c == '\r' || c == '\n') {
            advance(lex);
            continue;
        }
        // Single-line comment: -- until end of line
        if (c == '-' && peek_next(lex) == '-') {
            advance(lex);  // consume first -
            advance(lex);  // consume second -
            while (lex->current < lex->length && peek(lex) != '\n') {
                advance(lex);
            }
            continue;
        }
        // C-style block comment: /* ... */
        if (c == '/' && peek_next(lex) == '*') {
            advance(lex);  // consume /
            advance(lex);  // consume *
            while (lex->current < lex->length) {
                if (peek(lex) == '*' && peek_next(lex) == '/') {
                    advance(lex);  // consume *
                    advance(lex);  // consume /
                    break;
                }
                advance(lex);
            }
            continue;
        }
        break;  // not whitespace or comment
    }
}
```
The comment handling introduces another ambiguity. The `-` character could start a `--` comment or be the minus operator. The `skip_whitespace_and_comments` function checks for `--` before the main dispatch loop sees the first `-`. This is why comments are handled in the whitespace-skipping phase rather than as tokens — it removes the ambiguity before the operator handler ever runs.
---
## Error Reporting: Line and Column Tracking
Error reporting transforms a frustrating debugging experience into a productive one. When the tokenizer encounters an unrecognized character — say, a stray `@` or `#` — it must tell the user exactly where:
```
Error at line 3, column 15: unexpected character '@'
```
This is why the `Lexer` struct tracks `line` and `col`, and why every token records `start_line` and `start_col` at the moment it begins. The `advance` function updates these counters on every character consumed.
A helper for error messages:
```c
void lexer_error(Token *tok, const char *source) {
    // Extract the problematic character(s)
    char lexeme[64];
    int copy_len = tok->length < 63 ? tok->length : 63;
    memcpy(lexeme, tok->start, copy_len);
    lexeme[copy_len] = '\0';
    fprintf(stderr, "Lexer error at line %d, column %d: "
            "unexpected token '%s'\n", tok->line, tok->col, lexeme);
}
```
The line/column tracking pattern connects directly to how IDEs provide red squiggly underlines under syntax errors. The IDE runs a lexer and parser in the background; when either reports an error with a source position, the IDE highlights that position. Your tokenizer's error reporting is the foundation for that capability.
---
## Putting It All Together: The Public API
The tokenizer's public interface is minimal:
```c
// Initialize a lexer for the given SQL string
void lexer_init(Lexer *lex, const char *sql);
// Get the next token (advances the lexer)
Token lexer_next_token(Lexer *lex);
// Convenience: tokenize entire input into an array
int lexer_tokenize_all(Lexer *lex, Token *tokens, int max_tokens);
```
The `tokenize_all` function is a convenience wrapper:
```c
int lexer_tokenize_all(Lexer *lex, Token *tokens, int max_tokens) {
    int count = 0;
    while (count < max_tokens - 1) {
        tokens[count] = lexer_next_token(lex);
        if (tokens[count].type == TOK_EOF) {
            count++;
            break;
        }
        if (tokens[count].type == TOK_ERROR) {
            lexer_error(&tokens[count], lex->source);
            count++;
            break;
        }
        count++;
    }
    return count;
}
```
Let's trace through a complete example:

![Token Stream Trace Example](./diagrams/diag-tokenizer-trace.svg)

Input: `SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age >= 21;`
Expected token stream:
| # | Type | Lexeme | Line | Col |
|---|------|--------|------|-----|
| 1 | KEYWORD | SELECT | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | IDENTIFIER | name | 1 | 8 |
| 3 | COMMA | , | 1 | 12 |
| 4 | IDENTIFIER | age | 1 | 14 |
| 5 | KEYWORD | FROM | 1 | 18 |
| 6 | IDENTIFIER | users | 1 | 23 |
| 7 | KEYWORD | WHERE | 1 | 29 |
| 8 | IDENTIFIER | age | 1 | 35 |
| 9 | GE | >= | 1 | 39 |
| 10 | INTEGER | 21 | 1 | 42 |
| 11 | SEMICOLON | ; | 1 | 44 |
| 12 | EOF | | 1 | 45 |
---
## The Token Stream as Abstraction Boundary
The token stream is an **abstraction boundary** — one of the most important software design concepts you'll encounter in this project. The parser, which you'll build next, never sees raw characters. It sees tokens. This means:
1. **You can change the tokenizer** without touching the parser. Want to support MySQL's backtick-quoted identifiers (\`column\`)? Add a handler in the tokenizer. The parser never knows.
2. **You can change the SQL dialect** by swapping the keyword table. Want PostgreSQL-style `::` type casts? Add a `TOK_CAST` operator in the tokenizer. The parser just sees a new token type.
3. **The parser's job is simpler.** It pattern-matches on token types, not on characters. "Is the next token a KEYWORD with text 'SELECT'?" is cleaner than "skip whitespace, read characters, check if they spell SELECT case-insensitively."
This is the same pattern used throughout the database engine. The VM never sees B-tree pages directly — it uses cursor operations. The B-tree never sees raw disk — it uses the buffer pool. Each layer provides a clean abstraction boundary. Your tokenizer is the first one.
---
## Building the Test Suite
Your acceptance criteria demand a test suite of 20+ diverse SQL statements. Here's a framework and representative test cases:
```c
typedef struct {
    const char *sql;
    int expected_count;  // number of tokens (including EOF)
    TokenType first_type;
    const char *description;
} TestCase;
TestCase tests[] = {
    // Basic statements
    {"SELECT * FROM t;", 6, TOK_KEYWORD, "simple select star"},
    {"select * from t;", 6, TOK_KEYWORD, "lowercase keywords"},
    {"SeLeCt * FrOm t;", 6, TOK_KEYWORD, "mixed case keywords"},
    // String literals
    {"SELECT 'hello';", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "simple string literal"},
    {"SELECT 'it''s';", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "escaped quote in string"},
    {"SELECT '';", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "empty string"},
    {"SELECT 'it''s a ''test''';", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "multiple escaped quotes"},
    // Numeric literals
    {"SELECT 42;", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "integer literal"},
    {"SELECT 3.14;", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "float literal"},
    {"SELECT 1e10;", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "scientific notation"},
    {"SELECT 0;", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "zero"},
    // Operators
    {"SELECT * FROM t WHERE a >= 5;", 10, TOK_KEYWORD, "GE operator"},
    {"SELECT * FROM t WHERE a <> 5;", 10, TOK_KEYWORD, "NE operator (<>)"},
    {"SELECT * FROM t WHERE a != 5;", 10, TOK_KEYWORD, "NE operator (!=)"},
    {"SELECT * FROM t WHERE a <= 5;", 10, TOK_KEYWORD, "LE operator"},
    // Quoted identifiers
    {"SELECT \"First Name\" FROM t;", 6, TOK_KEYWORD, "quoted identifier"},
    // INSERT
    {"INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x');", 11, TOK_KEYWORD, "insert statement"},
    // CREATE TABLE
    {"CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL);", 16, 
     TOK_KEYWORD, "create table"},
    // Comments
    {"SELECT 1; -- comment", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "line comment ignored"},
    {"SELECT /* inline */ 1;", 4, TOK_KEYWORD, "block comment ignored"},
    // Complex
    {"SELECT a, b FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.id = t2.id WHERE a > 10 ORDER BY b;",
     22, TOK_KEYWORD, "complex join with order by"},
    // Edge cases
    {"SELECT 10-42;", 6, TOK_KEYWORD, "minus as operator not negative"},
    {"SELECT a.b;", 6, TOK_KEYWORD, "dot-separated identifier"},
    {NULL, 0, 0, NULL}
};
void run_tests(void) {
    int passed = 0, failed = 0;
    for (int i = 0; tests[i].sql != NULL; i++) {
        Lexer lex;
        lexer_init(&lex, tests[i].sql);
        Token tokens[128];
        int count = lexer_tokenize_all(&lex, tokens, 128);
        bool ok = true;
        if (count != tests[i].expected_count) {
            fprintf(stderr, "FAIL [%s]: expected %d tokens, got %d\n",
                    tests[i].description, tests[i].expected_count, count);
            ok = false;
        }
        if (count > 0 && tokens[0].type != tests[i].first_type) {
            fprintf(stderr, "FAIL [%s]: wrong first token type\n",
                    tests[i].description);
            ok = false;
        }
        if (ok) passed++;
        else failed++;
    }
    printf("Tests: %d passed, %d failed\n", passed, failed);
}
```
The test suite should also verify error cases:
```c
// Error cases
{"SELECT @;",       -1, TOK_ERROR, "unrecognized character @"},
{"SELECT 'unterminated;", -1, TOK_ERROR, "unterminated string"},
{"SELECT \"unterminated;", -1, TOK_ERROR, "unterminated quoted identifier"},
```
For error cases, verify that the error token's `line` and `col` point to the exact position of the problem.
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens When You Tokenize
To see how this fits the larger architecture, consider the three levels of a tokenization call:
**Level 1 — Public API**: You call `lexer_next_token()`. You get back a `Token` struct with type, lexeme, line, and column. Clean, simple.
**Level 2 — State Machine Engine**: Inside, the lexer dispatches on the first character, enters a handler function (identifier, number, string, operator), and that handler loops through characters using `peek` and `advance` until the token is complete. The FSM transitions are implicit in the control flow — each handler's if/while structure encodes the state transitions.
**Level 3 — Memory and I/O**: The lexer reads from a `const char*` that's already entirely in memory. There's no disk I/O, no system calls, no buffering. The `source` pointer and `current` index are all in CPU registers or L1 cache during tokenization. This is why the tokenizer is never a bottleneck — it processes characters at memory speed, which is nanoseconds per character.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What the Tokenizer Unlocks
Now that you understand how a finite state machine converts a character stream into a token stream, you've unlocked understanding across multiple domains:
**→ The Parser (Next Milestone)**: The parser will call `lexer_next_token()` repeatedly, pattern-matching on token types to build an AST. The parser's design is entirely shaped by the clean token stream you produce here. If your tokenizer correctly handles `!=` vs `<>`, the parser never needs to think about it.
**→ Network Protocol Parsing**: HTTP, DNS, Redis protocol (RESP), and every other text-based network protocol uses the same FSM approach. An HTTP parser transitions between states like `IN_METHOD`, `IN_URL`, `IN_HEADER_NAME`, `IN_HEADER_VALUE` — structurally identical to your SQL tokenizer's states. The concept transfers directly.
**→ Regular Expressions**: Every regex engine compiles your pattern into an FSM (either NFA or DFA). When you write `[0-9]+\.?[0-9]*`, the regex engine builds exactly the same kind of state machine your `lex_number` function implements by hand. Understanding FSMs from tokenizer work makes regex internals transparent.
**→ Hardware Design**: Finite state machines aren't just software. Digital circuits use FSMs for everything from traffic light controllers to CPU instruction decoders. The same state-transition table you implicitly defined in your tokenizer could be implemented in Verilog or VHDL as a hardware circuit. This is the cross-domain bridge between software engineering and electrical engineering.
**→ Error Recovery and IDE Tooling**: The line/column tracking in your tokenizer is the foundation of IDE error reporting. Language servers (LSP) run tokenizers and parsers continuously as you type, using source positions to provide syntax highlighting, error squiggles, and autocomplete suggestions. Your `Token.line` and `Token.col` fields are exactly what a language server would need.
---
## System Awareness
You've built the tokenizer — the outermost layer of the SQL frontend. It connects to the **parser** (downstream, Milestone 2), which will consume your token stream and build an AST. Nothing else in the system depends on the tokenizer directly; the abstraction boundary is clean.
Without the tokenizer, no SQL string can enter the system. It's the gate through which every query passes. In the next milestone, you'll build the parser that transforms this flat token stream into a tree — giving the bytecode compiler (Milestone 3) the structured input it needs to generate executable instructions.
---
<!-- END_MS -->


<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m2 -->
<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m2 -->
# Milestone 2: SQL Parser (AST)
## Where We Are in the System
Your tokenizer is operational. It takes raw SQL text and produces a clean stream of typed tokens — keywords, identifiers, literals, operators, punctuation. Now you need to give that flat stream *structure*.

![SQL Grammar Rule Hierarchy](./diagrams/diag-parser-grammar-rules.svg)

The parser sits between the tokenizer and the bytecode compiler. It consumes tokens and produces an **Abstract Syntax Tree** (AST) — a tree data structure that captures the *syntactic structure* of a SQL statement. The bytecode compiler (Milestone 3) will walk this tree to generate executable instructions. Without the parser, your compiler would receive a flat list of tokens with no information about which columns belong to which clause, how operators group, or where one expression ends and another begins.
---
## The Revelation: Why Flat Pattern-Matching Fails
You might think parsing is sequential pattern-matching. See `SELECT`, grab tokens until `FROM`, grab the table name, grab tokens until `WHERE`, done. This flat approach works for trivial queries:
```sql
SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 21
```
Grab `name`. Grab `users`. Grab `age > 21`. Easy. But now consider:
```sql
SELECT name FROM users WHERE (age > 21 AND status = 'active') OR (role = 'admin' AND NOT suspended)
```
Where does the first expression end? The `AND` after `21` — does it bind to `age > 21` or to the entire parenthesized group? The `OR` — does it separate two top-level conditions, or is it inside a group? The `NOT` — does it negate `suspended` or the entire clause that follows?
The answer depends on **operator precedence** and **recursive nesting**. Parentheses create sub-expressions that contain their own operators, which contain their own sub-expressions, to arbitrary depth. A flat scan cannot handle this. You need a parser that mirrors the grammar's recursive structure: each grammar rule becomes a C function, and those functions call each other.
This is **recursive-descent parsing** — the technique you'll implement in this milestone.
---
## Defining the AST Node Types
Before writing the parser, define what it produces. The AST is a tree where each node represents a syntactic construct. Here are the node types for your SQL subset:
```c
typedef enum {
    // Statements
    NODE_SELECT,
    NODE_INSERT,
    NODE_CREATE_TABLE,
    // Expressions
    NODE_BINARY_EXPR,      // a AND b, x > 5, x + y
    NODE_UNARY_EXPR,       // NOT x, -x
    NODE_LITERAL,          // 42, 3.14, 'hello', NULL
    NODE_IDENTIFIER,       // column_name, table_name
    NODE_COLUMN_REF,       // table.column (dot-separated)
    NODE_WILDCARD,         // *
    NODE_FUNCTION_CALL,    // COUNT(*), SUM(x)
    // Clauses (used as sub-nodes)
    NODE_COLUMN_DEF,       // column definition in CREATE TABLE
    NODE_ORDER_BY_ITEM,    // column + ASC/DESC
} NodeType;
```
The core structure uses a tagged union — a common C pattern where a `type` field determines which branch of a `union` is active:
```c
typedef struct AstNode AstNode;
struct AstNode {
    NodeType type;
    int line;    // source position for error reporting
    int col;
    union {
        // NODE_SELECT
        struct {
            AstNode **columns;      // array of expression nodes
            int column_count;
            char *from_table;       // table name (owned, heap-allocated)
            AstNode *where_clause;  // expression node, or NULL
            AstNode **order_by;     // array of ORDER_BY_ITEM nodes
            int order_by_count;
            int limit;              // -1 if no LIMIT
        } select;
        // NODE_INSERT
        struct {
            char *table_name;
            char **column_names;    // optional column list, or NULL
            int column_count;
            AstNode ***value_rows;  // array of arrays of expressions
            int row_count;
            int values_per_row;
        } insert;
        // NODE_CREATE_TABLE
        struct {
            char *table_name;
            AstNode **columns;      // array of NODE_COLUMN_DEF
            int column_count;
        } create_table;
        // NODE_BINARY_EXPR
        struct {
            AstNode *left;
            AstNode *right;
            TokenType op;           // TOK_EQ, TOK_GT, TOK_KEYWORD (AND/OR)
            char op_keyword[8];     // "AND", "OR" for keyword operators
        } binary;
        // NODE_UNARY_EXPR
        struct {
            AstNode *operand;
            char op_keyword[8];     // "NOT", "-"
        } unary;
        // NODE_LITERAL
        struct {
            TokenType literal_type; // TOK_INTEGER, TOK_FLOAT, TOK_STRING, TOK_KEYWORD (NULL)
            char *value;            // string representation
        } literal;
        // NODE_IDENTIFIER
        struct {
            char *name;
        } identifier;
        // NODE_COLUMN_REF (table.column)
        struct {
            char *table_name;
            char *column_name;
        } column_ref;
        // NODE_COLUMN_DEF
        struct {
            char *name;
            char *type_name;        // "INTEGER", "TEXT", "REAL", "BLOB"
            bool is_primary_key;
            bool is_not_null;
            bool is_unique;
        } column_def;
        // NODE_ORDER_BY_ITEM
        struct {
            AstNode *expr;
            bool descending;        // false = ASC (default), true = DESC
        } order_by_item;
    } data;
};
```
Two design decisions to note:
**Why a tagged union instead of separate structs?** Because the parser needs to return `AstNode*` from functions that could produce any node type. A tagged union lets you return a single pointer type and `switch` on `type` downstream. This is the standard pattern in C for representing sum types (also called variant types or discriminated unions). Languages with algebraic data types (Rust's `enum`, Haskell's data types) express this more elegantly, but in C, the tagged union is idiomatic.
**Why store `line` and `col` on every node?** Because when the bytecode compiler or a later analysis pass finds a semantic error (e.g., column doesn't exist), it needs to point back to the source location. Every AST node carries its source position, inherited from the first token that formed it.
---
## The Parser Infrastructure
The parser struct mirrors the lexer — it tracks the current position in the token stream and provides peek/advance operations:
```c
typedef struct {
    Token *tokens;      // array of all tokens from lexer
    int count;          // total number of tokens
    int current;        // index of current token
    bool had_error;     // set to true on first error
    char error_msg[256]; // human-readable error message
    int error_line;
    int error_col;
} Parser;
void parser_init(Parser *p, Token *tokens, int count) {
    p->tokens = tokens;
    p->count = count;
    p->current = 0;
    p->had_error = false;
    p->error_msg[0] = '\0';
    p->error_line = 0;
    p->error_col = 0;
}
```
The core navigation functions:
```c
static Token* peek(Parser *p) {
    if (p->current >= p->count) return NULL;
    return &p->tokens[p->current];
}
static Token* advance(Parser *p) {
    Token *tok = peek(p);
    if (tok && tok->type != TOK_EOF) {
        p->current++;
    }
    return tok;
}
static bool check(Parser *p, TokenType type) {
    Token *tok = peek(p);
    return tok && tok->type == type;
}
static bool check_keyword(Parser *p, const char *kw) {
    Token *tok = peek(p);
    if (!tok || tok->type != TOK_KEYWORD) return false;
    return strncasecmp(tok->start, kw, tok->length) == 0 
           && strlen(kw) == (size_t)tok->length;
}
static Token* expect(Parser *p, TokenType type) {
    if (check(p, type)) return advance(p);
    Token *got = peek(p);
    parser_error(p, got, "expected %s, got '%.*s'", 
                 token_type_name(type), got->length, got->start);
    return NULL;
}
static Token* expect_keyword(Parser *p, const char *kw) {
    if (check_keyword(p, kw)) return advance(p);
    Token *got = peek(p);
    parser_error(p, got, "expected '%s', got '%.*s'", 
                 kw, got->length, got->start);
    return NULL;
}
```
The `expect` function is critical — it's the mechanism that produces clear error messages. If the parser expects a closing parenthesis and finds a semicolon instead, `expect` generates: `"line 3, col 22: expected ), got ;"`. Every call to `expect` is a potential error report.

![Parser Error Reporting Flow](./diagrams/diag-parser-error-recovery.svg)

```c
static void parser_error(Parser *p, Token *tok, const char *fmt, ...) {
    if (p->had_error) return;  // report only the first error
    p->had_error = true;
    if (tok) {
        p->error_line = tok->line;
        p->error_col = tok->col;
    }
    va_list args;
    va_start(args, fmt);
    vsnprintf(p->error_msg, sizeof(p->error_msg), fmt, args);
    va_end(args);
}
```
Reporting only the first error is a pragmatic choice. After the first syntax error, the parser's state is unreliable — subsequent "errors" are often cascading noise caused by the initial mismatch. Production parsers implement sophisticated error recovery (synchronizing to the next semicolon or statement keyword), but for your build, stopping at the first error is sufficient and avoids misleading diagnostics.
---
## The Grammar: From Rules to Functions

> **🔑 Foundation: Pratt parsing / precedence climbing**
> 
> ## Pratt Parsing / Precedence Climbing
**What it is:** Pratt parsing (also called "top-down operator precedence parsing") is a technique for parsing expressions that handles operator precedence and associativity without needing a separate grammar rule for every precedence level. Instead of writing rules like `term → factor (("+" | "-") factor)*` and `factor → unary (("*" | "/") unary)*` — one per level — you assign each operator a numeric **binding power** and use a single recursive function that decides when to "keep going" versus "stop and return."
The core loop looks like this (pseudocode):
```
function parse_expression(min_binding_power):
    left = parse_prefix_token()     // a number, variable, or prefix op
    while next_operator.binding_power >= min_binding_power:
        op = consume_operator()
        right = parse_expression(op.right_binding_power)
        left = make_binary_node(left, op, right)
    return left
```
When you call `parse_expression(0)`, it parses a full expression. The magic is in the recursive call: by passing a *higher* minimum binding power, you ensure that tighter-binding operators (like `*`) grab their operands before looser ones (like `+`). For example, `2 + 3 * 4` correctly becomes `2 + (3 * 4)` because `*` has binding power 6 vs `+`'s 4 — when parsing the right side of `+`, the inner call sees `*` and claims `3 * 4` before returning.
**Why you need it now:** You're building a database, and SQL has expressions everywhere — `WHERE` clauses, computed columns, `HAVING` conditions. These expressions involve arithmetic (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`), comparisons (`=`, `<`, `>`), logical operators (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`), and potentially even `BETWEEN`, `LIKE`, and `IS NULL`. A Pratt parser lets you handle all of these with one function and a table of binding powers, rather than a tangled mess of mutually recursive grammar rules. Adding a new operator later is just one table entry.
**Key insight:** Think of binding power as "gravitational pull." Higher binding power means an operator pulls its operands closer. When two operators compete for the same operand (like `+` and `*` both wanting `3` in `2 + 3 * 4`), the one with stronger gravity wins. Left-associativity vs right-associativity is handled by a small asymmetry: for left-associative operators, the right-side recursive call uses `binding_power + 1`; for right-associative ones (like exponentiation), it uses `binding_power` unchanged — letting the same operator "win" again on the right.

Recursive-descent parsing is a direct encoding of a grammar. Each grammar rule becomes a C function. Here is the grammar your parser implements, written in a notation called **BNF** (Backus-Naur Form) — a standard way to describe programming language syntax where `::=` means "is defined as," `|` means "or," and `[...]` means "optional":
```
statement       ::= select_stmt | insert_stmt | create_table_stmt
select_stmt     ::= SELECT column_list FROM IDENTIFIER
                     [WHERE expression]
                     [ORDER BY order_list]
                     [LIMIT INTEGER]
insert_stmt     ::= INSERT INTO IDENTIFIER [(column_list)] 
                     VALUES value_tuple_list
create_table_stmt ::= CREATE TABLE IDENTIFIER (column_def_list)
column_list     ::= '*' | expression (',' expression)*
order_list      ::= order_item (',' order_item)*
order_item      ::= expression [ASC | DESC]
value_tuple_list ::= '(' expression_list ')' (',' '(' expression_list ')')*
column_def_list ::= column_def (',' column_def)*
column_def      ::= IDENTIFIER type_name [constraints]
type_name       ::= INTEGER | TEXT | REAL | BLOB
constraints     ::= (PRIMARY KEY | NOT NULL | UNIQUE)*
expression      ::= or_expr
or_expr         ::= and_expr (OR and_expr)*
and_expr        ::= not_expr (AND not_expr)*
not_expr        ::= NOT not_expr | comparison
comparison      ::= addition ((= | < | > | <= | >= | != | <>) addition)?
addition        ::= primary ((+ | -) primary)*
primary         ::= INTEGER | FLOAT | STRING | NULL | IDENTIFIER
                   | IDENTIFIER '.' IDENTIFIER
                   | '(' expression ')'
```

![AST for a Complex SELECT](./diagrams/diag-parser-ast-example.svg)

Notice the expression grammar's structure. It's layered: `or_expr` calls `and_expr`, which calls `not_expr`, which calls `comparison`, which calls `addition`, which calls `primary`. This layering encodes operator precedence — operators lower in the call chain bind tighter. `NOT` binds tighter than `AND`, which binds tighter than `OR`, because `not_expr` is called from inside `and_expr`, which is called from inside `or_expr`. A deeper call means higher precedence.
This is **precedence climbing** — one of the two standard techniques for handling operator precedence in recursive-descent parsers. The alternative is Pratt parsing (described in the Foundation block above), which uses a numeric precedence table rather than separate functions per level. Both produce identical ASTs. Precedence climbing is easier to understand when each level has a clear semantic meaning (like SQL's `OR` → `AND` → `NOT` → comparison hierarchy), so that's what you'll implement.
---
## Parsing SELECT Statements
The `SELECT` parser is the most complex because it has the most optional clauses. Here's the function, built directly from the grammar rule:
```c
static AstNode* parse_select(Parser *p) {
    Token *select_tok = expect_keyword(p, "SELECT");
    if (!select_tok) return NULL;
    AstNode *node = ast_alloc(NODE_SELECT, select_tok->line, select_tok->col);
    // Parse column list
    if (check(p, TOK_STAR)) {
        // SELECT *
        Token *star = advance(p);
        node->data.select.columns = malloc(sizeof(AstNode*));
        node->data.select.columns[0] = ast_alloc(NODE_WILDCARD, 
                                                   star->line, star->col);
        node->data.select.column_count = 1;
    } else {
        // SELECT col1, col2, ...
        int capacity = 8;
        node->data.select.columns = malloc(capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
        node->data.select.column_count = 0;
        do {
            AstNode *col_expr = parse_expression(p);
            if (!col_expr) return NULL;
            if (node->data.select.column_count >= capacity) {
                capacity *= 2;
                node->data.select.columns = realloc(
                    node->data.select.columns, capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
            }
            node->data.select.columns[node->data.select.column_count++] = col_expr;
        } while (check(p, TOK_COMMA) && advance(p));
    }
    // FROM clause (required)
    if (!expect_keyword(p, "FROM")) return NULL;
    Token *table_tok = expect(p, TOK_IDENTIFIER);
    if (!table_tok) return NULL;
    node->data.select.from_table = token_to_string(table_tok);
    // WHERE clause (optional)
    node->data.select.where_clause = NULL;
    if (check_keyword(p, "WHERE")) {
        advance(p);
        node->data.select.where_clause = parse_expression(p);
        if (!node->data.select.where_clause) return NULL;
    }
    // ORDER BY clause (optional)
    node->data.select.order_by = NULL;
    node->data.select.order_by_count = 0;
    if (check_keyword(p, "ORDER")) {
        advance(p);
        if (!expect_keyword(p, "BY")) return NULL;
        int capacity = 4;
        node->data.select.order_by = malloc(capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
        do {
            AstNode *item = ast_alloc(NODE_ORDER_BY_ITEM, 
                                       peek(p)->line, peek(p)->col);
            item->data.order_by_item.expr = parse_expression(p);
            if (!item->data.order_by_item.expr) return NULL;
            item->data.order_by_item.descending = false;
            if (check_keyword(p, "DESC")) {
                advance(p);
                item->data.order_by_item.descending = true;
            } else if (check_keyword(p, "ASC")) {
                advance(p);
            }
            if (node->data.select.order_by_count >= capacity) {
                capacity *= 2;
                node->data.select.order_by = realloc(
                    node->data.select.order_by, capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
            }
            node->data.select.order_by[node->data.select.order_by_count++] = item;
        } while (check(p, TOK_COMMA) && advance(p));
    }
    // LIMIT clause (optional)
    node->data.select.limit = -1;
    if (check_keyword(p, "LIMIT")) {
        advance(p);
        Token *limit_tok = expect(p, TOK_INTEGER);
        if (!limit_tok) return NULL;
        node->data.select.limit = token_to_int(limit_tok);
    }
    return node;
}
```
Study the structure. Each optional clause follows the same pattern: check if the keyword is present, advance past it if so, parse what follows. The parser never backtracks — it looks at the current token, decides what to do, and moves forward. This is possible because SQL's syntax is designed so that the next keyword always tells you which clause is coming. `WHERE` means a filter follows. `ORDER` means sorting follows. `LIMIT` means a row cap follows. No ambiguity.
The helper `token_to_string` copies the token's lexeme into a heap-allocated string:
```c
static char* token_to_string(Token *tok) {
    char *s = malloc(tok->length + 1);
    memcpy(s, tok->start, tok->length);
    s[tok->length] = '\0';
    // Strip quotes if it's a quoted identifier
    if (s[0] == '"' && s[tok->length - 1] == '"') {
        memmove(s, s + 1, tok->length - 2);
        s[tok->length - 2] = '\0';
    }
    return s;
}
```
---
## Parsing INSERT Statements
INSERT is more straightforward. The grammar is fixed: `INSERT INTO table [(columns)] VALUES (values)`.
```c
static AstNode* parse_insert(Parser *p) {
    Token *insert_tok = expect_keyword(p, "INSERT");
    if (!insert_tok) return NULL;
    if (!expect_keyword(p, "INTO")) return NULL;
    Token *table_tok = expect(p, TOK_IDENTIFIER);
    if (!table_tok) return NULL;
    AstNode *node = ast_alloc(NODE_INSERT, insert_tok->line, insert_tok->col);
    node->data.insert.table_name = token_to_string(table_tok);
    node->data.insert.column_names = NULL;
    node->data.insert.column_count = 0;
    // Optional column list: INSERT INTO t (col1, col2) VALUES ...
    if (check(p, TOK_LPAREN) && !check_keyword_ahead(p, "VALUES", 1)) {
        advance(p);  // consume (
        int capacity = 8;
        node->data.insert.column_names = malloc(capacity * sizeof(char*));
        do {
            Token *col = expect(p, TOK_IDENTIFIER);
            if (!col) return NULL;
            if (node->data.insert.column_count >= capacity) {
                capacity *= 2;
                node->data.insert.column_names = realloc(
                    node->data.insert.column_names, capacity * sizeof(char*));
            }
            node->data.insert.column_names[node->data.insert.column_count++] = 
                token_to_string(col);
        } while (check(p, TOK_COMMA) && advance(p));
        if (!expect(p, TOK_RPAREN)) return NULL;
    }
    // VALUES clause
    if (!expect_keyword(p, "VALUES")) return NULL;
    int row_capacity = 4;
    node->data.insert.value_rows = malloc(row_capacity * sizeof(AstNode**));
    node->data.insert.row_count = 0;
    do {
        if (!expect(p, TOK_LPAREN)) return NULL;
        int val_capacity = 8;
        AstNode **values = malloc(val_capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
        int val_count = 0;
        do {
            AstNode *expr = parse_expression(p);
            if (!expr) return NULL;
            if (val_count >= val_capacity) {
                val_capacity *= 2;
                values = realloc(values, val_capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
            }
            values[val_count++] = expr;
        } while (check(p, TOK_COMMA) && advance(p));
        if (!expect(p, TOK_RPAREN)) return NULL;
        if (node->data.insert.row_count >= row_capacity) {
            row_capacity *= 2;
            node->data.insert.value_rows = realloc(
                node->data.insert.value_rows, row_capacity * sizeof(AstNode**));
        }
        node->data.insert.value_rows[node->data.insert.row_count] = values;
        node->data.insert.values_per_row = val_count;
        node->data.insert.row_count++;
    } while (check(p, TOK_COMMA) && advance(p));
    return node;
}
```
The tricky part is distinguishing the optional column list from the VALUES keyword. When the parser sees `(` after the table name, it could be the start of a column list `(col1, col2)` or the parser might be looking at `VALUES (1, 2)` where the `(` belongs to the VALUES clause. The `check_keyword_ahead` helper peeks past the current token to see if VALUES follows:
```c
static bool check_keyword_ahead(Parser *p, const char *kw, int offset) {
    int idx = p->current + offset;
    if (idx >= p->count) return false;
    Token *tok = &p->tokens[idx];
    return tok->type == TOK_KEYWORD && 
           strncasecmp(tok->start, kw, tok->length) == 0 &&
           strlen(kw) == (size_t)tok->length;
}
```
This limited lookahead — peeking one or two tokens ahead — is sometimes necessary in recursive-descent parsers. SQL's grammar is almost **LL(1)** (parseable with a single token of lookahead), but a few constructs like this optional column list need an extra peek.
---
## Parsing CREATE TABLE Statements
CREATE TABLE parses column definitions, each with a name, type, and optional constraints:
```c
static AstNode* parse_create_table(Parser *p) {
    Token *create_tok = expect_keyword(p, "CREATE");
    if (!create_tok) return NULL;
    if (!expect_keyword(p, "TABLE")) return NULL;
    Token *table_tok = expect(p, TOK_IDENTIFIER);
    if (!table_tok) return NULL;
    AstNode *node = ast_alloc(NODE_CREATE_TABLE, 
                               create_tok->line, create_tok->col);
    node->data.create_table.table_name = token_to_string(table_tok);
    if (!expect(p, TOK_LPAREN)) return NULL;
    int capacity = 8;
    node->data.create_table.columns = malloc(capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
    node->data.create_table.column_count = 0;
    do {
        AstNode *col = parse_column_def(p);
        if (!col) return NULL;
        if (node->data.create_table.column_count >= capacity) {
            capacity *= 2;
            node->data.create_table.columns = realloc(
                node->data.create_table.columns, capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
        }
        node->data.create_table.columns[node->data.create_table.column_count++] = col;
    } while (check(p, TOK_COMMA) && advance(p));
    if (!expect(p, TOK_RPAREN)) return NULL;
    return node;
}
static AstNode* parse_column_def(Parser *p) {
    Token *name_tok = expect(p, TOK_IDENTIFIER);
    if (!name_tok) return NULL;
    AstNode *node = ast_alloc(NODE_COLUMN_DEF, name_tok->line, name_tok->col);
    node->data.column_def.name = token_to_string(name_tok);
    node->data.column_def.is_primary_key = false;
    node->data.column_def.is_not_null = false;
    node->data.column_def.is_unique = false;
    // Type name (required)
    Token *type_tok = expect(p, TOK_KEYWORD);
    if (!type_tok) return NULL;
    node->data.column_def.type_name = token_to_string(type_tok);
    // Constraints (optional, can appear in any order)
    while (true) {
        if (check_keyword(p, "PRIMARY")) {
            advance(p);
            if (!expect_keyword(p, "KEY")) return NULL;
            node->data.column_def.is_primary_key = true;
            node->data.column_def.is_not_null = true; // PK implies NOT NULL
        } else if (check_keyword(p, "NOT")) {
            advance(p);
            if (!expect_keyword(p, "NULL")) return NULL;
            node->data.column_def.is_not_null = true;
        } else if (check_keyword(p, "UNIQUE")) {
            advance(p);
            node->data.column_def.is_unique = true;
        } else {
            break;
        }
    }
    return node;
}
```
Notice how constraints are parsed in a loop. A column can have multiple constraints in any order: `id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL`, `name TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL`. The loop keeps consuming constraint keywords until it sees something that isn't a constraint (a comma, a closing paren, or EOF).
---
## The Expression Parser: Precedence by Structure
This is the heart of the parser — and the part most likely to go wrong. SQL operator precedence, from lowest to highest:
| Precedence | Operators | Associativity |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 (lowest) | OR | Left |
| 2 | AND | Left |
| 3 | NOT | Right (unary) |
| 4 | =, <, >, <=, >=, !=, <>, IS | Left |
| 5 (highest) | +, - | Left |
Each precedence level becomes a function. Lower precedence = called first (outer). Higher precedence = called last (inner). The outermost function is the entry point:
```c
static AstNode* parse_expression(Parser *p) {
    return parse_or_expr(p);
}
```

![Operator Precedence Climbing](./diagrams/diag-parser-precedence-climbing.svg)

**Level 1: OR expressions**
```c
static AstNode* parse_or_expr(Parser *p) {
    AstNode *left = parse_and_expr(p);
    if (!left) return NULL;
    while (check_keyword(p, "OR")) {
        Token *op_tok = advance(p);
        AstNode *right = parse_and_expr(p);
        if (!right) return NULL;
        AstNode *bin = ast_alloc(NODE_BINARY_EXPR, op_tok->line, op_tok->col);
        bin->data.binary.left = left;
        bin->data.binary.right = right;
        bin->data.binary.op = TOK_KEYWORD;
        strncpy(bin->data.binary.op_keyword, "OR", 8);
        left = bin;
    }
    return left;
}
```
The `while` loop handles left-associativity. The expression `a OR b OR c` parses as `(a OR b) OR c`. Each iteration wraps the accumulated `left` node with a new `right` operand, building the tree leftward.
**Level 2: AND expressions**
```c
static AstNode* parse_and_expr(Parser *p) {
    AstNode *left = parse_not_expr(p);
    if (!left) return NULL;
    while (check_keyword(p, "AND")) {
        Token *op_tok = advance(p);
        AstNode *right = parse_not_expr(p);
        if (!right) return NULL;
        AstNode *bin = ast_alloc(NODE_BINARY_EXPR, op_tok->line, op_tok->col);
        bin->data.binary.left = left;
        bin->data.binary.right = right;
        bin->data.binary.op = TOK_KEYWORD;
        strncpy(bin->data.binary.op_keyword, "AND", 8);
        left = bin;
    }
    return left;
}
```
Identical structure to OR, but calls `parse_not_expr` instead of `parse_and_expr`. This nesting is what makes AND bind tighter than OR. When parsing `a OR b AND c`, `parse_or_expr` calls `parse_and_expr` for its right operand. `parse_and_expr` grabs `b AND c` as a single unit before returning, so OR sees it as: `a OR (b AND c)`. The tree structure encodes the precedence without any explicit precedence table.
**Level 3: NOT (unary)**
```c
static AstNode* parse_not_expr(Parser *p) {
    if (check_keyword(p, "NOT")) {
        Token *not_tok = advance(p);
        AstNode *operand = parse_not_expr(p);  // recursive: NOT NOT x is valid
        if (!operand) return NULL;
        AstNode *unary = ast_alloc(NODE_UNARY_EXPR, not_tok->line, not_tok->col);
        unary->data.unary.operand = operand;
        strncpy(unary->data.unary.op_keyword, "NOT", 8);
        return unary;
    }
    return parse_comparison(p);
}
```
NOT is right-associative and unary. `NOT NOT x` parses as `NOT (NOT x)` via the recursive call. If there's no NOT, it falls through to comparison.
**Level 4: Comparison operators**
```c
static AstNode* parse_comparison(Parser *p) {
    AstNode *left = parse_addition(p);
    if (!left) return NULL;
    // IS NULL / IS NOT NULL
    if (check_keyword(p, "IS")) {
        Token *is_tok = advance(p);
        bool negated = false;
        if (check_keyword(p, "NOT")) {
            advance(p);
            negated = true;
        }
        if (!expect_keyword(p, "NULL")) return NULL;
        AstNode *null_lit = ast_alloc(NODE_LITERAL, is_tok->line, is_tok->col);
        null_lit->data.literal.literal_type = TOK_KEYWORD;
        null_lit->data.literal.value = strdup("NULL");
        AstNode *bin = ast_alloc(NODE_BINARY_EXPR, is_tok->line, is_tok->col);
        bin->data.binary.left = left;
        bin->data.binary.right = null_lit;
        bin->data.binary.op = TOK_EQ;
        strncpy(bin->data.binary.op_keyword, negated ? "ISNOT" : "IS", 8);
        return bin;
    }
    // Standard comparison operators
    if (check(p, TOK_EQ) || check(p, TOK_NE) || check(p, TOK_LT) ||
        check(p, TOK_GT) || check(p, TOK_LE) || check(p, TOK_GE)) {
        Token *op_tok = advance(p);
        AstNode *right = parse_addition(p);
        if (!right) return NULL;
        AstNode *bin = ast_alloc(NODE_BINARY_EXPR, op_tok->line, op_tok->col);
        bin->data.binary.left = left;
        bin->data.binary.right = right;
        bin->data.binary.op = op_tok->type;
        bin->data.binary.op_keyword[0] = '\0';
        return bin;
    }
    return left;
}
```
Note that comparison uses `if` (not `while`) — comparisons are not chainable. `a < b < c` is a syntax error in SQL, not a valid expression. This differs from arithmetic, where `a + b + c` is valid.
**Level 5: Addition/Subtraction**
```c
static AstNode* parse_addition(Parser *p) {
    AstNode *left = parse_primary(p);
    if (!left) return NULL;
    while (check(p, TOK_PLUS) || check(p, TOK_MINUS)) {
        Token *op_tok = advance(p);
        AstNode *right = parse_primary(p);
        if (!right) return NULL;
        AstNode *bin = ast_alloc(NODE_BINARY_EXPR, op_tok->line, op_tok->col);
        bin->data.binary.left = left;
        bin->data.binary.right = right;
        bin->data.binary.op = op_tok->type;
        bin->data.binary.op_keyword[0] = '\0';
        left = bin;
    }
    return left;
}
```
**Level 6: Primary expressions (leaves of the expression tree)**
```c
static AstNode* parse_primary(Parser *p) {
    Token *tok = peek(p);
    if (!tok) {
        parser_error(p, tok, "unexpected end of input in expression");
        return NULL;
    }
    // NULL keyword → literal, not identifier
    if (check_keyword(p, "NULL")) {
        advance(p);
        AstNode *node = ast_alloc(NODE_LITERAL, tok->line, tok->col);
        node->data.literal.literal_type = TOK_KEYWORD;
        node->data.literal.value = strdup("NULL");
        return node;
    }
    // Numeric literals
    if (check(p, TOK_INTEGER) || check(p, TOK_FLOAT)) {
        advance(p);
        AstNode *node = ast_alloc(NODE_LITERAL, tok->line, tok->col);
        node->data.literal.literal_type = tok->type;
        node->data.literal.value = token_to_string(tok);
        return node;
    }
    // String literals
    if (check(p, TOK_STRING)) {
        advance(p);
        AstNode *node = ast_alloc(NODE_LITERAL, tok->line, tok->col);
        node->data.literal.literal_type = TOK_STRING;
        node->data.literal.value = token_to_string(tok);
        return node;
    }
    // Parenthesized expression
    if (check(p, TOK_LPAREN)) {
        advance(p);
        AstNode *inner = parse_expression(p);
        if (!inner) return NULL;
        if (!expect(p, TOK_RPAREN)) return NULL;
        return inner;
    }
    // Star (wildcard) — handled in SELECT column list, but also valid here
    if (check(p, TOK_STAR)) {
        advance(p);
        return ast_alloc(NODE_WILDCARD, tok->line, tok->col);
    }
    // Identifier (possibly dot-qualified: table.column)
    if (check(p, TOK_IDENTIFIER)) {
        advance(p);
        // Check for table.column
        if (check(p, TOK_DOT)) {
            advance(p);
            Token *col_tok = expect(p, TOK_IDENTIFIER);
            if (!col_tok) return NULL;
            AstNode *node = ast_alloc(NODE_COLUMN_REF, tok->line, tok->col);
            node->data.column_ref.table_name = token_to_string(tok);
```
