```c
            node->data.column_ref.column_name = token_to_string(col_tok);
            return node;
        }
        AstNode *node = ast_alloc(NODE_IDENTIFIER, tok->line, tok->col);
        node->data.identifier.name = token_to_string(tok);
        return node;
    }
    parser_error(p, tok, "unexpected token '%.*s' in expression", 
                 tok->length, tok->start);
    return NULL;
}
```
**The critical `NULL` check.** Notice that `NULL` is checked *before* identifiers, even though `NULL` is a keyword token. This is deliberate. The SQL keyword `NULL` represents the null value — a literal, not a column reference. If you parsed it as an identifier, your VM would later try to look up a column named "NULL", which doesn't exist. This is a common parsing bug in SQL implementations.
**Parenthesized expressions.** When the parser encounters `(`, it calls `parse_expression` recursively — which starts the entire precedence chain from OR again. This is how parentheses override precedence: `(a OR b) AND c` forces OR to bind first because the parenthesized sub-expression is fully resolved before AND sees it. The parentheses themselves don't appear in the AST — they're structural brackets that influence tree shape, not semantic nodes.
---
## Tracing Through a Complex Expression
Let's trace the expression parser on: `age > 21 AND status = 'active' OR role = 'admin'`
1. `parse_expression()` → `parse_or_expr()`
2. `parse_or_expr()` calls `parse_and_expr()` for left side
3. `parse_and_expr()` calls `parse_not_expr()` → `parse_comparison()` → `parse_addition()` → `parse_primary()` → identifier `age`
4. Back in `parse_comparison()`: sees `>`, consumes it, parses right side → literal `21`
5. Returns binary node `(age > 21)` to `parse_and_expr()`
6. `parse_and_expr()` sees `AND`, consumes it, calls `parse_not_expr()` again
7. Eventually returns binary node `(status = 'active')`
8. `parse_and_expr()` wraps both in `((age > 21) AND (status = 'active'))`, returns to `parse_or_expr()`
9. `parse_or_expr()` sees `OR`, consumes it, calls `parse_and_expr()` again
10. Gets `(role = 'admin')`, wraps everything in `(((age > 21) AND (status = 'active')) OR (role = 'admin'))`
The resulting AST:
```
        OR
       /  \
     AND   (role = 'admin')
    /    \
(age>21)  (status='active')
```
AND binds tighter than OR. The tree structure makes this unambiguous — the bytecode compiler will evaluate the AND first, then the OR, regardless of the textual order.
---
## The Top-Level Dispatcher
The parser's entry point dispatches to the correct statement parser based on the first keyword:
```c
AstNode* parser_parse(Parser *p) {
    Token *tok = peek(p);
    if (!tok || tok->type == TOK_EOF) {
        parser_error(p, tok, "empty input");
        return NULL;
    }
    AstNode *result = NULL;
    if (check_keyword(p, "SELECT")) {
        result = parse_select(p);
    } else if (check_keyword(p, "INSERT")) {
        result = parse_insert(p);
    } else if (check_keyword(p, "CREATE")) {
        result = parse_create_table(p);
    } else if (check_keyword(p, "EXPLAIN")) {
        // EXPLAIN wraps another statement
        advance(p);
        result = parser_parse(p);  // recursive: parse the inner statement
        // Mark it as EXPLAIN'd (your compiler checks this flag)
    } else {
        parser_error(p, tok, "expected SELECT, INSERT, CREATE, or EXPLAIN");
    }
    // Optional semicolon
    if (result && check(p, TOK_SEMICOLON)) {
        advance(p);
    }
    return result;
}
```
---
## Memory Management: AST Allocation
Every AST node is heap-allocated. The simplest approach for a learning project: use a linear arena that frees everything at once when the AST is no longer needed.
```c
static AstNode* ast_alloc(NodeType type, int line, int col) {
    AstNode *node = calloc(1, sizeof(AstNode));
    node->type = type;
    node->line = line;
    node->col = col;
    return node;
}
void ast_free(AstNode *node) {
    if (!node) return;
    switch (node->type) {
        case NODE_SELECT:
            for (int i = 0; i < node->data.select.column_count; i++)
                ast_free(node->data.select.columns[i]);
            free(node->data.select.columns);
            free(node->data.select.from_table);
            ast_free(node->data.select.where_clause);
            for (int i = 0; i < node->data.select.order_by_count; i++)
                ast_free(node->data.select.order_by[i]);
            free(node->data.select.order_by);
            break;
        case NODE_INSERT:
            free(node->data.insert.table_name);
            for (int i = 0; i < node->data.insert.column_count; i++)
                free(node->data.insert.column_names[i]);
            free(node->data.insert.column_names);
            for (int i = 0; i < node->data.insert.row_count; i++) {
                for (int j = 0; j < node->data.insert.values_per_row; j++)
                    ast_free(node->data.insert.value_rows[i][j]);
                free(node->data.insert.value_rows[i]);
            }
            free(node->data.insert.value_rows);
            break;
        case NODE_BINARY_EXPR:
            ast_free(node->data.binary.left);
            ast_free(node->data.binary.right);
            break;
        case NODE_UNARY_EXPR:
            ast_free(node->data.unary.operand);
            break;
        case NODE_LITERAL:
            free(node->data.literal.value);
            break;
        case NODE_IDENTIFIER:
            free(node->data.identifier.name);
            break;
        // ... similar for other types
        default:
            break;
    }
    free(node);
}
```
> **Production alternative:** SQLite uses a single memory allocation pool per SQL statement. All AST nodes, strings, and compiler temporaries are allocated from the pool. When the statement finishes, the entire pool is freed in one call — no need to walk the tree. This is called **arena allocation** or **region-based memory management**. It's dramatically faster than individual `free()` calls for trees with many small nodes. For your build, individual `calloc`/`free` per node is fine — the AST is small and short-lived.
---
## Integrating the Tokenizer and Parser
The pipeline from SQL string to AST is two function calls:
```c
AstNode* sql_parse(const char *sql) {
    // Step 1: Tokenize
    Lexer lex;
    lexer_init(&lex, sql);
    Token tokens[1024];
    int count = lexer_tokenize_all(&lex, tokens, 1024);
    if (count > 0 && tokens[count-1].type == TOK_ERROR) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Lexer error: %.*s at line %d, col %d\n",
                tokens[count-1].length, tokens[count-1].start,
                tokens[count-1].line, tokens[count-1].col);
        return NULL;
    }
    // Step 2: Parse
    Parser parser;
    parser_init(&parser, tokens, count);
    AstNode *ast = parser_parse(&parser);
    if (parser.had_error) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Parse error at line %d, col %d: %s\n",
                parser.error_line, parser.error_col, parser.error_msg);
        return NULL;
    }
    return ast;
}
```
This is the abstraction boundary at work. The parser never calls `peek` on the `Lexer` — it only reads from the `Token` array. The tokenizer's internal state machine is invisible. And the bytecode compiler (Milestone 3) will call `sql_parse()` and receive an AST — it never sees tokens or raw SQL text.
---
## Building the Test Suite
Your acceptance criteria require 15+ valid and 10+ invalid statements. Here's a testing framework:
```c
typedef struct {
    const char *sql;
    NodeType expected_type;
    bool should_succeed;
    const char *description;
} ParseTest;
ParseTest parse_tests[] = {
    // Valid SELECT statements
    {"SELECT * FROM t;", NODE_SELECT, true, "select star"},
    {"SELECT a, b FROM t;", NODE_SELECT, true, "select columns"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t WHERE x > 5;", NODE_SELECT, true, "select with where"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t WHERE x > 5 AND y = 3;", NODE_SELECT, true, "where with AND"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t WHERE x > 5 OR y = 3;", NODE_SELECT, true, "where with OR"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t WHERE NOT x > 5;", NODE_SELECT, true, "where with NOT"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t WHERE (x > 5 OR y = 3) AND z = 1;", 
     NODE_SELECT, true, "parenthesized expression"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t ORDER BY a ASC;", NODE_SELECT, true, "order by asc"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t ORDER BY a DESC;", NODE_SELECT, true, "order by desc"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t LIMIT 10;", NODE_SELECT, true, "limit"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t WHERE x IS NULL;", NODE_SELECT, true, "is null"},
    {"SELECT a FROM t WHERE x IS NOT NULL;", NODE_SELECT, true, "is not null"},
    {"SELECT t1.a FROM t1;", NODE_SELECT, true, "dot-qualified column"},
    // Valid INSERT statements
    {"INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x');", NODE_INSERT, true, "simple insert"},
    {"INSERT INTO t (a, b) VALUES (1, 'x');", NODE_INSERT, true, 
     "insert with columns"},
    {"INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y');", NODE_INSERT, true, 
     "insert multiple rows"},
    // Valid CREATE TABLE
    {"CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL);", 
     NODE_CREATE_TABLE, true, "create table"},
    {"CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER, val REAL UNIQUE);", 
     NODE_CREATE_TABLE, true, "create with unique"},
    // Invalid statements (should fail with error)
    {"SELECT;", 0, false, "select missing columns"},
    {"SELECT * FROM;", 0, false, "from missing table"},
    {"SELECT * FROM t WHERE;", 0, false, "where missing expression"},
    {"SELECT * FROM t WHERE x >;", 0, false, "comparison missing right operand"},
    {"INSERT INTO;", 0, false, "insert missing table"},
    {"INSERT INTO t VALUES;", 0, false, "values missing parens"},
    {"INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, );", 0, false, "trailing comma in values"},
    {"CREATE TABLE;", 0, false, "create missing table name"},
    {"CREATE TABLE t;", 0, false, "create missing column defs"},
    {"CREATE TABLE t (id);", 0, false, "column missing type"},
    {"FROM t SELECT *;", 0, false, "wrong keyword order"},
    {"SELECT * FROM t WHERE (x > 5;", 0, false, "mismatched parenthesis"},
    {NULL, 0, false, NULL}
};
void run_parse_tests(void) {
    int passed = 0, failed = 0;
    for (int i = 0; parse_tests[i].sql != NULL; i++) {
        AstNode *ast = sql_parse(parse_tests[i].sql);
        bool succeeded = (ast != NULL);
        if (succeeded != parse_tests[i].should_succeed) {
            fprintf(stderr, "FAIL [%s]: expected %s, got %s\n",
                    parse_tests[i].description,
                    parse_tests[i].should_succeed ? "success" : "failure",
                    succeeded ? "success" : "failure");
            failed++;
        } else if (succeeded && ast->type != parse_tests[i].expected_type) {
            fprintf(stderr, "FAIL [%s]: wrong node type\n",
                    parse_tests[i].description);
            failed++;
        } else {
            passed++;
        }
        ast_free(ast);
    }
    printf("Parse tests: %d passed, %d failed\n", passed, failed);
}
```
For more rigorous validation, write helper functions that inspect specific AST fields:
```c
void test_select_where_precedence(void) {
    AstNode *ast = sql_parse("SELECT a FROM t WHERE x > 5 AND y = 3 OR z = 1;");
    assert(ast && ast->type == NODE_SELECT);
    // Top level of WHERE should be OR
    AstNode *where = ast->data.select.where_clause;
    assert(where->type == NODE_BINARY_EXPR);
    assert(strcmp(where->data.binary.op_keyword, "OR") == 0);
    // Left of OR should be AND
    AstNode *left = where->data.binary.left;
    assert(left->type == NODE_BINARY_EXPR);
    assert(strcmp(left->data.binary.op_keyword, "AND") == 0);
    // Right of OR should be (z = 1)
    AstNode *right = where->data.binary.right;
    assert(right->type == NODE_BINARY_EXPR);
    assert(right->data.binary.op == TOK_EQ);
    printf("PASS: precedence test (AND binds tighter than OR)\n");
    ast_free(ast);
}
```
This test directly verifies the most important property of your expression parser: that AND binds tighter than OR.
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens During Parsing
**Level 1 — Public API**: You call `sql_parse("SELECT ...")`. You get back an `AstNode*` representing the structured statement, or `NULL` with an error message.
**Level 2 — Recursive-Descent Engine**: Inside, the parser dispatches to `parse_select`, which calls `parse_expression` for the WHERE clause, which calls `parse_or_expr` → `parse_and_expr` → `parse_not_expr` → `parse_comparison` → `parse_addition` → `parse_primary`, building a tree bottom-up as each function returns its subtree to the caller.
**Level 3 — Memory**: Each `ast_alloc` call invokes `calloc`, which asks the OS for a page of heap memory (typically 4096 bytes, fitting many small nodes). The token array — the parser's input — is stack-allocated and points into the original SQL string in the caller's memory. No disk I/O, no system calls beyond heap allocation. The entire parsing phase is CPU-bound and cache-friendly.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What the Parser Unlocks
**→ The Bytecode Compiler (Next Milestone)**: The compiler will walk your AST using a recursive visitor: `compile_node(AstNode *node)` dispatches on `node->type`, calling `compile_select`, `compile_insert`, etc. Each AST node type maps to a bytecode generation pattern. The AST is the **intermediate representation (IR)** — a concept you'll see everywhere in compilation. The same AST-as-IR pattern powers JavaScript engines (V8 compiles AST → bytecode → machine code), linters (ESLint walks the AST to check rules), and formatters (Prettier walks the AST to emit formatted text).

> **🔑 Foundation: Write-ahead logging theory**
> 
> ## Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) Theory
**What it is:** Write-ahead logging is a durability and recovery protocol. The rule is deceptively simple: **before any change hits the actual database file, a record of that change must first be written (and fsynced) to a sequential log file.** That's the "write-ahead" part — the log is always ahead of the data.
Each log record typically contains:
- A transaction ID
- Which page/record was modified
- The **before-image** (old value) and **after-image** (new value)
- A log sequence number (LSN) for ordering
On crash recovery, the system replays the log:
1. **Redo** — scan forward, reapply changes from committed transactions that might not have reached the database file yet.
2. **Undo** — scan backward, reverse changes from transactions that were active (uncommitted) at crash time.
This is the classic **ARIES** algorithm (Redo history, Undo losers).
**Why you need it now:** Without WAL, your database has two bad options: either flush every write to disk immediately (catastrophically slow) or buffer writes in memory and lose them on crash (unacceptable). WAL gives you both performance *and* durability. Dirty pages can linger in a memory buffer cache while the log — which is append-only and sequential — provides the crash safety guarantee. This is the foundation that makes transactions actually *durable* (the D in ACID).
**Key insight:** WAL exploits a hardware asymmetry: **sequential writes are dramatically faster than random writes.** The database file has random-access updates scattered across pages, but the log is purely append-only. By ensuring the log hits disk first, you convert the expensive random I/O into cheap sequential I/O for the critical path. The actual database pages can be flushed lazily in the background. Think of the log as a "receipt" — as long as you have the receipt, you can always reconstruct what happened, even if the shelves (data pages) are in disarray.


> **🔑 Foundation: Snapshot isolation**
> 
> ## Snapshot Isolation
**What it is:** Snapshot isolation is a concurrency control scheme where each transaction sees a **frozen-in-time view of the database** as it existed when the transaction started. Other transactions can commit changes after your snapshot was taken, but you won't see them — you're reading from your private "snapshot."
Concretely: when Transaction A starts at timestamp T=100, it reads only data committed before T=100. If Transaction B commits a new row at T=105, Transaction A never sees it, even if A reads that table again at T=110. This eliminates dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads — all without taking read locks.
**Writes** are where it gets interesting. If two concurrent transactions both modify the same row, a **write-write conflict** is detected. The first one to commit wins; the second is aborted and must retry. This is often called "first-committer-wins."
**Why you need it now:** You're implementing a database that needs to handle concurrent access. Traditional locking (readers block writers, writers block readers) is simple but creates bottlenecks. Snapshot isolation is the strategy used by SQLite's WAL mode, PostgreSQL (as its default "Read Committed" and "Repeatable Read" levels), and many modern databases because **readers never block writers and writers never block readers.** It's the practical sweet spot between correctness and performance.
Implementation ties directly to your WAL: each transaction's snapshot can be defined by the latest committed LSN at the time it started. When reading a page, you follow version chains or check transaction visibility to find the version that was current as of your snapshot.
**Key insight:** Snapshot isolation is **not** the same as full serializability. It permits a subtle anomaly called **write skew**: two transactions can each read a value the other is about to modify, and both commit successfully with a result that couldn't have happened in any serial order. Classic example — two doctors are on call, each checks "are there at least 2 doctors on call?" (yes), each removes themselves. Both commit. Now zero doctors are on call. If your application can tolerate write skew (most can), snapshot isolation gives you excellent concurrency. If not, you need serializable snapshot isolation (SSI), which adds dependency tracking on top.

**→ Programming Language Compilers**: Every recursive-descent parser is a direct encoding of a BNF grammar. The technique you just built is identical to how GCC parses C, how the Go compiler parses Go, and how TypeScript's parser parses TypeScript. The grammar rules differ; the technique is the same. If you can parse SQL, you can parse any LL(k) language.
**→ IDE Tooling and Language Servers**: The AST you produce is exactly what a Language Server Protocol (LSP) implementation uses for syntax highlighting, autocompletion, and error reporting. The `line` and `col` fields on every AST node enable mapping tree nodes back to source positions — the foundation of "go to definition," "find all references," and inline error display.
**→ Query Rewriting and Optimization**: Later milestones (Milestone 8, Query Planner) will transform the AST before compilation — reordering joins, pushing predicates down, eliminating dead branches. These transformations are **tree-to-tree rewrites** on the AST. The same pattern appears in compiler optimizations (constant folding, dead code elimination) and in database query optimizers (predicate pushdown, join reordering). Your AST is not just an intermediate step — it's the foundation for all semantic analysis.
**→ Error Recovery in Real-Time Editors**: Production parsers don't stop at the first error. They implement **panic mode recovery**: when an unexpected token appears, the parser skips tokens until it finds a synchronization point (like a semicolon or a closing keyword) and resumes parsing. This allows IDEs to parse partially-typed SQL and still provide syntax highlighting for the parts that are valid. Your current stop-at-first-error approach is correct for a batch compiler; the recovery extension is what separates batch compilers from interactive tools.
---
## System Awareness
You've built the parser — the second layer of the SQL frontend. It connects to the **tokenizer** (upstream, Milestone 1) for input and to the **bytecode compiler** (downstream, Milestone 3) for output. The AST is the contract between these layers: the tokenizer guarantees classified tokens; the parser guarantees a well-formed tree; the compiler guarantees executable bytecode. Each layer trusts the layer below it and provides guarantees to the layer above.
Without the parser, tokens are a meaningless sequence. With it, your database engine has structured representations of SQL statements — ready to be compiled into the bytecode instructions your virtual machine will execute.
---
<!-- END_MS -->


<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m3 -->
<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m3 -->
# Milestone 3: Bytecode Compiler (VDBE)
## Where We Are in the System
You have a tokenizer that converts SQL text into tokens, and a parser that converts tokens into an AST. These are the **frontend** of your database engine. Now you build the component that bridges frontend and backend — the **Virtual Database Engine** (VDBE), SQLite's bytecode compiler and virtual machine.

![VDBE Architecture Overview](./diagrams/diag-vdbe-architecture.svg)

The VDBE sits at the exact center of the architecture. Above it: SQL text, tokens, ASTs — the world of human-readable queries. Below it: buffer pools, B-tree pages, disk I/O — the world of bytes on storage. The VDBE's job is to translate the semantic intent of an AST ("find all users older than 21") into a sequence of primitive operations that the storage engine can execute one step at a time.

![Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page](./diagrams/diag-data-flow-sql-to-disk.svg)

This milestone has two parts: the **compiler** (AST → bytecode program) and the **virtual machine** (bytecode program → results). You'll build both.
---
## The Revelation: Why Not Just Walk the Tree?
You already have an AST. The AST for `SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 21` is a tree with a SELECT node, an IDENTIFIER node for `name`, and a BINARY_EXPR node for `age > 21`. Why not just walk this tree at execution time?
You could. It's called a **tree-walking interpreter**, and it works like this:
```c
// Tree-walking approach (DON'T do this)
void execute_select(AstNode *select_node, BTree *table) {
    Cursor *cur = btree_cursor_open(table);
    btree_cursor_rewind(cur);
    while (!btree_cursor_eof(cur)) {
        Row *row = btree_cursor_read(cur);
        if (evaluate_where(select_node->data.select.where_clause, row)) {
            output_columns(select_node->data.select.columns, row);
        }
        btree_cursor_next(cur);
    }
}
```
This is clean, readable, and — for a 10,000-row table scan — catastrophically wasteful. Here's why:
**Per-row overhead.** Every time `evaluate_where` is called, it walks the WHERE clause AST. For `age > 21 AND status = 'active'`, that's: dispatch on NODE_BINARY_EXPR (AND) → recurse left → dispatch on NODE_BINARY_EXPR (>) → recurse left → dispatch on NODE_IDENTIFIER → look up "age" → recurse right → dispatch on NODE_LITERAL → get value 21 → compare → return to AND → recurse right → dispatch on NODE_BINARY_EXPR (=) → ... and so on. Each dispatch involves checking `node->type`, following pointers through heap-allocated nodes (likely cache misses), and recursing. For 10,000 rows, you perform this entire tree walk 10,000 times — even though the tree never changes.
**Bytecode eliminates the tree walk entirely.** The compiler traverses the AST *once* and emits a flat array of instructions. The VM then processes these instructions sequentially — incrementing a program counter is a single integer addition. No pointer chasing, no type dispatching, no recursion. The instruction stream is a compact array that fits in CPU cache.
The numbers matter. On modern hardware, a function call with pointer indirection costs ~5-10ns (including branch prediction misses and cache misses for heap-allocated nodes). A program counter increment costs <1ns. For a WHERE clause with 5 AST nodes evaluated 10,000 times, the tree-walking overhead is roughly 50,000 × 5ns = 250μs — just for dispatching, before any actual work. Bytecode eliminates that overhead entirely.
But performance isn't the only reason. **Bytecode decouples frontend from backend.** Your SQL parser could change completely — support new syntax, new dialects, new expression types — and the VM doesn't care, as long as the compiler emits valid opcodes. Conversely, you could replace the B-tree storage engine with an LSM-tree, and the compiler doesn't care, as long as the cursor opcodes work. This is the same decoupling that makes the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) language-agnostic: Java, Kotlin, Scala, and Clojure all compile to the same bytecode.

![AST to Bytecode: SELECT Compilation](./diagrams/diag-vdbe-compilation-select.svg)

---
## Designing the Instruction Set
Every virtual machine needs an instruction set — the vocabulary of operations it can perform. SQLite's real VDBE has ~180 opcodes. You need far fewer. Here is the minimal set that covers SELECT, INSERT, CREATE TABLE, and WHERE filtering:

![Core Opcode Reference](./diagrams/diag-vdbe-opcode-catalog.svg)

```c
typedef enum {
    // Control flow
    OP_INIT,          // Jump to start of program (p2 = addr of first real opcode)
    OP_HALT,          // Stop execution. p1 = return code (0 = success)
    OP_GOTO,          // Unconditional jump. p2 = target address
    // Table/cursor operations
    OP_OPEN_READ,     // Open cursor p1 for reading table with root page p2
    OP_OPEN_WRITE,    // Open cursor p1 for writing table with root page p2
    OP_CLOSE,         // Close cursor p1
    OP_REWIND,        // Move cursor p1 to first row. If table empty, jump to p2
    OP_NEXT,          // Advance cursor p1. If past end, jump to p2
    // Data access
    OP_COLUMN,        // Read column p2 from cursor p1 into register p3
    OP_ROWID,         // Read rowid from cursor p1 into register p2
    OP_RESULT_ROW,    // Output registers p1..p1+p2-1 as a result row
    // Record operations
    OP_MAKE_RECORD,   // Pack registers p1..p1+p2-1 into a record in register p3
    OP_INSERT,        // Insert record from register p2 into cursor p1 with key from register p3
    // Constants
    OP_INTEGER,       // Store integer p1 into register p2
    OP_STRING,        // Store string (from string pool at index p1) into register p2
    OP_NULL,          // Store NULL into register p2
    OP_REAL,          // Store float (from constant pool at index p1) into register p2
    // Comparison (conditional jump)
    OP_EQ,            // If reg[p1] == reg[p3], jump to p2
    OP_NE,            // If reg[p1] != reg[p3], jump to p2
    OP_LT,            // If reg[p1] <  reg[p3], jump to p2
    OP_LE,            // If reg[p1] <= reg[p3], jump to p2
    OP_GT,            // If reg[p1] >  reg[p3], jump to p2
    OP_GE,            // If reg[p1] >= reg[p3], jump to p2
    // Schema operations
    OP_CREATE_TABLE,  // Create a new B-tree, store root page in register p1
    OP_OPEN_EPHEMERAL,// Open ephemeral (temporary) table on cursor p1
    // Arithmetic
    OP_ADD,           // reg[p3] = reg[p1] + reg[p2]
    OP_SUBTRACT,      // reg[p3] = reg[p1] - reg[p2]
    OP_MULTIPLY,      // reg[p3] = reg[p1] * reg[p2]
    OP_DIVIDE,        // reg[p3] = reg[p1] / reg[p2]
    // Copy
    OP_COPY,          // reg[p2] = reg[p1]
    OP_SCOPY,         // Shallow copy reg[p1] to reg[p2]
} Opcode;
```
Each instruction is a fixed-size struct with three operand fields — a design called a **three-address instruction format**:
```c
typedef struct {
    Opcode opcode;    // what to do
    int p1;           // first operand (usually register or cursor number)
    int p2;           // second operand (register, address, or page number)
    int p3;           // third operand (register or count)
    char *comment;    // for EXPLAIN output (NULL in production)
} VdbeOp;
```
**Why three operands?** Because most operations need at most three pieces of information: two inputs and one output. `OP_ADD` takes register p1 and register p2 as inputs and writes the result to register p3. `OP_COLUMN` takes cursor p1 and column index p2 as inputs and writes the value to register p3. This uniformity — every instruction is the same size and shape — makes the VM's fetch-decode loop trivially simple.
### Design Decision: Register-Based vs Stack-Based VM

> **🔑 Foundation: Register-based vs stack-based VMs**
> 
> ## Register-Based vs Stack-Based Virtual Machines
**What it is:** These are two fundamentally different architectures for how a virtual machine performs computation — specifically, where intermediate values live during execution.
A **stack-based VM** uses an implicit operand stack. Instructions push values onto the stack and pop them off to operate. To add two numbers:
```
PUSH 3
PUSH 5
ADD      // pops 3 and 5, pushes 8
```
The operands are implicit — `ADD` doesn't say *what* to add; it just grabs the top two stack elements.
A **register-based VM** uses named slots (registers) that instructions reference explicitly:
```
LOAD R0, 3
LOAD R1, 5
ADD R2, R0, R1   // R2 = R0 + R1 = 8
```
Here, every instruction spells out exactly which registers to read from and write to.
**Real-world examples:** The JVM and CPython are stack-based. Lua 5.0+, Dalvik (Android's original VM), and most physical CPUs are register-based.
**Why you need this now:** You're building a virtual machine, and this is the first architectural fork in the road. Everything downstream — your instruction format, your bytecode compiler, your dispatch loop — depends on which model you choose. It's not a detail you can change later without rewriting most of your VM.
**Key insight:** Stack-based VMs are simpler to *target* (a compiler emitting stack bytecode doesn't need to worry about register assignment), but register-based VMs are simpler to *execute efficiently* because they avoid the constant push/pop overhead. Research on Dalvik vs. the JVM showed register-based bytecode can be ~47% smaller in instruction count for equivalent programs, though individual instructions are wider (they carry register operands). The tradeoff is: **simpler compiler, chattier bytecode (stack) vs. smarter compiler, denser execution (register)**.

| Option | Pros | Cons | Used By |
|--------|------|------|---------|
| **Register-based ✓** | Fewer instructions per operation, direct operand addressing, easier to optimize | Wider instruction encoding (3 fields), compiler must manage register allocation | SQLite VDBE, Lua 5, Android Dalvik |
| Stack-based | Simpler instruction encoding (no operands), simpler compiler (just push/pop) | More instructions per operation (~2x), implicit operand flow harder to debug | JVM, CPython, WebAssembly |
SQLite uses a register-based VM because database operations naturally have multiple inputs and outputs. `OP_COLUMN` needs a cursor number, a column index, and a destination — three distinct pieces of information that map cleanly to p1, p2, p3. A stack-based VM would need separate push/pop instructions for each, increasing instruction count and making EXPLAIN output harder to read.
---
## The Bytecode Program Structure
A bytecode program is an array of `VdbeOp` instructions, plus some metadata:
```c
#define MAX_REGISTERS 256
#define MAX_INSTRUCTIONS 4096
#define MAX_STRINGS 256
#define MAX_CURSORS 16
typedef enum {
    VAL_NULL,
    VAL_INTEGER,
    VAL_REAL,
    VAL_STRING,
    VAL_BLOB,
} ValueType;
typedef struct {
    ValueType type;
    union {
        int64_t i;       // VAL_INTEGER
        double r;        // VAL_REAL
        struct {
            char *data;
            int length;
        } s;             // VAL_STRING, VAL_BLOB
    };
} Value;
typedef struct {
    VdbeOp *ops;          // instruction array
    int op_count;         // number of instructions
    int op_capacity;      // allocated capacity
    // String constant pool — strings referenced by OP_STRING
    char **strings;
    int string_count;
    // Register count needed
    int n_registers;
} Program;
```
The string pool deserves explanation. When the compiler encounters a string literal like `'active'` in a WHERE clause, it doesn't embed the string pointer directly in the instruction (the instruction's fields are `int`, not `char*`). Instead, it adds the string to the `strings` array and stores the index in the instruction's `p1` field. The VM retrieves the string from the pool at execution time. This keeps instructions fixed-size and makes program serialization (saving to disk or sending over a network) straightforward.
---
## The Compiler
The compiler walks the AST exactly once and emits instructions into a `Program`. It's a recursive visitor — structurally identical to the recursive-descent parser, but instead of consuming tokens and producing nodes, it consumes nodes and produces bytecode.
```c
typedef struct {
    Program *program;
    int next_register;    // next free register
    int next_cursor;      // next free cursor number
    // Schema lookup (for resolving table names to root pages)
    // In a real implementation, this queries sqlite_master
    struct {
        char *name;
        int root_page;
        int column_count;
        char **column_names;
    } *tables;
    int table_count;
} Compiler;
void compiler_init(Compiler *c, Program *prog) {
    c->program = prog;
    c->next_register = 1;  // register 0 reserved for scratch
    c->next_cursor = 0;
    prog->op_count = 0;
}
```
### Helper: Emitting Instructions
```c
static int emit(Compiler *c, Opcode op, int p1, int p2, int p3, 
                const char *comment) {
    Program *prog = c->program;
    if (prog->op_count >= prog->op_capacity) {
        prog->op_capacity = prog->op_capacity ? prog->op_capacity * 2 : 64;
        prog->ops = realloc(prog->ops, prog->op_capacity * sizeof(VdbeOp));
    }
    int addr = prog->op_count;
    VdbeOp *instr = &prog->ops[addr];
    instr->opcode = op;
    instr->p1 = p1;
    instr->p2 = p2;
    instr->p3 = p3;
    instr->comment = comment ? strdup(comment) : NULL;
    prog->op_count++;
    return addr;  // return address of this instruction (for patching jumps)
}
static int alloc_register(Compiler *c) {
    return c->next_register++;
}
static int alloc_cursor(Compiler *c) {
    return c->next_cursor++;
}
```
The `emit` function returns the address (index) of the emitted instruction. This is crucial for **jump patching** — when you emit a conditional jump, you don't yet know the target address. You emit the jump with a placeholder, then later fill in the correct address once you know where the target instruction lands.
```c
static void patch_jump(Compiler *c, int jump_addr, int target_addr) {
    c->program->ops[jump_addr].p2 = target_addr;
}
```
---
## Compiling SELECT
This is the most instructive compilation because it shows how a declarative query ("give me all names where age > 21") transforms into an imperative loop ("open cursor, rewind, check condition, output column, advance, repeat").
The bytecode template for `SELECT col1, col2 FROM table WHERE condition` is:
```
0  Init         0  <addr of OpenRead>  0
1  OpenRead     cursor  root_page  column_count
2  Rewind       cursor  <addr of Close>
3  Column       cursor  col_idx1  reg1        ; load col1
4  Column       cursor  col_idx2  reg2        ; load col2
   ... (WHERE evaluation writes condition result) ...
   ... (conditional jump to Next if condition false) ...
5  ResultRow    reg1  col_count               ; output row
6  Next         cursor  <addr of first Column> ; loop back
7  Close        cursor
8  Halt         0  0  0
```
Here's the compiler function:
```c
static void compile_select(Compiler *c, AstNode *node) {
    // Look up table metadata
    int table_idx = find_table(c, node->data.select.from_table);
    if (table_idx < 0) {
        // Table not found — emit error and halt
        emit(c, OP_HALT, 1, 0, 0, "error: table not found");
        return;
    }
    int root_page = c->tables[table_idx].root_page;
    int n_cols = c->tables[table_idx].column_count;
    // Allocate cursor
    int cursor = alloc_cursor(c);
    // OP_INIT: jump past any setup code to the first real instruction
    int init_addr = emit(c, OP_INIT, 0, 0, 0, "Start");
    // Open cursor for reading
    int open_addr = emit(c, OP_OPEN_READ, cursor, root_page, n_cols, 
                          node->data.select.from_table);
    patch_jump(c, init_addr, open_addr);
    // Rewind cursor to first row; if empty, jump to close
    int rewind_addr = emit(c, OP_REWIND, cursor, 0, 0, "Rewind; jump if empty");
    // --- Loop body starts here ---
    int loop_start = c->program->op_count;
    // Load columns into registers
    int first_reg = c->next_register;
    int result_col_count = 0;
    if (node->data.select.column_count == 1 && 
        node->data.select.columns[0]->type == NODE_WILDCARD) {
        // SELECT * — load all columns
        for (int i = 0; i < n_cols; i++) {
            int reg = alloc_register(c);
            emit(c, OP_COLUMN, cursor, i, reg, 
                 c->tables[table_idx].column_names[i]);
        }
        result_col_count = n_cols;
    } else {
        // SELECT col1, col2 — load specific columns
        for (int i = 0; i < node->data.select.column_count; i++) {
            AstNode *col_node = node->data.select.columns[i];
            int reg = alloc_register(c);
            if (col_node->type == NODE_IDENTIFIER) {
                int col_idx = find_column(c, table_idx, 
                                          col_node->data.identifier.name);
                emit(c, OP_COLUMN, cursor, col_idx, reg, 
                     col_node->data.identifier.name);
            }
            // Handle expressions, function calls, etc.
            result_col_count++;
        }
    }
    // Compile WHERE clause (if present)
    int skip_addr = -1;
    if (node->data.select.where_clause) {
        skip_addr = compile_where(c, node->data.select.where_clause, cursor, 
                                   table_idx);
        // skip_addr is a jump that skips ResultRow if condition is false
    }
    // Output result row
    emit(c, OP_RESULT_ROW, first_reg, result_col_count, 0, "Output row");
    // Patch the WHERE skip to jump here (past ResultRow, to Next)
    int next_addr = emit(c, OP_NEXT, cursor, loop_start, 0, "Advance; loop");
    if (skip_addr >= 0) {
        patch_jump(c, skip_addr, next_addr);
    }
    // Close cursor
    int close_addr = emit(c, OP_CLOSE, cursor, 0, 0, "Close cursor");
    patch_jump(c, rewind_addr, close_addr);
    // Halt
    emit(c, OP_HALT, 0, 0, 0, "Done");
    // Reset register allocator for next compilation (registers are per-loop-iteration)
    c->program->n_registers = c->next_register;
}
```
### Register Allocation

![Register Allocation for Nested Expressions](./diagrams/diag-vdbe-register-allocation.svg)

Notice the register allocation strategy: the compiler keeps a monotonically increasing counter (`next_register`) and allocates registers sequentially. This is called **linear scan allocation** — the simplest possible approach. Each intermediate value gets its own register, and registers are never reused within a single compilation.
For `SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age > 21`:
- Register 1: `name` (loaded by OP_COLUMN)
- Register 2: `age` (loaded by OP_COLUMN)
- Register 3: constant `21` (loaded by OP_INTEGER)
The compiler never clobbers register 1 while computing the WHERE clause because the WHERE clause gets fresh registers (3 and beyond). This eliminates the register clobbering bug that plagues more sophisticated allocators. The cost is using more registers than strictly necessary, but with a 256-register limit and queries that rarely use more than 20, this is a non-issue.

> **🔑 Foundation: Register allocation**
> 
> ## Register Allocation — Mapping Values to Registers
**What it is:** Register allocation is the compiler's process of deciding which intermediate values (variables, temporaries from subexpressions) get stored in which registers at any given point during execution.
The problem is straightforward to state: you have *N* values alive at some point in the program, but only *K* registers. When N > K, some values must be "spilled" — written to memory and reloaded later. The compiler's job is to minimize spilling because memory access is far slower than register access.
The classic approach models this as **graph coloring**: build an "interference graph" where each node is a live value and edges connect values that are alive at the same time (and therefore can't share a register). Coloring this graph with K colors = assigning K registers. If the graph isn't K-colorable, you pick a node to spill.
A simpler approach that works well for VMs with many virtual registers is **linear scan allocation** — walk through the code linearly, track live ranges, and greedily assign registers, spilling the value whose next use is furthest away.
**Why you need this now:** If you're building a register-based VM, your bytecode compiler needs to emit instructions that reference specific registers. That means the compiler must solve the allocation problem: given an expression like `(a + b) * (c - d)`, it must decide that `a + b` goes into `R0`, `c - d` into `R1`, and the multiply reads both. For a VM with a generous number of virtual registers, this is manageable — but you still need a systematic strategy, or complex expressions will silently clobber each other's values.
**Key insight:** Think of register allocation as a **scheduling problem for scarce desk space**. You're working on a desk with K slots. Each task (value) occupies a slot for some duration (its live range). When the desk is full and a new task arrives, you shelve the one you won't need again soonest. That "furthest next use" heuristic is the core of most practical allocators and is often good enough for a VM compiler.

---
## Compiling WHERE Clauses
The WHERE clause is an expression tree that must be evaluated per row. The compiler flattens this tree into a sequence of comparison and conditional jump instructions. The key insight: **instead of computing a boolean result and then branching on it, each comparison directly jumps to "skip this row" if the condition fails.**
This is an optimization called **short-circuit evaluation via jump threading**. For `WHERE age > 21 AND status = 'active'`, if `age > 21` is false, there's no point evaluating `status = 'active'`. The compiler emits a jump that skips the entire row immediately.
```c
// Returns the address of the skip-jump instruction (needs patching)
static int compile_where(Compiler *c, AstNode *expr, int cursor, 
                          int table_idx) {
    return compile_condition(c, expr, cursor, table_idx, /*invert=*/true);
}
// Compile a condition. If invert=true, jump when condition is FALSE.
// Returns the address of the emitted jump (for patching).
static int compile_condition(Compiler *c, AstNode *expr, int cursor,
                              int table_idx, bool invert) {
    if (expr->type == NODE_BINARY_EXPR) {
        // AND: if either side is false, skip
        if (strcmp(expr->data.binary.op_keyword, "AND") == 0) {
            int left_skip = compile_condition(c, expr->data.binary.left, 
                                               cursor, table_idx, true);
            int right_skip = compile_condition(c, expr->data.binary.right, 
                                                cursor, table_idx, true);
            // Both jumps skip to the same target (patched by caller)
            // For now, chain them: both jump forward past ResultRow
            return right_skip;  
            // Note: left_skip also needs patching to same target
        }
        // OR: if either side is true, don't skip
        if (strcmp(expr->data.binary.op_keyword, "OR") == 0) {
            // More complex: need "if left true, skip right check"
            int left_true = compile_condition(c, expr->data.binary.left, 
                                               cursor, table_idx, false);
            int right_skip = compile_condition(c, expr->data.binary.right, 
                                                cursor, table_idx, true);
            // Patch left_true to jump past right evaluation to ResultRow
            // This is where OR short-circuit works
            return right_skip;
        }
        // Comparison operators: =, <, >, <=, >=, !=
        int left_reg = compile_expr_to_register(c, expr->data.binary.left, 
                                                  cursor, table_idx);
        int right_reg = compile_expr_to_register(c, expr->data.binary.right, 
                                                   cursor, table_idx);
        // Emit the appropriate comparison opcode
        // The comparison jumps if the condition IS met (or isn't, based on invert)
        Opcode cmp_op;
        if (invert) {
            // We want to jump when condition is FALSE
            // So invert the comparison: > becomes <=, = becomes !=, etc.
            cmp_op = invert_comparison(expr->data.binary.op);
        } else {
            cmp_op = comparison_to_opcode(expr->data.binary.op);
        }
        int jump_addr = emit(c, cmp_op, left_reg, 0, right_reg, "WHERE check");
        return jump_addr;
    }
    if (expr->type == NODE_UNARY_EXPR) {
        // NOT: invert the sense and recurse
        if (strcmp(expr->data.unary.op_keyword, "NOT") == 0) {
            return compile_condition(c, expr->data.unary.operand, 
                                     cursor, table_idx, !invert);
        }
    }
    // Shouldn't reach here for well-formed WHERE clauses
    return -1;
}
```
The `invert_comparison` function maps each comparison to its logical inverse:
```c
static Opcode invert_comparison(TokenType op) {
    switch (op) {
        case TOK_GT: return OP_LE;   // !(a > b) ≡ a <= b
        case TOK_GE: return OP_LT;   // !(a >= b) ≡ a < b
        case TOK_LT: return OP_GE;   // !(a < b) ≡ a >= b
        case TOK_LE: return OP_GT;   // !(a <= b) ≡ a > b
        case TOK_EQ: return OP_NE;   // !(a = b) ≡ a != b
        case TOK_NE: return OP_EQ;   // !(a != b) ≡ a = b
        default: return OP_NE;       // fallback
    }
}
```
This inversion trick is important. Instead of computing `age > 21` into a boolean register and then branching on it (two instructions), the compiler emits a single `OP_LE` that jumps directly when `age <= 21` (the negation of the desired condition). One instruction instead of two, per row, for the entire scan.
### Compiling Expressions into Registers
Leaf expressions (identifiers, literals) load values into registers. Compound expressions (arithmetic) combine registers:
```c
static int compile_expr_to_register(Compiler *c, AstNode *expr, 
                                     int cursor, int table_idx) {
    switch (expr->type) {
        case NODE_IDENTIFIER: {
            int col_idx = find_column(c, table_idx, expr->data.identifier.name);
            int reg = alloc_register(c);
            emit(c, OP_COLUMN, cursor, col_idx, reg, expr->data.identifier.name);
            return reg;
        }
        case NODE_LITERAL: {
            int reg = alloc_register(c);
            if (expr->data.literal.literal_type == TOK_INTEGER) {
                int val = atoi(expr->data.literal.value);
                emit(c, OP_INTEGER, val, reg, 0, expr->data.literal.value);
            } else if (expr->data.literal.literal_type == TOK_STRING) {
                int str_idx = add_string_constant(c, expr->data.literal.value);
                emit(c, OP_STRING, str_idx, reg, 0, expr->data.literal.value);
            } else if (expr->data.literal.literal_type == TOK_KEYWORD) {
                // NULL
                emit(c, OP_NULL, 0, reg, 0, "NULL");
            }
            return reg;
        }
        case NODE_BINARY_EXPR: {
            // Arithmetic: +, -, *, /
            int left_reg = compile_expr_to_register(c, expr->data.binary.left, 
                                                      cursor, table_idx);
            int right_reg = compile_expr_to_register(c, expr->data.binary.right, 
                                                       cursor, table_idx);
            int result_reg = alloc_register(c);
            Opcode arith;
            switch (expr->data.binary.op) {
                case TOK_PLUS:  arith = OP_ADD;      break;
                case TOK_MINUS: arith = OP_SUBTRACT; break;
                case TOK_STAR:  arith = OP_MULTIPLY; break;
                case TOK_SLASH: arith = OP_DIVIDE;   break;
                default: arith = OP_ADD; break;
            }
            emit(c, arith, left_reg, right_reg, result_reg, "arithmetic");
            return result_reg;
        }
        default:
            return alloc_register(c);  // return an empty register (error case)
    }
}
```
Notice the recursive structure. Compiling `price * quantity + tax` first recurses into `price * quantity` (allocating registers for `price`, `quantity`, and the multiplication result), then recurses into `tax` (allocating one register), and finally emits `OP_ADD` with the multiplication result register and the tax register as inputs. The tree traversal order is post-order (left, right, then operate), which naturally handles nested expressions.
---
## Compiling INSERT
INSERT is simpler than SELECT because there's no loop — you're adding rows, not scanning them.
```c
static void compile_insert(Compiler *c, AstNode *node) {
    int table_idx = find_table(c, node->data.insert.table_name);
    if (table_idx < 0) {
        emit(c, OP_HALT, 1, 0, 0, "error: table not found");
        return;
    }
    int root_page = c->tables[table_idx].root_page;
    int n_cols = c->tables[table_idx].column_count;
    int cursor = alloc_cursor(c);
    int init_addr = emit(c, OP_INIT, 0, 0, 0, "Start");
    int open_addr = emit(c, OP_OPEN_WRITE, cursor, root_page, n_cols,
                          node->data.insert.table_name);
    patch_jump(c, init_addr, open_addr);
    // For each row in VALUES (...)
    for (int row = 0; row < node->data.insert.row_count; row++) {
        // Compile each value expression into a register
        int first_reg = c->next_register;
        int val_count = node->data.insert.values_per_row;
        for (int col = 0; col < val_count; col++) {
            AstNode *val_expr = node->data.insert.value_rows[row][col];
            compile_expr_to_register(c, val_expr, -1, table_idx);
        }
        // Pack values into a record
        int record_reg = alloc_register(c);
        emit(c, OP_MAKE_RECORD, first_reg, val_count, record_reg, "Pack record");
        // Generate rowid (for now, use a simple auto-increment)
        int rowid_reg = alloc_register(c);
        emit(c, OP_ROWID, cursor, rowid_reg, 0, "New rowid");
        // Insert the record
        emit(c, OP_INSERT, cursor, record_reg, rowid_reg, "Insert row");
    }
    emit(c, OP_CLOSE, cursor, 0, 0, "Close cursor");
    emit(c, OP_HALT, 0, 0, 0, "Done");
    c->program->n_registers = c->next_register;
}
```
The `OP_MAKE_RECORD` instruction is where the transition from "values in registers" to "bytes in a B-tree page" begins. It takes a range of registers (p1 through p1+p2-1) and serializes them into a single binary record — the same variable-length format you'll implement in Milestone 5. For now, the VM can use a simple serialization: a type byte followed by the value for each column.
---
## Compiling CREATE TABLE
CREATE TABLE doesn't read or write data rows — it modifies the schema catalog:
```c
static void compile_create_table(Compiler *c, AstNode *node) {
    int init_addr = emit(c, OP_INIT, 0, 0, 0, "Start");
    // Allocate a new B-tree root page
    int root_reg = alloc_register(c);
    int create_addr = emit(c, OP_CREATE_TABLE, root_reg, 0, 0, 
                            "Allocate B-tree root");
    patch_jump(c, init_addr, create_addr);
    // In a full implementation, you would:
    // 1. Open the sqlite_master table
    // 2. Insert a row with (type='table', name=<name>, rootpage=<root_reg>, sql=<original>)
    // 3. Update the in-memory schema cache
    // For now, store schema metadata in the compiler's table list
    // The VM will handle the actual catalog update
    emit(c, OP_HALT, 0, 0, 0, "Done");
    c->program->n_registers = c->next_register;
}
```
The full CREATE TABLE compilation requires writing a row into the system catalog (your equivalent of `sqlite_master`). This is itself an INSERT — you're inserting a schema row. This recursive structure (CREATE TABLE compiles to an INSERT into the catalog table) is a key insight about how databases bootstrap themselves: the schema is stored in a table, and modifying the schema is just a DML operation on that table.
---
## The Virtual Machine
Now for the execution engine. The VM is a **fetch-decode-execute loop** — it reads the next instruction, determines which operation to perform, executes it, and advances to the next instruction.

> **🔑 Foundation: The fetch-decode-execute cycle**
> 
> ## The Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle
**What it is:** This is the fundamental loop that drives every processor — physical or virtual:
1. **Fetch:** Read the next instruction from memory (pointed to by the program counter / instruction pointer).
2. **Decode:** Figure out what the instruction means — what operation, which operands.
3. **Execute:** Perform the operation (arithmetic, memory access, jump, etc.).
4. Advance the program counter (unless the instruction was a jump) and repeat.
In a physical CPU, this is hardwired in silicon. In a VM, you implement it explicitly — typically as a loop with a switch statement or a function dispatch table:
```python
while True:
    opcode = bytecode[pc]        # FETCH
    match opcode:                 # DECODE + EXECUTE
        case OP_ADD:
            regs[a] = regs[b] + regs[c]
        case OP_JUMP:
            pc = target; continue
        case OP_HALT:
            break
    pc += 1
```
**Why you need this now:** This loop *is* your VM's execution engine. It's the first thing you'll write and the last thing you'll optimize. Every feature — arithmetic, control flow, function calls — plugs into this cycle as a new case in the decode step. Getting the structure right early means every subsequent milestone is just adding new opcodes.
**Key insight:** The cycle is deceptively simple, but its performance characteristics dominate your VM. The single biggest bottleneck in interpreted VMs is the **decode step** — specifically, the branch prediction miss on the switch/dispatch. This is why advanced VMs use techniques like **threaded dispatch** (each instruction handler jumps directly to the next, eliminating the central switch). But start with the simple loop — it's correct, debuggable, and you can optimize the dispatch later without changing your instruction set.

```c
typedef struct {
    // The program being executed
    Program *program;
    int pc;                    // program counter (index into ops array)
    // Register file
    Value registers[MAX_REGISTERS];
    // Cursors (B-tree iterators)
    BTreeCursor *cursors[MAX_CURSORS];
    // Result callback — called for each ResultRow
    void (*result_callback)(Value *row, int col_count, void *ctx);
    void *callback_ctx;
    // Execution state
    bool halted;
    int halt_code;
} VM;
void vm_init(VM *vm, Program *prog) {
    vm->program = prog;
    vm->pc = 0;
    vm->halted = false;
    vm->halt_code = 0;
    memset(vm->registers, 0, sizeof(vm->registers));
    memset(vm->cursors, 0, sizeof(vm->cursors));
}
```
The execution loop:
```c
int vm_execute(VM *vm) {
    while (!vm->halted && vm->pc < vm->program->op_count) {
        VdbeOp *op = &vm->program->ops[vm->pc];
        switch (op->opcode) {
            case OP_INIT: {
                vm->pc = op->p2;
                continue;  // skip the pc++ at the bottom
            }
            case OP_HALT: {
                vm->halted = true;
                vm->halt_code = op->p1;
                return op->p1;
            }
            case OP_GOTO: {
                vm->pc = op->p2;
                continue;
            }
            case OP_OPEN_READ: {
                int cursor_id = op->p1;
                int root_page = op->p2;
                vm->cursors[cursor_id] = btree_cursor_open(root_page, 
                                                            CURSOR_READ);
                break;
            }
            case OP_OPEN_WRITE: {
                int cursor_id = op->p1;
                int root_page = op->p2;
                vm->cursors[cursor_id] = btree_cursor_open(root_page, 
                                                            CURSOR_WRITE);
                break;
            }
            case OP_CLOSE: {
                int cursor_id = op->p1;
                if (vm->cursors[cursor_id]) {
                    btree_cursor_close(vm->cursors[cursor_id]);
                    vm->cursors[cursor_id] = NULL;
                }
                break;
            }
            case OP_REWIND: {
                int cursor_id = op->p1;
                bool empty = btree_cursor_rewind(vm->cursors[cursor_id]);
                if (empty) {
                    vm->pc = op->p2;  // jump to close if table is empty
                    continue;
                }
                break;
            }
            case OP_NEXT: {
                int cursor_id = op->p1;
                bool has_next = btree_cursor_next(vm->cursors[cursor_id]);
                if (has_next) {
                    vm->pc = op->p2;  // jump back to loop start
                    continue;
                }
                // Fall through to next instruction (the Close)
                break;
            }
            case OP_COLUMN: {
                int cursor_id = op->p1;
                int col_idx = op->p2;
                int dest_reg = op->p3;
                btree_cursor_column(vm->cursors[cursor_id], col_idx, 
                                     &vm->registers[dest_reg]);
                break;
            }
            case OP_ROWID: {
                int cursor_id = op->p1;
                int dest_reg = op->p2;
                vm->registers[dest_reg].type = VAL_INTEGER;
                vm->registers[dest_reg].i = 
                    btree_cursor_rowid(vm->cursors[cursor_id]);
                break;
            }
            case OP_RESULT_ROW: {
                int first_reg = op->p1;
                int col_count = op->p2;
                if (vm->result_callback) {
                    vm->result_callback(&vm->registers[first_reg], 
                                        col_count, vm->callback_ctx);
                }
                break;
            }
            case OP_INTEGER: {
                int dest_reg = op->p2;
                vm->registers[dest_reg].type = VAL_INTEGER;
                vm->registers[dest_reg].i = op->p1;
                break;
            }
            case OP_STRING: {
                int str_idx = op->p1;
                int dest_reg = op->p2;
                vm->registers[dest_reg].type = VAL_STRING;
                vm->registers[dest_reg].s.data = vm->program->strings[str_idx];
                vm->registers[dest_reg].s.length = 
                    strlen(vm->program->strings[str_idx]);
                break;
            }
            case OP_NULL: {
                int dest_reg = op->p2;
                vm->registers[dest_reg].type = VAL_NULL;
                break;
            }
            case OP_MAKE_RECORD: {
                int first_reg = op->p1;
                int n_fields = op->p2;
                int dest_reg = op->p3;
                // Serialize registers into a binary record
                serialize_record(&vm->registers[first_reg], n_fields,
                                  &vm->registers[dest_reg]);
                break;
            }
            case OP_INSERT: {
                int cursor_id = op->p1;
                int record_reg = op->p2;
                int key_reg = op->p3;
                btree_cursor_insert(vm->cursors[cursor_id],
                                     vm->registers[key_reg].i,
                                     &vm->registers[record_reg]);
                break;
            }
            // Comparison opcodes — all follow the same pattern
            case OP_EQ: case OP_NE: case OP_LT: 
            case OP_LE: case OP_GT: case OP_GE: {
                int left_reg = op->p1;
                int right_reg = op->p3;
                bool result = compare_values(&vm->registers[left_reg],
                                              &vm->registers[right_reg],
                                              op->opcode);
                if (result) {
                    vm->pc = op->p2;  // jump to target
                    continue;
                }
                break;
            }
            // Arithmetic opcodes
            case OP_ADD: case OP_SUBTRACT: 
            case OP_MULTIPLY: case OP_DIVIDE: {
                Value *a = &vm->registers[op->p1];
                Value *b = &vm->registers[op->p2];
                Value *dest = &vm->registers[op->p3];
                arithmetic_op(a, b, dest, op->opcode);
                break;
            }
            case OP_COPY: case OP_SCOPY: {
                vm->registers[op->p2] = vm->registers[op->p1];
                break;
            }
            default:
                fprintf(stderr, "Unknown opcode: %d\n", op->opcode);
                vm->halted = true;
                return -1;
        }
        vm->pc++;
    }
    return vm->halt_code;
}
```
Study this loop carefully. It's the **entire execution engine**. Every SQL query — no matter how complex — runs through this same switch statement. A `SELECT * FROM t` with 10,000 rows executes OP_REWIND once, then OP_COLUMN + OP_RESULT_ROW + OP_NEXT 10,000 times, then OP_CLOSE + OP_HALT once. The per-iteration cost is: one switch dispatch per opcode, one function call to the B-tree cursor, and one program counter increment. No tree walking, no recursive AST traversal, no pointer chasing through heap-allocated nodes.

![VDBE Execution Trace](./diagrams/diag-vdbe-execution-trace.svg)

### Value Comparison
The comparison function handles type coercion and NULL semantics:
```c
static bool compare_values(Value *a, Value *b, Opcode op) {
    // NULL comparisons: NULL compared to anything yields NULL (falsy)
    if (a->type == VAL_NULL || b->type == VAL_NULL) {
        // In SQL, NULL = NULL is not true. NULL comparisons always fail.
        // Exception: IS NULL is handled by a separate opcode path
        return false;
    }
    int cmp;
    if (a->type == VAL_INTEGER && b->type == VAL_INTEGER) {
        cmp = (a->i > b->i) - (a->i < b->i);
    } else if (a->type == VAL_REAL || b->type == VAL_REAL) {
        double da = (a->type == VAL_REAL) ? a->r : (double)a->i;
        double db = (b->type == VAL_REAL) ? b->r : (double)b->i;
        cmp = (da > db) - (da < db);
    } else if (a->type == VAL_STRING && b->type == VAL_STRING) {
        cmp = strcmp(a->s.data, b->s.data);
    } else {
        // Mixed types — coerce to string for comparison
        // (SQLite has more nuanced type affinity rules)
        cmp = 0;
    }
    switch (op) {
        case OP_EQ: return cmp == 0;
        case OP_NE: return cmp != 0;
        case OP_LT: return cmp < 0;
        case OP_LE: return cmp <= 0;
        case OP_GT: return cmp > 0;
        case OP_GE: return cmp >= 0;
        default:    return false;
    }
}
```
The NULL handling is crucial. In SQL, `NULL = NULL` evaluates to NULL (which is falsy), not TRUE. This means a WHERE clause like `WHERE x = NULL` matches zero rows — you must use `WHERE x IS NULL` instead. Your comparison function returns `false` for any comparison involving NULL, which correctly implements this three-valued logic behavior. The `IS NULL` check requires a separate opcode or a special case in the compiler (checking `value.type == VAL_NULL` directly, without going through the comparison function).
---
## The EXPLAIN Command
EXPLAIN is your debugger. It shows the compiled bytecode for any SQL statement without executing it. This is invaluable during development — when a query returns wrong results, EXPLAIN lets you inspect the bytecode to see if the compilation is correct.
```c
void explain_program(Program *prog) {
    printf("addr  opcode         p1    p2    p3    comment\n");
    printf("----  -------------  ----  ----  ----  -------\n");
    for (int i = 0; i < prog->op_count; i++) {
        VdbeOp *op = &prog->ops[i];
        printf("%-4d  %-13s  %-4d  %-4d  %-4d  %s\n",
               i,
               opcode_name(op->opcode),
               op->p1, op->p2, op->p3,
               op->comment ? op->comment : "");
    }
}
static const char* opcode_name(Opcode op) {
    switch (op) {
        case OP_INIT:        return "Init";
        case OP_HALT:        return "Halt";
        case OP_GOTO:        return "Goto";
        case OP_OPEN_READ:   return "OpenRead";
        case OP_OPEN_WRITE:  return "OpenWrite";
        case OP_CLOSE:       return "Close";
        case OP_REWIND:      return "Rewind";
        case OP_NEXT:        return "Next";
        case OP_COLUMN:      return "Column";
        case OP_ROWID:       return "Rowid";
        case OP_RESULT_ROW:  return "ResultRow";
        case OP_MAKE_RECORD: return "MakeRecord";
        case OP_INSERT:      return "Insert";
        case OP_INTEGER:     return "Integer";
        case OP_STRING:      return "String8";
        case OP_NULL:        return "Null";
        case OP_EQ:          return "Eq";
        case OP_NE:          return "Ne";
        case OP_LT:          return "Lt";
        case OP_LE:          return "Le";
        case OP_GT:          return "Gt";
        case OP_GE:          return "Ge";
        case OP_ADD:         return "Add";
        case OP_SUBTRACT:    return "Subtract";
        case OP_MULTIPLY:    return "Multiply";
        case OP_DIVIDE:      return "Divide";
        case OP_COPY:        return "Copy";
        case OP_SCOPY:       return "SCopy";
        case OP_CREATE_TABLE:return "CreateTable";
        default:             return "???";
    }
}
```

![EXPLAIN Output Anatomy](./diagrams/diag-explain-output.svg)

Here's what EXPLAIN shows for `SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age > 21`:
```
addr  opcode         p1    p2    p3    comment
----  -------------  ----  ----  ----  -------
0     Init           0     1     0     Start
1     OpenRead       0     2     3     users
2     Rewind         0     8     0     Rewind; jump if empty
3     Column         0     0     1     name
4     Column         0     1     2     age
5     Integer        21    3     0     21
6     Le             2     7     3     WHERE check
7     ResultRow      1     2     0     Output row
8     Next           0     3     0     Advance; loop
9     Close          0     0     0     Close cursor
10    Halt           0     0     0     Done
```
Read this line by line:
- **Addr 0**: Jump to instruction 1 (the Init opcode sets up the PC).
- **Addr 1**: Open cursor 0 on the B-tree rooted at page 2 (the `users` table), which has 3 columns.
- **Addr 2**: Rewind cursor 0 to the first row. If the table is empty, jump to addr 8 (Close).
- **Addr 3-4**: Load columns 0 (`name`) and 1 (`age`) from the current cursor position into registers 1 and 2.
- **Addr 5**: Load the constant 21 into register 3.
- **Addr 6**: If register 2 (age) ≤ register 3 (21), jump to addr 7 (Next) — skipping ResultRow. This is the inverted comparison: the original `age > 21` becomes `LE` (jump if ≤) to skip rows that *don't* match.
- **Addr 7**: Output registers 1-2 as a result row.
- **Addr 8**: Advance cursor 0. If there's another row, jump back to addr 3. Otherwise fall through.
- **Addr 9-10**: Clean up and halt.
Wait — there's a subtle bug in the example above. Addr 6 jumps to addr 7, but addr 7 is ResultRow. The skip should jump to addr 8 (Next), bypassing ResultRow. Let me correct:
```
addr  opcode         p1    p2    p3    comment
----  -------------  ----  ----  ----  -------
0     Init           0     1     0     Start
1     OpenRead       0     2     3     users
2     Rewind         0     9     0     Rewind; jump if empty
3     Column         0     0     1     name
4     Column         0     1     2     age
5     Integer        21    3     0     21
6     Le             2     8     3     WHERE: skip if age <= 21
7     ResultRow      1     2     0     Output row
8     Next           0     3     0     Advance; loop back to addr 3
9     Close          0     0     0     Close cursor
10    Halt           0     0     0     Done
```
Now addr 6's jump target (p2=8) correctly points to OP_NEXT, skipping ResultRow for rows that fail the WHERE condition. This is the kind of off-by-one error that EXPLAIN helps you catch immediately.
---
## Integrating the Full Pipeline
The complete flow from SQL string to results:
```c
void execute_sql(const char *sql, Database *db) {
    // 1. Tokenize
    Lexer lex;
    lexer_init(&lex, sql);
    Token tokens[1024];
    int count = lexer_tokenize_all(&lex, tokens, 1024);
    // 2. Parse
    Parser parser;
    parser_init(&parser, tokens, count);
    AstNode *ast = parser_parse(&parser);
    if (parser.had_error) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Parse error: %s\n", parser.error_msg);
        return;
    }
    // Check for EXPLAIN
    bool is_explain = false;
    // (handle EXPLAIN flag from parser)
    // 3. Compile
    Program prog = {0};
    Compiler compiler;
    compiler_init(&compiler, &prog);
    compiler.tables = db->schema;      // connect to schema catalog
    compiler.table_count = db->table_count;
    compile_statement(&compiler, ast);
    if (is_explain) {
        // 4a. Just print the bytecode
        explain_program(&prog);
    } else {
        // 4b. Execute
        VM vm;
        vm_init(&vm, &prog);
        vm.result_callback = print_result_row;
        vm_execute(&vm);
    }
    ast_free(ast);
    program_free(&prog);
}
```
---
## Cursor Abstraction: The Iterator Interface
The opcodes `OP_OPEN_READ`, `OP_REWIND`, `OP_NEXT`, `OP_COLUMN`, and `OP_CLOSE` define an **iterator interface** over B-tree data. This is the **Cursor pattern** — the same Iterator pattern from object-oriented design, applied to database storage.
The cursor abstraction is the boundary between the VM and the storage engine. The VM says "give me the next row" (OP_NEXT); it doesn't know or care whether the underlying storage is a B-tree, an LSM-tree, a flat file, or a network connection to a remote server. This decoupling means you can build and test the VM entirely with an in-memory mock storage layer, then later swap in the real B-tree implementation (Milestone 5) without changing a single line of VM code.
Here's the cursor interface the VM expects:
```c
typedef struct BTreeCursor BTreeCursor;
// Open a cursor on the B-tree rooted at root_page
BTreeCursor* btree_cursor_open(int root_page, int mode);
// Move to the first row. Returns true if table is empty.
bool btree_cursor_rewind(BTreeCursor *cur);
// Move to the next row. Returns true if there are more rows.
bool btree_cursor_next(BTreeCursor *cur);
// Read column col_idx from current row into dest
void btree_cursor_column(BTreeCursor *cur, int col_idx, Value *dest);
// Read the rowid of the current row
int64_t btree_cursor_rowid(BTreeCursor *cur);
// Insert a record with the given key
void btree_cursor_insert(BTreeCursor *cur, int64_t key, Value *record);
// Close the cursor and release resources
void btree_cursor_close(BTreeCursor *cur);
```
For this milestone, implement a **mock cursor** backed by an in-memory array of rows. This lets you test the entire compiler and VM without needing the B-tree or buffer pool:
```c
typedef struct {
    Value **rows;       // array of rows, each row is an array of Values
    int row_count;
    int col_count;
    int position;       // current row index (-1 = before first)
} MockBTreeCursor;
BTreeCursor* mock_cursor_open(Value **data, int rows, int cols) {
    MockBTreeCursor *cur = calloc(1, sizeof(MockBTreeCursor));
    cur->rows = data;
    cur->row_count = rows;
    cur->col_count = cols;
    cur->position = -1;
    return (BTreeCursor*)cur;
}
bool mock_cursor_rewind(BTreeCursor *raw) {
    MockBTreeCursor *cur = (MockBTreeCursor*)raw;
    cur->position = 0;
    return cur->row_count == 0;
}
bool mock_cursor_next(BTreeCursor *raw) {
    MockBTreeCursor *cur = (MockBTreeCursor*)raw;
    cur->position++;
    return cur->position < cur->row_count;
}
void mock_cursor_column(BTreeCursor *raw, int col_idx, Value *dest) {
    MockBTreeCursor *cur = (MockBTreeCursor*)raw;
    *dest = cur->rows[cur->position][col_idx];
}
```
This mock-then-replace strategy is a powerful development pattern. You validate the compiler's output and the VM's logic against simple, controllable data before introducing the complexity of page-based storage. If EXPLAIN shows correct bytecode and the VM produces correct results with mock data, then any bugs you encounter after integrating the B-tree are storage bugs, not compiler or VM bugs. You've isolated the failure domain.
---
## Performance: The 10,000-Row Benchmark
Your acceptance criteria require `SELECT * FROM t` on 10,000 rows to complete in under 100ms. Let's verify this is achievable.
The bytecode for `SELECT * FROM t` (assuming 3 columns) is roughly 6 instructions per iteration: 3× OP_COLUMN, 1× OP_RESULT_ROW, 1× OP_NEXT, plus the switch dispatch overhead. At ~5ns per switch dispatch (a conservative estimate on modern hardware), that's ~30ns per row for VM overhead alone.
For 10,000 rows: 30ns × 10,000 = 300μs = 0.3ms.
Even if you add the mock cursor overhead (array indexing, value copying), you're well under 1ms. The 100ms budget is generous — it accounts for the eventual B-tree cursor, buffer pool lookups, and possible disk I/O. For this milestone with in-memory mock data, you should see sub-millisecond execution.
To measure:
```c
#include <time.h>
void benchmark_select_star(void) {
    // Create a mock table with 10,000 rows, 3 columns each
    int n_rows = 10000;
    int n_cols = 3;
    Value **rows = malloc(n_rows * sizeof(Value*));
    for (int i = 0; i < n_rows; i++) {
        rows[i] = malloc(n_cols * sizeof(Value));
        rows[i][0].type = VAL_INTEGER;
        rows[i][0].i = i;                    // id
        rows[i][1].type = VAL_STRING;
        rows[i][1].s.data = "test_user";
        rows[i][1].s.length = 9;             // name
        rows[i][2].type = VAL_INTEGER;
        rows[i][2].i = 20 + (i % 50);        // age
    }
    // Compile SELECT * FROM t
    // ... (set up compiler with mock schema) ...
    // Execute and time
    struct timespec start, end;
    clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &start);
    VM vm;
    vm_init(&vm, &prog);
    vm.result_callback = null_callback;  // discard results for benchmarking
    vm_execute(&vm);
    clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &end);
    double ms = (end.tv_sec - start.tv_sec) * 1000.0 + 
                (end.tv_nsec - start.tv_nsec) / 1e6;
    printf("10,000-row SELECT *: %.2f ms\n", ms);
    assert(ms < 100.0);
}
```
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens When You Execute a Query
**Level 1 — Public API**: You call `execute_sql("SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 21", db)`. Results stream back through a callback, one row at a time.
**Level 2 — VDBE Engine**: The compiler walks the AST once and emits ~10 bytecode instructions. The VM enters its fetch-decode-execute loop. OP_OPEN_READ opens a cursor. OP_REWIND positions it at the first row. Each iteration: OP_COLUMN loads `name` and `age` into registers, OP_INTEGER loads 21, OP_LE checks the condition and jumps past OP_RESULT_ROW if it fails, OP_NEXT advances the cursor and loops. OP_CLOSE and OP_HALT clean up.
**Level 3 — Storage (stubbed for now)**: OP_COLUMN calls `btree_cursor_column()`, which (in the mock) is an array index lookup — O(1), ~1ns. In the real implementation (Milestone 5), this will deserialize a variable-length record from a 4096-byte page, which involves reading a cell pointer, jumping to the cell offset, and decoding a varint-encoded record header. OP_NEXT will follow a pointer to the next cell in the page, or to the next leaf page if the current page is exhausted. Each page access goes through the buffer pool (Milestone 4), which may trigger an LRU eviction and a disk read if the page isn't cached. The VM doesn't know any of this — it just calls `btree_cursor_next()` and gets a boolean back.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What the VDBE Unlocks
**→ Virtual Machines Everywhere**: The fetch-decode-execute loop you just built is the same pattern used by the JVM (Java bytecode), CPython (Python bytecode), Lua (Lua bytecode), WebAssembly (wasm), and the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM). Every one of these maintains a program counter, decodes instructions from a flat array, and dispatches to handler functions. If you understand the VDBE, you understand the core of every language runtime. The difference is in the instruction set — the JVM has opcodes for object allocation and method dispatch; the VDBE has opcodes for cursor movement and record serialization — but the execution model is identical.
**→ Register Allocation and Compiler Backends**: Your simple "increment a counter" register allocation works here because query bytecode programs are short (rarely >100 instructions) and register pressure is low. Real compilers (GCC, LLVM) face programs with thousands of live variables competing for ~16 hardware registers. They use **graph coloring** — building an interference graph where edges connect simultaneously-live variables, then coloring the graph with the number of available registers. Variables that can't be colored "spill" to memory (the stack). Your VDBE sidesteps this entirely by having 256 virtual registers — no spilling needed. But the core problem is the same: deciding which intermediate value lives where.
**→ The Cursor as Iterator Pattern**: The OP_OPEN_READ / OP_REWIND / OP_NEXT / OP_COLUMN / OP_CLOSE sequence implements the GoF **Iterator pattern** in bytecode form. This same pattern appears in Java's `Iterator<T>` interface (`hasNext()` / `next()`), Python's `__iter__` / `__next__` protocol, and Rust's `Iterator` trait. In databases, cursors are fundamental — a join is two cursors nested; a sort is a cursor over sorted output; an aggregate is a cursor that accumulates values. Every query plan is a tree of cursors.
**→ EXPLAIN as Disassembly**: Your EXPLAIN command is the database equivalent of `objdump -d` (disassembling machine code) or `javap -c` (disassembling Java bytecode). The principle is the same: making the machine's actual execution plan human-readable for debugging. PostgreSQL's `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` goes further — it executes the plan and reports actual timing per operation, like a profiler (`perf`) for queries. This is the foundational insight of **observability**: systems that expose their internal execution are easier to debug, optimize, and trust.
**→ The Frontend/Backend Split**: Your tokenizer, parser, and compiler form the **frontend**. Your VM and cursor interface form the **backend**. The bytecode is the **intermediate representation** (IR) that connects them. This is the universal compiler architecture: GCC has a C frontend and an x86 backend connected by GIMPLE IR; LLVM has frontends for C/C++/Rust/Swift and backends for x86/ARM/RISC-V connected by LLVM IR. Your VDBE bytecode is your IR. When you later add JOIN support or subqueries, you modify only the compiler (emitting new opcode patterns) — the VM is unchanged, as long as the opcodes already exist.
---
## System Awareness
You've built the VDBE — the execution engine at the heart of your database. It connects to the **parser** (upstream, Milestone 2) via AST consumption and to the **B-tree storage engine** (downstream, Milestones 4-5) via the cursor interface. Currently, the cursor is a mock backed by in-memory arrays.
