In the next two milestones, you'll replace that mock with a buffer pool managing 4096-byte pages (Milestone 4) and a B-tree that stores rows in those pages (Milestone 5).
Without the VDBE, you have a parser that produces beautiful ASTs that nobody executes. With it, your database can actually answer queries — tokenize SQL, parse it, compile it to bytecode, and run it against data. EXPLAIN gives you a window into the machine's mind. And the cursor abstraction means the VM will work identically whether data comes from an in-memory array, a B-tree on disk, or (eventually) an index scan that skips 99% of rows.
---
<!-- END_MS -->


<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m4 -->
<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m4 -->
# Milestone 4: Buffer Pool Manager
## Where We Are in the System
In Milestone 3, you built a virtual machine that executes bytecode. Every `OP_COLUMN`, `OP_NEXT`, and `OP_INSERT` instruction calls through a cursor interface — `btree_cursor_column()`, `btree_cursor_next()`, `btree_cursor_insert()`. You stubbed that interface with a mock backed by in-memory arrays. Now you build the layer that sits directly beneath the B-tree: the **buffer pool manager**.

![SQLite Engine — Full System Architecture Map](./diagrams/diag-satellite-system-map.svg)

The buffer pool is the boundary between memory and disk. The B-tree layer (Milestone 5) will think in terms of *pages* — fixed-size blocks of bytes, each identified by a page number. It will say "give me page 42" and expect to get a pointer to 4096 bytes of data. Whether those bytes are already in RAM or need to be loaded from disk is the buffer pool's problem. The B-tree never calls `read()` or `write()` directly. It never manages memory allocation for page data. It asks the buffer pool for pages, reads or modifies them, and tells the buffer pool when it's done.

![Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page](./diagrams/diag-data-flow-sql-to-disk.svg)

This is the same architectural role that a CPU cache plays between the processor and main memory, or that a CDN plays between users and origin servers. The buffer pool is a **cache** — but one with responsibilities no general-purpose cache has. It must track which pages have been modified (dirty tracking), prevent eviction of pages in active use (pin counting), and flush dirty pages in the correct order for crash safety (write ordering). These responsibilities are why every serious database — SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle — manages its own buffer pool rather than relying on the operating system.
---
## The Revelation: Why Not Just Use `mmap`?
You might think: the operating system already caches file pages in memory. When you `read()` a file, the kernel loads the data into the **OS page cache** — a kernel-managed LRU cache of recently-accessed disk pages. When you `mmap()` a file, the OS maps file pages directly into your process's virtual address space, and page faults trigger on-demand loading. The OS handles caching, eviction, and write-back. Why build your own?
This is a reasonable intuition, and it's wrong for databases. Here's why.
**Problem 1: No eviction control.** The OS page cache uses a general-purpose LRU (actually a variant called "two-list LRU" or "CLOCK" on Linux). It has no idea which pages your database *needs* to keep. If the OS is under memory pressure — another process allocates a large buffer, or the kernel needs memory for its own structures — it will evict your hot database pages. You have no say in the matter. With your own buffer pool, you control exactly which pages stay resident.
**Problem 2: No pin counting.** A B-tree traversal may follow a chain of pages: root → internal → internal → leaf. While traversing, all four pages must remain in memory — if the root page gets evicted while you're halfway through the traversal, you'll read garbage or crash. `mmap` provides no mechanism to say "don't evict this page right now." Your buffer pool provides **pin counting**: a page's pin count is incremented when someone is using it and decremented when they're done. Pinned pages are never evicted.
**Problem 3: No dirty tracking.** When you modify a page through `mmap`, the kernel marks it dirty in its own page tables — but you have no way to query "which of my pages are dirty?" or to control *when* dirty pages are written back. The kernel will flush them at its convenience, possibly in any order. For crash safety, you need to flush the rollback journal (or WAL) *before* any dirty data pages — this is the **write-ahead logging** protocol (Milestone 9). `mmap` gives you no control over flush ordering.
**Problem 4: No write ordering.** This is the killer. With `mmap`, calling `msync()` flushes dirty pages, but you can't control which pages get flushed first. If the OS writes a dirty data page to the database file before your journal entry is on disk, and then the system crashes, you've lost the ability to recover. Your buffer pool ensures that `FlushAll` writes pages in the correct order, and that individual dirty pages are only written back through controlled paths.
LMDB (Lightning Memory-Mapped Database) does use `mmap` successfully, but it accepts significant constraints: it uses copy-on-write semantics (never modifying pages in place), requires the entire database to fit in virtual address space, and relies on the OS for all caching decisions. These are real tradeoffs, not free lunches.

![Buffer Pool Architecture](./diagrams/diag-buffer-pool-architecture.svg)

The buffer pool gives you a **controlled cache** with four guarantees no OS-level mechanism provides: you choose what stays (eviction policy), you prevent premature removal (pin counting), you know what changed (dirty tracking), and you control write order (flush protocol).
---
## The Core Data Structures
The buffer pool manages a fixed number of **frames** — slots in memory that can each hold one page. Each frame is a contiguous 4096-byte buffer plus metadata about which page it currently holds:
```c
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdbool.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <errno.h>
#define DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE 4096
#define DEFAULT_POOL_SIZE 1000
typedef uint32_t page_id_t;
#define INVALID_PAGE_ID ((page_id_t)-1)
typedef struct {
    page_id_t page_id;      // which page is stored here (INVALID if empty)
    uint8_t  *data;          // pointer to PAGE_SIZE bytes of page data
    bool      is_dirty;      // has this page been modified since loading?
    int       pin_count;     // number of active users of this page
    // LRU tracking (doubly-linked list pointers)
    int       lru_prev;      // index of previous frame in LRU list (-1 = head)
    int       lru_next;      // index of next frame in LRU list (-1 = tail)
} Frame;
```
The buffer pool itself:
```c
typedef struct {
    // Configuration
    int       page_size;     // bytes per page (default 4096)
    int       pool_size;     // number of frames
    // Storage
    Frame    *frames;        // array of pool_size frames
    int       fd;            // file descriptor for the database file
    // Page-to-frame mapping: hash table for O(1) lookup
    int      *page_table;    // hash table: page_id → frame index
    int       page_table_size; // size of hash table (prime, > pool_size)
    // LRU list (doubly-linked through frame indices)
    int       lru_head;      // most recently used (front)
    int       lru_tail;      // least recently used (back) — evict from here
    // Free list
    int      *free_list;     // stack of unused frame indices
    int       free_count;    // number of free frames
    // Statistics
    uint64_t  hits;          // page found in pool
    uint64_t  misses;        // page loaded from disk
    uint64_t  evictions;     // pages evicted
    uint64_t  dirty_writes;  // dirty pages written back
} BufferPool;
```
Three data structures deserve detailed attention: the **page table** (hash map), the **LRU list** (doubly-linked list), and the **free list** (stack).
**The page table** maps page IDs to frame indices. When the B-tree requests page 42, the buffer pool needs to know instantly whether page 42 is already in a frame. A hash table gives O(1) average-case lookup. The simplest approach is **open addressing with linear probing** — a flat array where the hash of the page ID gives the starting index, and collisions are resolved by scanning forward:
```c
#define EMPTY_SLOT (-1)
static int page_table_lookup(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id) {
    int idx = page_id % pool->page_table_size;
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->page_table_size; i++) {
        int slot = (idx + i) % pool->page_table_size;
        if (pool->page_table[slot] == EMPTY_SLOT) {
            return -1;  // not found
        }
        if (pool->frames[pool->page_table[slot]].page_id == page_id) {
            return pool->page_table[slot];  // found: return frame index
        }
    }
    return -1;  // table full (shouldn't happen if sized correctly)
}
static void page_table_insert(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id, 
                                int frame_idx) {
    int idx = page_id % pool->page_table_size;
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->page_table_size; i++) {
        int slot = (idx + i) % pool->page_table_size;
        if (pool->page_table[slot] == EMPTY_SLOT) {
            pool->page_table[slot] = frame_idx;
            return;
        }
    }
    // Should never reach here — table is sized larger than pool
}
static void page_table_remove(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id) {
    int idx = page_id % pool->page_table_size;
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->page_table_size; i++) {
        int slot = (idx + i) % pool->page_table_size;
        if (pool->page_table[slot] == EMPTY_SLOT) return;
        if (pool->frames[pool->page_table[slot]].page_id == page_id) {
            pool->page_table[slot] = EMPTY_SLOT;
            // Rehash subsequent entries to fix probing chain
            // (omitted for clarity — needed in production)
            return;
        }
    }
}
```
The page table size should be a prime number at least 2× the pool size to keep load factor below 0.5, ensuring fast lookups even with collisions.
**The LRU list** is a doubly-linked list threaded *through* the frame array. Rather than allocating separate linked-list nodes, each frame has `lru_prev` and `lru_next` fields that store frame indices. The head of the list is the most recently used page; the tail is the least recently used. When a page is accessed, it moves to the head. When a page must be evicted, we scan from the tail backward, looking for the first unpinned frame.
**The free list** is a simple stack of frame indices that have never been used (or have been freed). On startup, all frame indices are on the free list. As pages are loaded, frames come off the free list. Once the free list is empty, eviction is needed.
---
## Initialization
```c
BufferPool* buffer_pool_create(const char *db_path, int pool_size, 
                                int page_size) {
    BufferPool *pool = calloc(1, sizeof(BufferPool));
    pool->page_size = page_size > 0 ? page_size : DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE;
    pool->pool_size = pool_size > 0 ? pool_size : DEFAULT_POOL_SIZE;
    // Open database file (create if not exists)
    pool->fd = open(db_path, O_RDWR | O_CREAT, 0644);
    if (pool->fd < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to open database file: %s\n", strerror(errno));
        free(pool);
        return NULL;
    }
    // Allocate frames
    pool->frames = calloc(pool->pool_size, sizeof(Frame));
    // Allocate one contiguous block for all page data
    uint8_t *data_block = aligned_alloc(pool->page_size, 
                                         pool->pool_size * pool->page_size);
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->pool_size; i++) {
        pool->frames[i].page_id = INVALID_PAGE_ID;
        pool->frames[i].data = data_block + (i * pool->page_size);
        pool->frames[i].is_dirty = false;
        pool->frames[i].pin_count = 0;
        pool->frames[i].lru_prev = -1;
        pool->frames[i].lru_next = -1;
    }
    // Initialize page table (prime size > 2 * pool_size)
    pool->page_table_size = next_prime(pool->pool_size * 2 + 1);
    pool->page_table = malloc(pool->page_table_size * sizeof(int));
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->page_table_size; i++) {
        pool->page_table[i] = EMPTY_SLOT;
    }
    // Initialize free list (all frames are free)
    pool->free_list = malloc(pool->pool_size * sizeof(int));
    pool->free_count = pool->pool_size;
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->pool_size; i++) {
        pool->free_list[i] = i;
    }
    // LRU starts empty
    pool->lru_head = -1;
    pool->lru_tail = -1;
    // Statistics
    pool->hits = 0;
    pool->misses = 0;
    pool->evictions = 0;
    pool->dirty_writes = 0;
    return pool;
}
```
Notice the `aligned_alloc` call for page data. Allocating one contiguous block aligned to the page size has two benefits. First, all page data occupies a single memory region, which is cache-friendly — the CPU's hardware prefetcher handles sequential access patterns well. Second, page-aligned buffers enable **direct I/O** (`O_DIRECT`) in the future, which bypasses the OS page cache entirely and gives your buffer pool full control over caching. Direct I/O requires buffers aligned to the filesystem's block size (typically 512 or 4096 bytes).
---
## FetchPage: The Central Operation
`FetchPage` is the buffer pool's core API. The B-tree calls it to get a pointer to a page's data. The buffer pool either returns the page from its cache (a **hit**) or loads it from disk (a **miss**), potentially evicting another page to make room.

![FetchPage Decision Flow](./diagrams/diag-buffer-pool-fetch-flow.svg)

```c
// Returns a pointer to the page data, or NULL on error.
// The page is PINNED on return — caller must call unpin_page() when done.
uint8_t* fetch_page(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id) {
    // Step 1: Check if page is already in the pool
    int frame_idx = page_table_lookup(pool, page_id);
    if (frame_idx >= 0) {
        // HIT: page is already in memory
        pool->hits++;
        Frame *frame = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
        frame->pin_count++;
        // Move to front of LRU (most recently used)
        lru_move_to_front(pool, frame_idx);
        return frame->data;
    }
    // MISS: page is not in memory — need a free frame
    pool->misses++;
    frame_idx = get_free_frame(pool);
    if (frame_idx < 0) {
        // No free frames and no evictable pages
        fprintf(stderr, "Buffer pool exhausted: all pages are pinned\n");
        return NULL;
    }
    // Step 2: Load page from disk into the frame
    Frame *frame = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
    frame->page_id = page_id;
    frame->is_dirty = false;
    frame->pin_count = 1;
    disk_read_page(pool, page_id, frame->data);
    // Step 3: Register in page table and LRU
    page_table_insert(pool, page_id, frame_idx);
    lru_push_front(pool, frame_idx);
    return frame->data;
}
```
The function's contract is critical: **the returned page is pinned**. The caller holds a "lease" on this page — the buffer pool will not evict it until the caller calls `unpin_page()`. This is the **pin/unpin protocol**, and violating it causes either data corruption (evicting a page someone is reading) or deadlock (never unpinning, exhausting the pool).
### Disk I/O
Reading and writing pages uses `pread` and `pwrite` — POSIX functions that perform I/O at a specific file offset without changing the file descriptor's position. This is essential for a multi-threaded database where multiple cursors might be reading different pages concurrently:
```c
static void disk_read_page(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id, 
                            uint8_t *dest) {
    off_t offset = (off_t)page_id * pool->page_size;
    ssize_t bytes = pread(pool->fd, dest, pool->page_size, offset);
    if (bytes < pool->page_size) {
        // Page doesn't exist on disk yet (new page) — zero-fill
        if (bytes < 0) bytes = 0;
        memset(dest + bytes, 0, pool->page_size - bytes);
    }
}
static void disk_write_page(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id, 
                              uint8_t *src) {
    off_t offset = (off_t)page_id * pool->page_size;
    ssize_t bytes = pwrite(pool->fd, src, pool->page_size, offset);
    if (bytes != pool->page_size) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to write page %u: %s\n", 
                page_id, strerror(errno));
    }
    pool->dirty_writes++;
}
```
The zero-fill for new pages is important. When the B-tree creates a new page (for a node split, or for a new table's root page), it calls `fetch_page` with a page ID that doesn't exist on disk yet. Rather than returning an error, the buffer pool returns a zero-filled frame. The B-tree layer will immediately write its page header and initial structure into this buffer, and the next `FlushAll` or eviction will write it to disk.
---
## Pin and Unpin: The Lease Protocol

> **🔑 Foundation: LRU cache eviction strategy**
> 
> ## LRU Cache Eviction Strategy
**What it is:** LRU (Least Recently Used) is a cache replacement policy that, when the cache is full and a new entry needs space, evicts the entry that hasn't been accessed for the longest time. The core assumption: if you haven't touched something recently, you're less likely to need it soon.
**Why you need it now:** Your database's buffer pool holds a fixed number of pages in memory. When a query needs a page that isn't cached, you must load it from disk — but if the pool is full, something has to go. LRU gives you a principled, efficient way to decide *what* to discard. Without an eviction strategy, you'd either run out of memory or evict pages you're about to need again (causing expensive extra disk reads).
**Key mental model:** Picture a stack of plates. Every time you use a plate, you pull it out and put it on top. When you need to get rid of one, you always take from the bottom — that's the plate nobody has touched in the longest time. In implementation terms, this is a **doubly-linked list + hash map** combo: the linked list tracks access order (O(1) move-to-front on access), and the hash map gives you O(1) lookup by page ID. On eviction, you pop the tail.
```
Access page A → [A]
Access page B → [B, A]
Access page A → [A, B]        ← A moves to front
Access page C → [C, A, B]
Cache full, need page D:
  Evict B (tail) → [D, C, A]
```
One practical wrinkle: if an evicted page has been modified ("dirty"), you must write it back to disk *before* reclaiming that slot — which connects directly to durability concerns.

Pin counting is **reference counting** applied to cached pages. The concept is identical to `std::shared_ptr` in C++, `Arc<T>` in Rust, or reference counting in Python's garbage collector. Each time someone acquires a reference to a page, the count increments. Each time they release it, the count decrements. When the count reaches zero, the page is eligible for eviction (though it's not evicted immediately — it stays in the cache for future hits).
```c
void unpin_page(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id, bool is_dirty) {
    int frame_idx = page_table_lookup(pool, page_id);
    if (frame_idx < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "unpin_page: page %u not in buffer pool\n", page_id);
        return;
    }
    Frame *frame = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
    if (frame->pin_count <= 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "unpin_page: page %u pin count already 0\n", page_id);
        return;
    }
    frame->pin_count--;
    // If the caller modified the page, mark it dirty
    if (is_dirty) {
        frame->is_dirty = true;
    }
}
```
The `is_dirty` parameter is crucial. The caller tells the buffer pool whether it modified the page's data. This is how the buffer pool knows which pages need to be written back to disk. The B-tree calls `unpin_page(pool, page_id, true)` after modifying a page (e.g., inserting a row), and `unpin_page(pool, page_id, false)` after only reading it.

![Dirty Page Lifecycle](./diagrams/diag-buffer-pool-dirty-tracking.svg)

This is **write-back caching** — modifications accumulate in memory and are written to disk lazily (on eviction or explicit flush). The alternative is **write-through caching**, where every modification is immediately written to disk. Write-through is simpler (no dirty tracking needed) but dramatically slower — every `INSERT` would require a synchronous disk write, costing ~10ms on spinning disk or ~100μs on SSD. Write-back batches these writes, amortizing the I/O cost across many modifications. CPU caches use both strategies depending on the cache coherence protocol; L1 caches are typically write-back for performance, while I/O-mapped device memory uses write-through for correctness.
### The Pin Count Invariant
There is one invariant that must never be violated:
> **A page with pin_count > 0 must never be evicted.**
If this invariant breaks, the B-tree holds a pointer to a frame whose contents have been replaced with a different page. It reads data from page 42 but gets page 117's bytes. The result is silent data corruption — the most dangerous kind of bug because it may not manifest until much later when the corrupted data is read by a different query.
Enforce this invariant in the eviction function. Never "trust" callers to unpin correctly — always check.
---
## LRU Eviction
When `FetchPage` encounters a miss and the free list is empty, it must **evict** an existing page to make room. The eviction policy determines which page is sacrificed.

![LRU Eviction State Evolution](./diagrams/diag-buffer-pool-lru-eviction.svg)

**LRU (Least Recently Used)** evicts the page that hasn't been accessed for the longest time. The intuition: if a page hasn't been touched recently, it's less likely to be needed soon. This is a temporal locality heuristic — it assumes that access patterns exhibit **temporal locality**, meaning pages accessed recently will be accessed again soon.
LRU is implemented as a doubly-linked list threaded through the frame array. Every time a page is accessed (fetched or pinned), it moves to the head (most recently used). Eviction starts from the tail (least recently used) and scans toward the head, looking for the first frame with pin_count == 0.
```c
// LRU list operations — O(1) each
static void lru_remove(BufferPool *pool, int frame_idx) {
    Frame *f = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
    if (f->lru_prev >= 0) {
        pool->frames[f->lru_prev].lru_next = f->lru_next;
    } else {
        pool->lru_head = f->lru_next;  // was the head
    }
    if (f->lru_next >= 0) {
        pool->frames[f->lru_next].lru_prev = f->lru_prev;
    } else {
        pool->lru_tail = f->lru_prev;  // was the tail
    }
    f->lru_prev = -1;
    f->lru_next = -1;
}
static void lru_push_front(BufferPool *pool, int frame_idx) {
    Frame *f = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
    f->lru_prev = -1;
    f->lru_next = pool->lru_head;
    if (pool->lru_head >= 0) {
        pool->frames[pool->lru_head].lru_prev = frame_idx;
    }
    pool->lru_head = frame_idx;
    if (pool->lru_tail < 0) {
        pool->lru_tail = frame_idx;  // first element
    }
}
static void lru_move_to_front(BufferPool *pool, int frame_idx) {
    if (pool->lru_head == frame_idx) return;  // already at front
    lru_remove(pool, frame_idx);
    lru_push_front(pool, frame_idx);
}
```
The eviction scan:
```c
// Find a free frame: try free list first, then evict.
// Returns frame index, or -1 if all frames are pinned.
static int get_free_frame(BufferPool *pool) {
    // Try the free list first
    if (pool->free_count > 0) {
        pool->free_count--;
        return pool->free_list[pool->free_count];
    }
    // Free list empty — evict from LRU tail
    int frame_idx = pool->lru_tail;
    while (frame_idx >= 0) {
        Frame *f = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
        if (f->pin_count == 0) {
            // Found an evictable frame
            evict_frame(pool, frame_idx);
            return frame_idx;
        }
        // This frame is pinned — try the next one toward the head
        frame_idx = f->lru_prev;
    }
    // Every frame in the pool is pinned — cannot evict
    return -1;
}
static void evict_frame(BufferPool *pool, int frame_idx) {
    Frame *f = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
    // Write back dirty page before evicting
    if (f->is_dirty) {
        disk_write_page(pool, f->page_id, f->data);
        f->is_dirty = false;
    }
    // Remove from page table and LRU
    page_table_remove(pool, f->page_id);
    lru_remove(pool, frame_idx);
    // Mark frame as empty
    f->page_id = INVALID_PAGE_ID;
    pool->evictions++;
}
```
Study the eviction logic carefully. The scan starts at the LRU tail (the *least* recently used page) and walks toward the head. The first unpinned frame it finds is evicted. If that frame is dirty, it's written back to disk *before* its data buffer is reused. This ordering — write-back then reuse — is essential. If you reused the buffer first and *then* tried to write it back, you'd write the new page's data to the old page's location on disk, corrupting both pages.
### Design Decision: LRU vs Clock vs LRU-K
| Policy | Pros | Cons | Used By |
|--------|------|------|---------|
| **True LRU ✓** | Optimal for pure recency, simple to understand | O(1) per access but requires doubly-linked list; susceptible to scan pollution | Teaching implementations |
| Clock (Second Chance) | O(1) amortized, no linked list, very cache-friendly | Approximation — ~95% of LRU hit rate, slightly worse under adversarial patterns | PostgreSQL, Linux page cache |
| LRU-K (K=2 typically) | Considers frequency + recency; resistant to scan pollution | More complex, needs history of last K accesses per page | SQL Server |
| LFU (Least Frequently Used) | Handles hot pages well | Stale popular pages never evict; needs aging | Some CDNs with decay |
True LRU is the right choice for your build. It's correct, understandable, and has O(1) operations via the doubly-linked list. The main weakness — **scan pollution**, where a full table scan loads every page once, evicting hot pages that will be needed again immediately — is a real problem in production databases but won't affect your initial build.
> **🔭 Deep Dive**: The Clock algorithm (also called "second-chance" or "not-recently-used") approximates LRU using a circular buffer and a reference bit per page. It's used by PostgreSQL and the Linux kernel because it avoids the linked-list overhead and is more cache-friendly. For a thorough treatment, see *Database Internals* by Alex Petrov, Chapter 5 ("Buffer Management"), or the original CLOCK paper by Corbató (1968).
---
## FlushAll and Controlled Write-Back
`FlushAll` writes every dirty page to disk. This is called before checkpoint (in WAL mode), before shutdown, and as part of transaction commit (in rollback journal mode). It must also call `fsync` to ensure the data reaches persistent storage rather than lingering in the OS's write-back buffers.

> **🔑 Foundation: fsync system call and durability guarantees**
> 
> ## fsync and Durability Guarantees
**What it is:** `fsync(fd)` is a system call that forces the OS to flush all modified in-memory data (and metadata) for a given file descriptor to the physical storage device. It blocks until the disk confirms the write is complete. Without it, `write()` calls may only update the OS page cache — data that lives in RAM and vanishes on a crash.
**Why you need it now:** A database that claims a transaction is "committed" must guarantee that committed data survives a power failure or crash. This is the **D in ACID** (Durability). When your buffer pool evicts a dirty page or when you append to a write-ahead log, calling `write()` alone is not enough — the data could be sitting in the kernel's buffer cache. `fsync` is what actually gets bits onto the platter (or flash cells).
**Key insight:** Think of `write()` and `fsync()` as a two-phase process:
```
write(fd, data, len)   →  data lands in kernel page cache (RAM)
fsync(fd)              →  kernel flushes cache to disk hardware
```
The critical tradeoff is **performance vs. safety**. `fsync` is *expensive* — it can take milliseconds because it waits for physical I/O. That's why databases don't fsync after every single write. Instead, they batch writes (e.g., writing multiple log records, then issuing one fsync), or use the WAL so that only the sequential log file needs to be fsynced on commit, while data pages can be flushed lazily.
A common pitfall: `fsync` on the file alone may not be enough. If you just *created* the file, you should also `fsync` the parent **directory** to ensure the directory entry pointing to your file is durable. Otherwise, after a crash the file might not exist at all even though its contents were fsynced.

```c
void buffer_pool_flush_all(BufferPool *pool) {
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->pool_size; i++) {
        Frame *f = &pool->frames[i];
        if (f->page_id != INVALID_PAGE_ID && f->is_dirty) {
            disk_write_page(pool, f->page_id, f->data);
            f->is_dirty = false;
        }
    }
    // Ensure all writes reach persistent storage
    fsync(pool->fd);
}
```
You also need the ability to flush a *single* page — the transaction layer (Milestone 9) will need to flush specific journal pages before modifying data pages:
```c
void buffer_pool_flush_page(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id) {
    int frame_idx = page_table_lookup(pool, page_id);
    if (frame_idx < 0) return;  // not in pool, nothing to flush
    Frame *f = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
    if (f->is_dirty) {
        disk_write_page(pool, f->page_id, f->data);
        f->is_dirty = false;
    }
}
```
The `fsync` call in `flush_all` deserves emphasis. Without it, `pwrite` merely hands data to the kernel, which may buffer it in the OS page cache indefinitely. The data is "written" from your process's perspective, but it hasn't reached the physical disk platter (or SSD flash cells). If the machine crashes before the kernel flushes its buffers, your "written" data is lost. `fsync` forces the kernel to push all pending writes for this file descriptor to the storage device and wait for the device to confirm persistence. On Linux, `fdatasync` is a lighter alternative that skips metadata updates (modification time) — acceptable for data pages where you don't need file metadata durability.
---
## Allocating New Pages
The B-tree layer needs to allocate new pages — for node splits, new table roots, and new index roots. The buffer pool tracks the total number of pages in the database file:
```c
page_id_t buffer_pool_new_page(BufferPool *pool) {
    // Determine next page ID from file size
    off_t file_size = lseek(pool->fd, 0, SEEK_END);
    page_id_t new_page_id = (page_id_t)(file_size / pool->page_size);
    // Extend the file by one page (write zeros)
    uint8_t zeros[DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE];
    memset(zeros, 0, pool->page_size);
    pwrite(pool->fd, zeros, pool->page_size, 
           (off_t)new_page_id * pool->page_size);
    // Fetch it into the buffer pool (which will load the zeros)
    fetch_page(pool, new_page_id);
    return new_page_id;
}
```
In a more sophisticated implementation, you'd maintain a **free page list** — a list of pages that were previously allocated but have since been freed (e.g., after dropping a table or compacting a B-tree). New allocations would first check this free list before extending the file. SQLite maintains such a list in its file header. For your initial build, always extending the file is sufficient.
---
## Mark Dirty: Separating the Decision
An alternative to passing `is_dirty` through `unpin_page` is a separate `mark_dirty` function. This gives the B-tree layer more flexibility — it can mark a page dirty at the moment of modification, rather than remembering to pass `true` when unpinning:
```c
void mark_page_dirty(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id) {
    int frame_idx = page_table_lookup(pool, page_id);
    if (frame_idx < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "mark_dirty: page %u not in pool\n", page_id);
        return;
    }
    pool->frames[frame_idx].is_dirty = true;
}
```
Both approaches work. SQLite uses the `unpin`-with-dirty-flag pattern. PostgreSQL uses a separate `MarkBufferDirty` call. For your build, supporting both gives the most flexibility.
---
## Statistics and Hit Rate Monitoring
The buffer pool tracks hits, misses, evictions, and dirty writes. The **hit rate** — `hits / (hits + misses)` — is the single most important performance metric for a database's memory subsystem.
```c
typedef struct {
    uint64_t hits;
    uint64_t misses;
    uint64_t evictions;
    uint64_t dirty_writes;
    double   hit_rate;
} BufferPoolStats;
BufferPoolStats buffer_pool_get_stats(BufferPool *pool) {
    BufferPoolStats stats;
    stats.hits = pool->hits;
    stats.misses = pool->misses;
    stats.evictions = pool->evictions;
    stats.dirty_writes = pool->dirty_writes;
    uint64_t total = pool->hits + pool->misses;
    stats.hit_rate = total > 0 ? (double)pool->hits / total : 0.0;
    return stats;
}
void buffer_pool_print_stats(BufferPool *pool) {
    BufferPoolStats s = buffer_pool_get_stats(pool);
    printf("Buffer Pool Stats:\n");
    printf("  Pool size:    %d frames (%d KB)\n", 
           pool->pool_size, pool->pool_size * pool->page_size / 1024);
    printf("  Hits:         %lu\n", s.hits);
    printf("  Misses:       %lu\n", s.misses);
    printf("  Hit rate:     %.2f%%\n", s.hit_rate * 100);
    printf("  Evictions:    %lu\n", s.evictions);
    printf("  Dirty writes: %lu\n", s.dirty_writes);
}
```
A healthy database should have a hit rate above 95%. If your hit rate drops below 90%, it means the working set (the set of pages actively being accessed) doesn't fit in the buffer pool, and you're thrashing — constantly evicting pages only to reload them immediately. The fix is either to increase pool size or to optimize the query's access pattern (e.g., use an index to avoid a full table scan that touches every page).
In production, PostgreSQL exposes this via `pg_stat_bgwriter` and `pg_buffercache`. Your equivalent is `buffer_pool_print_stats`, which you can call after each query or periodically to monitor cache behavior.
---
## Cleanup and Shutdown
```c
void buffer_pool_destroy(BufferPool *pool) {
    // Flush all dirty pages before closing
    buffer_pool_flush_all(pool);
    // Free memory
    free(pool->frames[0].data);  // free the contiguous data block
    free(pool->frames);
    free(pool->page_table);
    free(pool->free_list);
    close(pool->fd);
    free(pool);
}
```
The `flush_all` call in `destroy` is essential. Without it, dirty pages in the pool are lost when the process exits — the data the user thought was saved is gone. This is a correctness guarantee: `buffer_pool_destroy` ensures all modifications are durable.
---
## The Complete Public API
Summarizing the buffer pool's interface — this is the contract between the buffer pool and the B-tree layer:
```c
// Lifecycle
BufferPool* buffer_pool_create(const char *db_path, int pool_size, 
                                int page_size);
void        buffer_pool_destroy(BufferPool *pool);
// Page access
uint8_t*    fetch_page(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id);
void        unpin_page(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id, bool is_dirty);
void        mark_page_dirty(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id);
// Page allocation
page_id_t   buffer_pool_new_page(BufferPool *pool);
// Flush
void        buffer_pool_flush_page(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id);
void        buffer_pool_flush_all(BufferPool *pool);
// Statistics
BufferPoolStats buffer_pool_get_stats(BufferPool *pool);
void            buffer_pool_print_stats(BufferPool *pool);
```
This is a narrow interface — eight functions. The B-tree layer (Milestone 5) will use `fetch_page` and `unpin_page` dozens of times per query, but the buffer pool's internal complexity (hash tables, linked lists, eviction scans, disk I/O) is entirely hidden. The B-tree never knows whether a `fetch_page` call triggered a disk read or returned from cache. This abstraction boundary is what makes the system maintainable — you can swap LRU for Clock, change the hash table to a cuckoo hash, or add direct I/O, all without touching the B-tree code.
---
## Testing the Buffer Pool
### Test 1: Basic Fetch and Pin
```c
void test_basic_fetch(void) {
    BufferPool *pool = buffer_pool_create("test.db", 10, 4096);
    // Allocate and write a page
    page_id_t pid = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    uint8_t *data = fetch_page(pool, pid);
    assert(data != NULL);
    // Write recognizable data
    memcpy(data, "HELLO BUFFER POOL", 17);
    unpin_page(pool, pid, true);  // marked dirty
    // Fetch again — should be a hit
    uint64_t hits_before = pool->hits;
    data = fetch_page(pool, pid);
    assert(pool->hits == hits_before + 1);
    assert(memcmp(data, "HELLO BUFFER POOL", 17) == 0);
    unpin_page(pool, pid, false);
    BufferPoolStats stats = buffer_pool_get_stats(pool);
    assert(stats.hit_rate > 0.0);
    buffer_pool_destroy(pool);
    printf("PASS: basic fetch and pin\n");
}
```
### Test 2: Eviction Writes Dirty Pages
```c
void test_dirty_eviction(void) {
    // Small pool: only 3 frames
    BufferPool *pool = buffer_pool_create("test_evict.db", 3, 4096);
    // Fill all 3 frames with dirty pages
    for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
        page_id_t pid = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
        uint8_t *data = fetch_page(pool, pid);
        data[0] = (uint8_t)(i + 1);  // write distinguishable data
        unpin_page(pool, pid, true);
    }
    // Fetch a 4th page — forces eviction of the LRU page (page 0)
    page_id_t pid3 = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    uint8_t *data = fetch_page(pool, pid3);
    assert(data != NULL);
    unpin_page(pool, pid3, false);
    assert(pool->evictions == 1);
    // Fetch page 0 again — should reload from disk with the data we wrote
    data = fetch_page(pool, 0);
    assert(data[0] == 1);  // the dirty data was written before eviction
    unpin_page(pool, 0, false);
    buffer_pool_destroy(pool);
    unlink("test_evict.db");
    printf("PASS: dirty eviction writes back to disk\n");
}
```
### Test 3: Pinned Pages Are Never Evicted
```c
void test_pin_prevents_eviction(void) {
    BufferPool *pool = buffer_pool_create("test_pin.db", 3, 4096);
    // Allocate 3 pages, keep page 0 pinned
    page_id_t p0 = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    uint8_t *data0 = fetch_page(pool, p0);  // pin_count = 1
    data0[0] = 0xAA;
    // DO NOT unpin page 0
    page_id_t p1 = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    uint8_t *d1 = fetch_page(pool, p1);
    unpin_page(pool, p1, false);
    page_id_t p2 = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    uint8_t *d2 = fetch_page(pool, p2);
    unpin_page(pool, p2, false);
    // Now fetch a 4th page — eviction must skip pinned page 0
    page_id_t p3 = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    uint8_t *d3 = fetch_page(pool, p3);
    unpin_page(pool, p3, false);
    // Page 0's data should still be valid (not evicted)
    assert(data0[0] == 0xAA);
    // Clean up: unpin page 0
    unpin_page(pool, p0, true);
    buffer_pool_destroy(pool);
    unlink("test_pin.db");
    printf("PASS: pinned pages are never evicted\n");
}
```
### Test 4: LRU Order
```c
void test_lru_order(void) {
    BufferPool *pool = buffer_pool_create("test_lru.db", 3, 4096);
    // Load pages 0, 1, 2 (in that order)
    for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
        buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
        uint8_t *d = fetch_page(pool, i);
        d[0] = (uint8_t)i;
        unpin_page(pool, i, true);
    }
    // LRU order (tail to head): 0, 1, 2
    // Access page 0 — moves it to head
    uint8_t *d = fetch_page(pool, 0);
    unpin_page(pool, 0, false);
    // LRU order (tail to head): 1, 2, 0
    // Fetch page 3 — should evict page 1 (now the LRU tail)
    buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    d = fetch_page(pool, 3);
    unpin_page(pool, 3, false);
    // Page 1 should have been evicted. Page 0 should still be resident.
    int frame_0 = page_table_lookup(pool, 0);
    int frame_1 = page_table_lookup(pool, 1);
    assert(frame_0 >= 0);   // page 0 is still in pool
    assert(frame_1 < 0);    // page 1 was evicted
    buffer_pool_destroy(pool);
    unlink("test_lru.db");
    printf("PASS: LRU eviction order correct\n");
}
```
### Test 5: Hit Rate Tracking
```c
void test_hit_rate(void) {
    BufferPool *pool = buffer_pool_create("test_stats.db", 100, 4096);
    // Create 5 pages
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
        uint8_t *d = fetch_page(pool, i);
        unpin_page(pool, i, false);
    }
    // 5 misses so far (first load of each page via new_page + fetch)
    // Access them again — all hits
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        uint8_t *d = fetch_page(pool, i);
        unpin_page(pool, i, false);
    }
    // 5 more hits
    BufferPoolStats stats = buffer_pool_get_stats(pool);
    // Hits should be 5, misses 5, hit rate 50%
    // (exact numbers depend on whether new_page internally calls fetch_page)
    assert(stats.hit_rate > 0.0);
    assert(stats.hits > 0);
    assert(stats.misses > 0);
    buffer_pool_print_stats(pool);
    buffer_pool_destroy(pool);
    unlink("test_stats.db");
    printf("PASS: hit rate tracking\n");
}
```
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens When You Fetch a Page
**Level 1 — Public API**: The B-tree calls `fetch_page(pool, 42)`. It receives a `uint8_t*` pointing to 4096 bytes of page 42's data. The page is pinned. After reading or modifying, the B-tree calls `unpin_page(pool, 42, dirty)`.
**Level 2 — Buffer Pool Engine**: Inside, the buffer pool hashes page 42 to check the page table. On a hit: increment pin count, move the frame to LRU head, return the data pointer. On a miss: pop a frame from the free list (or scan the LRU tail for an unpinned frame, writing back dirty data if needed), call `pread` to load the page from disk, register the frame in the page table, push it to LRU head, set pin count to 1, return the data pointer.
**Level 3 — OS and Hardware**: `pread` triggers a system call (`syscall` instruction), transitioning from user mode to kernel mode. The kernel checks its own page cache — if the requested file region is already cached, it copies from kernel memory to your buffer (a `memcpy` at ~10 GB/s, ~400ns for 4KB). If not, it issues a block I/O request to the disk driver. On SSD, this takes ~100μs; on spinning disk, ~10ms (seek time dominates). The data traverses: SSD/HDD → SATA/NVMe controller → DMA to kernel buffer → copy to your buffer. Once in your buffer pool frame, the 4KB page sits in user-space memory — likely in CPU L2 or L3 cache after the first access, where subsequent reads of individual bytes take ~4-10ns.
Your buffer pool sits between levels 1 and 3, absorbing the enormous latency difference between memory (~10ns) and disk (~100μs on SSD) by keeping hot pages resident. A 99% hit rate means 99% of page accesses cost 10ns instead of 100,000ns — a 10,000× speedup for those operations.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What the Buffer Pool Unlocks
**→ Cache Hierarchy (computer architecture)**: Your buffer pool is one layer in a hierarchy that spans the entire computer: CPU registers (~0.3ns) → L1 cache (~1ns, 64KB) → L2 cache (~4ns, 256KB) → L3 cache (~10ns, 8MB+) → RAM (~100ns, GB) → **your buffer pool** (~100ns for hit, ~100μs for miss) → OS page cache → SSD (~100μs) → HDD (~10ms). Every layer uses the same principle: keep frequently-accessed data closer to the consumer, evict the least-useful data when space runs out. The eviction algorithms (LRU, Clock, pseudo-LRU) reappear at every level. Understanding your buffer pool means understanding the universal caching pattern that permeates all of computing.
**→ Reference Counting as Memory Management**: Pin counting is reference counting — the same pattern that powers `shared_ptr<T>` in C++, `Arc<T>` in Rust, `Py_INCREF`/`Py_DECREF` in CPython, and `retain`/`release` in Objective-C. The insight is universal: when multiple consumers share a resource, track how many are using it, and only release (or evict) when the count reaches zero. The failure mode is also universal: if you forget to decrement (memory leak / buffer pool exhaustion) or decrement too many times (use-after-free / reading evicted page data), you get a bug that may not manifest until much later.
**→ Write-Back vs Write-Through Caching**: Your buffer pool uses write-back caching — modifications accumulate in memory and are flushed lazily. This is the same strategy used by CPU caches (L1/L2 are typically write-back), SSDs (flash translation layers buffer writes in DRAM), and distributed caches (Redis with `appendfsync everysec`). The tradeoff is always the same: write-back gives higher throughput (batch writes, reduce I/O) at the cost of a durability window — data can be lost if the system crashes before flush. Write-through eliminates the durability window but costs a synchronous I/O per write. Your transaction layer (Milestones 9-10) will layer WAL or journal on top of the buffer pool to close this durability gap — ensuring that even though data pages use write-back, the *log* is write-through (fsynced immediately), which is enough to guarantee recoverability.
**→ The Clock Algorithm**: Production databases rarely use true LRU because the doubly-linked list operations, while O(1), involve pointer updates that cause cache-line bouncing under concurrent access. The **Clock algorithm** (also called "second-chance") approximates LRU using a circular buffer and a single reference bit per page. A "clock hand" sweeps the buffer: if the reference bit is set, clear it and move on (give it a second chance); if clear, evict. This achieves ~95% of LRU's hit rate at lower implementation cost and is trivially parallelizable. PostgreSQL's buffer manager uses a clock sweep. Understanding LRU first makes the Clock algorithm immediately intuitive — it's just "lazy LRU" where you approximate recency with a single bit.
**→ Virtual Memory (the OS's buffer pool)**: The operating system's virtual memory subsystem is, structurally, a buffer pool. The OS manages a pool of physical page frames, maps virtual addresses to frames, uses a page replacement algorithm (usually Clock), tracks dirty pages via hardware page table dirty bits, and writes dirty pages back to swap before evicting. When your process accesses a virtual address whose page is not in physical memory, a **page fault** triggers — the kernel loads the page from disk (swap file or memory-mapped file), exactly like your `fetch_page` loading from the database file. You've just built in userspace what the OS kernel does in kernel space, but with database-specific intelligence (pin counting, crash-safe flush ordering) that the general-purpose OS implementation can't provide.
---
## System Awareness
You've built the buffer pool — the memory management layer that sits between the B-tree (upstream consumer, Milestone 5) and the disk (downstream storage). The B-tree will call `fetch_page` to get page data, `unpin_page` to release it, and `buffer_pool_new_page` to allocate fresh pages for node splits. The transaction layer (Milestones 9-10) will call `buffer_pool_flush_page` and `buffer_pool_flush_all` to enforce write ordering for crash safety.
Without the buffer pool, the B-tree would need to manage its own memory, perform its own disk I/O, and track its own dirty pages — mixing storage engine logic with I/O logic in an unmaintainable mess. With the buffer pool, the B-tree operates entirely on in-memory pointers, and the buffer pool transparently handles the memory-disk boundary. This separation of concerns mirrors the real SQLite architecture, where the `Pager` module (SQLite's name for the buffer pool) is the sole intermediary between B-tree operations and file I/O.
In the next milestone, you'll build the B-tree page format and table storage — defining how rows are serialized into 4096-byte pages, how pages link to form a tree, and how node splitting maintains balance. Every page the B-tree creates or accesses will flow through the buffer pool you just built.
---
<!-- END_MS -->


<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m5 -->
<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m5 -->
# Milestone 5: B-tree Page Format & Table Storage
## Where We Are in the System
You have a complete SQL frontend — tokenizer, parser, bytecode compiler, and virtual machine — plus a buffer pool that caches 4096-byte pages in memory and handles disk I/O transparently. But those pages are currently blank slates. Your VM's cursor calls (`btree_cursor_open`, `btree_cursor_next`, `btree_cursor_column`) still point at mock in-memory arrays. In this milestone, you replace the mock with the real thing: structured pages that store actual rows in a B-tree, managed through the buffer pool.

![SQLite Engine — Full System Architecture Map](./diagrams/diag-satellite-system-map.svg)

This is where your database becomes a *database*. Before this milestone, you had a SQL interpreter with in-memory tables. After it, you have persistent, crash-recoverable, page-based storage — data that survives process restarts because it lives on disk in a structured binary format.

![Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page](./diagrams/diag-data-flow-sql-to-disk.svg)

The B-tree page format defines three things: how individual rows are serialized into bytes (**record format**), how those bytes are arranged within a 4096-byte page (**page layout**), and how pages link together to form a searchable tree (**tree structure**). Every decision here has a direct performance consequence — a poorly designed page format wastes space, increases tree height, and multiplies disk I/O.
---
## The Revelation: B-trees and B+trees Are Not the Same Thing
You probably think you know what a B-tree is. A balanced search tree where each node has many children, stored on disk. Textbook material. Insert, search, delete in O(log n). You might even think "B+tree" is just another name for the same thing — or a minor variant that doesn't matter in practice.
This misconception will cost you real bugs if you carry it into implementation.

> **🔑 Foundation: B-tree structure and invariants**
> 
> ## B-Tree Structure and Invariants
A B-tree is a self-balancing tree data structure designed for systems that read and write large blocks of data — most notably, disk-based storage engines. Unlike a binary search tree (max 2 children per node), a B-tree node can hold **many keys** and have **many children**, which keeps the tree shallow and minimizes expensive disk I/O.
**Structure:**
A B-tree of order *m* has these properties:
- Each node holds between ⌈m/2⌉−1 and m−1 keys (the root can have fewer).
- Keys within a node are **sorted**.
- Each internal node with *k* keys has exactly *k+1* child pointers, where child *i* contains all keys between key *i−1* and key *i*.
- All **leaf nodes sit at the same depth** — the tree is perfectly balanced.
For example, in an order-4 B-tree, each node holds 1–3 keys and 2–4 children:
```
         [17 | 35]              ← root (2 keys, 3 children)
        /    |     \
  [3|8]   [20|29]   [40|52|60] ← leaves, all at depth 1
```
**The invariants** — the rules that must *never* be violated — are what make a B-tree self-balancing:
1. **Balance invariant:** Every root-to-leaf path has the same length.
2. **Occupancy invariant:** Every non-root node is at least half full.
3. **Order invariant:** Keys in each node are sorted, and child subtrees respect the key boundaries.
When an insertion overflows a node, you **split** it. When a deletion underflows a node, you **merge** or **redistribute**. These operations preserve all three invariants and keep the tree balanced without ever needing a global reorganization.
**Why you need this now:** You're building a database storage engine. The B-tree is the backbone of your on-disk index — every `INSERT`, `SELECT`, and `DELETE` ultimately translates to B-tree operations. If any invariant breaks, queries return wrong results or the tree degrades into an unbalanced mess with unpredictable performance.
**Key insight:** Think of the occupancy invariant as the B-tree's "immune system." It guarantees the tree stays shallow (height ≈ log_{m/2}(n)), which means even with millions of keys, you only touch 3–4 disk pages per lookup. Every split and merge exists solely to defend this property.


> **🔑 Foundation: B+tree vs B-tree distinction**
> 
> ## B+Tree vs B-Tree Distinction
Both are balanced, multi-way search trees — but they differ in **where data lives** and **how leaves connect**.
| Property | B-tree | B+tree |
|---|---|---|
| Data stored in | Internal nodes *and* leaves | **Leaves only** |
| Internal nodes contain | Keys + data + child pointers | Keys + child pointers (no data) |
| Leaf connectivity | No links between leaves | Leaves form a **linked list** |
| Duplicate keys in internals | No | Yes — internal keys are "signposts" copied up from leaves |
Concretely, in a B-tree, if you search for key 35 and find it in an internal node, you're done — the record is right there. In a B+tree, key 35 in an internal node is just a routing guide; you always descend to a leaf to get the actual data.
```
B+tree internal:    [17 | 35]        ← these are just separators
                   /    |    \
Leaves:       [3,8,17] → [20,29,35] → [40,52,60]
              ↑ data here   ↑ linked list connections
```
**Why you need this now:** Virtually every production database (SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB) uses **B+trees**, not plain B-trees. The reason is practical:
1. **Fatter internal nodes.** Without data payloads, internal nodes fit more keys per page, meaning higher fan-out, meaning a shallower tree and fewer disk reads.
2. **Efficient range scans.** Need all keys between 20 and 50? Find 20 in a leaf, then walk the linked list forward. No need to bounce up and down the tree. In a plain B-tree, range scans require an in-order traversal that zigzags between levels.
3. **Simpler leaf-level logic.** All records are in leaves, so your page format and serialization logic is uniform.
**Key insight:** Think of a B+tree as a B-tree that made a deliberate trade — it duplicates some keys into internal "signpost" nodes so that *all actual data lives at the leaf level*. This one architectural choice is what makes sequential scans fast and page layout uniform, which is exactly what a disk-based storage engine needs.


![B-tree vs B+tree: Side-by-Side Comparison](./diagrams/diag-btree-vs-bplustree.svg)

Here's the critical difference, and why it matters for your implementation:
**In a B-tree**, every node — leaf *and* internal — stores full data records alongside keys. When you search for rowid 42 and find it in an internal node, you're done. The row data is right there. SQLite uses B-trees for **table storage**, where the key is the rowid (an integer primary key) and the value is the entire row.
**In a B+tree**, only leaf nodes store data. Internal nodes contain *only* separator keys and child pointers — they exist purely for navigation. Data is never stored above the leaf level. SQLite uses B+trees for **secondary indexes**, where the leaf stores (indexed_column_value, rowid) pairs, and internal nodes store only the separator values that guide the search.
Why this distinction matters in concrete terms:
1. **Fanout.** An internal page in a B+tree has no row data — it's packed with keys and child pointers. With 4KB pages and 8-byte integer keys + 4-byte page pointers, you can fit roughly 340 entries per internal page. A B-tree internal page carrying full row data (say, 100 bytes per row) fits only ~35 entries. Higher fanout means a shallower tree: 340³ = 39 million rows in 3 levels for a B+tree, versus 35³ ≈ 43,000 rows in 3 levels for a B-tree with fat records. **Fanout determines tree height, and tree height determines disk I/O per query.**
2. **Range scans.** In a B+tree, leaf pages can be linked in a chain. To scan all rows WHERE `age BETWEEN 20 AND 30`, you find the first matching leaf via tree descent, then follow leaf-to-leaf pointers sequentially — no need to re-traverse the tree. In a B-tree, some matching records live in internal nodes and some in leaves, making range scans awkward (you'd need an in-order traversal touching every level).
3. **Double lookup.** When you find a matching entry in a B+tree index, you have the rowid but not the full row. To get the actual row data, you must look up that rowid in the table's B-tree — a second tree traversal. This **double lookup** (index B+tree → rowid → table B-tree → row) is the price you pay for B+tree's higher fanout and better range scans.
SQLite's choice — B-tree for tables, B+tree for indexes — is a deliberate engineering tradeoff. Tables are accessed by rowid (point lookups via the B-tree are fast because data is in every node), while indexes need efficient range scans (B+tree's linked leaves win) and high fanout (index keys are smaller than full rows, so B+tree's data-only-in-leaves approach wastes less internal page space).
---
## Page Layout: The Slotted Page
Every page in your database — leaf or internal, table or index — follows the same fundamental layout called a **slotted page**.

> **🔑 Foundation: Slotted page layout**
> 
> ## Slotted Page Layout
A slotted page is a way to organize variable-length records inside a fixed-size disk page (typically 4 KB or 8 KB). It solves a fundamental problem: how do you pack records of different sizes into a page while still being able to find, insert, and delete any record efficiently?
The layout has three regions:
```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Page Header  │ Slot Array →        ... free ...  │
│ (item count, │ [off1][off2][off3]                │
│  free space  │                                   │
│  pointers)   │                                   │
│              │                                   │
│              │         ← FREE SPACE →            │
│              │                                   │
│              │            [Record C][Record B][Record A] │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
               grows →                      ← grows
```
1. **Page header** (fixed, at the start): stores metadata like the number of slots and pointers to the free-space boundaries.
2. **Slot array** (grows forward from the header): each slot is a small fixed-size entry (typically an offset + length) pointing to where a record lives in the page.
3. **Record data** (grows backward from the end of the page): actual variable-length records are packed from the page's tail toward the middle.
Free space sits between the slot array and the records. When you insert, you add a slot entry at the end of the slot array and write the record at the beginning of the record area. When you delete, you can mark a slot as empty and optionally compact the records.
**Why you need this now:** Your B+tree leaf nodes *are* pages. Each leaf stores multiple key-value pairs that may vary in size. The slotted page layout lets you reference records by **slot number** (a stable logical index) rather than by raw byte offset. This means that when you compact or rearrange data within a page, external pointers (like "page 5, slot 3") remain valid — you just update the slot array.
**Key insight:** The slot array is an indirection layer. External code refers to "slot N," and only the slot array knows the actual byte offset. This indirection is what makes inserts, deletes, and compaction possible without invalidating references from other pages or from the tree's internal nodes.


![Page Binary Layout (Slotted Page)](./diagrams/diag-page-format-layout.svg)

The slotted page uses **bidirectional growth**: a fixed-size header sits at the top, a cell pointer array grows downward from the header, and cell content (the actual data) grows upward from the bottom of the page. Free space sits in the middle, and as you add cells, the two regions grow toward each other until they meet (page full).
Here's the page header:
```c
#define PAGE_SIZE 4096
#define PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF     0x0D  // 13 — SQLite's actual value
#define PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_INTERNAL 0x05  // 5
#define PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_LEAF     0x0A  // 10
#define PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_INTERNAL 0x02  // 2
typedef struct {
    uint8_t  page_type;         // one of the PAGE_TYPE_* constants
    uint16_t first_freeblock;   // offset to first free block (0 = none)
    uint16_t cell_count;        // number of cells on this page
    uint16_t cell_content_start;// offset to start of cell content area
    uint8_t  fragmented_bytes;  // total fragmented free bytes
    uint32_t right_child;       // right-most child page (internal pages only)
                                // 0 for leaf pages
} PageHeader;
// Size: 8 bytes for leaf pages, 12 bytes for internal pages (right_child adds 4)
#define LEAF_HEADER_SIZE    8
#define INTERNAL_HEADER_SIZE 12
```
After the header comes the **cell pointer array** — an array of 2-byte offsets, one per cell, pointing to where each cell's data lives within the page. The pointers are sorted by key order, but the cells themselves are **not** stored in sorted order in the content area. This separation is what makes the slotted page powerful:
- **Insertion** writes cell data into the next available slot at the bottom of the page and appends a 2-byte pointer at the end of the pointer array. No existing data needs to move.
- **Deletion** removes the pointer from the array (shifting subsequent pointers left by 2 bytes) and marks the cell's space as free. No data compaction needed — the freed space becomes a "fragment" or is added to a free block list.
- **Sorted access** iterates the pointer array in order. The pointers are sorted even though the cells aren't.
```c
// Read the cell pointer at index i
static uint16_t get_cell_pointer(uint8_t *page, int header_size, int i) {
    int offset = header_size + (i * 2);
    return (uint16_t)((page[offset] << 8) | page[offset + 1]);  // big-endian
}
// Write a cell pointer at index i
static void set_cell_pointer(uint8_t *page, int header_size, int i, 
                              uint16_t ptr) {
    int offset = header_size + (i * 2);
    page[offset]     = (uint8_t)(ptr >> 8);     // big-endian
    page[offset + 1] = (uint8_t)(ptr & 0xFF);
}
```
**Endianness note:** SQLite uses **big-endian** byte order for all multi-byte integers stored on disk. This is a portability choice — a database file created on a little-endian x86 machine can be read on a big-endian ARM machine without conversion. Your page header fields, cell pointers, and all serialized integers must be written big-endian. The conversion functions:
```c
static uint16_t read_be16(const uint8_t *p) {
    return (uint16_t)((p[0] << 8) | p[1]);
}
static uint32_t read_be32(const uint8_t *p) {
    return ((uint32_t)p[0] << 24) | ((uint32_t)p[1] << 16) | 
           ((uint32_t)p[2] << 8)  | p[3];
}
static void write_be16(uint8_t *p, uint16_t val) {
    p[0] = (uint8_t)(val >> 8);
    p[1] = (uint8_t)(val & 0xFF);
}
static void write_be32(uint8_t *p, uint32_t val) {
    p[0] = (uint8_t)(val >> 24);
    p[1] = (uint8_t)(val >> 16);
    p[2] = (uint8_t)(val >> 8);
    p[3] = (uint8_t)(val & 0xFF);
}
```
### Initializing a New Page
When the B-tree creates a new page (via `buffer_pool_new_page`), it must write the header and initialize the free space:
```c
void page_init(uint8_t *page, uint8_t page_type) {
    memset(page, 0, PAGE_SIZE);
    int header_size = (page_type == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_INTERNAL || 
                       page_type == PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_INTERNAL) 
                      ? INTERNAL_HEADER_SIZE : LEAF_HEADER_SIZE;
    page[0] = page_type;
    write_be16(page + 1, 0);              // first_freeblock = 0 (none)
    write_be16(page + 3, 0);              // cell_count = 0
    write_be16(page + 5, PAGE_SIZE);      // cell_content_start = end of page
    page[7] = 0;                          // fragmented_bytes = 0
    if (page_type == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_INTERNAL || 
        page_type == PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_INTERNAL) {
        write_be32(page + 8, 0);          // right_child = 0 (not set yet)
    }
}
```
The `cell_content_start` field initializes to `PAGE_SIZE` (4096) because no cells have been written yet — the cell content area is empty, meaning its "start" is at the very end of the page. As cells are added, this value decreases (the content area grows upward toward the pointer array).
---
## Variable-Length Integer Encoding (Varint)
Before you can serialize rows, you need a way to encode integers that uses fewer bytes for small values. A 64-bit integer always takes 8 bytes, but most database values — column counts, string lengths, type codes — are small numbers that fit in 1 or 2 bytes. Wasting 8 bytes on the number `3` is unacceptable when you're packing hundreds of rows into 4096-byte pages.

> **🔑 Foundation: Variable-length integer encoding**
> 
> ## Variable-Length Integer Encoding (Varint)
A varint encodes an integer using **only as many bytes as it needs**. Small values use 1 byte; larger values use 2, 3, or more. This contrasts with fixed-width encoding where every integer costs the same (e.g., 4 bytes for a `uint32`, even to store the value `7`).
The most common scheme (used by Protocol Buffers, SQLite, and LEB128): the **high bit of each byte** is a continuation flag. If it's `1`, more bytes follow. If it's `0`, this is the last byte. The remaining 7 bits per byte carry actual data.
```
Value: 300 (binary: 1 0010 1100)
Fixed uint16:  [0x01] [0x2C]           ← always 2 bytes
Fixed uint32:  [0x00] [0x00] [0x01] [0x2C]  ← always 4 bytes
Varint:        [0xAC] [0x02]           ← 2 bytes
               1_0101100  0_0000010
               ↑ continue  ↑ last byte
               payload: 0000010_0101100 = 300
```
For the value `7`:
```
Varint:        [0x07]                   ← just 1 byte
               0_0000111
               ↑ last byte
```
**Why you need this now:** In a storage engine, you're serializing keys, record lengths, offsets, and page metadata — thousands of integers per page. Most of these values are small (a string length of 12, a column count of 5). Using fixed 4-byte integers wastes space and means fewer records fit per page, which means more disk I/O. Varints let you pack data tightly, which directly improves the fan-out of your B+tree nodes and the capacity of your slotted pages.
**Key insight:** Varint is a space-time trade: you save bytes on disk (critical for I/O-bound systems) but pay a tiny CPU cost to decode. In a storage engine where the bottleneck is almost always disk, this trade is overwhelmingly worth it. Think of it as "compression for integers that's cheap enough to use everywhere."


![Variable-Length Integer (Varint) Encoding](./diagrams/diag-varint-encoding.svg)

SQLite's varint encoding uses 1 to 9 bytes, where the high bit of each byte indicates whether more bytes follow:
- If the high bit is **0**, this is the last byte. The remaining 7 bits are the value.
- If the high bit is **1**, more bytes follow. The remaining 7 bits contribute to the value.
- The 9th byte (if reached) uses all 8 bits — no continuation bit needed.
This gives you 7 bits per byte for the first 8 bytes, plus 8 bits for the 9th, totaling 7×8 + 8 = 64 bits — enough for a full `uint64_t`.
```c
// Encode a 64-bit value as a varint. Returns number of bytes written.
int varint_encode(uint64_t value, uint8_t *buf) {
    if (value <= 0x7F) {
        // 1 byte: 0xxxxxxx
        buf[0] = (uint8_t)value;
        return 1;
    }
    // Multi-byte: encode in big-endian order with continuation bits
    uint8_t temp[9];
    int n = 0;
    // Special handling for 9-byte case
    if (value > 0x00FFFFFFFFFFFFFF) {
        // Need 9 bytes
        temp[8] = (uint8_t)(value & 0xFF);
        value >>= 8;
        for (int i = 7; i >= 0; i--) {
            temp[i] = 0x80 | (uint8_t)(value & 0x7F);
            value >>= 7;
        }
        memcpy(buf, temp, 9);
        return 9;
    }
    // 1-8 byte encoding
    // Work backwards to determine byte count
    uint64_t v = value;
    n = 0;
    while (v > 0x7F) {
        temp[n++] = (uint8_t)(v & 0x7F);
        v >>= 7;
    }
    temp[n++] = (uint8_t)v;
    // Write in big-endian order with continuation bits
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (i < n - 1) {
            buf[i] = 0x80 | temp[n - 1 - i];  // set continuation bit
        } else {
            buf[i] = temp[n - 1 - i];           // last byte, no continuation
        }
    }
    return n;
}
// Decode a varint from buf. Returns number of bytes consumed.
int varint_decode(const uint8_t *buf, uint64_t *value) {
    uint64_t result = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
        result = (result << 7) | (buf[i] & 0x7F);
        if ((buf[i] & 0x80) == 0) {
            *value = result;
            return i + 1;
        }
    }
    // 9th byte: uses all 8 bits
    result = (result << 8) | buf[8];
    *value = result;
    return 9;
}
```
Let's verify the encoding sizes:
| Value Range | Bytes | Example |
|-------------|-------|---------|
| 0 – 127 | 1 | `42` → `0x2A` |
| 128 – 16,383 | 2 | `300` → `0x82 0x2C` |
| 16,384 – 2,097,151 | 3 | `100000` → `0x86 0x8D 0x20` |
| Up to 2⁶³ - 1 | 1–9 | Scales with magnitude |
This is the same encoding principle used in **Protocol Buffers** (Google's serialization format), **UTF-8** (Unicode character encoding), and **LEB128** (used in DWARF debug information and WebAssembly). The insight is universal: when most values are small, variable-length encoding saves substantial space at the cost of slightly more complex encode/decode logic. In a database page, where you might have 50 rows each with 5 columns, saving 4–6 bytes per integer value across the header fields reclaims hundreds of bytes — enough for several additional rows per page.
---
## Row Serialization: The Record Format

![Row Serialization Format](./diagrams/diag-record-serialization.svg)

Each row stored in a table B-tree leaf cell is serialized as a **record** — a self-describing binary format that encodes both the types and values of all columns. The format consists of a **record header** followed by **column values**:
```
[header_size (varint)] [type1 (varint)] [type2 (varint)] ... [value1] [value2] ...
```
The first varint is the total size of the header (including itself). The subsequent varints are **serial type codes** — each one describes the type *and* size of the corresponding column value:
| Serial Type | Meaning | Value Size (bytes) |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|
| 0 | NULL | 0 |
| 1 | 8-bit signed integer | 1 |
| 2 | 16-bit big-endian signed integer | 2 |
| 3 | 24-bit big-endian signed integer | 3 |
| 4 | 32-bit big-endian signed integer | 4 |
| 5 | 48-bit big-endian signed integer | 6 |
| 6 | 64-bit big-endian signed integer | 8 |
| 7 | IEEE 754 double (big-endian) | 8 |
| 8 | Integer constant 0 | 0 |
| 9 | Integer constant 1 | 0 |
| N ≥ 12, even | BLOB of size (N-12)/2 | (N-12)/2 |
| N ≥ 13, odd | TEXT of size (N-13)/2 | (N-13)/2 |
The serial type codes for integers are chosen so that the smallest encoding that fits the value is used. The number `42` fits in 1 byte (serial type 1), while `100000` needs 4 bytes (serial type 4). This is a second layer of variable-length encoding on top of the varint header — the header tells you the type and size, and the value region contains only the payload bytes.
For strings and blobs, the serial type code encodes the length directly. A 5-character string has serial type `5*2 + 13 = 23`. A 10-byte blob has serial type `10*2 + 12 = 32`. The decoder reads the serial type, computes the size, and knows exactly how many bytes to read from the value region.
```c
typedef enum {
    COL_NULL = 0,
    COL_INTEGER,
    COL_REAL,
    COL_TEXT,
    COL_BLOB
} ColumnType;
typedef struct {
    ColumnType type;
    union {
        int64_t  i;
        double   r;
        struct {
            uint8_t *data;
            int      length;
        } s;  // text or blob
    };
} ColumnValue;
// Determine the serial type code for a value
static uint64_t serial_type_for(ColumnValue *val) {
    switch (val->type) {
        case COL_NULL:    return 0;
        case COL_INTEGER: {
            int64_t v = val->i;
            if (v == 0) return 8;
            if (v == 1) return 9;
            if (v >= -128       && v <= 127)        return 1;
            if (v >= -32768     && v <= 32767)      return 2;
            if (v >= -8388608   && v <= 8388607)    return 3;
            if (v >= -2147483648LL && v <= 2147483647LL) return 4;
            if (v >= -(1LL<<47) && v <= (1LL<<47)-1) return 5;
            return 6;
        }
        case COL_REAL:    return 7;
        case COL_TEXT:    return (uint64_t)(val->s.length * 2 + 13);
        case COL_BLOB:    return (uint64_t)(val->s.length * 2 + 12);
    }
    return 0;
}
// How many bytes does a value with this serial type consume?
static int serial_type_size(uint64_t serial_type) {
    switch (serial_type) {
        case 0:  return 0;  // NULL
        case 1:  return 1;
        case 2:  return 2;
        case 3:  return 3;
        case 4:  return 4;
        case 5:  return 6;
        case 6:  return 8;
        case 7:  return 8;  // double
        case 8:  return 0;  // constant 0
        case 9:  return 0;  // constant 1
        default:
            if (serial_type >= 12 && (serial_type % 2 == 0))
                return (int)(serial_type - 12) / 2;  // blob
            if (serial_type >= 13 && (serial_type % 2 == 1))
                return (int)(serial_type - 13) / 2;  // text
            return 0;
    }
}
```
### Serializing a Row
```c
// Serialize a row into a byte buffer.
// Returns the total number of bytes written.
int record_serialize(ColumnValue *columns, int col_count, uint8_t *buf, 
                      int buf_size) {
    // Phase 1: compute serial types and header size
    uint64_t serial_types[128];
    int header_body_size = 0;  // size of serial type varints
    for (int i = 0; i < col_count; i++) {
        serial_types[i] = serial_type_for(&columns[i]);
        // How many bytes does this serial type's varint take?
        uint8_t tmp[9];
        header_body_size += varint_encode(serial_types[i], tmp);
    }
    // The header_size varint includes itself — we need to account for
    // the fact that header_size might be 1 or 2 bytes
    int header_size_varint_len = 1;  // usually 1 byte
    int total_header = header_size_varint_len + header_body_size;
    if (total_header > 127) {
        header_size_varint_len = 2;
        total_header = header_size_varint_len + header_body_size;
    }
    // Phase 2: write header
    int offset = 0;
    offset += varint_encode((uint64_t)total_header, buf + offset);
    for (int i = 0; i < col_count; i++) {
        offset += varint_encode(serial_types[i], buf + offset);
    }
    // Phase 3: write values
    for (int i = 0; i < col_count; i++) {
        int size = serial_type_size(serial_types[i]);
        if (size == 0) continue;  // NULL, constant 0, constant 1
        switch (columns[i].type) {
            case COL_INTEGER: {
                // Write big-endian integer in 'size' bytes
                int64_t v = columns[i].i;
                for (int b = size - 1; b >= 0; b--) {
                    buf[offset + b] = (uint8_t)(v & 0xFF);
                    v >>= 8;
                }
                offset += size;
                break;
            }
            case COL_REAL: {
                // Write IEEE 754 double in big-endian
                uint64_t bits;
                memcpy(&bits, &columns[i].r, 8);
                for (int b = 7; b >= 0; b--) {
                    buf[offset + (7 - b)] = (uint8_t)((bits >> (b * 8)) & 0xFF);
                }
                offset += 8;
                break;
            }
            case COL_TEXT:
            case COL_BLOB:
                memcpy(buf + offset, columns[i].s.data, columns[i].s.length);
                offset += columns[i].s.length;
                break;
            default:
                break;
        }
    }
    return offset;
}
```
### Deserializing a Row
```c
// Deserialize a record from buf into columns array.
// Returns number of bytes consumed.
int record_deserialize(const uint8_t *buf, int buf_len, 
                        ColumnValue *columns, int max_cols, int *col_count) {
    int offset = 0;
    // Read header size
    uint64_t header_size;
    offset += varint_decode(buf + offset, &header_size);
    // Read serial types from header
    int n_cols = 0;
    uint64_t serial_types[128];
    while (offset < (int)header_size && n_cols < max_cols) {
        offset += varint_decode(buf + offset, &serial_types[n_cols]);
        n_cols++;
    }
    *col_count = n_cols;
    // Read values
    for (int i = 0; i < n_cols; i++) {
        uint64_t st = serial_types[i];
        int size = serial_type_size(st);
        if (st == 0) {
            columns[i].type = COL_NULL;
        } else if (st >= 1 && st <= 6) {
            // Integer
            columns[i].type = COL_INTEGER;
            int64_t v = 0;
            // Sign-extend from the most significant bit
            if (size > 0 && (buf[offset] & 0x80)) {
                v = -1;  // all 1s for sign extension
            }
            for (int b = 0; b < size; b++) {
                v = (v << 8) | buf[offset + b];
            }
            columns[i].i = v;
            offset += size;
        } else if (st == 7) {
            // Double
            columns[i].type = COL_REAL;
            uint64_t bits = 0;
            for (int b = 0; b < 8; b++) {
                bits = (bits << 8) | buf[offset + b];
            }
            memcpy(&columns[i].r, &bits, 8);
            offset += 8;
        } else if (st == 8) {
            columns[i].type = COL_INTEGER;
            columns[i].i = 0;
        } else if (st == 9) {
            columns[i].type = COL_INTEGER;
            columns[i].i = 1;
        } else if (st >= 12 && (st % 2 == 0)) {
            // Blob
            columns[i].type = COL_BLOB;
            columns[i].s.length = size;
            columns[i].s.data = malloc(size);
            memcpy(columns[i].s.data, buf + offset, size);
            offset += size;
        } else if (st >= 13 && (st % 2 == 1)) {
            // Text
            columns[i].type = COL_TEXT;
            columns[i].s.length = size;
            columns[i].s.data = malloc(size + 1);
            memcpy(columns[i].s.data, buf + offset, size);
            columns[i].s.data[size] = '\0';
            offset += size;
        }
    }
    return offset;
}
```
The record format is **self-describing** — the header contains all the type and size information needed to decode the values. This means you can deserialize a record without knowing the table schema. This property is important for debugging (you can dump any page and decode records), and for schema evolution (SQLite can add columns to a table without rewriting existing records — new columns simply have fewer serial types in old records, and the engine treats missing columns as NULL).
---
## Cell Formats: Leaf vs Internal

![Leaf Page vs Internal Page Structure](./diagrams/diag-leaf-vs-internal-pages.svg)

With the page layout and record format defined, you can now define the **cell format** — the structure of individual entries within a page.
### Table B-tree Leaf Cell
A leaf cell in a table B-tree stores a full row:
```
[payload_size (varint)] [rowid (varint)] [record_data (payload_size bytes)]
```
```c
// Write a table leaf cell into a page at the given offset.
// Returns the number of bytes written.
int write_table_leaf_cell(uint8_t *page, int offset, int64_t rowid,
                           uint8_t *record, int record_size) {
    int pos = offset;
    pos += varint_encode((uint64_t)record_size, page + pos);
    pos += varint_encode((uint64_t)rowid, page + pos);
    memcpy(page + pos, record, record_size);
    pos += record_size;
    return pos - offset;  // total cell size
}
```
### Table B-tree Internal Cell
