An internal cell in a table B-tree stores a child page pointer and a separator key (rowid). No row data — just navigation information:
```
[left_child_page (4 bytes, big-endian)] [rowid (varint)]
```
The left child page pointer means: "all rows with rowid ≤ this key are in the subtree rooted at left_child_page." The right-most child is stored in the page header's `right_child` field, not in a cell.
```c
int write_table_internal_cell(uint8_t *page, int offset, 
                                uint32_t left_child, int64_t rowid) {
    int pos = offset;
    write_be32(page + pos, left_child);
    pos += 4;
    pos += varint_encode((uint64_t)rowid, page + pos);
    return pos - offset;
}
```
### Index B+tree Leaf Cell
An index leaf cell stores the indexed value(s) and the rowid of the corresponding table row. The payload is itself a record (using the same serialization format), containing the indexed column values followed by the rowid:
```
[payload_size (varint)] [record: (indexed_col_value, rowid)]
```
### Index B+tree Internal Cell
An index internal cell stores a child pointer and a separator key (also a record):
```
[left_child_page (4 bytes)] [payload_size (varint)] [separator_key_record]
```
---
## The B-tree Structure
With page layout, record format, and cell format defined, you can now build the tree itself. The B-tree is a collection of pages linked by child pointers:

![B-tree Traversal for Point Lookup](./diagrams/diag-btree-traversal.svg)

```c
typedef struct {
    BufferPool *pool;
    page_id_t   root_page;
    uint8_t     page_type;    // TABLE_LEAF, TABLE_INTERNAL, INDEX_LEAF, INDEX_INTERNAL
} BTree;
typedef struct {
    BTree     *tree;
    page_id_t  page_id;       // current page
    int        cell_index;    // current cell within page (-1 = before first)
    bool       is_eof;        // past the last cell
} BTreeCursor;
```
### Inserting a Cell into a Page
This is the core low-level operation. Given a page and a cell (already serialized), insert it at the correct sorted position:
```c
// Insert a cell into a leaf page at the correct sorted position.
// Returns 0 on success, -1 if page is full (needs splitting).
int page_insert_cell(uint8_t *page, uint8_t *cell_data, int cell_size, 
                      int64_t key) {
    uint8_t page_type = page[0];
    int header_size = (page_type == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_INTERNAL || 
                       page_type == PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_INTERNAL)
                      ? INTERNAL_HEADER_SIZE : LEAF_HEADER_SIZE;
    uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(page + 3);
    uint16_t content_start = read_be16(page + 5);
    // Check if there's enough space
    int pointer_space = (cell_count + 1) * 2;  // existing + new pointer
    int available = content_start - header_size - (cell_count * 2);
    if (cell_size + 2 > available) {
        return -1;  // page full — caller must split
    }
    // Find insertion position (binary search on cell pointers)
    int insert_pos = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < cell_count; i++) {
        uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i);
        int64_t existing_key = read_cell_key(page, cp, page_type);
        if (key < existing_key) {
            break;
        }
        insert_pos = i + 1;
    }
    // Write cell data at the bottom of the content area
    uint16_t new_content_start = content_start - cell_size;
    memcpy(page + new_content_start, cell_data, cell_size);
    // Shift cell pointers to make room for the new pointer
    for (int i = cell_count; i > insert_pos; i--) {
        uint16_t prev = get_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i - 1);
        set_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i, prev);
    }
    set_cell_pointer(page, header_size, insert_pos, new_content_start);
    // Update header
    write_be16(page + 3, cell_count + 1);           // cell_count++
    write_be16(page + 5, new_content_start);         // new content_start
    return 0;
}
```
The binary search through cell pointers to find the insertion position is O(log n) in the number of cells per page. With ~50-200 cells per page, this is trivially fast — a few hundred nanoseconds at most.
### Reading a Cell's Key
```c
// Read the key (rowid) from a table B-tree cell
static int64_t read_cell_key(uint8_t *page, uint16_t cell_offset, 
                               uint8_t page_type) {
    int pos = cell_offset;
    uint64_t val;
    if (page_type == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_INTERNAL) {
        // Internal cell: [left_child (4 bytes)] [rowid (varint)]
        pos += 4;  // skip child pointer
        varint_decode(page + pos, &val);
        return (int64_t)val;
    } else if (page_type == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF) {
        // Leaf cell: [payload_size (varint)] [rowid (varint)]
        pos += varint_decode(page + pos, &val);  // skip payload size
        varint_decode(page + pos, &val);
        return (int64_t)val;
    }
    return 0;
}
```
---
## Node Splitting: Maintaining Balance

![B-tree Node Split on Insert Overflow](./diagrams/diag-node-split.svg)

When a page is full and a new cell needs to be inserted, the page must **split** into two pages. This is the operation that keeps the B-tree balanced — every split creates a new page at the same level and promotes a separator key to the parent, potentially triggering a recursive split up the tree.
The algorithm for splitting a table B-tree leaf:
1. **Allocate a new page** via `buffer_pool_new_page`.
2. **Determine the split point** — the median cell. Cells with keys ≤ median stay in the original page; cells with keys > median move to the new page.
3. **Copy cells** from the original page to the new page. This involves reading each cell's data and writing it into the new page.
4. **Promote the median key** to the parent internal page. The parent now has a new cell with the median key and a pointer to the new page.
5. If the parent is also full, split it recursively.
6. If the root splits, create a **new root page** with two children (the old root and the new page). This is the only operation that increases tree height.
```c
// Split a full leaf page and insert a new cell.
// Returns the page_id of the new page, and the promoted key.
// parent_page is the parent internal page (or INVALID if splitting root).
int btree_split_leaf(BTree *tree, page_id_t page_id, 
                      uint8_t *cell_data, int cell_size, int64_t key,
                      int64_t *promoted_key, page_id_t *new_page_id) {
    uint8_t *page = fetch_page(tree->pool, page_id);
    uint8_t page_type = page[0];
    int header_size = LEAF_HEADER_SIZE;
    uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(page + 3);
    // Collect all existing cells + the new cell, sorted by key
    typedef struct { int64_t key; uint8_t *data; int size; } CellEntry;
    CellEntry *all_cells = malloc((cell_count + 1) * sizeof(CellEntry));
    int total = 0;
    bool inserted = false;
    for (int i = 0; i < cell_count; i++) {
        uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i);
        int64_t existing_key = read_cell_key(page, cp, page_type);
        if (!inserted && key < existing_key) {
            all_cells[total].key = key;
            all_cells[total].data = cell_data;
            all_cells[total].size = cell_size;
            total++;
            inserted = true;
        }
        // Copy existing cell data
        int existing_size = compute_cell_size(page, cp, page_type);
        all_cells[total].key = existing_key;
        all_cells[total].data = malloc(existing_size);
        memcpy(all_cells[total].data, page + cp, existing_size);
        all_cells[total].size = existing_size;
        total++;
    }
    if (!inserted) {
        all_cells[total].key = key;
        all_cells[total].data = cell_data;
        all_cells[total].size = cell_size;
        total++;
    }
    // Split point: middle cell
    int split = total / 2;
    *promoted_key = all_cells[split].key;
    // Reinitialize original page with left half
    page_init(page, page_type);
    for (int i = 0; i < split; i++) {
        page_insert_cell(page, all_cells[i].data, all_cells[i].size, 
                          all_cells[i].key);
    }
    mark_page_dirty(tree->pool, page_id);
    unpin_page(tree->pool, page_id, true);
    // Create new page with right half
    *new_page_id = buffer_pool_new_page(tree->pool);
    uint8_t *new_page = fetch_page(tree->pool, *new_page_id);
    page_init(new_page, page_type);
    for (int i = split; i < total; i++) {
        page_insert_cell(new_page, all_cells[i].data, all_cells[i].size, 
                          all_cells[i].key);
    }
    unpin_page(tree->pool, *new_page_id, true);
    // Cleanup
    for (int i = 0; i < total; i++) {
        if (all_cells[i].data != cell_data) {
            free(all_cells[i].data);
        }
    }
    free(all_cells);
    return 0;
}
```
### Promoting the Key to the Parent
After a split, the promoted key and new child page must be added to the parent. If the root splits, a new root must be created:
```c
void btree_insert_into_parent(BTree *tree, page_id_t left_page, 
                                int64_t key, page_id_t right_page,
                                page_id_t parent_page) {
    if (parent_page == INVALID_PAGE_ID) {
        // Splitting the root — create a new root
        page_id_t new_root_id = buffer_pool_new_page(tree->pool);
        uint8_t *new_root = fetch_page(tree->pool, new_root_id);
        page_init(new_root, PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_INTERNAL);
        // Write the key as an internal cell with left_child = left_page
        uint8_t cell_buf[32];
        int cell_size = write_table_internal_cell(cell_buf, 0, 
                                                    left_page, key);
        page_insert_cell(new_root, cell_buf, cell_size, key);
        // Set right_child to right_page
        write_be32(new_root + 8, right_page);
        unpin_page(tree->pool, new_root_id, true);
        tree->root_page = new_root_id;
        // Update system catalog with new root page (see below)
        return;
    }
    // Non-root parent: insert new internal cell
    uint8_t *parent = fetch_page(tree->pool, parent_page);
    uint8_t cell_buf[32];
    int cell_size = write_table_internal_cell(cell_buf, 0, left_page, key);
    int result = page_insert_cell(parent, cell_buf, cell_size, key);
    if (result < 0) {
        // Parent is also full — split recursively
        int64_t promoted;
        page_id_t new_parent_page;
        btree_split_internal(tree, parent_page, cell_buf, cell_size, key,
                              &promoted, &new_parent_page);
        // Recursively insert promoted key into grandparent
        // (requires tracking parent chain — see note below)
    }
    unpin_page(tree->pool, parent_page, true);
}
```
The recursive nature of splitting is structurally similar to a **two-phase commit** in distributed systems: the child page creates a new sibling (phase 1), and the parent acknowledges the new child by adding the separator key (phase 2). If the system crashes between phase 1 and phase 2, the new sibling page exists on disk but no parent points to it — it's an orphan. Production databases handle this through their WAL/journal (Milestones 9-10), which ensures the entire split completes atomically or not at all. For your initial build, this orphan scenario is a known limitation that the transaction layer will address later.
---
## B-tree Search: Finding a Row by Rowid
To find a row with a specific rowid, you traverse the tree from root to leaf:
```c
// Search for a row by rowid. Returns the page_id and cell_index where found,
// or the leaf page where it would be inserted.
bool btree_search(BTree *tree, int64_t rowid, 
                   page_id_t *found_page, int *found_cell) {
    page_id_t current = tree->root_page;
    while (true) {
        uint8_t *page = fetch_page(tree->pool, current);
        uint8_t page_type = page[0];
        uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(page + 3);
        if (page_type == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF) {
            // Search leaf for exact match
            for (int i = 0; i < cell_count; i++) {
                int header_size = LEAF_HEADER_SIZE;
                uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i);
                int64_t key = read_cell_key(page, cp, page_type);
                if (key == rowid) {
                    *found_page = current;
                    *found_cell = i;
                    unpin_page(tree->pool, current, false);
                    return true;  // found
                }
                if (key > rowid) break;  // not here
            }
            *found_page = current;
            *found_cell = -1;
            unpin_page(tree->pool, current, false);
            return false;  // not found
        }
        // Internal page: find the correct child
        page_id_t child = read_be32(page + 8);  // default: right_child
        for (int i = 0; i < cell_count; i++) {
            int header_size = INTERNAL_HEADER_SIZE;
            uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i);
            int64_t key = read_cell_key(page, cp, page_type);
            if (rowid <= key) {
                // Go to left child of this cell
                child = read_be32(page + cp);  // left_child from cell
                break;
            }
        }
        unpin_page(tree->pool, current, false);
        current = child;
    }
}
```
The search follows at most `h` pages, where `h` is the tree height. With a fanout of ~100 (typical for table B-trees with moderate row sizes), a 3-level tree covers 100³ = 1 million rows. Each level is one `fetch_page` call — most likely a buffer pool hit after the first query, costing ~10ns. A full search: ~30ns plus comparison overhead. This is why B-tree point lookups feel instant.
---
## The B-tree INSERT Operation
The full INSERT path combines search, serialization, cell insertion, and potential splitting:
```c
void btree_insert(BTree *tree, int64_t rowid, ColumnValue *columns, 
                   int col_count) {
    // Step 1: Serialize the row into a record
    uint8_t record_buf[4096];
    int record_size = record_serialize(columns, col_count, record_buf, 
                                        sizeof(record_buf));
    // Step 2: Build the complete leaf cell
    uint8_t cell_buf[4096 + 32];  // record + varint overheads
    int cell_size = write_table_leaf_cell(cell_buf, 0, rowid, 
                                           record_buf, record_size);
    // Step 3: Find the correct leaf page
    page_id_t leaf_page;
    int cell_idx;
    btree_search(tree, rowid, &leaf_page, &cell_idx);
    // Step 4: Try to insert into the leaf
    uint8_t *page = fetch_page(tree->pool, leaf_page);
    int result = page_insert_cell(page, cell_buf, cell_size, rowid);
    if (result == 0) {
        // Success — cell fits in the page
        unpin_page(tree->pool, leaf_page, true);
        return;
    }
    // Step 5: Page full — split and retry
    unpin_page(tree->pool, leaf_page, false);
    int64_t promoted_key;
    page_id_t new_page;
    btree_split_leaf(tree, leaf_page, cell_buf, cell_size, rowid,
                      &promoted_key, &new_page);
    // Step 6: Insert promoted key into parent
    // (need parent tracking — simplified here)
    btree_insert_into_parent(tree, leaf_page, promoted_key, new_page,
                              INVALID_PAGE_ID);  // TODO: track parent
}
```
> **Implementation note: tracking the parent chain.** The search function above discards parent pages as it descends. But when a split needs to promote a key to the parent, you need to know *which* parent. Two approaches: (1) maintain a stack of page IDs during descent, so you can pop the parent when needed; (2) store parent pointers in each page (wastes 4 bytes per page and adds maintenance overhead). Approach (1) is simpler and what SQLite uses — it keeps a small stack of page references in the cursor structure.
---
## Full Table Scan: The Cursor Pattern

![B-tree Cursor Iterator Pattern](./diagrams/diag-cursor-pattern.svg)

The VM's `OP_REWIND` / `OP_NEXT` / `OP_COLUMN` opcodes need a cursor that iterates all rows in rowid order. For a table B-tree, this means visiting all leaf pages left-to-right. The cursor performs an **in-order traversal** — but optimized: since we only want leaf data, we descend to the leftmost leaf and then scan across.
```c
BTreeCursor* btree_cursor_open(BTree *tree) {
    BTreeCursor *cur = calloc(1, sizeof(BTreeCursor));
    cur->tree = tree;
    cur->page_id = INVALID_PAGE_ID;
    cur->cell_index = -1;
    cur->is_eof = false;
    return cur;
}
bool btree_cursor_rewind(BTreeCursor *cur) {
    // Descend to the leftmost leaf page
    page_id_t current = cur->tree->root_page;
    while (true) {
        uint8_t *page = fetch_page(cur->tree->pool, current);
        uint8_t page_type = page[0];
        if (page_type == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF) {
            uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(page + 3);
            unpin_page(cur->tree->pool, current, false);
            if (cell_count == 0) {
                cur->is_eof = true;
                return true;  // table is empty
            }
            cur->page_id = current;
            cur->cell_index = 0;
            return false;  // not empty
        }
        // Internal page: go to leftmost child
        // The leftmost child is the left_child of the first cell
        uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, INTERNAL_HEADER_SIZE, 0);
        page_id_t left_child = read_be32(page + cp);
        unpin_page(cur->tree->pool, current, false);
        current = left_child;
    }
}
```
The `next` operation advances within the current leaf page. When it reaches the end, it needs to find the next leaf. In a B+tree, leaf pages are linked — you'd just follow a `next_leaf` pointer. In SQLite's table B-tree, leaf pages are *not* linked. Instead, the cursor must ascend to the parent and descend to the next subtree. This requires maintaining a stack of (page_id, cell_index) pairs representing the path from root to current leaf:
```c
#define MAX_TREE_DEPTH 20
typedef struct {
    BTree     *tree;
    page_id_t  page_id;
    int        cell_index;
    bool       is_eof;
    // Parent chain for traversal
    struct {
        page_id_t page_id;
        int       cell_index;
    } stack[MAX_TREE_DEPTH];
    int stack_depth;
} BTreeCursor;
bool btree_cursor_next(BTreeCursor *cur) {
    if (cur->is_eof) return false;
    uint8_t *page = fetch_page(cur->tree->pool, cur->page_id);
    uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(page + 3);
    unpin_page(cur->tree->pool, cur->page_id, false);
    cur->cell_index++;
    if (cur->cell_index < cell_count) {
        return true;  // still have cells on this page
    }
    // Current leaf exhausted — ascend to parent and find next subtree
    while (cur->stack_depth > 0) {
        cur->stack_depth--;
        page_id_t parent_id = cur->stack[cur->stack_depth].page_id;
        int parent_cell = cur->stack[cur->stack_depth].cell_index;
        uint8_t *parent = fetch_page(cur->tree->pool, parent_id);
        uint16_t parent_count = read_be16(parent + 3);
        parent_cell++;  // move to next child in parent
        if (parent_cell <= parent_count) {
            // There's a next child — descend to its leftmost leaf
            page_id_t next_child;
            if (parent_cell == parent_count) {
                // Use right_child
                next_child = read_be32(parent + 8);
            } else {
                uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(parent, INTERNAL_HEADER_SIZE, 
                                                parent_cell);
                next_child = read_be32(parent + cp);
            }
            // Push updated parent back on stack
            cur->stack[cur->stack_depth].page_id = parent_id;
            cur->stack[cur->stack_depth].cell_index = parent_cell;
            cur->stack_depth++;
            unpin_page(cur->tree->pool, parent_id, false);
            // Descend to leftmost leaf of next_child
            page_id_t current = next_child;
            while (true) {
                uint8_t *p = fetch_page(cur->tree->pool, current);
                uint8_t pt = p[0];
                if (pt == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF) {
                    cur->page_id = current;
                    cur->cell_index = 0;
                    uint16_t cc = read_be16(p + 3);
                    unpin_page(cur->tree->pool, current, false);
                    return cc > 0;
                }
                // Push and descend
                uint16_t fcp = get_cell_pointer(p, INTERNAL_HEADER_SIZE, 0);
                page_id_t lc = read_be32(p + fcp);
                cur->stack[cur->stack_depth].page_id = current;
                cur->stack[cur->stack_depth].cell_index = 0;
                cur->stack_depth++;
                unpin_page(cur->tree->pool, current, false);
                current = lc;
            }
        }
        unpin_page(cur->tree->pool, parent_id, false);
    }
    // Exhausted the entire tree
    cur->is_eof = true;
    return false;
}
```
Reading a column from the current cursor position:
```c
void btree_cursor_column(BTreeCursor *cur, int col_idx, Value *dest) {
    uint8_t *page = fetch_page(cur->tree->pool, cur->page_id);
    uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, LEAF_HEADER_SIZE, cur->cell_index);
    // Parse cell: [payload_size (varint)] [rowid (varint)] [record]
    int pos = cp;
    uint64_t payload_size, rowid;
    pos += varint_decode(page + pos, &payload_size);
    pos += varint_decode(page + pos, &rowid);
    // Deserialize the specific column from the record
    ColumnValue columns[128];
    int col_count;
    record_deserialize(page + pos, (int)payload_size, columns, 128, &col_count);
    if (col_idx < col_count) {
        // Convert ColumnValue to VM's Value type
        switch (columns[col_idx].type) {
            case COL_NULL:
                dest->type = VAL_NULL;
                break;
            case COL_INTEGER:
                dest->type = VAL_INTEGER;
                dest->i = columns[col_idx].i;
                break;
            case COL_REAL:
                dest->type = VAL_REAL;
                dest->r = columns[col_idx].r;
                break;
            case COL_TEXT:
                dest->type = VAL_STRING;
                dest->s.data = columns[col_idx].s.data;  // transfer ownership
                dest->s.length = columns[col_idx].s.length;
                break;
            default:
                dest->type = VAL_NULL;
                break;
        }
    } else {
        dest->type = VAL_NULL;  // missing column → NULL
    }
    unpin_page(cur->tree->pool, cur->page_id, false);
}
```

![SELECT Execution End-to-End Trace](./diagrams/diag-select-execution-trace.svg)

---
## The System Catalog: Schema as Data

![System Catalog (sqlite_master) Structure](./diagrams/diag-system-catalog.svg)

Where does the database store the mapping from table name to root page number? In a **system catalog** — a special table that describes all other tables. SQLite calls this `sqlite_master`. It's stored as a regular table B-tree rooted at **page 1** (the first page of the database file, always).
The schema for `sqlite_master` is:
```sql
CREATE TABLE sqlite_master (
    type TEXT,        -- 'table' or 'index'
    name TEXT,        -- name of the table or index
    tbl_name TEXT,    -- table this object belongs to
    rootpage INTEGER, -- root page number of the B-tree
    sql TEXT          -- the CREATE statement that created it
);
```
When you execute `CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT)`, your engine:
1. Allocates a new B-tree root page via `buffer_pool_new_page`
2. Initializes it as a `PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF` (empty leaf — it's both root and leaf until data causes a split)
3. Inserts a row into `sqlite_master` with `type='table'`, `name='users'`, `rootpage=<new_page>`, `sql='CREATE TABLE ...'`
```c
typedef struct {
    char     name[64];
    int      root_page;
    int      column_count;
    char     column_names[32][64];
    char     column_types[32][16];
    bool     is_primary_key[32];
    bool     is_not_null[32];
} TableSchema;
typedef struct {
    BTree       *master_tree;    // B-tree for sqlite_master (root = page 1)
    TableSchema  tables[64];
    int          table_count;
} SystemCatalog;
int catalog_create_table(SystemCatalog *catalog, BufferPool *pool,
                          const char *table_name, AstNode *create_node) {
    // Allocate a new root page for this table
    page_id_t root_page = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    uint8_t *page = fetch_page(pool, root_page);
    page_init(page, PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF);
    unpin_page(pool, root_page, true);
    // Register in in-memory schema cache
    TableSchema *schema = &catalog->tables[catalog->table_count];
    strncpy(schema->name, table_name, 63);
    schema->root_page = root_page;
    schema->column_count = create_node->data.create_table.column_count;
    for (int i = 0; i < schema->column_count; i++) {
        AstNode *col = create_node->data.create_table.columns[i];
        strncpy(schema->column_names[i], col->data.column_def.name, 63);
        strncpy(schema->column_types[i], col->data.column_def.type_name, 15);
        schema->is_primary_key[i] = col->data.column_def.is_primary_key;
        schema->is_not_null[i] = col->data.column_def.is_not_null;
    }
    catalog->table_count++;
    // Persist to sqlite_master B-tree
    ColumnValue master_row[5];
    master_row[0].type = COL_TEXT;
    master_row[0].s.data = (uint8_t*)"table";
    master_row[0].s.length = 5;
    master_row[1].type = COL_TEXT;
    master_row[1].s.data = (uint8_t*)table_name;
    master_row[1].s.length = strlen(table_name);
    master_row[2].type = COL_TEXT;
    master_row[2].s.data = (uint8_t*)table_name;
    master_row[2].s.length = strlen(table_name);
    master_row[3].type = COL_INTEGER;
    master_row[3].i = root_page;
    master_row[4].type = COL_TEXT;
    // Store the original CREATE TABLE SQL
    master_row[4].s.data = (uint8_t*)"CREATE TABLE ...";  // reconstruct from AST
    master_row[4].s.length = 16;
    int64_t master_rowid = catalog->table_count;  // simple auto-increment
    btree_insert(catalog->master_tree, master_rowid, master_row, 5);
    return root_page;
}
```
On database open, the engine reads all rows from `sqlite_master` (page 1) to rebuild the in-memory schema cache. This is the **bootstrap** — the database describes itself using its own storage format. The schema is stored in a table, tables are B-trees, and the schema table's B-tree root is always page 1. There's no external configuration file, no separate schema store — just the data.
This self-describing pattern appears throughout computing: Python objects store their attributes in `__dict__`, Java's reflection API lets classes inspect their own structure, and the file system's superblock describes the file system's own layout. It's the principle of **reflexivity** — a system that can describe itself using its own mechanisms.
---
## Connecting to the VM: Replacing the Mock Cursor
With the real B-tree implemented, you can now replace the mock cursor from Milestone 3. The VM's opcode handlers call through the cursor interface:
```c
// OP_OPEN_READ handler (updated)
case OP_OPEN_READ: {
    int cursor_id = op->p1;
    int root_page = op->p2;
    BTree *btree = btree_open(db->pool, root_page);
    vm->cursors[cursor_id] = btree_cursor_open(btree);
    break;
}
// OP_REWIND handler (updated)
case OP_REWIND: {
    int cursor_id = op->p1;
    bool empty = btree_cursor_rewind(vm->cursors[cursor_id]);
    if (empty) {
        vm->pc = op->p2;
        continue;
    }
    break;
}
```
The rest of the VM code is unchanged — `OP_COLUMN`, `OP_NEXT`, `OP_INSERT` all call through the same cursor interface. The buffer pool transparently handles caching, eviction, and disk I/O. The B-tree handles page navigation, cell parsing, and node splitting. Each layer does its job; no layer reaches into another's internals.

![INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE Write Path](./diagrams/diag-dml-write-path.svg)

---
## Testing
### Test: Page Format Round-Trip
```c
void test_page_format(void) {
    uint8_t page[PAGE_SIZE];
    page_init(page, PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF);
    assert(page[0] == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF);
    assert(read_be16(page + 3) == 0);    // cell_count = 0
    assert(read_be16(page + 5) == PAGE_SIZE);  // content starts at end
    // Insert a cell
    ColumnValue cols[2] = {
        { .type = COL_INTEGER, .i = 42 },
        { .type = COL_TEXT, .s = { .data = (uint8_t*)"hello", .length = 5 } }
    };
    uint8_t record[256];
    int rec_size = record_serialize(cols, 2, record, 256);
    uint8_t cell[256];
    int cell_size = write_table_leaf_cell(cell, 0, /*rowid=*/1, record, rec_size);
    int result = page_insert_cell(page, cell, cell_size, 1);
    assert(result == 0);
    assert(read_be16(page + 3) == 1);  // cell_count = 1
    // Read it back
    uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, LEAF_HEADER_SIZE, 0);
    int64_t key = read_cell_key(page, cp, PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF);
    assert(key == 1);
    printf("PASS: page format round-trip\n");
}
```
### Test: Varint Round-Trip
```c
void test_varint(void) {
    uint64_t test_values[] = {0, 1, 127, 128, 255, 16383, 16384, 
                               1000000, 0x7FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFULL};
    int n = sizeof(test_values) / sizeof(test_values[0]);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        uint8_t buf[9];
        int written = varint_encode(test_values[i], buf);
        assert(written >= 1 && written <= 9);
        uint64_t decoded;
        int consumed = varint_decode(buf, &decoded);
        assert(consumed == written);
        assert(decoded == test_values[i]);
    }
    printf("PASS: varint round-trip (%d values)\n", n);
}
```
### Test: Record Serialization Round-Trip
```c
void test_record_round_trip(void) {
    ColumnValue cols[4] = {
        { .type = COL_INTEGER, .i = 42 },
        { .type = COL_TEXT, .s = { .data = (uint8_t*)"hello", .length = 5 } },
        { .type = COL_NULL },
        { .type = COL_REAL, .r = 3.14 }
    };
    uint8_t buf[512];
    int size = record_serialize(cols, 4, buf, 512);
    assert(size > 0);
    ColumnValue decoded[4];
    int col_count;
    record_deserialize(buf, size, decoded, 4, &col_count);
    assert(col_count == 4);
    assert(decoded[0].type == COL_INTEGER && decoded[0].i == 42);
    assert(decoded[1].type == COL_TEXT && decoded[1].s.length == 5);
    assert(memcmp(decoded[1].s.data, "hello", 5) == 0);
    assert(decoded[2].type == COL_NULL);
    assert(decoded[3].type == COL_REAL && fabs(decoded[3].r - 3.14) < 0.001);
    printf("PASS: record serialization round-trip\n");
}
```
### Test: B-tree Insert and Scan
```c
void test_btree_insert_scan(void) {
    BufferPool *pool = buffer_pool_create("test_btree.db", 100, 4096);
    // Create a table via catalog
    page_id_t root = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    uint8_t *rp = fetch_page(pool, root);
    page_init(rp, PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF);
    unpin_page(pool, root, true);
    BTree tree = { .pool = pool, .root_page = root };
    // Insert 100 rows
    for (int i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
        ColumnValue cols[2] = {
            { .type = COL_INTEGER, .i = i },
            { .type = COL_TEXT, .s = { .data = (uint8_t*)"test", .length = 4 } }
        };
        btree_insert(&tree, i, cols, 2);
    }
    // Full scan should return all 100 rows in order
    BTreeCursor *cur = btree_cursor_open(&tree);
    bool empty = btree_cursor_rewind(cur);
    assert(!empty);
    int count = 0;
    do {
        count++;
        Value v;
        btree_cursor_column(cur, 0, &v);
        assert(v.type == VAL_INTEGER);
        assert(v.i == count);
    } while (btree_cursor_next(cur));
    assert(count == 100);
    btree_cursor_close(cur);
    buffer_pool_destroy(pool);
    unlink("test_btree.db");
    printf("PASS: B-tree insert and scan (100 rows)\n");
}
```
### Test: Node Splitting
```c
void test_node_splitting(void) {
    BufferPool *pool = buffer_pool_create("test_split.db", 100, 4096);
    page_id_t root = buffer_pool_new_page(pool);
    uint8_t *rp = fetch_page(pool, root);
    page_init(rp, PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF);
    unpin_page(pool, root, true);
    BTree tree = { .pool = pool, .root_page = root };
    // Insert enough rows to force multiple splits
    // With ~50-byte rows, a 4096-byte page fits ~70 rows
    // Inserting 500 should cause several splits
    for (int i = 1; i <= 500; i++) {
        ColumnValue cols[3] = {
            { .type = COL_INTEGER, .i = i },
            { .type = COL_TEXT, .s = { .data = (uint8_t*)"name_value", .length = 10 } },
            { .type = COL_INTEGER, .i = i * 100 }
        };
        btree_insert(&tree, i, cols, 3);
    }
    // Verify: root should now be internal (tree grew in height)
    uint8_t *root_page = fetch_page(pool, tree.root_page);
    assert(root_page[0] == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_INTERNAL);
    unpin_page(pool, tree.root_page, false);
    // Verify: full scan returns all 500 rows in order
    BTreeCursor *cur = btree_cursor_open(&tree);
    btree_cursor_rewind(cur);
    int count = 0;
    int64_t last_key = -1;
    do {
        count++;
        Value v;
        btree_cursor_column(cur, 0, &v);
        assert(v.i > last_key);  // strictly increasing
        last_key = v.i;
    } while (btree_cursor_next(cur));
    assert(count == 500);
    btree_cursor_close(cur);
    buffer_pool_destroy(pool);
    unlink("test_split.db");
    printf("PASS: node splitting (500 rows, multi-level tree)\n");
}
```
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens When You INSERT a Row
**Level 1 — Public API**: The VM executes `OP_INSERT`. It passes a rowid and a serialized record to the B-tree cursor's insert function. One function call — "store this row."
**Level 2 — B-tree Engine**: The B-tree searches from root to leaf, descending through internal pages by comparing the rowid against separator keys. At the correct leaf, it serializes the row into a cell (varint-encoded payload size, varint-encoded rowid, record bytes), checks if the cell fits (is there enough free space between the cell pointer array and the content area?), and writes it. If the page is full, it splits: allocates a new page, redistributes cells, promotes the median key, and potentially splits parent nodes recursively.
**Level 3 — Buffer Pool / Disk**: Every page access — root, internal, leaf — goes through `fetch_page`, which checks the page table hash map for a hit (O(1), ~10ns). On a miss, the buffer pool runs LRU eviction (potentially writing a dirty page with `pwrite` + `fsync`), then loads the requested page with `pread`. The `pread` triggers a kernel system call; the kernel checks its own page cache, and if missing, issues a block I/O request to the storage device (~100μs on SSD, ~10ms on HDD). On the write side, `mark_page_dirty` sets a flag; the actual disk write happens later during eviction or `flush_all`. For an INSERT into a warm cache, the entire operation — search + serialize + insert — completes in microseconds, touching only memory. The disk write is deferred.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What B-tree Storage Unlocks
**→ Fanout and Information Theory**: A B+tree with 4KB pages and 8-byte keys fits ~500 keys per internal node. That's a branching factor of 500. Three levels: 500³ = 125 million rows. Four levels: 62.5 billion. This is why databases can handle billions of rows with only 3-4 disk reads per lookup — each page read eliminates 99.8% of the remaining search space. This is the same principle behind binary search (each comparison eliminates 50% of candidates), but cranked to a branching factor of 500 instead of 2. It's the Shannon information gain per I/O operation: each page read delivers log₂(500) ≈ 9 bits of information about where the target row lives.
**→ Slotted Pages as a Universal Pattern**: The bidirectional-growth layout — pointers growing down, data growing up, free space in the middle — appears far beyond databases. PostgreSQL's heap pages use the same structure. The classic `malloc` implementation grows the stack downward and the heap upward within a process's address space — same pattern, different scale. Some file systems (like ext4's extent trees) use slotted internal nodes. Even network packet headers follow a similar principle: fixed-size header fields at known offsets, variable-length payload afterward. Once you recognize the slotted page pattern, you see it everywhere data needs both random access (via fixed-size pointers) and efficient packing (via variable-length content).
**→ Variable-Length Encoding Everywhere**: The varint encoding you just built is used in Protocol Buffers (Google's serialization format), UTF-8 (the dominant character encoding on the internet — each Unicode code point uses 1-4 bytes), LEB128 in DWARF debug information and WebAssembly, and SQLite's own file format. The principle — use fewer bytes for common small values, more bytes for rare large values — is a direct application of Huffman's insight: encode frequent symbols with shorter codes. Every network protocol, file format, and serialization library makes this tradeoff between fixed-width simplicity and variable-width compactness.
**→ Node Splitting as Distributed Coordination**: When a B-tree node splits, the parent must acknowledge the new child before the operation is complete. If the system crashes after creating the new page but before updating the parent, you have an orphan page — allocated but unreachable. This is structurally identical to the **two-phase commit** problem in distributed systems: a coordinator (parent) must commit to knowing about a new participant (child page) atomically. Your WAL (Milestone 10) will solve this the same way distributed databases do — by logging the intent before performing the action, ensuring crash recovery can complete or roll back partial operations.
**→ Self-Describing Schemas (Reflexivity)**: Storing the schema in `sqlite_master` — a regular table using the same B-tree format as user tables — means the database can describe itself. This reflexive pattern appears throughout computing: Python's `type(type)` is `type`, JavaScript's `Object.getPrototypeOf(Object.prototype)` is `null` (the bottom of the chain), and Unix's `/proc` filesystem describes the kernel's own state as files. Self-description enables introspection, debugging, and tooling — `SELECT * FROM sqlite_master` is your database's equivalent of `ls` or `dir`.
---
## System Awareness
You've built the B-tree page format and table storage — the persistent storage engine that sits between the buffer pool (downstream, Milestone 4) and the VM's cursor interface (upstream, Milestone 3). The VM emits cursor opcodes; the B-tree translates them into page navigations through the buffer pool. The buffer pool handles memory caching and disk I/O. The chain is complete: SQL text → tokens → AST → bytecode → VM → cursor → B-tree → buffer pool → disk.
Without the B-tree storage engine, your database was an in-memory SQL interpreter — data vanished when the process exited. With it, data persists on disk in a structured binary format, organized for efficient point lookups (O(log n) tree descent) and sequential scans (leaf-to-leaf traversal). The system catalog means table schemas are themselves persistent — the database is fully self-contained.
In the next milestone, you'll build the query execution layer — SELECT with projection and filtering, INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE through the B-tree, and constraint enforcement. The B-tree cursor you built here is the interface that execution layer will use for every data access.
---
<!-- END_MS -->


<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m6 -->
<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m6 -->
# Milestone 6: SELECT Execution & DML
## Where We Are in the System
You have every layer of the stack built except the one that makes it useful. The tokenizer converts SQL text to tokens. The parser converts tokens to an AST. The bytecode compiler converts the AST to opcodes. The VM executes opcodes by calling through the cursor interface. The buffer pool caches 4096-byte pages. The B-tree stores rows in those pages. But right now, the VM's cursor calls are wired to real B-tree storage — and the only operations you've tested are raw insert-and-scan at the storage layer.

![SQLite Engine — Full System Architecture Map](./diagrams/diag-satellite-system-map.svg)

In this milestone, you connect the full pipeline end-to-end: a user types `SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 21`, and your engine tokenizes, parses, compiles, and executes it against real B-tree pages, deserializing records, projecting columns, evaluating WHERE predicates with correct NULL semantics, and streaming results back. You'll also implement INSERT (through the SQL interface, not just the raw B-tree API), UPDATE, DELETE, and constraint enforcement.

![Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page](./diagrams/diag-data-flow-sql-to-disk.svg)

This is where the database stops being a collection of components and becomes a *system*. Every layer participates in every query.
---
## The Revelation: NULL Is Not What You Think
You've been writing C for this entire project. In C, a null pointer (`NULL`) means "points to nothing." An uninitialized variable has an undefined value but is still *some* value. You probably think SQL's `NULL` works similarly — it's a placeholder for "no value," a kind of empty slot that you can compare and filter like any other value.
This intuition will silently corrupt every query result in your database.
Try this mental experiment. You have a table of employees. Alice's phone number is `NULL` — she hasn't provided one. Bob's phone number is also `NULL`. Is Alice's phone number equal to Bob's phone number?
Your instinct says "yes — they're both NULL, so they're equal." SQL says **no**. `NULL = NULL` evaluates to `NULL`, not `TRUE`. The reasoning: `NULL` means "unknown." Alice's phone number is unknown. Bob's phone number is unknown. Are two unknown values equal? *You don't know.* The answer is itself unknown — `NULL`.
This isn't a quirk. It's a fundamental design choice rooted in formal logic.

> **🔑 Foundation: Three-valued logic**
> 
> ## Three-Valued Logic (TRUE / FALSE / NULL)
Standard boolean logic has two values: true and false. SQL — and by extension, any query engine you're building — uses **three**: `TRUE`, `FALSE`, and `NULL` (unknown).
`NULL` doesn't mean "empty string" or "zero." It means **"I don't know."** This distinction changes everything about how logical operators behave:
| A | B | A AND B | A OR B | NOT A |
|---|---|---------|--------|-------|
| TRUE | NULL | NULL | TRUE | FALSE |
| FALSE | NULL | FALSE | NULL | TRUE |
| NULL | NULL | NULL | NULL | NULL |
Notice the asymmetry: `FALSE AND NULL` is `FALSE` (because no matter what the unknown value is, the result is false), but `TRUE AND NULL` is `NULL` (because the result depends on the unknown value).
**Why you need this now:** Your query engine evaluates WHERE clauses against rows. If a column value is `NULL` and the predicate is `salary > 50000`, the result isn't `FALSE` — it's `NULL`. A row only passes a filter when the predicate evaluates to `TRUE`. Both `FALSE` and `NULL` reject the row. If you implement this as simple boolean logic, you'll get subtly wrong query results that are extremely hard to debug.
**Key insight:** Treat `NULL` as a **third value that propagates**, not as a special case of false. Every comparison operator, every logical combinator in your evaluator needs an explicit code path for `NULL`. The cleanest implementation is a `TriValue` enum (or equivalent) rather than using nullable booleans, because nullable booleans tempt you into conflating "the answer is false" with "the answer is unknown."


![Three-Valued Logic Truth Tables](./diagrams/diag-three-valued-logic.svg)

The consequences ripple through every operation in your database:
**WHERE filtering:** `WHERE phone = NULL` matches *zero* rows — because `phone = NULL` evaluates to `NULL` for every row, and `NULL` is falsy. You must use `WHERE phone IS NULL`, which is a special operator that checks the type tag, not the value.
**Boolean operators:** `NULL AND TRUE` is `NULL`. `NULL OR TRUE` is `TRUE`. `NULL AND FALSE` is `FALSE`. The rules are consistent — AND returns FALSE if either side is FALSE (regardless of NULL), OR returns TRUE if either side is TRUE (regardless of NULL) — but they're not what you'd expect if you treated NULL as FALSE.
**Aggregates:** `COUNT(*)` counts all rows including NULLs. `COUNT(column)` skips NULLs. `SUM` of an all-NULL column returns `NULL`, not `0`. `AVG` ignores NULLs in both numerator and denominator.
If you implement NULL as "just another value" and use `==` for comparison, your WHERE clauses will silently include or exclude wrong rows. No crash, no error message — just wrong answers. This is the most dangerous class of bug: a correctness bug that looks correct.
---
## Implementing Three-Valued Logic
Your VM's `Value` type already has `VAL_NULL`. The work is in making every comparison and boolean operation respect it. Start with the comparison function from Milestone 3 — it already returns `false` for NULL comparisons, which is correct for the conditional-jump pattern your compiler uses. But now you need explicit three-valued evaluation for compound expressions.
Define a tri-state result type:
```c
typedef enum {
    TRI_FALSE = 0,
    TRI_TRUE  = 1,
    TRI_NULL  = 2,
} TriBool;
```
The truth tables for AND and OR:
```c
// AND truth table: TRI_FALSE wins over TRI_NULL
static TriBool tri_and(TriBool a, TriBool b) {
    if (a == TRI_FALSE || b == TRI_FALSE) return TRI_FALSE;
    if (a == TRI_NULL  || b == TRI_NULL)  return TRI_NULL;
    return TRI_TRUE;
}
// OR truth table: TRI_TRUE wins over TRI_NULL
static TriBool tri_or(TriBool a, TriBool b) {
    if (a == TRI_TRUE || b == TRI_TRUE) return TRI_TRUE;
    if (a == TRI_NULL || b == TRI_NULL) return TRI_NULL;
    return TRI_FALSE;
}
// NOT truth table: NOT NULL = NULL
static TriBool tri_not(TriBool a) {
    if (a == TRI_NULL) return TRI_NULL;
    return (a == TRI_TRUE) ? TRI_FALSE : TRI_TRUE;
}
```
For comparisons, any operand being NULL yields NULL:
```c
static TriBool tri_compare(Value *a, Value *b, Opcode op) {
    if (a->type == VAL_NULL || b->type == VAL_NULL) {
        return TRI_NULL;
    }
    // Existing comparison logic from Milestone 3
    int cmp;
    if (a->type == VAL_INTEGER && b->type == VAL_INTEGER) {
        cmp = (a->i > b->i) - (a->i < b->i);
    } else if (a->type == VAL_REAL || b->type == VAL_REAL) {
        double da = (a->type == VAL_REAL) ? a->r : (double)a->i;
        double db = (b->type == VAL_REAL) ? b->r : (double)b->i;
        cmp = (da > db) - (da < db);
    } else if (a->type == VAL_STRING && b->type == VAL_STRING) {
        cmp = strcmp(a->s.data, b->s.data);
    } else {
        return TRI_NULL;  // incomparable types
    }
    bool result;
    switch (op) {
        case OP_EQ: result = (cmp == 0); break;
        case OP_NE: result = (cmp != 0); break;
        case OP_LT: result = (cmp < 0);  break;
        case OP_LE: result = (cmp <= 0); break;
        case OP_GT: result = (cmp > 0);  break;
        case OP_GE: result = (cmp >= 0); break;
        default:    return TRI_NULL;
    }
    return result ? TRI_TRUE : TRI_FALSE;
}
```
Now update the VM's comparison opcode handlers. The existing pattern — "jump if condition is true" — naturally handles three-valued logic because NULL (like FALSE) doesn't trigger the jump:
```c
case OP_EQ: case OP_NE: case OP_LT:
case OP_LE: case OP_GT: case OP_GE: {
    Value *left = &vm->registers[op->p1];
    Value *right = &vm->registers[op->p3];
    TriBool result = tri_compare(left, right, op->opcode);
    // Jump only on TRUE — NULL and FALSE both fall through
    if (result == TRI_TRUE) {
        vm->pc = op->p2;
        continue;
    }
    break;
}
```
For the WHERE clause compilation (from Milestone 3), the compiler emits *inverted* comparisons that jump on condition-false to skip rows. With three-valued logic, this still works correctly: if `age > 21` evaluates to NULL (because `age` is NULL), the inverted `OP_LE` check asks "is age ≤ 21?" — that's also NULL. NULL doesn't trigger the jump, so the row falls through to `OP_RESULT_ROW`. Wait — that's wrong. A NULL comparison should *exclude* the row, not include it.
This is the subtlety. When the compiler compiles `WHERE age > 21`, it emits `OP_LE` (the inverse) that jumps to `OP_NEXT` if age ≤ 21. For NULLs, neither `>` nor `≤` is true — both return NULL. So the OP_LE doesn't jump (NULL isn't true), and the row proceeds to ResultRow. But a NULL comparison means "unknown" — the row should be *excluded* from results.
The fix: add a NULL check before the comparison:
```c
// In compile_where, for each comparison:
// 1. Check if either operand is NULL → skip row
// 2. Then do the actual comparison
static int compile_comparison(Compiler *c, AstNode *expr, int cursor,
                               int table_idx) {
    int left_reg = compile_expr_to_register(c, expr->data.binary.left,
                                              cursor, table_idx);
    int right_reg = compile_expr_to_register(c, expr->data.binary.right,
                                               cursor, table_idx);
    // Emit OP_ISNULL checks: if either operand is NULL, skip this row
    int null_skip1 = emit(c, OP_ISNULL, left_reg, 0, 0, "skip if left NULL");
    int null_skip2 = emit(c, OP_ISNULL, right_reg, 0, 0, "skip if right NULL");
    // Emit inverted comparison
    Opcode inv_op = invert_comparison(expr->data.binary.op);
    int cmp_skip = emit(c, inv_op, left_reg, 0, right_reg, "WHERE check");
    // All three skips need to jump to the same target (patched by caller to OP_NEXT)
    return cmp_skip;  // caller patches; null_skip1/2 also need patching
}
```
You'll need a new opcode for the NULL check:
```c
OP_ISNULL,    // If reg[p1] is NULL, jump to p2
```
And in the VM:
```c
case OP_ISNULL: {
    if (vm->registers[op->p1].type == VAL_NULL) {
        vm->pc = op->p2;
        continue;
    }
    break;
}
```
This is the `IS NULL` operator expressed as bytecode. The SQL `WHERE x IS NULL` compiles to `OP_ISNULL` that jumps to ResultRow (include the row). The NULL-safety check in comparisons uses `OP_ISNULL` that jumps to Next (skip the row). Same opcode, different jump targets.
---
## Column Projection: Extracting What You Need

![Column Projection from Serialized Record](./diagrams/diag-projection-deserialization.svg)

When the user writes `SELECT name, age FROM users`, they want only two columns out of a row that might have ten. The VM must deserialize the full record from the B-tree page, extract the requested columns, and discard the rest. This is **projection** — selecting a subset of columns.
The key insight: you don't actually need to deserialize *all* columns. The record format (from Milestone 5) is self-describing — the header contains serial type codes for every column, and each serial type tells you the exact byte size of that column's value. To read column 3 without reading columns 0-2, you parse the header to compute the byte offset of column 3, then jump directly to that offset.
```c
// Read a single column from a serialized record without deserializing all columns.
// Returns 0 on success, -1 if col_idx is out of range.
int record_read_column(const uint8_t *record, int record_len,
                        int col_idx, ColumnValue *dest) {
    int offset = 0;
    // Read header size
    uint64_t header_size;
    offset += varint_decode(record + offset, &header_size);
    // Parse serial types, tracking the byte offset of each column's value
    int value_offset = (int)header_size;  // values start after the header
    int col = 0;
    int header_end = (int)header_size;
    while (offset < header_end) {
        uint64_t serial_type;
        offset += varint_decode(record + offset, &serial_type);
        int size = serial_type_size(serial_type);
        if (col == col_idx) {
            // This is the column we want — decode it
            decode_value(record + value_offset, serial_type, dest);
            return 0;
        }
        value_offset += size;
        col++;
    }
    // Column index out of range — return NULL (schema evolution: new column added)
    dest->type = COL_NULL;
    return -1;
}
```
This optimization matters. If a table has 20 columns and the query selects 2, you avoid allocating and filling 18 `ColumnValue` structs per row. For a 10,000-row scan, that's 180,000 avoided deserializations. The header parsing is still O(n) in total columns (you must skip past each serial type varint to reach the target), but the value deserialization — which involves memory allocation for strings and blobs — is only done for requested columns.
The `OP_COLUMN` opcode in the VM calls this:
```c
case OP_COLUMN: {
    int cursor_id = op->p1;
    int col_idx = op->p2;
    int dest_reg = op->p3;
    // Get the raw record data from the B-tree cursor's current position
    uint8_t *page = fetch_page(vm->db->pool,
                                vm->cursors[cursor_id]->page_id);
    int header_size = LEAF_HEADER_SIZE;
    uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, header_size,
                                    vm->cursors[cursor_id]->cell_index);
    // Parse cell: skip payload_size varint, skip rowid varint
    int pos = cp;
    uint64_t payload_size, rowid;
    pos += varint_decode(page + pos, &payload_size);
    pos += varint_decode(page + pos, &rowid);
    // Read the specific column
    ColumnValue col_val;
    record_read_column(page + pos, (int)payload_size, col_idx, &col_val);
    // Convert to VM Value type
    column_value_to_vm_value(&col_val, &vm->registers[dest_reg]);
    unpin_page(vm->db->pool, vm->cursors[cursor_id]->page_id, false);
    break;
}
```
The conversion function maps between the storage layer's `ColumnValue` and the VM's `Value`:
```c
static void column_value_to_vm_value(ColumnValue *src, Value *dest) {
    switch (src->type) {
        case COL_NULL:
            dest->type = VAL_NULL;
            break;
        case COL_INTEGER:
            dest->type = VAL_INTEGER;
            dest->i = src->i;
            break;
        case COL_REAL:
            dest->type = VAL_REAL;
            dest->r = src->r;
            break;
        case COL_TEXT:
            dest->type = VAL_STRING;
            dest->s.data = (char*)src->s.data;  // transfer ownership
            dest->s.length = src->s.length;
            break;
        case COL_BLOB:
            dest->type = VAL_BLOB;
            dest->s.data = (char*)src->s.data;
            dest->s.length = src->s.length;
            break;
    }
}
```
---
## Full SELECT Execution: End-to-End
Let's trace the complete execution of `SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age > 21 AND name IS NOT NULL`:

![SELECT Execution End-to-End Trace](./diagrams/diag-select-execution-trace.svg)

**Step 1: Compile.** The compiler looks up `users` in the system catalog, finds root page 5, and emits:
```
addr  opcode       p1   p2   p3   comment
----  -----------  ---  ---  ---  -------
0     Init         0    1    0    Start
1     OpenRead     0    5    4    users (4 columns)
2     Rewind       0    13   0    Jump to Close if empty
3     Column       0    1    2    age → r2
4     Integer      21   3    0    21 → r3
5     IsNull       2    11   0    skip if age is NULL
6     Le           2    11   3    skip if age <= 21
7     Column       0    0    1    name → r1
8     IsNull       1    11   0    skip if name IS NULL
9     ResultRow    1    2    0    output r1, r2
10    Goto         0    11   0    (redundant, but clear)
11    Next         0    3    0    advance cursor, loop
12    Goto         0    13   0    fall through
13    Close        0    0    0
14    Halt         0    0    0
```
Notice the NULL-safety: `OP_ISNULL` at addr 5 catches rows where `age` is NULL before the comparison at addr 6. The `name IS NOT NULL` condition compiles to `OP_ISNULL` at addr 8 that skips the row if name is NULL — which is exactly the semantics of `IS NOT NULL` inverted (jump-to-skip when IS NULL).
**Step 2: Execute.** The VM opens cursor 0 on root page 5, rewinds to the first leaf. For each row:
1. `OP_COLUMN` deserializes column 1 (age) into register 2.
2. `OP_INTEGER` loads 21 into register 3.
3. `OP_ISNULL` checks r2 — if age is NULL, jump to Next.
4. `OP_LE` compares r2 ≤ r3 — if true, jump to Next (inverted condition).
5. `OP_COLUMN` deserializes column 0 (name) into register 1.
6. `OP_ISNULL` checks r1 — if name is NULL, jump to Next.
7. `OP_RESULT_ROW` outputs r1 and r2 through the callback.
8. `OP_NEXT` advances the cursor and loops back.
The lazy column loading is worth noting: name (column 0) is only loaded if age passes the filter. This is **predicate pushdown at the column level** — don't read data you won't output. The compiler can optimize this because it knows the WHERE clause references `age` but ResultRow references both `name` and `age`. Columns only used in filtering are loaded before the filter; columns only needed for output are loaded after.

> **🔑 Foundation: Predicate pushdown**
> 
> ## Predicate Pushdown
Predicate pushdown is an optimization where you **move filter conditions as close to the data source as possible** in your query execution plan, so you discard irrelevant rows before doing expensive work like joins or sorts.
Consider this query:
```sql
SELECT orders.id, customers.name
FROM orders JOIN customers ON orders.cust_id = customers.id
WHERE customers.country = 'Germany'
```
A naive execution plan might: (1) join every order with every customer, producing a huge intermediate result, then (2) filter for Germany. With predicate pushdown, the optimizer recognizes that `customers.country = 'Germany'` only touches the `customers` table, so it **pushes** that filter down below the join. Now you scan only German customers, join far fewer rows, and the query runs dramatically faster.
The "pushdown" metaphor comes from query plan trees — the data source (table scan) is at the bottom, and results flow upward. Pushing a predicate *down* means evaluating it *earlier*.
**Why you need this now:** Without pushdown, your query engine will produce correct results but will do orders-of-magnitude more work than necessary. This is often the single highest-impact optimization in a query engine, and it's relatively straightforward to implement compared to join reordering or cost-based optimization.
**Key insight:** A predicate can be pushed down past an operator if the operator **doesn't change the columns the predicate references**. A filter on `customers.country` can be pushed below a join (it only needs the `customers` table), but a filter on `total_revenue` computed by a `GROUP BY` cannot be pushed below the aggregation — that's the difference between `WHERE` and `HAVING`. When implementing this, walk your plan tree bottom-up and ask at each node: "Does this predicate depend only on columns available below this operator?" If yes, push it down.

---
## The Cursor Pattern for Full Table Scan

![B-tree Cursor Iterator Pattern](./diagrams/diag-cursor-pattern.svg)

Every SELECT starts with a full table scan (until you build indexes in Milestone 7 and a query planner in Milestone 8). The scan uses the B-tree cursor from Milestone 5:
```c
// The VM's scan loop, abstracted:
btree_cursor_rewind(cursor);       // OP_REWIND: go to first leaf
while (!cursor->is_eof) {
    // load columns, evaluate WHERE, maybe output
    btree_cursor_next(cursor);     // OP_NEXT: advance to next row
}
btree_cursor_close(cursor);        // OP_CLOSE: release resources
```
This open/rewind/next/close pattern is the **Iterator pattern** applied to database storage. It appears everywhere:
- Java's `Iterator<T>`: `hasNext()` / `next()`
- Python's `__iter__` / `__next__`
- Rust's `Iterator` trait: `next() -> Option<T>`
- JDBC `ResultSet`: `next()` / `getString(col)`
In every case, the consumer asks "give me the next item" without knowing how items are stored. The B-tree cursor hides the complexity of page-level navigation, parent-chain maintenance, and cross-page traversal behind a simple "advance" operation. This abstraction is what lets your VM handle both table scans and (later) index scans with the same opcode structure.
---
## INSERT Through the SQL Interface
In Milestone 5, you implemented `btree_insert` — the raw storage-layer operation. Now you need the full path: `INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ('Alice', 30)` compiled to bytecode and executed by the VM.

![INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE Write Path](./diagrams/diag-dml-write-path.svg)

The compiler (from Milestone 3) already emits `OP_OPEN_WRITE`, `OP_MAKE_RECORD`, and `OP_INSERT`. But several details need refinement:
### Auto-Increment Rowid
When the user doesn't specify a rowid (which is the common case — `INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ('Alice', 30)` has no explicit rowid), the engine must generate one. SQLite uses **max(rowid) + 1** as the default auto-increment strategy:
```c
// New opcode: OP_NEW_ROWID — generate a new unique rowid for cursor p1,
// store in register p2
case OP_NEW_ROWID: {
    int cursor_id = op->p1;
    int dest_reg = op->p2;
    // Find the maximum rowid in this table's B-tree
    int64_t max_rowid = btree_max_rowid(vm->cursors[cursor_id]->tree);
    vm->registers[dest_reg].type = VAL_INTEGER;
    vm->registers[dest_reg].i = max_rowid + 1;
    break;
}
```
The `btree_max_rowid` function descends to the rightmost leaf of the table B-tree (follow `right_child` pointers at each internal node) and reads the last cell's rowid. This is O(h) where h is the tree height — typically 3-4 levels.
However, there's a special case: if the user provides `NULL` for an `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` column, SQLite treats that as an auto-increment request. The INSERT compilation must detect this:
```c
// During INSERT compilation, if the value for the PK column is NULL literal:
if (is_integer_primary_key && value_is_null_literal) {
    emit(c, OP_NEW_ROWID, cursor, rowid_reg, 0, "auto-increment rowid");
} else {
    compile_expr_to_register(c, pk_expr, -1, table_idx);
}
```
### Constraint Enforcement
Before writing the record, the VM must check constraints. NOT NULL is the most common:
```c
// New opcode: OP_NOT_NULL — if reg[p1] is NULL, halt with error
case OP_NOT_NULL: {
    if (vm->registers[op->p1].type == VAL_NULL) {
        snprintf(vm->error_msg, sizeof(vm->error_msg),
                 "NOT NULL constraint failed: %s",
                 vm->program->strings[op->p3]);  // column name
        vm->halted = true;
        vm->halt_code = 19;  // SQLITE_CONSTRAINT
        return 19;
    }
    break;
}
```
The compiler emits `OP_NOT_NULL` for each column with a NOT NULL constraint, after loading the value into a register but before `OP_MAKE_RECORD`:
```c
// In compile_insert:
for (int col = 0; col < schema->column_count; col++) {
    int val_reg = first_reg + col;
    if (schema->is_not_null[col]) {
        int name_idx = add_string_constant(c, schema->column_names[col]);
        emit(c, OP_NOT_NULL, val_reg, 0, name_idx,
             "NOT NULL check");
    }
}
```
### Table Existence Check
The compiler must verify the target table exists before emitting any opcodes. This happens during compilation, not execution:
```c
static void compile_insert(Compiler *c, AstNode *node) {
    int table_idx = find_table(c, node->data.insert.table_name);
    if (table_idx < 0) {
        // Emit an immediate error halt
        int name_idx = add_string_constant(c, node->data.insert.table_name);
        emit(c, OP_HALT, 1, name_idx, 0,
             "error: no such table");
        snprintf(c->error_msg, sizeof(c->error_msg),
                 "no such table: %s", node->data.insert.table_name);
        c->had_error = true;
        return;
    }
    // ... proceed with compilation
}
```
The error message must include the table name — "no such table: users" is useful; "no such table" is not.
### Complete INSERT Bytecode
For `INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ('Alice', 30)` where users has columns (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, age INTEGER):
```
addr  opcode       p1   p2   p3   comment
----  -----------  ---  ---  ---  -------
0     Init         0    1    0    Start
1     OpenWrite    0    5    3    users (root=5, 3 cols)
2     NewRowid     0    1    0    auto-increment → r1
3     String8      0    2    0    'Alice' → r2
4     NotNull      2    0    0    NOT NULL check: name
5     Integer      30   3    0    30 → r3
6     MakeRecord   1    3    4    r1..r3 → record in r4
7     Insert       0    4    1    insert record r4 with key r1
8     Close        0    0    0
9     Halt         0    0    0
```
After execution, the B-tree for `users` contains a new leaf cell with the serialized record. A subsequent `SELECT * FROM users` will find it via the cursor's leaf-page traversal.
---
## UPDATE: Modify-in-Place vs Delete-and-Reinsert
UPDATE is the most complex DML operation because it must find matching rows, modify specific columns, and write the changes back — all while maintaining B-tree integrity.
The two implementation strategies:
| Strategy | Pros | Cons | Used By |
|----------|------|------|---------|
| **Delete + Reinsert ✓** | Simple, reuses existing insert/delete code, handles size changes naturally | Two B-tree operations per row, more I/O | SQLite (for most cases) |
| In-place modify | One B-tree operation, potentially less I/O | Complex when new record is larger/smaller than old, fragmentation | PostgreSQL HOT updates (same-page only) |
SQLite uses delete-and-reinsert because the record size can change (updating a TEXT column from 'hi' to 'hello world' makes the record larger, potentially overflowing the current cell slot). In-place modification only works if the new record fits in the same space — a constraint that's painful to enforce. Delete-and-reinsert handles this naturally.
### UPDATE Compilation
`UPDATE users SET age = 31 WHERE name = 'Alice'` compiles to:
```
addr  opcode       p1   p2   p3   comment
----  -----------  ---  ---  ---  -------
0     Init         0    1    0    Start
1     OpenWrite    0    5    3    users
2     Rewind       0    14   0    Jump if empty
3     Column       0    1    2    name → r2
4     String8      0    3    0    'Alice' → r3
5     IsNull       2    12   0    skip if name is NULL
6     Ne           2    12   3    skip if name != 'Alice'
7     Rowid        0    1    0    current rowid → r1
8     Column       0    1    4    name → r4 (keep unchanged)
9     Integer      31   5    0    new age → r5
10    NotNull      4    0    1    NOT NULL: name
11    MakeRecord   1    3    6    r1,r4,r5 → record r6
12    Delete       0    0    0    delete current row
13    Insert       0    6    1    insert updated row
14    Next         0    3    0    advance cursor
15    Close        0    0    0
16    Halt         0    0    0
```
Wait — there's a critical problem with this approach. The DELETE at addr 12 modifies the B-tree *while the cursor at addr 14 is iterating over it*. This is the **delete-during-iteration problem**, and it's a universal bug source.
### The Delete-During-Iteration Problem
If you delete the cell the cursor is currently pointing at, then call `OP_NEXT`, the cursor's state is invalid. The cell pointer array has shifted — cell index 5 now points to what was cell 6. `OP_NEXT` increments to cell 6, which is what was cell 7. You've skipped a row.
Worse: if the deletion causes a B-tree node merge (underflow), the entire page structure changes. The cursor's `page_id` might now point to a page that's been freed or repurposed.
This is the same problem that causes `ConcurrentModificationException` in Java's `ArrayList` iterator, segfaults from iterator invalidation in C++ STL containers, and subtle bugs in JavaScript's `for` loop when you `splice` an array during iteration.
The solution: a **two-pass approach**.
**Pass 1 (scan):** Iterate the B-tree, evaluate the WHERE clause, and collect the rowids of all matching rows into an array. Don't modify anything.
**Pass 2 (modify):** Iterate the collected rowids and perform the delete+reinsert for each one. The cursor from pass 1 is closed; modifications don't affect any active iterator.
```c
// UPDATE compilation: two-pass approach
static void compile_update(Compiler *c, AstNode *node) {
    int table_idx = find_table(c, node->data.update.table_name);
    if (table_idx < 0) {
        compiler_error(c, "no such table: %s", node->data.update.table_name);
        return;
    }
    int root_page = c->tables[table_idx].root_page;
    int n_cols = c->tables[table_idx].column_count;
    // --- Pass 1: Collect matching rowids ---
    int read_cursor = alloc_cursor(c);
    int ephemeral_cursor = alloc_cursor(c);  // temporary storage for rowids
    int init_addr = emit(c, OP_INIT, 0, 0, 0, "Start");
    // Open ephemeral table to store matching rowids
    int open_eph = emit(c, OP_OPEN_EPHEMERAL, ephemeral_cursor, 0, 0,
                         "temp rowid storage");
    patch_jump(c, init_addr, open_eph);
    emit(c, OP_OPEN_READ, read_cursor, root_page, n_cols,
         "scan for WHERE match");
    int rewind_addr = emit(c, OP_REWIND, read_cursor, 0, 0, "rewind");
    // --- Pass 1 loop body: check WHERE, save rowid if match ---
    int loop1_start = c->program->op_count;
    int skip_addr = -1;
    if (node->data.update.where_clause) {
        skip_addr = compile_where(c, node->data.update.where_clause,
                                    read_cursor, table_idx);
    }
    // Save matching rowid to ephemeral table
    int rowid_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_ROWID, read_cursor, rowid_reg, 0, "get rowid");
    int record_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_MAKE_RECORD, rowid_reg, 1, record_reg, "wrap rowid");
    emit(c, OP_INSERT, ephemeral_cursor, record_reg, rowid_reg, "save rowid");
    int next1_addr = emit(c, OP_NEXT, read_cursor, loop1_start, 0, "loop pass 1");
    if (skip_addr >= 0) patch_jump(c, skip_addr, next1_addr);
    emit(c, OP_CLOSE, read_cursor, 0, 0, "close read cursor");
    // --- Pass 2: For each saved rowid, update the row ---
    int write_cursor = alloc_cursor(c);
    emit(c, OP_OPEN_WRITE, write_cursor, root_page, n_cols, "open for write");
    int rewind2 = emit(c, OP_REWIND, ephemeral_cursor, 0, 0, "rewind ephem");
    int loop2_start = c->program->op_count;
    // Read saved rowid
    int saved_rowid_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_COLUMN, ephemeral_cursor, 0, saved_rowid_reg, "read saved rowid");
    // Seek to this rowid in the main table
    emit(c, OP_SEEK_ROWID, write_cursor, saved_rowid_reg, 0, "seek to row");
    // Load all current column values
    int col_regs_start = c->next_register;
    for (int i = 0; i < n_cols; i++) {
        int reg = alloc_register(c);
        emit(c, OP_COLUMN, write_cursor, i, reg, c->tables[table_idx].column_names[i]);
    }
    // Overwrite with SET values
    for (int i = 0; i < node->data.update.set_count; i++) {
        int col_idx = find_column(c, table_idx,
                                    node->data.update.set_columns[i]);
        int val_reg = col_regs_start + col_idx;
        compile_expr_to_register_at(c, node->data.update.set_values[i],
                                      write_cursor, table_idx, val_reg);
    }
    // Constraint checks
    for (int i = 0; i < n_cols; i++) {
        if (c->tables[table_idx].is_not_null[i]) {
            int name_idx = add_string_constant(c, c->tables[table_idx].column_names[i]);
            emit(c, OP_NOT_NULL, col_regs_start + i, 0, name_idx, "NOT NULL");
        }
    }
    // Delete old row, insert new row
    emit(c, OP_DELETE, write_cursor, 0, 0, "delete old");
    int new_record_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_MAKE_RECORD, col_regs_start, n_cols, new_record_reg, "new record");
    emit(c, OP_INSERT, write_cursor, new_record_reg, saved_rowid_reg, "insert updated");
    int next2_addr = emit(c, OP_NEXT, ephemeral_cursor, loop2_start, 0, "loop pass 2");
    int close2 = emit(c, OP_CLOSE, write_cursor, 0, 0, "close write");
    patch_jump(c, rewind2, close2);
    emit(c, OP_CLOSE, ephemeral_cursor, 0, 0, "close ephemeral");
    int halt = emit(c, OP_HALT, 0, 0, 0, "done");
    patch_jump(c, rewind_addr, halt);
    c->program->n_registers = c->next_register;
}
```
You need two new opcodes for this:
```c
OP_SEEK_ROWID,    // Position cursor p1 to the row with rowid in reg[p2]
OP_DELETE,        // Delete the row at cursor p1's current position
```
And their VM implementations:
```c
case OP_SEEK_ROWID: {
    int cursor_id = op->p1;
    int64_t rowid = vm->registers[op->p2].i;
    btree_cursor_seek(vm->cursors[cursor_id], rowid);
    break;
}
case OP_DELETE: {
    int cursor_id = op->p1;
    btree_cursor_delete(vm->cursors[cursor_id]);
    break;
}
```
The `btree_cursor_seek` function uses the B-tree search from Milestone 5 to position the cursor at a specific rowid. The `btree_cursor_delete` function removes the cell at the cursor's current position — which requires removing the cell pointer, marking the cell's space as free, and potentially merging underfull nodes.
### UPDATE Cannot Change the Rowid
If the SET clause includes the rowid (primary key) column, you must either reject the update or handle it as a delete-at-old-rowid + insert-at-new-rowid. The simplest correct approach is rejection:
```c
// During UPDATE compilation:
for (int i = 0; i < node->data.update.set_count; i++) {
    int col_idx = find_column(c, table_idx,
                                node->data.update.set_columns[i]);
    if (c->tables[table_idx].is_primary_key[col_idx]) {
        compiler_error(c, "cannot UPDATE primary key column '%s'; "
                       "use DELETE + INSERT instead",
                       node->data.update.set_columns[i]);
        return;
    }
}
```
The reason: changing a rowid means the row's position in the B-tree changes (the B-tree is ordered by rowid). A simple in-place update at the old position would leave the row at the wrong sort position, corrupting the B-tree ordering invariant. Delete + insert at the new position is required, but this also changes the row's identity — any index entries pointing to the old rowid become dangling. Production SQLite handles this carefully; for your build, rejecting rowid updates is the safe choice.
---
## DELETE: Two-Pass with Tombstones
DELETE follows the same two-pass pattern as UPDATE:
```c
static void compile_delete(Compiler *c, AstNode *node) {
    int table_idx = find_table(c, node->data.delete.table_name);
    if (table_idx < 0) {
        compiler_error(c, "no such table: %s", node->data.delete.table_name);
        return;
    }
    int root_page = c->tables[table_idx].root_page;
    int n_cols = c->tables[table_idx].column_count;
    int read_cursor = alloc_cursor(c);
    int ephemeral_cursor = alloc_cursor(c);
    int init_addr = emit(c, OP_INIT, 0, 0, 0, "Start");
    int open_eph = emit(c, OP_OPEN_EPHEMERAL, ephemeral_cursor, 0, 0, "temp");
    patch_jump(c, init_addr, open_eph);
    emit(c, OP_OPEN_READ, read_cursor, root_page, n_cols, "scan");
    int rewind_addr = emit(c, OP_REWIND, read_cursor, 0, 0, "rewind");
    // Pass 1: collect matching rowids
    int loop1_start = c->program->op_count;
    int skip_addr = -1;
    if (node->data.delete.where_clause) {
        skip_addr = compile_where(c, node->data.delete.where_clause,
                                    read_cursor, table_idx);
    }
    int rowid_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_ROWID, read_cursor, rowid_reg, 0, "get rowid");
    int rec_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_MAKE_RECORD, rowid_reg, 1, rec_reg, "wrap");
    emit(c, OP_INSERT, ephemeral_cursor, rec_reg, rowid_reg, "save");
    int next1 = emit(c, OP_NEXT, read_cursor, loop1_start, 0, "loop1");
    if (skip_addr >= 0) patch_jump(c, skip_addr, next1);
    emit(c, OP_CLOSE, read_cursor, 0, 0, "close read");
    // Pass 2: delete each saved rowid
    int write_cursor = alloc_cursor(c);
    emit(c, OP_OPEN_WRITE, write_cursor, root_page, n_cols, "write");
    int rewind2 = emit(c, OP_REWIND, ephemeral_cursor, 0, 0, "rewind ephem");
    int loop2_start = c->program->op_count;
    int saved_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_COLUMN, ephemeral_cursor, 0, saved_reg, "saved rowid");
    emit(c, OP_SEEK_ROWID, write_cursor, saved_reg, 0, "seek");
    emit(c, OP_DELETE, write_cursor, 0, 0, "delete");
    int next2 = emit(c, OP_NEXT, ephemeral_cursor, loop2_start, 0, "loop2");
    int close_write = emit(c, OP_CLOSE, write_cursor, 0, 0, "close");
    patch_jump(c, rewind2, close_write);
    emit(c, OP_CLOSE, ephemeral_cursor, 0, 0, "close ephem");
    int halt = emit(c, OP_HALT, 0, 0, 0, "done");
    patch_jump(c, rewind_addr, halt);
    c->program->n_registers = c->next_register;
}
```
### B-tree Deletion Implementation
At the storage layer, deleting a cell from a leaf page involves:
1. Remove the cell pointer from the pointer array (shift subsequent pointers left by 2 bytes).
2. Mark the freed cell space. The simplest approach: add the freed bytes to the `fragmented_bytes` counter in the page header. When fragmented bytes exceed a threshold, compact the page by rewriting all cells contiguously.
3. If the page becomes less than half full, consider merging with a sibling (to maintain the B-tree occupancy invariant).
For your initial build, skip the merge step. Mark deleted cells as free space, and accept that deleted rows leave "holes" in pages. This is what SQLite does — it defers space reclamation to `VACUUM`, which rewrites the entire database file. The B-tree remains valid because the remaining cells still satisfy the ordering invariant; the page is just less full than optimal.
```c
void btree_cursor_delete(BTreeCursor *cur) {
    uint8_t *page = fetch_page(cur->tree->pool, cur->page_id);
    int header_size = LEAF_HEADER_SIZE;
    uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(page + 3);
    // Get the cell being deleted
    uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, header_size, cur->cell_index);
    int cell_size = compute_cell_size(page, cp, page[0]);
    // Add to fragmented free bytes
    uint8_t frag = page[7];
    frag += cell_size;
    page[7] = frag;
    // Remove cell pointer by shifting array
    for (int i = cur->cell_index; i < cell_count - 1; i++) {
        uint16_t next_cp = get_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i + 1);
        set_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i, next_cp);
    }
    // Decrement cell count
    write_be16(page + 3, cell_count - 1);
    unpin_page(cur->tree->pool, cur->page_id, true);
    // Compact if fragmentation exceeds threshold (e.g., 25% of usable space)
    if (frag > (PAGE_SIZE - header_size) / 4) {
        page_defragment(cur->tree->pool, cur->page_id);
    }
}
```
The page compaction function rewrites all cells contiguously:
```c
static void page_defragment(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id) {
    uint8_t *page = fetch_page(pool, page_id);
    uint8_t page_type = page[0];
    int header_size = (page_type == PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_INTERNAL ||
                       page_type == PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_INTERNAL)
                      ? INTERNAL_HEADER_SIZE : LEAF_HEADER_SIZE;
    uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(page + 3);
    // Copy all cell data to a temporary buffer
    uint8_t temp[PAGE_SIZE];
    int write_pos = PAGE_SIZE;  // cells grow upward from bottom
    for (int i = 0; i < cell_count; i++) {
        uint16_t cp = get_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i);
        int size = compute_cell_size(page, cp, page_type);
        write_pos -= size;
        memcpy(temp + write_pos, page + cp, size);
        // Update cell pointer to new position
        set_cell_pointer(page, header_size, i, (uint16_t)write_pos);
    }
    // Copy compacted cells back to page
    memcpy(page + write_pos, temp + write_pos, PAGE_SIZE - write_pos);
    // Update header
    write_be16(page + 5, (uint16_t)write_pos);  // new content_start
    page[7] = 0;  // fragmented_bytes = 0
    unpin_page(pool, page_id, true);
}
```
---
