## Wiring It All Together: The Complete DML Dispatcher
The compiler's top-level `compile_statement` function now handles all four statement types:
```c
void compile_statement(Compiler *c, AstNode *ast) {
    switch (ast->type) {
        case NODE_SELECT:
            compile_select(c, ast);
            break;
        case NODE_INSERT:
            compile_insert(c, ast);
            break;
        case NODE_CREATE_TABLE:
            compile_create_table(c, ast);
            break;
        case NODE_UPDATE:
            compile_update(c, ast);
            break;
        case NODE_DELETE:
            compile_delete(c, ast);
            break;
        default:
            emit(c, OP_HALT, 1, 0, 0, "unknown statement type");
            break;
    }
}
```
And the end-to-end execution function:
```c
int execute_sql(Database *db, const char *sql,
                void (*callback)(Value *row, int ncols, void *ctx),
                void *ctx) {
    // Tokenize
    Lexer lex;
    lexer_init(&lex, sql);
    Token tokens[2048];
    int count = lexer_tokenize_all(&lex, tokens, 2048);
    if (count > 0 && tokens[count - 1].type == TOK_ERROR) {
        return SQLITE_ERROR;
    }
    // Parse
    Parser parser;
    parser_init(&parser, tokens, count);
    AstNode *ast = parser_parse(&parser);
    if (parser.had_error) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Parse error: line %d col %d: %s\n",
                parser.error_line, parser.error_col, parser.error_msg);
        return SQLITE_ERROR;
    }
    // Compile
    Program prog = {0};
    Compiler compiler;
    compiler_init(&compiler, &prog);
    compiler.tables = db->catalog.tables;
    compiler.table_count = db->catalog.table_count;
    compile_statement(&compiler, ast);
    // Execute
    VM vm;
    vm_init(&vm, &prog);
    vm.db = db;
    vm.result_callback = callback;
    vm.callback_ctx = ctx;
    int rc = vm_execute(&vm);
    ast_free(ast);
    program_free(&prog);
    return rc;
}
```
---
## Testing: Verifying the Full Pipeline
### Test: SELECT with Projection
```c
void test_select_projection(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_proj.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "name TEXT, age INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'Alice', 30);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 'Bob', 25);", NULL, NULL);
    // SELECT specific columns
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT name, age FROM t;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 2);
    assert(rc.col_count == 2);
    assert(strcmp(rc.rows[0][0].s.data, "Alice") == 0);
    assert(rc.rows[0][1].i == 30);
    assert(strcmp(rc.rows[1][0].s.data, "Bob") == 0);
    assert(rc.rows[1][1].i == 25);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_proj.db");
    printf("PASS: SELECT with column projection\n");
}
```
### Test: Three-Valued Logic in WHERE
```c
void test_null_where(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_null.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 10);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, NULL);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (3, 20);", NULL, NULL);
    // WHERE val > 5 should exclude NULL row (row 2)
    ResultCollector rc1 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id FROM t WHERE val > 5;", collect_results, &rc1);
    assert(rc1.row_count == 2);  // rows 1 and 3, NOT row 2
    assert(rc1.rows[0][0].i == 1);
    assert(rc1.rows[1][0].i == 3);
    // WHERE val = NULL should match ZERO rows (not row 2!)
    ResultCollector rc2 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id FROM t WHERE val = NULL;", collect_results, &rc2);
    assert(rc2.row_count == 0);  // NULL = NULL is NULL, not TRUE
    // WHERE val IS NULL should match row 2
    ResultCollector rc3 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id FROM t WHERE val IS NULL;",
                collect_results, &rc3);
    assert(rc3.row_count == 1);
    assert(rc3.rows[0][0].i == 2);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_null.db");
    printf("PASS: three-valued logic in WHERE\n");
}
```
### Test: INSERT with NOT NULL Constraint
```c
void test_not_null_constraint(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_constraint.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "name TEXT NOT NULL);", NULL, NULL);
    // Valid insert
    int rc1 = execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'Alice');",
                           NULL, NULL);
    assert(rc1 == 0);
    // Invalid insert — NULL for NOT NULL column
    int rc2 = execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, NULL);",
                           NULL, NULL);
    assert(rc2 == 19);  // SQLITE_CONSTRAINT
    // Verify only Alice was inserted
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT * FROM t;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 1);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_constraint.db");
    printf("PASS: NOT NULL constraint enforcement\n");
}
```
### Test: UPDATE with WHERE
```c
void test_update(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_update.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "name TEXT, age INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'Alice', 30);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 'Bob', 25);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "UPDATE t SET age = 31 WHERE name = 'Alice';",
                NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT name, age FROM t ORDER BY id;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 2);
    assert(rc.rows[0][1].i == 31);  // Alice's age updated
    assert(rc.rows[1][1].i == 25);  // Bob unchanged
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_update.db");
    printf("PASS: UPDATE with WHERE\n");
}
```
### Test: DELETE
```c
void test_delete(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_delete.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
        char sql[128];
        snprintf(sql, sizeof(sql), "INSERT INTO t VALUES (%d, %d);",
                 i, i * 10);
        execute_sql(db, sql, NULL, NULL);
    }
    // Delete even-valued rows
    execute_sql(db, "DELETE FROM t WHERE val > 50;", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id FROM t;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 5);  // rows 1-5 remain (val 10-50)
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_delete.db");
    printf("PASS: DELETE with WHERE\n");
}
```
### Test: Non-Existent Table Error
```c
void test_no_such_table(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_notable.db");
    int rc = execute_sql(db, "SELECT * FROM nonexistent;", NULL, NULL);
    assert(rc != 0);  // error
    // The error message should contain "nonexistent"
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_notable.db");
    printf("PASS: error on non-existent table\n");
}
```
### Test: Auto-Increment Rowid
```c
void test_auto_increment(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_autoinc.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "name TEXT);", NULL, NULL);
    // INSERT with NULL for INTEGER PRIMARY KEY → auto-increment
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (NULL, 'Alice');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (NULL, 'Bob');", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id, name FROM t;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 2);
    assert(rc.rows[0][0].i == 1);  // auto-assigned
    assert(rc.rows[1][0].i == 2);  // auto-incremented
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_autoinc.db");
    printf("PASS: auto-increment rowid\n");
}
```
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens During a WHERE-Filtered SELECT
**Level 1 — Public API**: You call `execute_sql(db, "SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 21", callback, ctx)`. For each matching row, `callback` is invoked with the projected column values.
**Level 2 — VM Engine**: The compiled bytecode opens a cursor, rewinds to the first leaf, and loops. Each iteration: `OP_COLUMN` calls through the cursor to the B-tree, which deserializes the `age` value from the record's binary format (parsing the varint header to find the column offset, reading the big-endian integer bytes). `OP_ISNULL` checks the type tag. `OP_LE` compares the value to 21 — if age ≤ 21 or NULL, the inverted jump skips to `OP_NEXT`. For matching rows, `OP_COLUMN` deserializes `name`, and `OP_RESULT_ROW` invokes the callback. `OP_NEXT` advances the cursor — which may mean incrementing the cell index within a leaf page, or ascending the parent chain and descending to the next leaf page's first cell.
**Level 3 — Buffer Pool / Disk**: Each `OP_COLUMN` pins a page via `fetch_page`. If the page is in the buffer pool (hit), this is a hash table lookup (~10ns) returning a pointer to the 4096-byte frame. The record deserialization reads bytes from this pinned buffer — varint decoding is a tight loop over cached memory, ~5ns. If the page is not in the buffer pool (miss), `pread` issues a system call to the kernel, which either serves from its page cache (~1μs including syscall overhead) or issues a disk I/O (~100μs on SSD). For a 10,000-row table with ~70 rows per page, that's ~143 leaf pages. With a 1000-frame buffer pool, all fit comfortably — hit rate is 100% after the first pass. Total scan time: ~143 page fetches × 10ns + 10,000 × (column read + compare) × ~20ns ≈ 0.2ms. Well under the 100ms budget.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What DML Execution Unlocks
**→ Three-Valued Logic Beyond SQL**: SQL's NULL semantics come from **Kleene's three-valued logic** (Stephen Kleene, 1952), a formal system in mathematical logic where the third value represents "unknown." The same three-valued pattern appears in completely different domains. In digital electronics, a **tri-state buffer** has three output states: high (1), low (0), and **high-impedance** (Z) — where Z means "disconnected from the bus," analogous to NULL meaning "not participating in this comparison." In hardware description languages (Verilog/VHDL), signal values can be `0`, `1`, `X` (unknown), or `Z` (high-impedance), and the simulation propagation rules mirror SQL's truth tables exactly. The isomorphism is deep: wherever you have a value that means "I don't know," you need three-valued logic to reason about it correctly.
**→ The Iterator/Cursor Pattern Everywhere**: The open/rewind/next/close cursor you use for B-tree traversal is the same **Iterator pattern** that drives functional programming's `map`, `filter`, and `reduce`. When you write `SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 21`, you're expressing `users.filter(row -> row.age > 21).map(row -> row.name)`. In Apache Spark, this is literally how query plans execute — each operator is an iterator that pulls rows from the child operator. In Java streams, `stream.filter(...).map(...)` creates a pipeline of iterators. The database cursor predates all of these by decades — it's one of the original instances of the pattern that programming language designers later generalized.
**→ Predicate Pushdown as Universal Optimization**: Evaluating WHERE during the scan — not after collecting all rows into memory — is the simplest form of **predicate pushdown**. The principle: push filters as close to the data source as possible to minimize the volume of data flowing through the pipeline. The same principle drives: (1) Apache Spark's physical planning, which pushes filter predicates down into Parquet file reads; (2) Google BigQuery's columnar storage, which skips entire column chunks based on pushed-down predicates; (3) eBPF in Linux, which pushes packet filters into the kernel to avoid copying unneeded packets to userspace. In every case, the insight is the same: filtering late is waste. Your bytecode compiler already does this — `OP_COLUMN` for the WHERE clause column is emitted before `OP_COLUMN` for the output columns, so non-matching rows never trigger the second deserialization.
**→ The Delete-During-Iteration Antipattern**: The two-pass approach you implemented for UPDATE and DELETE is the solution to a universal problem: modifying a data structure while iterating over it. Java's `ArrayList` throws `ConcurrentModificationException` if you call `remove()` during iteration (unless you use the iterator's own `remove()` method). C++ STL containers invalidate iterators on modification — using a stale iterator is undefined behavior. Python's `for item in list` silently skips elements if you `del` during iteration. Rust's borrow checker prevents this statically — you can't hold a mutable and immutable reference simultaneously. Your two-pass approach (collect targets, then modify) is the same strategy used by most database engines and is the general-purpose solution in any language: separate the "what to change" question from the "change it" action.
---
## System Awareness
You've built the DML execution layer — the component that makes your database actually useful. It connects the SQL frontend (tokenizer → parser → compiler) to the storage backend (B-tree → buffer pool → disk) through the VM's bytecode execution. SELECT reads rows through the cursor, deserializes records, projects columns, and evaluates WHERE with correct three-valued logic. INSERT serializes rows, enforces constraints, and writes through the B-tree. UPDATE and DELETE use the two-pass pattern to safely modify data during iteration.
Without this layer, you had components — a parser that produced ASTs nobody consumed beyond toy tests, a B-tree that stored bytes nobody queried through SQL. With it, you have a working database: `CREATE TABLE`, `INSERT`, `SELECT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` — all through the SQL interface, all executed as compiled bytecode, all stored durably on disk through page-based B-tree storage.
In the next milestone (Secondary Indexes), you'll build B+tree indexes that allow the query engine to skip full table scans for filtered queries — turning O(n) scans into O(log n) lookups. The cursor interface you've been using will extend to support index cursors, and the double-lookup pattern (index → rowid → table) will make your understanding of the B-tree vs B+tree distinction from Milestone 5 concrete.

![EXPLAIN Output Anatomy](./diagrams/diag-explain-output.svg)

---
<!-- END_MS -->


<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m7 -->
<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m7 -->
# Milestone 7: Secondary Indexes
## Where We Are in the System
Your database can execute SQL end-to-end. A user types `SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 21`, and your engine tokenizes, parses, compiles to bytecode, executes via the VM, traverses the table B-tree through the buffer pool, deserializes records, evaluates the WHERE clause with correct three-valued logic, projects columns, and streams results back. INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE all work through the two-pass pattern you built in Milestone 6.

![SQLite Engine — Full System Architecture Map](./diagrams/diag-satellite-system-map.svg)

There is one devastating problem: **every query reads every row**. Your `SELECT name FROM users WHERE age = 25` on a million-row table scans all million rows, deserializing each record's `age` column, comparing it to 25, and discarding 99.9% of them. The table B-tree is ordered by *rowid*, not by *age*. To find rows where age equals 25, you must examine every single row because age could appear anywhere in the rowid ordering.

![Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page](./diagrams/diag-data-flow-sql-to-disk.svg)

This milestone fixes that. You will build **secondary indexes** — separate B+tree data structures that map column values to rowids, enabling the VM to jump directly to matching rows instead of scanning everything. But indexes are not free magic. They come with costs that most developers never think about, and understanding those costs is what separates someone who "adds indexes when queries are slow" from someone who understands *why* the query was slow and *whether* an index is actually the right fix.
---
## The Revelation: An Index Is Not a Lookup Table
You probably think of an index like the index at the back of a textbook — a sorted list of terms pointing to page numbers. Find "B-tree" in the index, follow the page number, read the content. Simple, fast, always helpful.
This mental model is dangerously incomplete. It hides three costs that determine whether an index helps or hurts:
**Cost 1: The Double Lookup.** A textbook index says "B-tree: page 142." You flip to page 142 and read. One lookup. A database index says "age=25: rowid 7,042." But rowid 7,042 is somewhere in the table B-tree — you don't know which leaf page contains it. You must traverse the table B-tree *again* to find it. That's **two** tree traversals per matching row: one through the index B+tree to find the rowid, then one through the table B-tree to fetch the actual row. If your query matches 1,000 rows, that's 2,000 tree traversals instead of the single scan through ~14 leaf pages (at 70 rows per page) a table scan would perform.

![Double Lookup: Index → Table B-tree](./diagrams/diag-double-lookup.svg)

**Cost 2: Write Amplification.** Every INSERT into the table must also insert an entry into *every* index on that table. If you have 5 indexes, one INSERT becomes 6 B-tree insertions — the table plus 5 indexes. Each index insertion might trigger its own page splits, its own dirty pages, its own disk I/O. UPDATE is worse: you must delete the old index entry (the old column value) and insert the new one, for every index whose columns were modified. DELETE must remove entries from all indexes. A table with 5 indexes turns every write into roughly 6× the work.

![Index Maintenance on INSERT](./diagrams/diag-index-maintenance.svg)

**Cost 3: Selectivity Determines Whether the Index Helps.** If your query matches 0.1% of rows (high selectivity), the index is a massive win — 2 tree traversals per matching row is far less work than scanning 100% of rows. But if your query matches 30% of rows (low selectivity), the index performs 30,000 double lookups on a 100,000-row table — 60,000 tree traversals — while a full table scan would read ~1,400 leaf pages sequentially. Sequential reads are dramatically faster than random reads (both at the disk level and the CPU cache level), so the table scan wins despite reading more total data.
The crossover point — where an index scan becomes slower than a table scan — is typically around **10-20% selectivity**. Below that, the index saves work. Above it, the index *adds* work. Your query planner (Milestone 8) will make this decision automatically, but you need to understand the tradeoff now to build the index infrastructure correctly.

![Index Lookup vs Full Table Scan](./diagrams/diag-index-lookup-vs-scan.svg)

This is the fundamental tension of indexing: **indexes trade write performance for read performance, and only improve reads when selectivity is high enough to justify the double lookup.** An index is not a free speedup. It's a bet that certain queries are worth optimizing at the cost of making all writes slower.
---
## The Index B+tree: Structure and Cell Format
A secondary index is a **separate B+tree** stored in its own set of pages, with its own root page registered in the system catalog. The key distinction from the table B-tree (Milestone 5):

![Secondary Index B+tree Structure](./diagrams/diag-index-structure.svg)

| Property | Table B-tree | Index B+tree |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Key | rowid (integer) | Indexed column value(s) |
| Leaf data | Full row record | (column_value, rowid) pair |
| Internal data | Separator key + child pointers | Separator key + child pointers (no row data) |
| Leaf linking | No links between leaves | Leaves linked in a chain (for range scans) |
| Ordering | By rowid | By indexed column value |
The B+tree distinction matters here more than it did for tables. Recall from Milestone 5: in a B+tree, **data lives only in leaf nodes**, and **leaf nodes are linked**. Internal nodes contain only separator keys and child pointers — no payloads. This gives index B+trees two advantages over B-trees:
1. **Higher fanout in internal nodes.** Without data payloads, internal pages fit more separator keys. With 4KB pages and 8-byte keys + 4-byte child pointers, you fit ~340 entries per internal page. Higher fanout → shallower tree → fewer page reads per lookup.
2. **Efficient range scans.** Because leaves are linked, scanning all rows where `age BETWEEN 20 AND 30` means: descend to the leaf containing age=20, then follow leaf-to-leaf pointers forward until you pass age=30. No need to re-traverse the tree. This is what makes `ORDER BY indexed_column` fast — the data is already in sorted order at the leaf level.
### Index Leaf Cell Format
An index leaf cell stores the indexed column value and the rowid of the corresponding table row. The payload is a record (using the same serialization format from Milestone 5) containing the indexed value followed by the rowid:
```c
// Index leaf cell: [payload_size (varint)] [record: (indexed_value, rowid)]
// The record header + body follow the same serial type format as table records.
int write_index_leaf_cell(uint8_t *buf, int offset,
                           ColumnValue *index_key, int64_t rowid) {
    // Build a record with 2 fields: the indexed value and the rowid
    ColumnValue fields[2];
    fields[0] = *index_key;
    fields[1].type = COL_INTEGER;
    fields[1].i = rowid;
    uint8_t record_buf[4096];
    int record_size = record_serialize(fields, 2, record_buf, sizeof(record_buf));
    int pos = offset;
    pos += varint_encode((uint64_t)record_size, buf + pos);
    memcpy(buf + pos, record_buf, record_size);
    pos += record_size;
    return pos - offset;  // total cell size
}
```
### Index Internal Cell Format
An index internal cell stores a child page pointer and a separator key. The separator key is also a record (indexed_value, rowid) — including the rowid in the separator is essential for handling duplicate indexed values. If 500 rows all have `age = 25`, the separators between leaf pages must distinguish them, and the rowid serves as the tiebreaker:
```c
// Index internal cell: [left_child (4 bytes BE)] [payload_size (varint)] [separator_record]
int write_index_internal_cell(uint8_t *buf, int offset,
                                uint32_t left_child,
                                ColumnValue *key, int64_t rowid) {
    int pos = offset;
    write_be32(buf + pos, left_child);
    pos += 4;
    // Separator is a record: (indexed_value, rowid)
    ColumnValue fields[2];
    fields[0] = *key;
    fields[1].type = COL_INTEGER;
    fields[1].i = rowid;
    uint8_t record_buf[512];
    int record_size = record_serialize(fields, 2, record_buf, sizeof(record_buf));
    pos += varint_encode((uint64_t)record_size, buf + pos);
    memcpy(buf + pos, record_buf, record_size);
    pos += record_size;
    return pos - offset;
}
```
### Leaf Page Linking for Range Scans
Unlike table B-tree leaves, index B+tree leaves must be linked. Each leaf page stores the page ID of the next leaf in the chain. You can reuse one of the header fields for this — or add a dedicated field. The simplest approach: store the next-leaf pointer in the first 4 bytes of the cell content area (before any cells), reserving this space during page initialization:
```c
#define INDEX_LEAF_NEXT_OFFSET (PAGE_SIZE - 4)  // last 4 bytes of page
void index_leaf_set_next(uint8_t *page, page_id_t next_leaf) {
    write_be32(page + INDEX_LEAF_NEXT_OFFSET, next_leaf);
}
page_id_t index_leaf_get_next(uint8_t *page) {
    return read_be32(page + INDEX_LEAF_NEXT_OFFSET);
}
```
An alternative (cleaner) approach: add a `next_leaf` field to the page header for `PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_LEAF` pages. SQLite doesn't actually store next-leaf pointers; it uses the parent-chain traversal from Milestone 5. But explicit leaf linking is simpler to implement for range scans and is what most B+tree textbooks describe. Either approach works — the tradeoff is 4 bytes of space per leaf page (trivial) versus the complexity of parent-chain traversal for range scans (nontrivial).
---
## Key Comparison for Index Entries
Index B+trees are ordered by the indexed column value, not by rowid. This means your cell insertion, search, and split logic must compare column values rather than integers. The comparison is more complex because you must handle multiple data types and the rowid tiebreaker:
```c
// Compare two index keys: (column_value, rowid) pairs.
// Returns negative if a < b, 0 if equal, positive if a > b.
static int index_key_compare(ColumnValue *val_a, int64_t rowid_a,
                               ColumnValue *val_b, int64_t rowid_b) {
    // First, compare the indexed column values
    int cmp = column_value_compare(val_a, val_b);
    if (cmp != 0) return cmp;
    // Tiebreaker: compare rowids
    if (rowid_a < rowid_b) return -1;
    if (rowid_a > rowid_b) return 1;
    return 0;
}
// Compare two column values of potentially different types.
// NULL sorts before all other values (SQLite convention).
static int column_value_compare(ColumnValue *a, ColumnValue *b) {
    // NULL handling: NULL < everything else
    if (a->type == COL_NULL && b->type == COL_NULL) return 0;
    if (a->type == COL_NULL) return -1;
    if (b->type == COL_NULL) return 1;
    // SQLite type affinity ordering: NULL < INTEGER/REAL < TEXT < BLOB
    // For same-type comparison:
    if (a->type == COL_INTEGER && b->type == COL_INTEGER) {
        if (a->i < b->i) return -1;
        if (a->i > b->i) return 1;
        return 0;
    }
    if (a->type == COL_REAL && b->type == COL_REAL) {
        if (a->r < b->r) return -1;
        if (a->r > b->r) return 1;
        return 0;
    }
    if ((a->type == COL_INTEGER || a->type == COL_REAL) &&
        (b->type == COL_INTEGER || b->type == COL_REAL)) {
        double da = (a->type == COL_REAL) ? a->r : (double)a->i;
        double db = (b->type == COL_REAL) ? b->r : (double)b->i;
        if (da < db) return -1;
        if (da > db) return 1;
        return 0;
    }
    if (a->type == COL_TEXT && b->type == COL_TEXT) {
        int min_len = a->s.length < b->s.length ? a->s.length : b->s.length;
        int cmp = memcmp(a->s.data, b->s.data, min_len);
        if (cmp != 0) return cmp;
        if (a->s.length < b->s.length) return -1;
        if (a->s.length > b->s.length) return 1;
        return 0;
    }
    // Cross-type: use SQLite type order: INTEGER/REAL < TEXT < BLOB
    int order_a = type_sort_order(a->type);
    int order_b = type_sort_order(b->type);
    return order_a - order_b;
}
static int type_sort_order(ColumnType t) {
    switch (t) {
        case COL_NULL:    return 0;
        case COL_INTEGER: return 1;
        case COL_REAL:    return 1;
        case COL_TEXT:    return 2;
        case COL_BLOB:    return 3;
        default:          return 4;
    }
}
```
The NULL sorting convention deserves attention. SQLite sorts NULLs before all other values in indexes. This means a scan from the beginning of an index leaf chain encounters NULL entries first. The IS NULL optimization becomes free: `WHERE indexed_col IS NULL` is a range scan at the very start of the index.
---
## CREATE INDEX: Building the B+tree
`CREATE INDEX idx_users_age ON users(age)` must:
1. Look up the `users` table in the system catalog to get its root page and schema.
2. Allocate a new B+tree root page for the index.
3. Scan all existing rows in the `users` table, extracting the `age` column and the rowid from each row.
4. Insert each `(age, rowid)` pair into the index B+tree.
5. Register the index in the system catalog with `type='index'`.
```c
int create_index(Database *db, const char *index_name,
                  const char *table_name, const char *column_name,
                  bool is_unique) {
    // Step 1: Look up the table
    TableSchema *schema = catalog_find_table(&db->catalog, table_name);
    if (!schema) {
        fprintf(stderr, "no such table: %s\n", table_name);
        return -1;
    }
    int col_idx = -1;
    for (int i = 0; i < schema->column_count; i++) {
        if (strcasecmp(schema->column_names[i], column_name) == 0) {
            col_idx = i;
            break;
        }
    }
    if (col_idx < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "no such column: %s.%s\n", table_name, column_name);
        return -1;
    }
    // Step 2: Allocate a new root page for the index B+tree
    page_id_t index_root = buffer_pool_new_page(db->pool);
    uint8_t *root_page = fetch_page(db->pool, index_root);
    page_init(root_page, PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_LEAF);
    index_leaf_set_next(root_page, 0);  // no next leaf yet
    unpin_page(db->pool, index_root, true);
    // Step 3: Scan the table and populate the index
    BTree table_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = schema->root_page };
    BTree index_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = index_root,
                          .page_type = PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_LEAF };
    BTreeCursor *cur = btree_cursor_open(&table_tree);
    bool empty = btree_cursor_rewind(cur);
    if (!empty) {
        do {
            // Read the indexed column value
            ColumnValue col_val;
            btree_cursor_read_column(cur, col_idx, &col_val);
            // Read the rowid
            int64_t rowid = btree_cursor_rowid(cur);
            // Check UNIQUE constraint
            if (is_unique && col_val.type != COL_NULL) {
                if (index_contains_value(&index_tree, &col_val)) {
                    fprintf(stderr,
                            "UNIQUE constraint failed: %s.%s\n",
                            table_name, column_name);
                    btree_cursor_close(cur);
                    return -1;
                }
            }
            // Insert (column_value, rowid) into index B+tree
            index_btree_insert(&index_tree, &col_val, rowid);
        } while (btree_cursor_next(cur));
    }
    btree_cursor_close(cur);
    // Step 4: Register in system catalog
    IndexSchema *idx = &db->catalog.indexes[db->catalog.index_count];
    strncpy(idx->name, index_name, 63);
    strncpy(idx->table_name, table_name, 63);
    strncpy(idx->column_name, column_name, 63);
    idx->column_idx = col_idx;
    idx->root_page = index_root;
    idx->is_unique = is_unique;
    db->catalog.index_count++;
    // Persist to sqlite_master
    catalog_insert_index_row(&db->catalog, db->pool, idx);
    return 0;
}
```
The index B+tree insertion function is structurally identical to the table B-tree insertion from Milestone 5, but uses `index_key_compare` instead of integer rowid comparison, and uses the index cell formats instead of table cell formats:
```c
void index_btree_insert(BTree *tree, ColumnValue *key, int64_t rowid) {
    // Build the leaf cell
    uint8_t cell_buf[4096];
    int cell_size = write_index_leaf_cell(cell_buf, 0, key, rowid);
    // Search for the correct leaf page
    page_id_t leaf_page;
    int cell_idx;
    index_btree_search(tree, key, rowid, &leaf_page, &cell_idx);
    // Try inserting into the leaf
    uint8_t *page = fetch_page(tree->pool, leaf_page);
    int result = index_page_insert_cell(page, cell_buf, cell_size,
                                          key, rowid);
    if (result == 0) {
        unpin_page(tree->pool, leaf_page, true);
        return;
    }
    // Page full — split
    unpin_page(tree->pool, leaf_page, false);
    ColumnValue promoted_key;
    int64_t promoted_rowid;
    page_id_t new_page;
    index_btree_split_leaf(tree, leaf_page, cell_buf, cell_size,
                            key, rowid,
                            &promoted_key, &promoted_rowid, &new_page);
    // Update leaf chain: new_page's next = old_page's next, old_page's next = new_page
    uint8_t *old_p = fetch_page(tree->pool, leaf_page);
    page_id_t old_next = index_leaf_get_next(old_p);
    index_leaf_set_next(old_p, new_page);
    unpin_page(tree->pool, leaf_page, true);
    uint8_t *new_p = fetch_page(tree->pool, new_page);
    index_leaf_set_next(new_p, old_next);
    unpin_page(tree->pool, new_page, true);
    // Insert promoted key into parent
    index_btree_insert_into_parent(tree, leaf_page,
                                     &promoted_key, promoted_rowid,
                                     new_page, INVALID_PAGE_ID);
}
```
Note the leaf chain maintenance during splits. When a leaf page splits into two (left and right), the right page's `next` pointer must point to whatever the left page's `next` used to be, and the left page's `next` must now point to the right page. This preserves the linked-list ordering so range scans traverse all leaves correctly.
---
## Index Lookup: The Equality Search
An index equality lookup — `WHERE age = 25` — descends the index B+tree to find the first leaf entry with `age = 25`, then scans forward through the leaf chain collecting all matching entries. Each matching entry yields a rowid, which is then used to fetch the full row from the table B-tree.

![Index Lookup vs Full Table Scan](./diagrams/diag-index-lookup-vs-scan.svg)

```c
// Search the index B+tree for entries matching the given key.
// For each match, calls callback with the rowid.
// Returns the number of matching entries.
int index_equality_lookup(BTree *index_tree, ColumnValue *search_key,
                           void (*callback)(int64_t rowid, void *ctx),
                           void *ctx) {
    // Descend to the leftmost leaf that might contain search_key
    page_id_t current = index_tree->root_page;
    while (true) {
        uint8_t *page = fetch_page(index_tree->pool, current);
        uint8_t page_type = page[0];
        if (page_type == PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_LEAF) {
            // Search this leaf and subsequent leaves
            int matches = 0;
            page_id_t leaf = current;
            while (leaf != 0) {
                uint8_t *lp = (leaf == current) ? page :
                               fetch_page(index_tree->pool, leaf);
                uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(lp + 3);
                for (int i = 0; i < cell_count; i++) {
                    ColumnValue entry_key;
                    int64_t entry_rowid;
                    read_index_leaf_cell(lp, i, &entry_key, &entry_rowid);
                    int cmp = column_value_compare(&entry_key, search_key);
                    if (cmp < 0) continue;   // not yet reached search_key
                    if (cmp > 0) {
                        // Past search_key — done
                        if (leaf != current)
                            unpin_page(index_tree->pool, leaf, false);
                        else
                            unpin_page(index_tree->pool, current, false);
                        return matches;
                    }
                    // cmp == 0: match!
                    callback(entry_rowid, ctx);
                    matches++;
                }
                page_id_t next = index_leaf_get_next(lp);
                if (leaf != current)
                    unpin_page(index_tree->pool, leaf, false);
                else
                    unpin_page(index_tree->pool, current, false);
                leaf = next;
            }
            return matches;
        }
        // Internal page: descend to the correct child
        uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(page + 3);
        page_id_t child = read_be32(page + 8);  // default: right_child
        for (int i = 0; i < cell_count; i++) {
            ColumnValue sep_key;
            int64_t sep_rowid;
            uint32_t left_child;
            read_index_internal_cell(page, i, &left_child,
                                       &sep_key, &sep_rowid);
            int cmp = column_value_compare(search_key, &sep_key);
            if (cmp < 0 || (cmp == 0 && /* want leftmost match */ true)) {
                child = left_child;
                break;
            }
        }
        unpin_page(index_tree->pool, current, false);
        current = child;
    }
}
```
### The Double Lookup in the VM
The VM must perform two cursor operations per matching index entry: one to read the rowid from the index, and another to seek into the table B-tree to fetch the full row. This requires two open cursors — an **index cursor** and a **table cursor**:

![Double Lookup: Index → Table B-tree](./diagrams/diag-double-lookup.svg)

```c
// New opcodes for index operations
OP_OPEN_INDEX_READ,   // Open index cursor p1 on index root page p2
OP_IDX_KEY,           // Read indexed value from index cursor p1 into register p2
OP_IDX_ROWID,         // Read rowid from index cursor p1 into register p2
OP_SEEK_ROWID,        // Position table cursor p1 to rowid in register p2
```
The bytecode for `SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age = 25` with an index on `age`:
```
addr  opcode          p1   p2   p3   comment
----  --------------  ---  ---  ---  -------
0     Init            0    1    0    Start
1     OpenRead        0    5    3    users (table cursor, root=5)
2     OpenIndexRead   1    8    0    idx_users_age (index cursor, root=8)
3     Integer         25   1    0    search key → r1
4     IdxSeek         1    12   1    seek index cursor to r1; jump to Close if not found
5     IdxKey          1    2    0    read indexed value → r2
6     Integer         25   3    0    25 → r3
7     Ne              2    10   3    if r2 != 25, jump to IdxNext (past matching range)
8     IdxRowid        1    4    0    read rowid from index → r4
9     SeekRowid       0    4    0    position table cursor to rowid r4
10    Column          0    0    5    name → r5
11    Column          0    1    6    age → r6
12    ResultRow       5    2    0    output r5, r6
13    IdxNext         1    5    0    advance index cursor; loop if more entries
14    Close           1    0    0    close index cursor
15    Close           0    0    0    close table cursor
16    Halt            0    0    0
```

![EXPLAIN Output Anatomy](./diagrams/diag-explain-output.svg)

Study the double lookup at addresses 8-9: `OP_IDX_ROWID` extracts the rowid from the index cursor's current entry, then `OP_SEEK_ROWID` uses that rowid to position the *table* cursor so that subsequent `OP_COLUMN` calls read from the correct table row. Two tree traversals per matching row — one for the index seek (amortized via leaf scanning), one for the table seek.
The VM handlers:
```c
case OP_OPEN_INDEX_READ: {
    int cursor_id = op->p1;
    int root_page = op->p2;
    BTree *idx_tree = btree_open(vm->db->pool, root_page);
    idx_tree->page_type = PAGE_TYPE_INDEX_LEAF;
    vm->cursors[cursor_id] = index_cursor_open(idx_tree);
    break;
}
case OP_IDX_ROWID: {
    int cursor_id = op->p1;
    int dest_reg = op->p2;
    // Read the rowid field from the current index entry
    ColumnValue fields[2];
    int field_count;
    index_cursor_read_entry(vm->cursors[cursor_id], fields, &field_count);
    // The rowid is the second field in the index record
    vm->registers[dest_reg].type = VAL_INTEGER;
    vm->registers[dest_reg].i = fields[1].i;
    break;
}
case OP_IDX_KEY: {
    int cursor_id = op->p1;
    int dest_reg = op->p2;
    ColumnValue fields[2];
    int field_count;
    index_cursor_read_entry(vm->cursors[cursor_id], fields, &field_count);
    column_value_to_vm_value(&fields[0], &vm->registers[dest_reg]);
    break;
}
```
---
## Index Range Scan: Following the Leaf Chain
A range predicate like `WHERE age BETWEEN 20 AND 30` or `WHERE age > 21 AND age < 50` can use the index for efficient scanning. The algorithm:
1. Descend the index B+tree to find the first leaf entry where `age >= 20` (the lower bound).
2. Scan forward through the leaf chain, outputting every entry where `age <= 30`.
3. Stop when you encounter an entry where `age > 30`.

![Index Range Scan via Leaf Traversal](./diagrams/diag-index-range-scan.svg)

The leaf chain is what makes this efficient. Without it, you'd need to re-traverse the tree for every distinct value in the range. With the chain, you descend once and then follow sequential pointers — touching each leaf page exactly once. The I/O pattern is **sequential** (leaf pages are logically adjacent in the chain), which is dramatically faster than the **random** I/O of double lookups.
```c
// Range scan on an index.
// Scans entries where lower_bound <= key <= upper_bound.
// Either bound can be NULL to indicate "unbounded".
int index_range_scan(BTree *index_tree,
                      ColumnValue *lower_bound,
                      ColumnValue *upper_bound,
                      void (*callback)(int64_t rowid, void *ctx),
                      void *ctx) {
    // Find the starting leaf
    page_id_t start_leaf;
    int start_cell;
    if (lower_bound) {
        // Descend to the first entry >= lower_bound
        index_btree_find_ge(index_tree, lower_bound,
                             &start_leaf, &start_cell);
    } else {
        // Start from the leftmost leaf
        start_leaf = index_btree_leftmost_leaf(index_tree);
        start_cell = 0;
    }
    // Scan forward through the leaf chain
    int matches = 0;
    page_id_t leaf = start_leaf;
    int cell = start_cell;
    while (leaf != 0) {
        uint8_t *page = fetch_page(index_tree->pool, leaf);
        uint16_t cell_count = read_be16(page + 3);
        for (int i = cell; i < cell_count; i++) {
            ColumnValue entry_key;
            int64_t entry_rowid;
            read_index_leaf_cell(page, i, &entry_key, &entry_rowid);
            // Check upper bound
            if (upper_bound) {
                int cmp = column_value_compare(&entry_key, upper_bound);
                if (cmp > 0) {
                    unpin_page(index_tree->pool, leaf, false);
                    return matches;  // past upper bound — done
                }
            }
            callback(entry_rowid, ctx);
            matches++;
        }
        page_id_t next = index_leaf_get_next(page);
        unpin_page(index_tree->pool, leaf, false);
        leaf = next;
        cell = 0;  // start from beginning of next leaf
    }
    return matches;
}
```
The bytecode for a range scan looks similar to the equality scan but with two bound checks instead of one. The compiler emits `OP_IDX_GE` (seek to first entry ≥ lower bound) at the start, and an `OP_GT` check inside the loop that jumps to Close when the upper bound is exceeded.
---
## Index Maintenance: Keeping Indexes Consistent
Every DML operation — INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — must update all indexes on the affected table. This is **synchronous maintenance**: the indexes are updated within the same operation, not deferred to a background process. If the user inserts a row and immediately queries using an index, the index must already contain the new entry.
### INSERT Maintenance
After inserting a row into the table B-tree, insert a corresponding entry into every index:
```c
void insert_with_indexes(Database *db, TableSchema *schema,
                          int64_t rowid, ColumnValue *columns,
                          int col_count) {
    // Insert into the table B-tree
    BTree table_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = schema->root_page };
    btree_insert(&table_tree, rowid, columns, col_count);
    // Insert into every index on this table
    for (int i = 0; i < db->catalog.index_count; i++) {
        IndexSchema *idx = &db->catalog.indexes[i];
        if (strcasecmp(idx->table_name, schema->name) != 0) continue;
        ColumnValue *key_val = &columns[idx->column_idx];
        // UNIQUE check (if applicable)
        if (idx->is_unique && key_val->type != COL_NULL) {
            BTree idx_tree = { .pool = db->pool,
                                .root_page = idx->root_page };
            if (index_contains_value(&idx_tree, key_val)) {
                // Rollback: delete the table row we just inserted
                btree_delete_by_rowid(&table_tree, rowid);
                fprintf(stderr, "UNIQUE constraint failed: %s\n",
                        idx->name);
                return;
            }
        }
        // Insert into index B+tree
        BTree idx_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = idx->root_page };
        index_btree_insert(&idx_tree, key_val, rowid);
    }
}
```
### DELETE Maintenance
Before deleting a row from the table B-tree, delete the corresponding entry from every index. You need the old column values to find the correct index entries:
```c
void delete_with_indexes(Database *db, TableSchema *schema,
                          int64_t rowid) {
    BTree table_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = schema->root_page };
    // Read the row's current values (needed to find index entries)
    ColumnValue old_columns[32];
    int col_count;
    btree_read_row(&table_tree, rowid, old_columns, &col_count);
    // Delete from every index
    for (int i = 0; i < db->catalog.index_count; i++) {
        IndexSchema *idx = &db->catalog.indexes[i];
        if (strcasecmp(idx->table_name, schema->name) != 0) continue;
        BTree idx_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = idx->root_page };
        index_btree_delete(&idx_tree, &old_columns[idx->column_idx], rowid);
    }
    // Delete from table B-tree
    btree_delete_by_rowid(&table_tree, rowid);
}
```
The `index_btree_delete` function searches the index B+tree for the exact `(column_value, rowid)` pair and removes that cell. Using the rowid as part of the search key is essential — without it, you can't distinguish between multiple rows with the same indexed value.
### UPDATE Maintenance
UPDATE is the costliest operation because it must delete old index entries and insert new ones for every modified indexed column:
```c
void update_with_indexes(Database *db, TableSchema *schema,
                          int64_t rowid,
                          ColumnValue *old_columns,
                          ColumnValue *new_columns,
                          int col_count) {
    for (int i = 0; i < db->catalog.index_count; i++) {
        IndexSchema *idx = &db->catalog.indexes[i];
        if (strcasecmp(idx->table_name, schema->name) != 0) continue;
        ColumnValue *old_key = &old_columns[idx->column_idx];
        ColumnValue *new_key = &new_columns[idx->column_idx];
        // Only update the index if the indexed column actually changed
        if (column_value_compare(old_key, new_key) == 0) continue;
        BTree idx_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = idx->root_page };
        // UNIQUE check on the new value
        if (idx->is_unique && new_key->type != COL_NULL) {
            if (index_contains_value(&idx_tree, new_key)) {
                fprintf(stderr, "UNIQUE constraint failed: %s\n",
                        idx->name);
                return;  // abort the update
            }
        }
        // Remove old entry, insert new entry
        index_btree_delete(&idx_tree, old_key, rowid);
        index_btree_insert(&idx_tree, new_key, rowid);
    }
    // Update the table B-tree row
    BTree table_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = schema->root_page };
    btree_delete_by_rowid(&table_tree, rowid);
    btree_insert(&table_tree, rowid, new_columns, col_count);
}
```
The optimization `if (column_value_compare(old_key, new_key) == 0) continue` is important. If `UPDATE users SET name = 'Bob' WHERE id = 1` doesn't change the `age` column, the index on `age` doesn't need to be updated. This saves one B+tree deletion and one insertion per unaffected index.
---
## UNIQUE Index: Constraint Enforcement
A UNIQUE index adds a constraint: no two non-NULL entries in the index may have the same column value. NULL values are explicitly excluded — SQL allows multiple NULL values in a UNIQUE column because NULL is "unknown," and two unknowns might or might not be equal.
The check happens in two places:
1. **During CREATE UNIQUE INDEX**: scan all existing rows. If any two non-NULL values are duplicates, the CREATE fails.
2. **During INSERT and UPDATE**: before inserting a new index entry, check if the value already exists.
```c
bool index_contains_value(BTree *index_tree, ColumnValue *search_key) {
    // Use the equality lookup — if it finds any match, the value exists
    typedef struct { bool found; } CheckCtx;
    CheckCtx ctx = { .found = false };
    index_equality_lookup(index_tree, search_key,
                           check_callback, &ctx);
    return ctx.found;
}
static void check_callback(int64_t rowid, void *raw_ctx) {
    CheckCtx *ctx = (CheckCtx*)raw_ctx;
    ctx->found = true;
}
```
The UNIQUE check must happen *before* the index insert. If you insert first and check after, a concurrent writer could slip in between (not a concern for your single-threaded build, but the correct ordering matters for correctness of the algorithm). The sequence is: check → insert index entry → insert table row. If the check fails, nothing is written.
For compound UNIQUE constraints (multiple columns), the check involves comparing all columns — but that's a Milestone 8+ concern. For now, single-column UNIQUE indexes are sufficient.
---
## Integrating Index Scans into the Compiler
The compiler must decide when to use an index. For this milestone, use a simple rule: **if the WHERE clause contains an equality or range predicate on a column that has an index, use the index.** The query planner (Milestone 8) will later make this decision based on cost estimation; for now, always prefer the index when one is available.
```c
// During compile_select, check for usable indexes
static IndexSchema* find_usable_index(Compiler *c, int table_idx,
                                        AstNode *where_clause) {
    if (!where_clause) return NULL;
    // Look for simple comparisons: column = expr, column > expr, etc.
    if (where_clause->type == NODE_BINARY_EXPR) {
        AstNode *left = where_clause->data.binary.left;
        TokenType op = where_clause->data.binary.op;
        // Only handle: column {=, <, >, <=, >=} expr
        if (left->type != NODE_IDENTIFIER) return NULL;
        if (op != TOK_EQ && op != TOK_LT && op != TOK_GT &&
            op != TOK_LE && op != TOK_GE) return NULL;
        const char *col_name = left->data.identifier.name;
        // Search for an index on this column
        for (int i = 0; i < c->index_count; i++) {
            IndexSchema *idx = &c->indexes[i];
            if (strcasecmp(idx->table_name,
                            c->tables[table_idx].name) != 0) continue;
            if (strcasecmp(idx->column_name, col_name) != 0) continue;
            return idx;  // found a usable index
        }
    }
    return NULL;
}
```
When a usable index is found, `compile_select` emits index-scan bytecode instead of full-table-scan bytecode:
```c
static void compile_select(Compiler *c, AstNode *node) {
    int table_idx = find_table(c, node->data.select.from_table);
    if (table_idx < 0) { /* error */ return; }
    IndexSchema *idx = find_usable_index(c, table_idx,
                                           node->data.select.where_clause);
    if (idx) {
        compile_select_with_index(c, node, table_idx, idx);
    } else {
        compile_select_full_scan(c, node, table_idx);
    }
}
```
The `compile_select_with_index` function emits the double-lookup bytecode pattern shown earlier. For equality predicates, it emits `IdxSeek` + loop with `Ne` check. For range predicates, it emits `IdxGe` (seek to lower bound) + loop with upper bound check.
---
## Measuring the Improvement: Page Reads as Currency

![Cost Model: Table Scan vs Index Scan](./diagrams/diag-cost-model-comparison.svg)

To verify your index actually helps, count page reads. Add a counter to the buffer pool:
```c
// In BufferPool struct:
uint64_t page_reads_session;  // reset per query
void buffer_pool_reset_counters(BufferPool *pool) {
    pool->page_reads_session = 0;
}
// In fetch_page, after loading or returning:
pool->page_reads_session++;
```
Now measure:
```c
void test_index_reduces_pages(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_idx_perf.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER, name TEXT);", NULL, NULL);
    // Insert 10,000 rows
    for (int i = 1; i <= 10000; i++) {
        char sql[128];
        snprintf(sql, sizeof(sql),
                 "INSERT INTO t VALUES (%d, %d, 'name_%d');",
                 i, i % 100, i);
        execute_sql(db, sql, NULL, NULL);
    }
    // Query WITHOUT index
    buffer_pool_reset_counters(db->pool);
    ResultCollector rc1 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT name FROM t WHERE val = 42;",
                collect_results, &rc1);
    uint64_t pages_no_index = db->pool->page_reads_session;
    // Create index
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE INDEX idx_val ON t(val);", NULL, NULL);
    // Query WITH index
    buffer_pool_reset_counters(db->pool);
    ResultCollector rc2 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT name FROM t WHERE val = 42;",
                collect_results, &rc2);
    uint64_t pages_with_index = db->pool->page_reads_session;
    assert(rc1.row_count == rc2.row_count);  // same results
    assert(pages_with_index < pages_no_index / 2);
    // With 10,000 rows, full scan reads ~143 pages.
    // Index lookup for val=42 (100 matching rows): ~3 index pages + 100 table pages
    // Still much less than 143 for high selectivity (1% match rate)
    printf("Without index: %lu pages\n", pages_no_index);
    printf("With index:    %lu pages\n", pages_with_index);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_idx_perf.db");
    printf("PASS: index reduces page reads\n");
}
```
---
## System Catalog Updates for Indexes
The system catalog must track indexes alongside tables. Extend the schema structures:
```c
typedef struct {
    char     name[64];
    char     table_name[64];
    char     column_name[64];
    int      column_idx;
    int      root_page;
    bool     is_unique;
} IndexSchema;
// In SystemCatalog:
IndexSchema indexes[128];
int         index_count;
```
On database open, the catalog reads all rows from `sqlite_master` where `type = 'index'` to rebuild the in-memory index schema cache. The `sql` field stores the original `CREATE INDEX` statement for future reference.
```c
void catalog_load_indexes(SystemCatalog *catalog, BufferPool *pool) {
    BTree master_tree = { .pool = pool, .root_page = 1 };
    BTreeCursor *cur = btree_cursor_open(&master_tree);
    if (btree_cursor_rewind(cur)) {
        btree_cursor_close(cur);
        return;
    }
    do {
        ColumnValue cols[5];
        int n;
        btree_cursor_read_record(cur, cols, 5, &n);
        // Check if type = 'index'
        if (cols[0].type == COL_TEXT &&
            cols[0].s.length == 5 &&
            memcmp(cols[0].s.data, "index", 5) == 0) {
            IndexSchema *idx = &catalog->indexes[catalog->index_count];
            // Parse name, table_name, rootpage from cols[1..4]
            memcpy(idx->name, cols[1].s.data, cols[1].s.length);
            idx->name[cols[1].s.length] = '\0';
            memcpy(idx->table_name, cols[2].s.data, cols[2].s.length);
            idx->table_name[cols[2].s.length] = '\0';
            idx->root_page = (int)cols[3].i;
            // Parse column name from the SQL string (cols[4]) or store separately
            // (simplified: require column_name stored in tbl_name field or parsed from SQL)
            catalog->index_count++;
        }
    } while (btree_cursor_next(cur));
    btree_cursor_close(cur);
}
```
---
## Testing
### Test: CREATE INDEX and Equality Lookup
```c
void test_create_index_and_lookup(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_cidx.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "age INTEGER, name TEXT);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 25, 'Alice');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 30, 'Bob');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (3, 25, 'Charlie');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (4, 35, 'Diana');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE INDEX idx_age ON t(age);", NULL, NULL);
    // Equality lookup: should use index
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT name FROM t WHERE age = 25;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 2);
    // Should find Alice and Charlie (order may vary)
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_cidx.db");
    printf("PASS: CREATE INDEX and equality lookup\n");
}
```
### Test: Range Scan
```c
void test_index_range_scan(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_range.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "score INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    for (int i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
        char sql[128];
        snprintf(sql, sizeof(sql),
                 "INSERT INTO t VALUES (%d, %d);", i, i);
        execute_sql(db, sql, NULL, NULL);
    }
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE INDEX idx_score ON t(score);", NULL, NULL);
    // Range: score > 90
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id FROM t WHERE score > 90;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 10);  // scores 91-100
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_range.db");
    printf("PASS: index range scan\n");
}
```
### Test: UNIQUE Index Rejects Duplicates
```c
void test_unique_index(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_unique.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "email TEXT);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'alice@test.com');",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_email ON t(email);",
                NULL, NULL);
    // Duplicate should be rejected
    int rc = execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 'alice@test.com');",
                          NULL, NULL);
    assert(rc != 0);  // constraint violation
    // Different value should succeed
    rc = execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 'bob@test.com');",
                      NULL, NULL);
    assert(rc == 0);
    // NULL should be allowed even with UNIQUE
    rc = execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (3, NULL);", NULL, NULL);
    assert(rc == 0);
    rc = execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (4, NULL);", NULL, NULL);
    assert(rc == 0);  // multiple NULLs are OK
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_unique.db");
    printf("PASS: UNIQUE index rejects duplicates\n");
}
```
### Test: Index Maintained on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
```c
void test_index_maintenance(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_maint.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE INDEX idx_val ON t(val);", NULL, NULL);
    // INSERT
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 10);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 20);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (3, 30);", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc1 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id FROM t WHERE val = 20;",
                collect_results, &rc1);
    assert(rc1.row_count == 1);
    assert(rc1.rows[0][0].i == 2);
    // UPDATE: change val from 20 to 25
    execute_sql(db, "UPDATE t SET val = 25 WHERE id = 2;", NULL, NULL);
    // Old value should return no results
    ResultCollector rc2 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id FROM t WHERE val = 20;",
                collect_results, &rc2);
    assert(rc2.row_count == 0);
    // New value should be findable
    ResultCollector rc3 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id FROM t WHERE val = 25;",
                collect_results, &rc3);
    assert(rc3.row_count == 1);
    assert(rc3.rows[0][0].i == 2);
    // DELETE
    execute_sql(db, "DELETE FROM t WHERE id = 2;", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc4 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id FROM t WHERE val = 25;",
                collect_results, &rc4);
    assert(rc4.row_count == 0);  // deleted row no longer in index
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_maint.db");
    printf("PASS: index maintained across INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE\n");
}
```
### Test: Double Lookup Correctness
```c
void test_double_lookup(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_dbl.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "category INTEGER, description TEXT);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE INDEX idx_cat ON t(category);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 5, 'first');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 3, 'second');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (3, 5, 'third');", NULL, NULL);
    // Query using index — the description column is NOT in the index,
    // so the VM must perform a double lookup (index → rowid → table)
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT description FROM t WHERE category = 5;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 2);
    // Should find 'first' and 'third'
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_dbl.db");
    printf("PASS: double lookup fetches non-indexed columns\n");
}
```
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens During an Index Lookup
**Level 1 — Public API**: You execute `SELECT name FROM users WHERE age = 25`. The engine returns only the rows where age is 25, without scanning the entire table.
**Level 2 — VM Engine**: The compiler detects that `idx_users_age` exists for the `age` column and emits index-scan bytecode. The VM opens two cursors: an index cursor on the `idx_users_age` B+tree and a table cursor on the `users` table B-tree. `OP_IDX_SEEK` descends the index B+tree (3 levels for millions of rows — 3 page reads). The loop reads each matching index entry's rowid via `OP_IDX_ROWID`, then `OP_SEEK_ROWID` descends the table B-tree (another 3 page reads) to position at that row. `OP_COLUMN` deserializes the `name` column from the table row's record. For 100 matching rows, that's ~3 index pages + ~100 table page seeks (many of which hit the same pages and are buffer pool hits).
**Level 3 — Buffer Pool / Disk**: Each `fetch_page` checks the hash table for a hit (~10ns). The index's internal pages (root + 1-2 internal levels) are accessed for every query on this index and quickly become "hot" in the buffer pool — essentially free after the first query. The leaf pages carrying the matching entries are accessed sequentially (via the leaf chain), which is cache-friendly. The table page lookups for the double lookup are **random access** — rowid 7,042 and rowid 83,291 probably live on different pages. These random reads are the dominant cost. On SSD, each random page read costs ~100μs. For 100 matching rows hitting 80 distinct table pages, that's ~8ms — versus ~14ms for a full table scan of 143 pages read sequentially. The index wins because 80 < 143, but the margin is thinner than you might expect because random reads are slower than sequential reads per page.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What Secondary Indexes Unlock
**→ Covering Indexes — Eliminating the Double Lookup.** If all columns needed by a query are present in the index itself, the table B-tree lookup can be skipped entirely. For `SELECT age FROM users WHERE age > 21`, the `age` value is already in the index leaf entry — no need to follow the rowid to the table. This is called a **covering index** (or "index-only scan"). PostgreSQL's `INCLUDE` clause (`CREATE INDEX idx ON users(age) INCLUDE (name)`) adds non-key columns to the index leaf specifically to enable covering scans. SQL Server's covering indexes serve the same purpose. The query planner (Milestone 8) detects covering opportunities and eliminates the double lookup, turning the I/O cost from `index_pages + table_pages` to just `index_pages`. This connects directly to the cost model you'll build next.

> **🔑 Foundation: Write amplification**
> 
> ## Write Amplification
**What it is:** Write amplification is when a single logical write from your application triggers *multiple* physical writes to storage. If a user inserts one key-value pair and your system ends up writing that data 10 times across different structures, your write amplification factor is 10×.
**Why you need this now:** When you maintain multiple data structures — say a sorted on-disk table, a write-ahead log, and a bloom filter — every incoming write must update *all* of them. During compaction in an LSM-tree, the problem compounds: data that was already written gets read, merged, and rewritten to a new sorted file. A single 100-byte insert might cause kilobytes of actual disk I/O over its lifetime as it gets compacted through multiple levels.
**Key insight:** Think of write amplification as a tax you pay for read performance. An LSM-tree accepts high write amplification (often 10×–30× in leveled compaction) because it converts random writes into sequential ones and keeps reads fast via sorted structure. Every design decision in your storage engine — compaction strategy, level size ratios, number of levels — is essentially tuning this tradeoff. When you see your disk writing 20 GB for 1 GB of user data, that's not a bug; it's the cost of the organizational work happening behind the scenes. The goal isn't to eliminate it, but to understand and control it.

**→ Write Amplification as a Universal Storage Cost.** Each index doubles the write path: one B-tree insert for the table, one B+tree insert per index. With 5 indexes, that's 6× write amplification. This is the same phenomenon that plagues **LSM-trees** (used in RocksDB, LevelDB, Cassandra): data written to the memtable is compacted through multiple levels, each level re-writing the data — 10× to 30× write amplification is common. In **SSDs**, write amplification has a physical dimension: the flash translation layer (FTL) must erase entire blocks (128KB-4MB) to rewrite a single page (4KB), causing write amplification at the hardware level. The insight is universal: **any system that maintains derived data structures (indexes, replicas, materialized views) pays a write amplification tax proportional to the number of derived structures.** Understanding this in your database makes the amplification costs in LSM-trees, SSDs, and distributed replication immediately intuitive.
**→ Inverted Indexes — The Same Concept, Different Domain.** Your secondary index maps `column_value → rowid`. A search engine's **inverted index** maps `term → document_id_list`. Lucene (the engine behind Elasticsearch and Solr) builds inverted indexes where each word in a document becomes a key, and the value is a sorted list of document IDs containing that word. The query `WHERE description CONTAINS 'database'` scans the inverted index for the term "database" and retrieves the document ID list — structurally identical to your `WHERE age = 25` scanning the B+tree for entries with age=25. The difference is that inverted indexes store *posting lists* (sorted arrays of IDs with frequency information) rather than individual `(value, rowid)` B+tree entries, because text search typically matches thousands of documents and storing each as a separate B+tree cell would be space-inefficient. But the fundamental pattern — **a separate data structure that maps derived keys to primary identifiers for fast lookup** — is identical. Once you understand secondary indexes, inverted indexes are a variation on the theme.
**→ Index Selection as an Optimization Problem.** Choosing which indexes to create is itself an optimization problem. With *n* columns, there are 2ⁿ − 1 possible single-column indexes (and exponentially more multi-column combinations). Each index speeds up certain queries but slows down all writes. The **Database Tuning Advisor** problem — given a workload (set of queries with frequencies), find the optimal set of indexes — is NP-hard in the general case. Microsoft's AutoAdmin, PostgreSQL's `pg_qualstats` + `dexter`, and Oracle's SQL Tuning Advisor all approximate this optimization using workload analysis and what-if cost estimation. This connects to operations research and combinatorial optimization — the same class of problems as scheduling, routing, and resource allocation.
> **🔭 Deep Dive**: The theory of index selection and physical database design is covered thoroughly in *Database Internals* by Alex Petrov, Chapter 6, and in the seminal paper "An Efficient, Cost-Driven Index Selection Tool for Microsoft SQL Server" (Chaudhuri & Narasayya, VLDB 1997).
---
## System Awareness
You've built secondary indexes — a separate B+tree infrastructure that maps column values to rowids, enabling the VM to skip full table scans for filtered queries. The index B+tree integrates with the buffer pool (downstream, Milestone 4), uses the same page format primitives (Milestone 5), and is invoked by the VM's new opcodes (Milestone 3 extension). Index maintenance hooks into the DML execution path (Milestone 6) to keep indexes synchronized with table data.
Without indexes, every query is a full table scan — O(n) in the number of rows, regardless of how selective the predicate is. With indexes, point lookups become O(log n) via the index B+tree descent, and range scans touch only the relevant portion of the index leaf chain. The cost is write amplification: every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE now maintains all associated indexes.
In the next milestone (Query Planner & Statistics), you'll build the intelligence to decide *when* an index scan is actually cheaper than a table scan. The planner will collect statistics (row counts, distinct value counts via ANALYZE), estimate selectivity, and compute the I/O cost of each access path — choosing the plan that minimizes total page reads. The index infrastructure you just built provides the *mechanism*; the query planner provides the *decision*.

![Query Planner Decision Flow](./diagrams/diag-planner-decision-tree.svg)

---
<!-- END_MS -->


<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m8 -->
<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m8 -->
# Milestone 8: Query Planner & Statistics
## Where We Are in the System
Your database executes SQL end-to-end. The tokenizer, parser, and bytecode compiler form the frontend. The VM executes bytecode through cursor operations. The buffer pool caches pages. The B-tree stores table rows. Secondary B+tree indexes enable fast lookups. INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE maintain indexes synchronously. Everything works.

![SQLite Engine — Full System Architecture Map](./diagrams/diag-satellite-system-map.svg)

But your compiler makes a terrible decision every single time: if an index exists on the WHERE column, it uses the index; otherwise, it does a full table scan. This is a rule, not an analysis. In Milestone 7, you hard-coded `find_usable_index` — "if there's an index on this column, use it." That rule is wrong in a way that can make your database **thousands of times slower** than it should be.

![Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page](./diagrams/diag-data-flow-sql-to-disk.svg)

This milestone builds the intelligence that replaces that rule with a **cost-based decision**. You will implement the `ANALYZE` command to collect statistics about your data, build a cost model that estimates the I/O cost of different access paths, and construct a query planner that chooses the cheapest plan. For multi-table queries, you'll implement dynamic-programming join ordering that finds the optimal join sequence. And you'll extend `EXPLAIN` to show the planner's reasoning — not just the bytecode, but *why* it chose that bytecode.
---
## The Revelation: When Indexes Make Things Worse
You probably think indexes always help. In Milestone 7, you measured the page reads and saw the index reduce I/O. That was for a query matching 1% of rows. Now consider this:
You have a `users` table with 1,000,000 rows. There's an index on `status`. You run:
```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE status = 'active';
```
If 95% of users are active, your index "optimization" does the following:
1. **Index scan**: Descend the index B+tree (3 pages). Then scan 950,000 index leaf entries across ~4,000 index leaf pages.
2. **Double lookup**: For each of the 950,000 matching entries, seek into the table B-tree — 3 page reads per seek (root, internal, leaf). That's 950,000 × 3 = 2,850,000 page reads. Most of these hit the buffer pool, but the access pattern is **random** — rowid 7,042 and rowid 999,001 live on different pages, so the CPU cache and OS read-ahead predictions are useless.
3. **Total**: ~4,000 index pages + ~2,850,000 table page reads (heavily cached, but still ~14,000 distinct table pages accessed in random order).
The full table scan alternative:
1. **Sequential scan**: Read all ~14,000 leaf pages of the table B-tree in order. Sequential I/O — the OS prefetcher loads the next page before you ask for it. The buffer pool processes them in order, maximizing cache utilization.
2. **Total**: ~14,000 page reads, all sequential.
The index scan touches more total pages, in a random pattern, with higher per-page overhead. The table scan reads the same 14,000 data pages but sequentially, which is 10-100× faster per page on spinning disks and 2-5× faster on SSDs. **The index made the query slower.**

![Cost Model: Table Scan vs Index Scan](./diagrams/diag-cost-model-comparison.svg)

This isn't a edge case. It's the normal situation for low-selectivity predicates. A query matching 30% of rows, 50% of rows, or 95% of rows should almost always use a table scan. The crossover point — where an index scan becomes cheaper than a table scan — depends on the data distribution, the number of matching rows, the page layout, and the ratio of sequential-to-random I/O cost on the hardware. Your compiler from Milestone 7 knows none of this. It sees an index and uses it, blindly.

> **🔑 Foundation: Cost-based query optimization fundamentals**
> 
> ## Cost-Based Query Optimization Fundamentals
**What it is:** A cost-based query optimizer is the component of a database engine that examines multiple possible *execution plans* for a given SQL query and picks the one with the lowest estimated "cost." Cost here is a numeric score representing expected resource consumption — typically a weighted combination of disk I/O, CPU cycles, memory usage, and network transfer.
Consider a simple query joining three tables `A`, `B`, and `C`. The optimizer doesn't just run it in the order you wrote it. Instead, it enumerates candidate plans — different join orders, different join algorithms (nested-loop, hash, sort-merge), different index choices — estimates the cost of each, and selects the cheapest.
The process follows a common structure:
1. **Plan enumeration** — generate candidate execution plans (or a smart subset of them).
2. **Cost estimation** — for each plan, estimate cost using a *cost model* and *statistics* about the data (table sizes, index availability, value distributions).
3. **Plan selection** — pick the plan with the lowest estimated cost.
The cost model is where the math lives. A simplified disk-I/O cost for a sequential table scan might be:
```
cost(scan T) = pages(T)
```
While an index lookup might be:
```
cost(index_lookup T, predicate) = height(index) + estimated_matching_pages
```
The optimizer relies on **catalog statistics** — row counts, page counts, histograms of column value distributions, number of distinct values — to plug real numbers into these formulas.
**Why you need this now:** You're building a query optimizer. The cost-based approach is *the* architecture you'll implement. Every subsequent milestone — join ordering, algorithm selection, plan comparison — plugs into this framework. Without understanding the enumeration → estimation → selection loop, the individual pieces won't connect.
**Key insight:** A cost-based optimizer doesn't find the *truly* optimal plan — it finds the plan with the lowest *estimated* cost. The quality of your optimizer is only as good as your cost model and your statistics. Bad cardinality estimates cascade into bad cost estimates, which cascade into terrible plan choices. This is why real-world optimizers invest heavily in statistics collection and why estimation accuracy matters more than enumeration cleverness.

The planner's job is to ask: "Given what I know about the data — how many rows, how many distinct values, how the values are distributed — what is the cheapest way to execute this query?" This requires **statistics** (collected by ANALYZE), a **cost model** (converting statistics to estimated I/O), and a **plan enumerator** (comparing alternative access paths). Without all three, the planner is either guessing or applying rigid rules that fail on real data.
---
## ANALYZE: Collecting Statistics
The `ANALYZE` command scans tables and indexes to collect summary statistics. These statistics are not the data itself — they're compact summaries that the planner uses to estimate costs without re-scanning the data at query time.
### What Statistics to Collect
For each table:
- **Row count**: How many rows are in the table? This determines the cost of a full table scan.
- **Page count**: How many leaf pages does the table B-tree have? This is the actual I/O cost of a scan.
For each indexed column:
- **Distinct value count** (NDV — Number of Distinct Values): How many unique values does this column contain? This drives selectivity estimation.
- **NULL count**: How many rows have NULL for this column? Needed for `IS NULL` selectivity.
- **Min/Max values**: The range of the column, useful for range predicate estimation.
```c
typedef struct {
    char     table_name[64];
    int64_t  row_count;       // total rows in table
    int      page_count;      // leaf pages in table B-tree
} TableStats;
typedef struct {
    char     index_name[64];
    char     column_name[64];
    int64_t  distinct_count;  // number of distinct non-NULL values
    int64_t  null_count;      // number of NULL values
    // Min/Max for range estimation
    ColumnValue min_value;
    ColumnValue max_value;
} IndexStats;
typedef struct {
    TableStats  table_stats[64];
    int         table_stats_count;
    IndexStats  index_stats[128];
    int         index_stats_count;
} Statistics;
```
### Implementing ANALYZE
The ANALYZE command scans each table and each index to populate these structures:
```c
void analyze_table(Database *db, TableSchema *schema, Statistics *stats) {
    BTree table_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = schema->root_page };
    BTreeCursor *cur = btree_cursor_open(&table_tree);
    int64_t row_count = 0;
    int page_count = 0;
    page_id_t last_page = INVALID_PAGE_ID;
    bool empty = btree_cursor_rewind(cur);
    if (!empty) {
        do {
            row_count++;
            // Track distinct leaf pages
            if (cur->page_id != last_page) {
                page_count++;
                last_page = cur->page_id;
            }
        } while (btree_cursor_next(cur));
    }
    btree_cursor_close(cur);
    // Store table stats
    TableStats *ts = &stats->table_stats[stats->table_stats_count++];
    strncpy(ts->table_name, schema->name, 63);
    ts->row_count = row_count;
    ts->page_count = page_count;
}
```
For index statistics, you scan the index B+tree and count distinct values. Because the index is sorted, distinct values are easy to count — every time the value changes from the previous entry, increment the counter:
```c
void analyze_index(Database *db, IndexSchema *idx_schema,
                    int64_t table_row_count, Statistics *stats) {
    BTree idx_tree = { .pool = db->pool, .root_page = idx_schema->root_page };
    BTreeCursor *cur = index_cursor_open(&idx_tree);
    int64_t distinct_count = 0;
    int64_t null_count = 0;
    ColumnValue prev_value;
    prev_value.type = COL_NULL;  // sentinel
    bool first = true;
    ColumnValue min_val, max_val;
    min_val.type = COL_NULL;
    max_val.type = COL_NULL;
    bool empty = btree_cursor_rewind((BTreeCursor*)cur);
    if (!empty) {
        do {
            ColumnValue entry_key;
            int64_t entry_rowid;
            index_cursor_read_entry(cur, &entry_key, &entry_rowid);
```
