COMMIT performs the final steps of the protocol: flush all dirty pages to the database, fsync the database, then delete the journal.
```c
int txn_commit(TransactionManager *txn, BufferPool *pool) {
    if (txn->state == TXN_NONE) {
        // No transaction active — nothing to do
        return 0;
    }
    if (txn->state == TXN_ACTIVE) {
        // Transaction started but no writes occurred — clean exit
        txn->state = TXN_NONE;
        return 0;
    }
    // state == TXN_DIRTY: we have a journal and modified pages
    // Step 1: Ensure all journal pages are durable
    fsync(txn->journal_fd);
    // Step 2: Flush all dirty pages from buffer pool to database file
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->pool_size; i++) {
        Frame *f = &pool->frames[i];
        if (f->page_id != INVALID_PAGE_ID && f->is_dirty) {
            // Journal should already contain this page's original data,
            // but verify just in case
            if (f->page_id < (page_id_t)txn->journaled_capacity &&
                !txn->journaled_pages[f->page_id]) {
                // This page was modified but not journaled — journal it now
                txn_journal_page(txn, pool, f->page_id);
                fsync(txn->journal_fd);
            }
            disk_write_page(pool, f->page_id, f->data);
            f->is_dirty = false;
        }
    }
    // Step 3: fsync the database file — all changes are now durable
    fsync(pool->fd);
    // Step 4: Delete the journal — THIS IS THE COMMIT POINT
    close(txn->journal_fd);
    txn->journal_fd = -1;
    unlink(txn->journal_path);
    // fsync the directory to ensure journal deletion is durable
    int dir_fd = open(".", O_RDONLY);
    if (dir_fd >= 0) {
        fsync(dir_fd);
        close(dir_fd);
    }
    // Clean up transaction state
    if (txn->journaled_pages) {
        free(txn->journaled_pages);
        txn->journaled_pages = NULL;
    }
    txn->journaled_capacity = 0;
    txn->journal_page_count = 0;
    txn->state = TXN_NONE;
    return 0;
}
```
The **commit point** — the precise instant when the transaction becomes permanent — is the `unlink(txn->journal_path)` call. Before that call, the journal exists, and crash recovery would roll back the transaction. After that call, the journal is gone, and the database file contains the committed data. This is a beautifully simple mechanism: the *existence* of a file is the commit/abort decision. No flags, no counters, no metadata to get out of sync. Just: does the journal file exist?
---
## ROLLBACK: Undoing Everything
Manual rollback reads the journal and restores every page to its original state:
```c
int txn_rollback(TransactionManager *txn, BufferPool *pool) {
    if (txn->state == TXN_NONE) {
        return 0;  // nothing to roll back
    }
    if (txn->state == TXN_ACTIVE) {
        // No writes occurred — just reset state
        txn->state = TXN_NONE;
        return 0;
    }
    // state == TXN_DIRTY: restore pages from journal
    // Step 1: Read journal and restore original pages
    int result = restore_from_journal(txn, pool);
    // Step 2: Discard all dirty pages in the buffer pool
    // (they contain modifications from the rolled-back transaction)
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->pool_size; i++) {
        Frame *f = &pool->frames[i];
        if (f->page_id != INVALID_PAGE_ID && f->is_dirty) {
            // Reload from disk (which now has restored data)
            disk_read_page(pool, f->page_id, f->data);
            f->is_dirty = false;
        }
    }
    // Step 3: Delete the journal
    close(txn->journal_fd);
    txn->journal_fd = -1;
    unlink(txn->journal_path);
    // Clean up
    if (txn->journaled_pages) {
        free(txn->journaled_pages);
        txn->journaled_pages = NULL;
    }
    txn->journaled_capacity = 0;
    txn->journal_page_count = 0;
    txn->state = TXN_NONE;
    return result;
}
```
The `restore_from_journal` function reads each page record from the journal and writes it back to the database file:
```c
static int restore_from_journal(TransactionManager *txn, BufferPool *pool) {
    // Seek to just past the header
    lseek(txn->journal_fd, JOURNAL_HEADER_SIZE, SEEK_SET);
    int restored_count = 0;
    uint8_t id_buf[4];
    uint8_t page_data[DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE];
    uint8_t cksum_buf[4];
    while (true) {
        // Read page_id
        ssize_t bytes = read(txn->journal_fd, id_buf, 4);
        if (bytes < 4) break;  // end of journal (or incomplete record)
        uint32_t page_id = read_be32(id_buf);
        // Read page data
        bytes = read(txn->journal_fd, page_data, pool->page_size);
        if (bytes < pool->page_size) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Incomplete journal record for page %u\n",
                    page_id);
            break;  // torn journal write — stop here
        }
        // Read and verify checksum
        bytes = read(txn->journal_fd, cksum_buf, 4);
        if (bytes < 4) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Incomplete checksum for page %u\n", page_id);
            break;
        }
        uint32_t stored_checksum = read_be32(cksum_buf);
        uint32_t computed_checksum = journal_checksum(page_data,
                                                        pool->page_size,
                                                        txn->checksum_init);
        if (stored_checksum != computed_checksum) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Checksum mismatch for page %u — "
                    "journal may be corrupt\n", page_id);
            break;  // stop processing — don't apply corrupt data
        }
        // Restore: write original page data back to database file
        off_t offset = (off_t)page_id * pool->page_size;
        pwrite(pool->fd, page_data, pool->page_size, offset);
        restored_count++;
    }
    // fsync the database to ensure restored pages are durable
    fsync(pool->fd);
    printf("Rolled back %d pages\n", restored_count);
    return 0;
}
```
The checksum verification on each page record is essential. If the system crashed while writing to the journal (between Step 1 and Step 3 of the protocol), the last journal record may be incomplete. The checksum detects this: if it doesn't match, we stop processing. All pages before the torn record were fully written and are safe to restore. The torn record's page was never written to the database (because the journal fsync didn't complete before the crash), so it doesn't need restoration.
---
## Crash Recovery: The Hot Journal

![Crash Recovery: Four Scenarios](./diagrams/diag-crash-recovery-scenarios.svg)

Crash recovery happens automatically at database open. If the database engine finds a journal file when opening the database, it knows the previous process crashed with an uncommitted transaction. The journal is called a **hot journal** — it's "hot" because it contains data that needs to be acted upon.
```c
int db_open_with_recovery(Database *db, const char *path) {
    // Open the database file
    db->pool = buffer_pool_create(path, DEFAULT_POOL_SIZE, DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE);
    if (!db->pool) return -1;
    // Initialize transaction manager
    txn_init(&db->txn, path);
    // Check for a hot journal
    char journal_path[256];
    snprintf(journal_path, sizeof(journal_path), "%s-journal", path);
    if (access(journal_path, F_OK) == 0) {
        // Hot journal found — perform crash recovery
        printf("Hot journal detected: %s\n", journal_path);
        int result = recover_hot_journal(db, journal_path);
        if (result != 0) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Crash recovery failed!\n");
            return -1;
        }
        printf("Crash recovery complete.\n");
    }
    // Load schema from sqlite_master
    catalog_load(&db->catalog, db->pool);
    catalog_load_indexes(&db->catalog, db->pool);
    return 0;
}
```
The recovery function:
```c
static int recover_hot_journal(Database *db, const char *journal_path) {
    int jfd = open(journal_path, O_RDONLY);
    if (jfd < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Cannot open journal: %s\n", strerror(errno));
        return -1;
    }
    // Read and validate journal header
    uint8_t header[JOURNAL_HEADER_SIZE];
    ssize_t bytes = read(jfd, header, JOURNAL_HEADER_SIZE);
    if (bytes < JOURNAL_HEADER_SIZE) {
        // Journal header incomplete — journal was being created when crash happened.
        // No pages were modified; safe to delete.
        close(jfd);
        unlink(journal_path);
        return 0;
    }
    // Verify magic number
    uint64_t magic = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
        magic = (magic << 8) | header[i];
    }
    if (magic != JOURNAL_MAGIC) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid journal magic number\n");
        close(jfd);
        unlink(journal_path);
        return -1;
    }
    // Read header fields
    uint32_t page_size = read_be32(header + 12);
    uint32_t db_page_count = read_be32(header + 20);
    uint32_t checksum_init = read_be32(header + 24);
    if (page_size != (uint32_t)db->pool->page_size) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Journal page size mismatch: %u vs %d\n",
                page_size, db->pool->page_size);
        close(jfd);
        return -1;
    }
    // Restore pages from journal
    int restored = 0;
    uint8_t id_buf[4];
    uint8_t page_data[DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE];
    uint8_t cksum_buf[4];
    while (true) {
        bytes = read(jfd, id_buf, 4);
        if (bytes < 4) break;
        uint32_t page_id = read_be32(id_buf);
        bytes = read(jfd, page_data, page_size);
        if (bytes < (ssize_t)page_size) break;
        bytes = read(jfd, cksum_buf, 4);
        if (bytes < 4) break;
        uint32_t stored_cksum = read_be32(cksum_buf);
        uint32_t computed_cksum = journal_checksum(page_data, page_size,
                                                     checksum_init);
        if (stored_cksum != computed_cksum) {
            // Torn journal write — stop processing
            break;
        }
        // Write original page back to database
        off_t offset = (off_t)page_id * page_size;
        pwrite(db->pool->fd, page_data, page_size, offset);
        restored++;
    }
    // Truncate database back to its original size
    // (in case the transaction allocated new pages)
    off_t original_size = (off_t)db_page_count * page_size;
    ftruncate(db->pool->fd, original_size);
    // fsync the database file
    fsync(db->pool->fd);
    // Delete the journal
    close(jfd);
    unlink(journal_path);
    // fsync the directory
    int dir_fd = open(".", O_RDONLY);
    if (dir_fd >= 0) {
        fsync(dir_fd);
        close(dir_fd);
    }
    printf("Recovered %d pages from hot journal\n", restored);
    return 0;
}
```
The `ftruncate` call is important. If the crashed transaction allocated new pages (via `buffer_pool_new_page` for B-tree splits), those pages extended the database file. Rolling back the transaction means those pages should not exist. Truncating the file to its pre-transaction size reclaims them.
### The Four Crash Scenarios
Let's trace what happens for a crash at each stage of the protocol:
**Scenario 1: Crash before journal is created (during Step 1).**
The journal file either doesn't exist or contains an incomplete header. Recovery deletes the partial journal. The database is untouched — no pages were modified. **Result: safe.**
**Scenario 2: Crash after journaling pages but before journal fsync (between Steps 2 and 3).**
The journal file contains page records, but they may not be durable — the OS write buffers might have only partially flushed them. The database file is untouched (no database writes have happened yet — write ordering prevents it). Recovery opens the journal. If checksum verification fails for any record, recovery stops at that point. Pages that were fully journaled *and* whose data is durable will be restored (they were already unchanged in the database, so restoring is a no-op). Pages with torn journal records are ignored — and since the database wasn't modified, there's nothing to fix. **Result: safe.**
**Scenario 3: Crash during database page writes (between Steps 4 and 5).**
Some database pages have new data, some have old data. The database file is inconsistent. The journal is complete and durable (Step 3 guaranteed this). Recovery reads the journal and restores all original pages. The database returns to its pre-transaction state. **Result: safe — this is the primary case the journal protects against.**
**Scenario 4: Crash during or after journal deletion (Step 6).**
The database file has been fully updated and fsynced (Step 5). If the journal deletion doesn't complete (the directory entry isn't durable), recovery finds the journal and "rolls back" — but since the database already contains the committed data, restoring original pages would revert the commit. This sounds dangerous, but consider: if the crash happened *during* the unlink, the database writes were already durable (Step 5 fsynced). The rollback would restore old pages, effectively un-committing the transaction. This is the one case where recovery is conservative — it prefers safety (rollback) over liveness (preserving the commit).
To handle Scenario 4 more precisely, SQLite writes the `page_count` field in the journal header as the *last* step before transitioning to commit. A journal with `page_count = -1` (the initial value) is treated differently from one with a valid page count. However, for your build, the conservative approach — always roll back if a journal exists — is correct and safe. The worst case is that a committed transaction's effects are lost on a crash that occurs in the narrow window between database fsync and journal deletion. This window is typically microseconds.
---
## Implicit Transactions (Autocommit)
When the user executes a DML statement without an explicit BEGIN, the engine wraps it in an **implicit transaction** — also called **autocommit mode**. Each statement is its own transaction:
```c
int execute_sql_with_txn(Database *db, const char *sql,
                           void (*callback)(Value *row, int ncols, void *ctx),
                           void *ctx) {
    bool implicit_txn = (db->txn.state == TXN_NONE);
    // For write operations without explicit BEGIN, start implicit transaction
    // (Detection: if the statement is INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/CREATE)
    // For simplicity, we start after parsing to check statement type
    // ... tokenize, parse ...
    if (implicit_txn && is_write_statement(ast)) {
        txn_begin(&db->txn, db->pool);
    }
    // ... compile, execute ...
    int rc = vm_execute(&vm);
    if (implicit_txn && db->txn.state != TXN_NONE) {
        if (rc == 0) {
            txn_commit(&db->txn, db->pool);
        } else {
            txn_rollback(&db->txn, db->pool);
        }
    }
    // ... cleanup ...
    return rc;
}
```
This ensures that every write operation is atomic — either it fully completes or it's fully rolled back. The user doesn't need to explicitly manage transactions for single statements.
---
## Compiling BEGIN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK
These are new SQL commands that the parser must recognize and the compiler must translate into VM opcodes:
```c
// New opcodes
OP_TRANSACTION,   // p1=0: BEGIN, p1=1: COMMIT, p1=2: ROLLBACK
// In the parser, add recognition for these keywords as statement types
// In the compiler:
static void compile_begin(Compiler *c) {
    emit(c, OP_TRANSACTION, 0, 0, 0, "BEGIN");
    emit(c, OP_HALT, 0, 0, 0, "Done");
}
static void compile_commit(Compiler *c) {
    emit(c, OP_TRANSACTION, 1, 0, 0, "COMMIT");
    emit(c, OP_HALT, 0, 0, 0, "Done");
}
static void compile_rollback(Compiler *c) {
    emit(c, OP_TRANSACTION, 2, 0, 0, "ROLLBACK");
    emit(c, OP_HALT, 0, 0, 0, "Done");
}
```
And in the VM:
```c
case OP_TRANSACTION: {
    int op_type = op->p1;
    int rc;
    switch (op_type) {
        case 0:  // BEGIN
            rc = txn_begin(&vm->db->txn, vm->db->pool);
            break;
        case 1:  // COMMIT
            rc = txn_commit(&vm->db->txn, vm->db->pool);
            break;
        case 2:  // ROLLBACK
            rc = txn_rollback(&vm->db->txn, vm->db->pool);
            break;
        default:
            rc = -1;
    }
    if (rc != 0) {
        vm->halted = true;
        vm->halt_code = rc;
        return rc;
    }
    break;
}
```
---
## Read Isolation: Invisible Uncommitted Writes
In a single-process, single-threaded database (like your build), read isolation is straightforward: there is only one connection, so uncommitted writes in the buffer pool are visible to all queries in the same connection. This is correct — within a transaction, you should see your own modifications.
For multiple connections (multiple processes sharing the same database file), the rollback journal provides basic isolation. Since uncommitted modifications exist only in one process's buffer pool (not yet flushed to the database file), other processes reading the database file see only committed data. If a process does flush dirty pages mid-transaction (during buffer pool eviction), other processes could see partial updates.
SQLite solves this with **file-level locking** — a SHARED lock for readers and an EXCLUSIVE lock for writers. When a writer holds the EXCLUSIVE lock, readers are blocked (in rollback journal mode). This is a simple but effective isolation mechanism:
```c
// Simplified file locking for read isolation
typedef enum {
    LOCK_NONE,
    LOCK_SHARED,      // for reading — multiple shared locks allowed
    LOCK_RESERVED,    // intent to write — one allowed, readers still OK
    LOCK_EXCLUSIVE,   // writing — blocks everyone
} LockLevel;
int acquire_lock(Database *db, LockLevel level) {
    struct flock fl;
    fl.l_whence = SEEK_SET;
    fl.l_start = 0;
    fl.l_len = 0;  // entire file
    switch (level) {
        case LOCK_SHARED:
            fl.l_type = F_RDLCK;
            break;
        case LOCK_EXCLUSIVE:
            fl.l_type = F_WRLCK;
            break;
        default:
            return -1;
    }
    if (fcntl(db->pool->fd, F_SETLK, &fl) < 0) {
        if (errno == EAGAIN || errno == EACCES) {
            return -1;  // lock busy — another connection holds it
        }
        return -1;
    }
    return 0;
}
void release_lock(Database *db) {
    struct flock fl;
    fl.l_type = F_UNLCK;
    fl.l_whence = SEEK_SET;
    fl.l_start = 0;
    fl.l_len = 0;
    fcntl(db->pool->fd, F_SETLK, &fl);
}
```
In the transaction lifecycle:
- `BEGIN` acquires a SHARED lock (allowing concurrent readers).
- The first write operation upgrades to EXCLUSIVE lock (blocking all other connections).
- `COMMIT` or `ROLLBACK` releases the lock.
This means that in rollback journal mode, **writers block readers and readers block writers.** This is the primary limitation that WAL mode (Milestone 10) addresses — WAL allows concurrent readers during writes.
---
## Testing: Verifying ACID Properties
### Test: BEGIN/COMMIT Persists Data
```c
void test_begin_commit(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_txn.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val TEXT);",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'hello');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 'world');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "COMMIT;", NULL, NULL);
    // Data should be visible
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT val FROM t ORDER BY id;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 2);
    assert(strcmp(rc.rows[0][0].s.data, "hello") == 0);
    assert(strcmp(rc.rows[1][0].s.data, "world") == 0);
    // Close and reopen — data should survive
    db_close(db);
    db = db_open("test_txn.db");
    ResultCollector rc2 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT val FROM t ORDER BY id;", collect_results, &rc2);
    assert(rc2.row_count == 2);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_txn.db");
    printf("PASS: BEGIN/COMMIT persists data\n");
}
```
### Test: ROLLBACK Undoes All Changes
```c
void test_rollback(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_rollback.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val INTEGER);",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 100);", NULL, NULL);
    // Start transaction, modify, then rollback
    execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "UPDATE t SET val = 999 WHERE id = 1;", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 200);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "ROLLBACK;", NULL, NULL);
    // Original data should be intact
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT id, val FROM t;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 1);       // row 2 was rolled back
    assert(rc.rows[0][1].i == 100);  // val not modified
    // Journal file should be deleted
    assert(access("test_rollback.db-journal", F_OK) != 0);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_rollback.db");
    printf("PASS: ROLLBACK undoes all changes\n");
}
```
### Test: Journal File Exists During Transaction
```c
void test_journal_exists_during_txn(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_journal.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY);", NULL, NULL);
    // No journal before transaction
    assert(access("test_journal.db-journal", F_OK) != 0);
    execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1);", NULL, NULL);
    // Journal should exist now
    assert(access("test_journal.db-journal", F_OK) == 0);
    execute_sql(db, "COMMIT;", NULL, NULL);
    // Journal should be deleted after commit
    assert(access("test_journal.db-journal", F_OK) != 0);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_journal.db");
    printf("PASS: journal exists during transaction, deleted after\n");
}
```
### Test: Journal Contains Original Page Data
```c
void test_journal_contains_original_data(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_jdata.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val INTEGER);",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 42);", NULL, NULL);
    // Read page 2 (first table page) directly from disk
    uint8_t original_page[DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE];
    pread(db->pool->fd, original_page, DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE,
          2 * DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE);
    // Start transaction and modify
    execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "UPDATE t SET val = 999 WHERE id = 1;", NULL, NULL);
    // Force a flush to trigger journaling
    buffer_pool_flush_all(db->pool);
    // Read the journal and verify it contains the original page data
    int jfd = open("test_jdata.db-journal", O_RDONLY);
    assert(jfd >= 0);
    uint8_t header[JOURNAL_HEADER_SIZE];
    read(jfd, header, JOURNAL_HEADER_SIZE);
    // Read first page record
    uint8_t id_buf[4];
    read(jfd, id_buf, 4);
    uint8_t journal_page[DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE];
    read(jfd, journal_page, DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE);
    // The journal page should match the original (pre-update) data
    assert(memcmp(journal_page, original_page, DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE) == 0);
    close(jfd);
    execute_sql(db, "ROLLBACK;", NULL, NULL);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_jdata.db");
    printf("PASS: journal contains original page data\n");
}
```
### Test: Crash Recovery (Simulated Hot Journal)
```c
void test_crash_recovery(void) {
    // Phase 1: Create a database and simulate a crash
    {
        Database *db = db_open("test_crash.db");
        execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                        "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
        execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 100);", NULL, NULL);
        // Start a transaction and modify data
        execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
        execute_sql(db, "UPDATE t SET val = 999 WHERE id = 1;", NULL, NULL);
        execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 200);", NULL, NULL);
        // Force flush to write journal pages
        // (this writes journal + potentially database pages)
        buffer_pool_flush_all(db->pool);
        // SIMULATE CRASH: close without commit or rollback
        // Don't call db_close (which would commit/rollback) —
        // just close file descriptors directly
        close(db->pool->fd);
        if (db->txn.journal_fd >= 0) {
            close(db->txn.journal_fd);
        }
        // Don't free — we're simulating a crash, memory is lost
    }
    // Phase 2: Reopen — should detect hot journal and recover
    {
        Database *db = db_open("test_crash.db");
        // db_open should have detected and rolled back the hot journal
        // Verify data is in pre-transaction state
        ResultCollector rc = {0};
        execute_sql(db, "SELECT id, val FROM t;", collect_results, &rc);
        assert(rc.row_count == 1);       // row 2 was rolled back
        assert(rc.rows[0][0].i == 1);
        assert(rc.rows[0][1].i == 100);  // original value, not 999
        // Journal should be cleaned up
        assert(access("test_crash.db-journal", F_OK) != 0);
        db_close(db);
    }
    unlink("test_crash.db");
    printf("PASS: crash recovery via hot journal\n");
}
```
### Test: fsync Ordering (Write-Ordering Guarantee)
```c
void test_write_ordering(void) {
    // This test verifies the structural invariant:
    // journal fsync happens BEFORE any database page write.
    // We instrument the buffer pool to track operation order.
    Database *db = db_open("test_order.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    // Insert enough data to require multiple pages
    execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
    for (int i = 1; i <= 200; i++) {
        char sql[128];
        snprintf(sql, sizeof(sql), "INSERT INTO t VALUES (%d, %d);",
                 i, i * 10);
        execute_sql(db, sql, NULL, NULL);
    }
    // At this point, the transaction manager should have the journal
    // in TXN_DIRTY state
    assert(db->txn.state == TXN_DIRTY);
    assert(db->txn.journal_page_count > 0);
    execute_sql(db, "COMMIT;", NULL, NULL);
    assert(db->txn.state == TXN_NONE);
    // Verify all data persists
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t;", collect_results, &rc);
    // (Assumes COUNT is implemented; otherwise just scan)
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_order.db");
    printf("PASS: write ordering maintained during commit\n");
}
```
### Test: Transaction State Machine
```c
void test_txn_state_machine(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_state.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY);", NULL, NULL);
    // Initial state: no transaction
    assert(db->txn.state == TXN_NONE);
    // BEGIN → ACTIVE
    execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
    assert(db->txn.state == TXN_ACTIVE);
    // Write → DIRTY
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1);", NULL, NULL);
    assert(db->txn.state == TXN_DIRTY);
    // COMMIT → NONE
    execute_sql(db, "COMMIT;", NULL, NULL);
    assert(db->txn.state == TXN_NONE);
    // BEGIN + ROLLBACK
    execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
    assert(db->txn.state == TXN_ACTIVE);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2);", NULL, NULL);
    assert(db->txn.state == TXN_DIRTY);
    execute_sql(db, "ROLLBACK;", NULL, NULL);
    assert(db->txn.state == TXN_NONE);
    // Double BEGIN should fail
    execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
    int rc = execute_sql(db, "BEGIN;", NULL, NULL);
    assert(rc != 0);  // error: transaction already active
    execute_sql(db, "ROLLBACK;", NULL, NULL);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_state.db");
    printf("PASS: transaction state machine\n");
}
```
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens During a Transaction
**Level 1 — Public API**: The user executes `BEGIN`, then `INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'hello')`, then `COMMIT`. The INSERT returns success, the COMMIT returns success, and the data is durable. If they'd called `ROLLBACK` instead, the INSERT would be undone.
**Level 2 — Transaction Engine**: `BEGIN` sets state to `TXN_ACTIVE`. The INSERT triggers the B-tree insert path, which modifies pages in the buffer pool and marks them dirty. The first dirty page triggers journal creation: the `.db-journal` file is created, the header is written and fsynced, and the state moves to `TXN_DIRTY`. Before the buffer pool writes any modified page to disk, the transaction manager reads the *original* page from the database file and appends it to the journal. `COMMIT` fsyncs the journal (ensuring all original pages are durable), flushes all dirty pages to the database file, fsyncs the database, then deletes the journal. The journal deletion is the commit point — the instant the transaction becomes permanent.
**Level 3 — OS and Hardware**: The journal `write()` calls copy data from user space into the kernel's page cache. The `fsync()` on the journal triggers a VFS (Virtual File System) flush that walks the file's dirty pages in the page cache, submitting them as block I/O requests to the storage device. On NVMe SSD, each 4KB write request takes ~20-50μs for the controller to write to NAND flash. The `fsync()` doesn't return until the controller confirms all writes are in persistent storage (or at least in a battery-backed write cache — 
> **🔑 Foundation: disk write barriers and battery-backed caches**
> 
> ## Disk Write Barriers and Battery-Backed Caches
**What it is:** Modern storage devices have their own internal volatile write caches (DRAM buffers on SSDs, track buffers on HDDs). When the OS sends a write command, the drive may report "done!" while the data is still in this volatile cache — not yet on the persistent medium. A **disk write barrier** (also called a cache flush or FUA — Force Unit Access) is a command the OS sends to the drive saying: "Actually flush your internal cache to stable media before acknowledging."
When you call `fsync`, the kernel typically issues a write barrier / cache flush to the device. Without this, `fsync` would be meaningless — data would still be in a volatile drive cache that dies on power loss.
Here's where **battery-backed write caches (BBWCs)** change the equation. Enterprise RAID controllers and some SSDs include a battery or capacitor that keeps the volatile cache powered during an outage, giving the device time to flush to persistent storage. With a BBWC:
- The drive cache is effectively **non-volatile** (it survives power loss)
- Write barriers can be safely **disabled** because cached-but-unwritten data won't be lost
- This dramatically improves write throughput — sometimes 10x — because the drive doesn't have to wait for slow media commits
The practical matrix looks like this:
| Configuration | Write barrier needed? | Performance |
|---|---|---|
| No battery, cache enabled, no barriers | ❌ **Data loss risk** | Fast but unsafe |
| No battery, barriers enabled | ✅ Safe | Slower |
| Battery-backed cache, barriers disabled | ✅ Safe | Fast |
**Why you need this now:** When you test your storage engine's durability, the behavior you see depends on hardware you might not control. On a developer laptop with no BBWC, disabling write barriers means your "durable" commits aren't durable. On a production server with BBWC, being unaware of this means you might be leaving significant performance on the table, or worse, making incorrect assumptions about what `fsync` actually guarantees on that hardware.
**Key insight:** `fsync` is a contract between your application and the kernel, but **the kernel's contract with the disk depends on hardware**. Battery-backed caches turn volatile caches into durable ones, making barriers unnecessary — but you must *know* what's underneath you. Your storage engine should never assume; ideally, document or detect the durability guarantees of the target environment.
). The database `write()` calls then follow the same path. The final `unlink()` removes the directory entry — itself requiring a metadata write and potentially an fsync on the directory's inode.
---
## The ACID Properties — How the Journal Delivers Each One
Now that you've built the mechanism, let's verify that it actually delivers the four ACID guarantees:
**Atomicity (All or Nothing):** The rollback journal ensures that if a transaction fails partway through — due to a crash, an error, or an explicit ROLLBACK — *all* modifications are undone. The journal contains the original version of every modified page. Recovery restores all of them. There is no state where "some INSERTs succeeded and others didn't" — either they all succeed (COMMIT deletes the journal) or they all fail (journal exists → recovery restores original pages).
**Consistency (Constraints Always Hold):** Within a transaction, constraint checks (NOT NULL, UNIQUE) are enforced before any modification commits. If a constraint fails, the transaction can be rolled back. The rollback journal ensures that the database file on disk always represents a consistent state — either the pre-transaction state or the fully-committed post-transaction state. The B-tree invariants (balanced, ordered, half-full) are maintained because either all page modifications from a split operation commit together or none do.
**Isolation (Concurrent Transactions Don't Interfere):** In rollback journal mode with file locking, a writer holds an EXCLUSIVE lock that prevents other connections from reading or writing the database. This is the simplest form of isolation — serialized access. It's correct but limits concurrency. WAL mode (Milestone 10) will provide snapshot isolation, where readers see a consistent database state without blocking writers.
**Durability (Committed Data Survives Crashes):** The write ordering protocol guarantees that by the time the journal is deleted (commit point), all modified pages have been fsynced to the database file. If the system crashes after commit, the database file contains all committed data. If it crashes before commit, the journal enables recovery to the pre-transaction state.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What the Rollback Journal Unlocks
**→ Write Ordering as a Universal Durability Pattern.** The before-image journaling protocol you just built is not unique to databases. **NTFS** (Windows' file system) uses a log that records metadata changes before applying them to the file system structure — structurally identical to your rollback journal. **ext4** with `data=journal` mode writes both data and metadata to its journal before committing them to the main file system. **ZFS** takes a different approach — copy-on-write — which never overwrites data in place, instead writing new data to a new location and atomically updating a pointer. All three systems solve the same fundamental problem: making a multi-step modification appear atomic on a storage medium that can only write one sector at a time. Understanding your rollback journal means you understand the core mechanism behind every journaling file system. The specific data structures differ; the write-ordering invariant is identical.
**→ Undo vs Redo Logging — The Full Spectrum of Recovery.** Your rollback journal is an **undo log** — it stores *old values* (before-images) so you can undo modifications. The WAL you'll build in Milestone 10 is a **redo log** — it stores *new values* (after-images) so you can redo modifications that may not have reached the database file. These are dual strategies:
| Property | Undo Log (Rollback Journal) | Redo Log (WAL) |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------|
| Stores | Old page contents | New page contents |
| On crash | Restore old pages | Replay new pages |
| Commit requires | Flush modified pages to DB, then delete journal | Append commit record to log |
| Write pattern | Random (database pages written in-place) | Sequential (log is append-only) |
| Concurrency | Writers block readers | Writers don't block readers |
| Used by | SQLite (journal mode), Oracle (undo tablespace) | SQLite (WAL mode), PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB |
The ARIES algorithm (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics), used by most modern databases, combines *both* — it uses a redo log for normal operation and undo information for rolling back incomplete transactions during crash recovery. Understanding your undo-only journal and the redo-only WAL gives you both halves of ARIES.
**→ Two-Phase Commit Similarity.** The rollback journal protocol has a two-phase structure that mirrors the **two-phase commit (2PC)** protocol in distributed systems. The journal write + fsync is the **prepare phase** — you've recorded enough information to either commit or abort. The database write + fsync is the **commit phase** — you've made the changes permanent. The journal deletion is the **acknowledgment**. In distributed 2PC, a coordinator asks all participants to prepare (lock resources, write to local logs), then asks them all to commit. If any participant fails to prepare, everyone aborts. The structure is identical: prepare → commit → cleanup. This same two-phase pattern appears in double-checked locking (check → lock → check again → proceed), in software transactional memory (log → validate → commit), and even in everyday life (a contract is drafted, reviewed, then signed — not signed then reviewed).
**→ Torn Page Detection — The Same Problem at Every Layer.** The torn page problem you solved with journal checksums appears at every level of the storage stack. **SSD firmware** must handle interrupted program operations — if power fails while writing a NAND page, the flash translation layer must detect and handle the partial write. **RAID controllers** face the same issue with stripe writes — a torn stripe leaves the parity data inconsistent with the data blocks. **Distributed systems** handle partial message delivery — a message that's half-received is detected via checksums or sequence numbers and either retransmitted or discarded. **TCP** checksums detect corruption in transit. The universal pattern: **any system that writes data in chunks larger than the hardware's atomic write unit must have a detection mechanism for partial writes.** Your journal checksum is one instance of this universal pattern.
**→ fsync as a Trust Boundary.** Your entire transaction protocol depends on `fsync` actually doing what it claims: making data durable on persistent storage. Everything *above* fsync in the software stack — your buffer pool, your transaction manager, your application — assumes durability after fsync returns. Everything *below* fsync — the disk firmware, the disk controller, the physical media — is trusted to deliver on that promise. But this trust can be violated. Some disk drives lie about fsync — they report success but keep data in a volatile write cache. Enterprise drives with battery-backed write caches are safe (the cache survives power loss). Consumer drives may not be. Some file systems on Linux (`ext3` in `data=writeback` mode) may not flush all relevant data on fsync. This is a **trust boundary** — the same concept used in security analysis. Everything inside the trust boundary is assumed correct; threats come from outside it. In your database, fsync is the trust boundary between "I guarantee durability" and "I trust the hardware to be durable." The same pattern appears in TLS (you trust the certificate authority), in sandboxed code execution (you trust the sandbox), and in any system with an explicit boundary between "verified" and "assumed."
---
## System Awareness
You've built the transaction layer — the component that transforms your database from a data structure that can be corrupted by any unexpected crash into a system that guarantees ACID properties. It sits between the VM (which executes DML operations that modify pages) and the buffer pool (which manages the pages in memory and on disk). The transaction manager intercepts every page write, ensuring the original contents are journaled before any modification reaches the database file.

![EXPLAIN Output Anatomy](./diagrams/diag-explain-output.svg)

Without the transaction layer, a power failure during a multi-index INSERT could leave the table and indexes in an inconsistent state — some reflecting the insert, others not. With it, the INSERT either fully commits (all indexes updated, all pages consistent) or fully rolls back (everything restored to the pre-INSERT state). The user never sees a half-completed operation.
The rollback journal has one fundamental limitation: **writers block readers.** During a write transaction, no other connection can read the database. This is because the database file might contain partially-written pages (between journal fsync and database fsync), and a reader at that moment would see inconsistent data. In the next milestone (WAL Mode), you'll build an alternative that eliminates this limitation. WAL inverts the journal's strategy: instead of recording old data and writing new data in place, it writes new data to a log and leaves the original database untouched. Readers can always read the original database file safely — they never see partial writes because the original pages are never modified until checkpoint.
---
<!-- END_MS -->


<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m10 -->
<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m10 -->
# Milestone 10: WAL Mode
## Where We Are in the System
In Milestone 9, you built ACID transactions using a rollback journal. The journal records original page contents *before* modification, and crash recovery restores those originals if the transaction never completes. It works. It's correct. And it has a fundamental limitation that you've likely already felt: **writers block readers, and readers block writers.**

![SQLite Engine — Full System Architecture Map](./diagrams/diag-satellite-system-map.svg)

When a write transaction holds the EXCLUSIVE lock in rollback journal mode, every other connection — even those that only want to *read* — must wait. For a single-user embedded database, this is tolerable. For a web application serving 50 concurrent readers while one background process ingests data, it's a bottleneck that no amount of hardware can fix. The lock is the serialization point, and Amdahl's law dictates that no matter how fast you make the individual operations, the serial portion limits total throughput.

![Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page](./diagrams/diag-data-flow-sql-to-disk.svg)

This milestone builds **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** — an alternative transaction mechanism that inverts the rollback journal's strategy and eliminates the reader-writer conflict entirely. WAL is not a minor tweak. It is a fundamentally different durability architecture that trades one set of problems for another. Understanding *which* problems, and *why*, is what this milestone teaches.
---
## The Revelation: WAL Is Not "A Better Rollback Journal"
You probably think WAL is simply an upgrade — the rollback journal, but faster and with better concurrency. A drop-in replacement with no downsides. Everyone says "use WAL mode" as if it's a setting you flip and forget.
This understanding is wrong, and it will mislead your architectural decisions.
The rollback journal and WAL are not two implementations of the same strategy. They are **inverted strategies** that make opposite tradeoffs. Let me show you concretely.

![WAL vs Rollback Journal: Inverted Strategies](./diagrams/diag-wal-vs-journal.svg)

**Rollback journal mode:**
1. Before modifying page 42, copy the *original* page 42 into the journal file. (Undo log — stores old data.)
2. Modify page 42 *in the database file* directly.
3. On commit: delete the journal. The database file already contains the new data.
4. On crash: restore original pages from the journal. The database reverts to its pre-transaction state.
**WAL mode:**
1. Instead of modifying page 42 in the database file, append the *new version* of page 42 to the WAL file. (Redo log — stores new data.)
2. The database file is **never modified** during normal operations. It remains frozen at whatever state the last checkpoint left it in.
3. On commit: append a commit frame to the WAL. The database file is still unchanged.
4. On crash: ignore uncommitted WAL frames. Committed frames are replayed into the database during the next checkpoint.
The inversion is total. The journal writes old data and modifies the database in-place. WAL writes new data and leaves the database untouched. This one structural difference cascades into every property of the system:
| Property | Rollback Journal | WAL |
|----------|-----------------|-----|
| What's written during transaction | Old pages (to journal) + new pages (to database) | New pages (to WAL only) |
| Database file during transaction | Modified (potentially inconsistent) | Untouched (always consistent) |
| Readers during write | **Blocked** (database file is inconsistent) | **Not blocked** (database file is always readable) |
| Commit mechanism | Delete journal file | Append commit marker to WAL |
| Recovery mechanism | Restore original pages from journal (undo) | Replay committed WAL frames (redo) |
| Write I/O pattern | Random (database pages scattered across file) | Sequential (WAL is append-only) |
| File growth | Journal is temporary; deleted on commit | WAL grows unboundedly without checkpointing |
| Network filesystem support | ✅ Works (only needs file locking) | ❌ Fails (needs shared memory for WAL index) |
The concurrent reader support — the headline feature — is a direct consequence of the inversion. In journal mode, readers can't read the database file mid-transaction because it contains half-written pages. In WAL mode, the database file is *never* modified during a transaction, so it's always in a consistent state. Readers can read it at any time. They see a frozen snapshot — the database as of the last checkpoint, plus any committed WAL frames that existed when the reader started.
But WAL introduces problems the journal doesn't have:
**Problem 1: Unbounded WAL growth.** Every write appends to the WAL. If you insert 10 million rows, the WAL contains 10 million rows' worth of page frames — potentially gigabytes. The rollback journal is temporary; it's deleted on commit. The WAL persists until a checkpoint copies its frames into the database file. Without checkpointing, the WAL grows forever.
**Problem 2: Checkpoint I/O bursts.** When a checkpoint runs, it must copy potentially thousands of modified pages from the WAL back into the database file. This is a burst of random I/O (the database file has pages scattered across its address space) that can cause latency spikes. The rollback journal spreads this cost across the transaction; WAL concentrates it at checkpoint time.
**Problem 3: Long-running readers pin WAL frames.** A reader that started 10 minutes ago sees the database as of its start time. All WAL frames older than that reader's snapshot must be preserved — even if they've already been checkpointed — because the reader might still need them. One stale reader can prevent the entire WAL from being truncated, causing unbounded growth even with checkpointing enabled.
**Problem 4: Shared memory requirement.** Readers need to know which WAL frames contain the most recent version of each page. This lookup structure (the "WAL index" or "wal-index") is stored in shared memory (`mmap`'d from a shared-memory file). This means WAL mode doesn't work on network file systems (NFS, CIFS) where shared memory semantics aren't reliable.
Each mode has *fundamental* tradeoffs, not just performance differences. WAL is better for read-heavy workloads with occasional writes. Rollback journal is better for write-heavy workloads, single-connection scenarios, and environments without shared memory support. Choosing between them is an architectural decision, not a tuning knob.
---
## WAL File Format

![WAL Frame Format](./diagrams/diag-wal-frame-format.svg)

The WAL file is a sequential log of **frames**, each containing one modified page. The file starts with a header, followed by frames in append order.
### WAL Header
```c
#define WAL_MAGIC 0x377F0682   // big-endian magic (SQLite's actual value)
#define WAL_MAGIC_LE 0x377F0683 // little-endian variant
typedef struct {
    uint32_t magic;            // WAL_MAGIC or WAL_MAGIC_LE
    uint32_t format_version;   // file format version (3007000 for SQLite 3.7+)
    uint32_t page_size;        // database page size (4096)
    uint32_t checkpoint_seq;   // checkpoint sequence number
    uint32_t salt1;            // random salt value 1 (changes each transaction)
    uint32_t salt2;            // random salt value 2
    uint32_t checksum1;        // cumulative checksum part 1
    uint32_t checksum2;        // cumulative checksum part 2
} WalHeader;
#define WAL_HEADER_SIZE 32
```
The **salt values** serve a critical purpose: they change with each write transaction, which means frames from different transactions have different salt values. During recovery, if you encounter a frame with a salt that doesn't match the header's salt, you know you've reached frames from a prior (already committed or rolled-back) transaction cycle — and you stop processing. The salts also feed into the checksum calculation, binding each frame's integrity check to the specific transaction that wrote it.
### WAL Frame
Each frame contains a 24-byte frame header followed by the page data:
```c
typedef struct {
    uint32_t page_id;          // which database page this frame contains
    uint32_t db_size;          // size of database in pages after this commit
                               // (non-zero only for commit frames; 0 otherwise)
    uint32_t salt1;            // must match WAL header salt1
    uint32_t salt2;            // must match WAL header salt2
    uint32_t checksum1;        // cumulative checksum part 1
    uint32_t checksum2;        // cumulative checksum part 2
} WalFrameHeader;
#define WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE 24
```
The **db_size field** is the commit marker. During a transaction, frames are appended with `db_size = 0`. The final frame of a committed transaction has `db_size` set to the total number of pages in the database. This is how recovery distinguishes committed transactions from incomplete ones: scan the WAL, and the last frame with a non-zero `db_size` marks the end of the last committed transaction. Everything after that is uncommitted and should be ignored.
```c
typedef struct {
    WalHeader header;
    int       fd;              // file descriptor for the WAL file
    char      path[256];       // path to WAL file (<db>.db-wal)
    int       frame_count;     // total frames in the WAL
    uint32_t  checksum1;       // running checksum state
    uint32_t  checksum2;
    // WAL index (in-memory: page_id → frame_number mapping)
    int      *wal_index;       // array: wal_index[page_id] = latest frame# for this page
    int       wal_index_size;  // capacity of wal_index array
    // Snapshot management
    int       min_reader_frame;// oldest frame any active reader needs
    int       checkpoint_seq;  // checkpoint sequence counter
} Wal;
```
### Checksums: Cumulative Integrity
WAL checksums are **cumulative** — each frame's checksum incorporates the previous frame's checksum, creating a chain. If any frame in the chain is corrupted or missing, every subsequent checksum fails. This means you can detect the exact point where corruption begins.
```c
// WAL checksum function — processes data in 8-byte chunks
// This matches SQLite's actual WAL checksum algorithm
static void wal_checksum(int is_native_byte_order,
                          const uint8_t *data, int len,
                          uint32_t *cksum1, uint32_t *cksum2) {
    uint32_t s1 = *cksum1;
    uint32_t s2 = *cksum2;
    const uint32_t *words = (const uint32_t *)data;
    int n_words = len / 4;
    for (int i = 0; i < n_words; i += 2) {
        uint32_t d0, d1;
        if (is_native_byte_order) {
            d0 = words[i];
            d1 = words[i + 1];
        } else {
            // Byte-swap for non-native order
            d0 = __builtin_bswap32(words[i]);
            d1 = __builtin_bswap32(words[i + 1]);
        }
        s1 += d0 + s2;
        s2 += d1 + s1;
    }
    *cksum1 = s1;
    *cksum2 = s2;
}
```
The cumulative checksum chain means:
- **Frame 1's checksum** covers the WAL header + frame 1's header + frame 1's data.
- **Frame 2's checksum** starts from frame 1's checksum, then incorporates frame 2's header + data.
- If frame 3 is torn (partial write), frame 3's checksum won't match — and recovery knows to stop at frame 2.
This is more robust than per-frame independent checksums. With independent checksums, a torn write that happens to produce a valid checksum (extremely unlikely but not impossible) would go undetected. With cumulative checksums, the chain guarantees that any corruption anywhere invalidates everything after it.
---
## WAL Architecture

![WAL Mode Architecture](./diagrams/diag-wal-architecture.svg)

The WAL system consists of three files and one shared-memory region:
1. **Database file** (`<name>.db`) — The main database. In WAL mode, this file is only modified during checkpoints.
2. **WAL file** (`<name>.db-wal`) — The append-only log of modified pages.
3. **Shared memory file** (`<name>.db-shm`) — Memory-mapped file containing the WAL index, used to coordinate between multiple reader connections.
4. **WAL index** (in shared memory) — A hash table mapping `page_id → most_recent_frame_number`, enabling O(1) page version lookup.
For your single-process build, the WAL index can be a simple in-memory data structure rather than a shared-memory-mapped file. The shared memory mechanism is needed for multi-process coordination; within a single process, a regular array works identically.
### Initialization
```c
int wal_open(Wal *wal, const char *db_path, int page_size) {
    snprintf(wal->path, sizeof(wal->path), "%s-wal", db_path);
    // Check if a WAL file already exists (from a previous crash or session)
    bool wal_exists = (access(wal->path, F_OK) == 0);
    if (wal_exists) {
        // Open existing WAL for recovery
        wal->fd = open(wal->path, O_RDWR, 0644);
        if (wal->fd < 0) return -1;
        // Read and validate header
        uint8_t hdr_buf[WAL_HEADER_SIZE];
        if (read(wal->fd, hdr_buf, WAL_HEADER_SIZE) < WAL_HEADER_SIZE) {
            close(wal->fd);
            wal->fd = -1;
            unlink(wal->path);
            wal_exists = false;
        } else {
            wal->header.magic = read_be32(hdr_buf);
            wal->header.page_size = read_be32(hdr_buf + 8);
            wal->header.checkpoint_seq = read_be32(hdr_buf + 12);
            wal->header.salt1 = read_be32(hdr_buf + 16);
            wal->header.salt2 = read_be32(hdr_buf + 20);
            if (wal->header.magic != WAL_MAGIC ||
                wal->header.page_size != (uint32_t)page_size) {
                close(wal->fd);
                wal->fd = -1;
                unlink(wal->path);
                wal_exists = false;
            }
        }
    }
    if (!wal_exists) {
        // Create a new WAL file
        wal->fd = open(wal->path, O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
        if (wal->fd < 0) return -1;
        // Initialize header
        wal->header.magic = WAL_MAGIC;
        wal->header.format_version = 3007000;
        wal->header.page_size = page_size;
        wal->header.checkpoint_seq = 0;
        wal->header.salt1 = (uint32_t)time(NULL);
        wal->header.salt2 = (uint32_t)getpid() ^ (uint32_t)time(NULL);
        // Compute header checksum
        wal->checksum1 = 0;
        wal->checksum2 = 0;
        uint8_t hdr_buf[WAL_HEADER_SIZE];
        write_wal_header(hdr_buf, &wal->header);
        wal_checksum(1, hdr_buf, WAL_HEADER_SIZE,
                     &wal->checksum1, &wal->checksum2);
        wal->header.checksum1 = wal->checksum1;
        wal->header.checksum2 = wal->checksum2;
        write(wal->fd, hdr_buf, WAL_HEADER_SIZE);
        fsync(wal->fd);
    }
    wal->frame_count = 0;
    // Initialize WAL index
    wal->wal_index_size = 4096;  // initial capacity for page IDs
    wal->wal_index = calloc(wal->wal_index_size, sizeof(int));
    // -1 means "no frame for this page"
    for (int i = 0; i < wal->wal_index_size; i++) {
        wal->wal_index[i] = -1;
    }
    wal->min_reader_frame = 0;
    if (wal_exists) {
        // Recover: scan WAL to rebuild the index
        wal_recover(wal, page_size);
    }
    return 0;
}
```
### WAL Recovery
When the database opens and finds an existing WAL file, it must scan the WAL to determine which frames belong to committed transactions. Only committed frames are valid; uncommitted frames at the tail are discarded.
```c
static int wal_recover(Wal *wal, int page_size) {
    lseek(wal->fd, WAL_HEADER_SIZE, SEEK_SET);
    uint32_t running_cksum1 = wal->header.checksum1;
    uint32_t running_cksum2 = wal->header.checksum2;
    int frame_num = 0;
    int last_commit_frame = -1;
    uint32_t last_commit_db_size = 0;
    uint8_t frame_hdr[WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE];
    uint8_t *page_data = malloc(page_size);
    while (1) {
        // Read frame header
        ssize_t bytes = read(wal->fd, frame_hdr, WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE);
        if (bytes < WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE) break;
        // Read page data
        bytes = read(wal->fd, page_data, page_size);
        if (bytes < page_size) break;
        // Verify salts
        uint32_t frame_salt1 = read_be32(frame_hdr + 8);
        uint32_t frame_salt2 = read_be32(frame_hdr + 12);
        if (frame_salt1 != wal->header.salt1 ||
            frame_salt2 != wal->header.salt2) {
            break;  // different transaction cycle — stop
        }
        // Verify cumulative checksum
        uint32_t expected_cksum1 = running_cksum1;
        uint32_t expected_cksum2 = running_cksum2;
        wal_checksum(1, frame_hdr, 8, &expected_cksum1, &expected_cksum2);
        wal_checksum(1, page_data, page_size,
                     &expected_cksum1, &expected_cksum2);
        uint32_t stored_cksum1 = read_be32(frame_hdr + 16);
        uint32_t stored_cksum2 = read_be32(frame_hdr + 20);
        if (stored_cksum1 != expected_cksum1 ||
            stored_cksum2 != expected_cksum2) {
            break;  // checksum mismatch — torn write
        }
        running_cksum1 = expected_cksum1;
        running_cksum2 = expected_cksum2;
        // Valid frame — update WAL index
        uint32_t page_id = read_be32(frame_hdr);
        wal_index_set(wal, page_id, frame_num);
        frame_num++;
        // Check for commit marker
        uint32_t db_size = read_be32(frame_hdr + 4);
        if (db_size > 0) {
            last_commit_frame = frame_num - 1;
            last_commit_db_size = db_size;
        }
    }
    free(page_data);
    // Discard any frames after the last commit
    if (last_commit_frame >= 0 && frame_num > last_commit_frame + 1) {
        // Truncate WAL to just past the last committed frame
        off_t valid_size = WAL_HEADER_SIZE +
            (off_t)(last_commit_frame + 1) * (WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE + page_size);
        ftruncate(wal->fd, valid_size);
        // Rebuild index considering only committed frames
        for (int i = 0; i < wal->wal_index_size; i++) {
            if (wal->wal_index[i] > last_commit_frame) {
                wal->wal_index[i] = -1;
            }
        }
        frame_num = last_commit_frame + 1;
    }
    wal->frame_count = frame_num;
    wal->checksum1 = running_cksum1;
    wal->checksum2 = running_cksum2;
    printf("WAL recovery: %d valid frames, last commit at frame %d\n",
           frame_num, last_commit_frame);
    return 0;
}
```
### WAL Index: O(1) Page Lookup
The WAL index maps page IDs to the most recent frame number containing that page. When the buffer pool needs page 42, it first checks the WAL index: "Is there a WAL frame for page 42?" If yes, read from the WAL. If no, read from the database file.
```c
static void wal_index_set(Wal *wal, uint32_t page_id, int frame_num) {
    if (page_id >= (uint32_t)wal->wal_index_size) {
        int new_size = (int)page_id + 1024;
        wal->wal_index = realloc(wal->wal_index, new_size * sizeof(int));
        for (int i = wal->wal_index_size; i < new_size; i++) {
            wal->wal_index[i] = -1;
        }
        wal->wal_index_size = new_size;
    }
    wal->wal_index[page_id] = frame_num;
}
static int wal_index_get(Wal *wal, uint32_t page_id, int max_frame) {
    if (page_id >= (uint32_t)wal->wal_index_size) return -1;
    int frame = wal->wal_index[page_id];
    if (frame < 0 || frame > max_frame) return -1;
    return frame;
}
```
The `max_frame` parameter is what enables snapshot isolation — each reader has a `max_frame` value recording the latest committed frame when it started. Even if new frames are appended after the reader begins, the reader only sees frames up to its `max_frame`.
---
## The Write Path: Appending to the WAL

![INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE Write Path](./diagrams/diag-dml-write-path.svg)

When the buffer pool needs to "write" a dirty page in WAL mode, it doesn't write to the database file. Instead, it appends a frame to the WAL:
```c
int wal_write_frame(Wal *wal, uint32_t page_id, const uint8_t *page_data,
                     int page_size, bool is_commit, uint32_t db_size_if_commit) {
    // Build frame header
    uint8_t frame_hdr[WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE];
    write_be32(frame_hdr, page_id);
    write_be32(frame_hdr + 4, is_commit ? db_size_if_commit : 0);
    write_be32(frame_hdr + 8, wal->header.salt1);
    write_be32(frame_hdr + 12, wal->header.salt2);
    // Compute cumulative checksum
    uint32_t cksum1 = wal->checksum1;
    uint32_t cksum2 = wal->checksum2;
    wal_checksum(1, frame_hdr, 8, &cksum1, &cksum2);
    wal_checksum(1, page_data, page_size, &cksum1, &cksum2);
    write_be32(frame_hdr + 16, cksum1);
    write_be32(frame_hdr + 20, cksum2);
    // Append frame header + page data to WAL file
    // Seek to end of WAL
    off_t write_pos = WAL_HEADER_SIZE +
        (off_t)wal->frame_count * (WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE + page_size);
    pwrite(wal->fd, frame_hdr, WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE, write_pos);
    pwrite(wal->fd, page_data, page_size,
           write_pos + WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE);
    // Update running checksum
    wal->checksum1 = cksum1;
    wal->checksum2 = cksum2;
    // Update WAL index
    wal_index_set(wal, page_id, wal->frame_count);
    wal->frame_count++;
    // If this is a commit frame, fsync the WAL
    if (is_commit) {
        fsync(wal->fd);
    }
    return 0;
}
```
Notice that `fsync` is only called on **commit frames**. During a transaction, intermediate frames are written but not necessarily fsynced — they can be lost on crash without consequence because the transaction isn't committed yet. Only the commit frame's fsync matters, because that's what makes the transaction durable. This is a critical performance optimization: a transaction that modifies 100 pages calls `fsync` once (on the commit frame), not 100 times.
[[EXPLAIN:fsync-semantics-and-write-ordering|fsync semantics and write ordering]]
The append-only write pattern is what makes WAL fast for writes. The rollback journal requires random I/O to the database file (page 42 might be at offset 172,032, page 7,891 at offset 32,329,728). WAL writes sequentially to the end of a single file. Sequential writes are dramatically faster than random writes — 10-100× on spinning disks, 2-5× on SSDs — because the disk head (or SSD controller) doesn't need to seek between scattered locations.
---
## The Read Path: Page Version Lookup

![WAL Read Path: Page Version Lookup](./diagrams/diag-wal-read-path.svg)

When the buffer pool receives a `fetch_page` request in WAL mode, the lookup path changes. Instead of always reading from the database file, it first checks the WAL:
```c
// Modified fetch_page for WAL mode
uint8_t* fetch_page_wal(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id,
                          Wal *wal, int reader_max_frame) {
    // Step 1: Check if page is already in the buffer pool
    int frame_idx = page_table_lookup(pool, page_id);
    if (frame_idx >= 0) {
        pool->hits++;
        Frame *frame = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
        frame->pin_count++;
        lru_move_to_front(pool, frame_idx);
        return frame->data;
    }
    // Step 2: Check WAL for the most recent version of this page
    pool->misses++;
    frame_idx = get_free_frame(pool);
    if (frame_idx < 0) return NULL;
    Frame *frame = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
    frame->page_id = page_id;
    frame->is_dirty = false;
    frame->pin_count = 1;
    int wal_frame = wal_index_get(wal, page_id, reader_max_frame);
    if (wal_frame >= 0) {
        // Page found in WAL — read from WAL file
        wal_read_frame(wal, wal_frame, frame->data, pool->page_size);
    } else {
        // Page not in WAL — read from database file
        disk_read_page(pool, page_id, frame->data);
    }
    page_table_insert(pool, page_id, frame_idx);
    lru_push_front(pool, frame_idx);
    return frame->data;
}
// Read a specific frame's page data from the WAL file
static void wal_read_frame(Wal *wal, int frame_num, uint8_t *dest,
                             int page_size) {
    off_t offset = WAL_HEADER_SIZE +
        (off_t)frame_num * (WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE + page_size) +
        WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE;  // skip frame header, read page data
    pread(wal->fd, dest, page_size, offset);
}
```
The `reader_max_frame` parameter is the snapshot boundary. A reader that started when the WAL had 500 frames will only see frames 0-499, even if 200 more frames have been appended since. This is the mechanism that enables snapshot isolation.
---
## Snapshot Isolation: Frozen-in-Time Views
[[EXPLAIN:snapshot-isolation|Snapshot isolation]]

![Concurrent Readers and Writer in WAL Mode](./diagrams/diag-wal-concurrent-access.svg)

Snapshot isolation in WAL mode works through a single number: the **end mark** — the index of the last committed WAL frame at the moment a reader begins its transaction. Each reader records this number when it starts, and all subsequent page lookups use it as the `max_frame` ceiling.
```c
typedef struct {
    int  max_frame;      // latest committed frame visible to this reader
    bool is_active;      // whether this reader is still running
} ReaderSnapshot;
#define MAX_READERS 32
typedef struct {
    ReaderSnapshot readers[MAX_READERS];
    int            writer_max_frame;  // latest committed frame overall
} SnapshotManager;
// Called when a read transaction begins
int snapshot_begin_read(SnapshotManager *sm, int *reader_id) {
    for (int i = 0; i < MAX_READERS; i++) {
        if (!sm->readers[i].is_active) {
            sm->readers[i].max_frame = sm->writer_max_frame;
            sm->readers[i].is_active = true;
            *reader_id = i;
            return sm->readers[i].max_frame;
        }
    }
    return -1;  // too many concurrent readers
}
// Called when a read transaction ends
void snapshot_end_read(SnapshotManager *sm, int reader_id) {
    sm->readers[reader_id].is_active = false;
}
// Called after a successful write commit
void snapshot_update_writer(SnapshotManager *sm, int new_max_frame) {
    sm->writer_max_frame = new_max_frame;
}
// Find the oldest frame still needed by any active reader
int snapshot_min_reader_frame(SnapshotManager *sm) {
    int min_frame = sm->writer_max_frame;
    for (int i = 0; i < MAX_READERS; i++) {
        if (sm->readers[i].is_active &&
            sm->readers[i].max_frame < min_frame) {
            min_frame = sm->readers[i].max_frame;
        }
    }
    return min_frame;
}
```
Here's the concrete scenario. Three connections access the database:
1. **Writer** inserts 50 rows, producing WAL frames 0-49. Commits — `writer_max_frame = 49`.
2. **Reader A** starts a SELECT. Its `max_frame = 49`. It sees all 50 rows.
3. **Writer** inserts 30 more rows, producing frames 50-79. Commits — `writer_max_frame = 79`.
4. **Reader B** starts a SELECT. Its `max_frame = 79`. It sees all 80 rows.
5. **Reader A** continues its query. Its `max_frame` is still 49. It does **not** see the 30 new rows. It sees a frozen snapshot of the database as it existed when Reader A started.
Reader A and Reader B run concurrently, seeing different consistent views of the database. Neither blocks the other. Neither blocks the writer. This is the power of snapshot isolation — and it emerges naturally from the `max_frame` mechanism.
[[EXPLAIN:write-ahead-logging-theory|Write-ahead logging theory]]
---
## WAL Commit
In WAL mode, committing a transaction is dramatically simpler than in journal mode. There's no need to flush dirty pages to the database file, no journal to delete. You append a commit frame to the WAL and fsync:
```c
int wal_commit(Wal *wal, BufferPool *pool, SnapshotManager *sm) {
    // Step 1: Write all dirty pages as WAL frames
    uint32_t db_page_count = 0;
    int last_dirty_frame = -1;
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->pool_size; i++) {
        Frame *f = &pool->frames[i];
        if (f->page_id != INVALID_PAGE_ID && f->is_dirty) {
            // Track the highest page_id to compute db_size
            if (f->page_id + 1 > db_page_count) {
                db_page_count = f->page_id + 1;
            }
            last_dirty_frame = i;
        }
    }
    // Step 2: Append frames for all dirty pages
    // The LAST frame gets the commit marker (db_size > 0)
    for (int i = 0; i < pool->pool_size; i++) {
        Frame *f = &pool->frames[i];
        if (f->page_id != INVALID_PAGE_ID && f->is_dirty) {
            bool is_last = (i == last_dirty_frame);
            wal_write_frame(wal, f->page_id, f->data, pool->page_size,
                            is_last, is_last ? db_page_count : 0);
            f->is_dirty = false;
        }
    }
    // Step 3: fsync is already done inside wal_write_frame for the commit frame
    // Step 4: Update snapshot manager
    snapshot_update_writer(sm, wal->frame_count - 1);
    // Step 5: Check if auto-checkpoint is needed
    if (wal->frame_count >= wal->auto_checkpoint_threshold) {
        wal_checkpoint(wal, pool, sm);
    }
    return 0;
}
```
Compare this to the rollback journal commit (Milestone 9):
- **Journal commit**: fsync journal → flush all dirty pages to database → fsync database → delete journal. Four barriers, random I/O to database.
- **WAL commit**: append frames to WAL → fsync WAL. One barrier, sequential I/O only.
The WAL commit is faster because it avoids random database file I/O entirely. The cost of eventually transferring WAL frames into the database is deferred to checkpoint time — a classic example of **amortizing** expensive work.
---
## Checkpoint: Merging WAL into the Database

![WAL Checkpoint Process](./diagrams/diag-wal-checkpoint.svg)

The checkpoint copies committed WAL frames back into the main database file. This is the operation that keeps the WAL from growing indefinitely and that brings the database file up to date.
```c
#define DEFAULT_AUTO_CHECKPOINT_THRESHOLD 1000  // frames
int wal_checkpoint(Wal *wal, BufferPool *pool, SnapshotManager *sm) {
    // Step 1: Determine how far we can checkpoint
    // We cannot checkpoint past the oldest active reader's max_frame,
    // because that reader still needs those WAL frames.
    int safe_frame = snapshot_min_reader_frame(sm);
    if (safe_frame < 0) {
        // No active readers — can checkpoint everything
        safe_frame = wal->frame_count - 1;
    }
    if (safe_frame < 0) return 0;  // nothing to checkpoint
    printf("Checkpoint: copying frames 0-%d to database "
           "(total WAL frames: %d)\n", safe_frame, wal->frame_count);
    // Step 2: Copy each WAL frame's page data into the database file
    uint8_t *page_buf = malloc(pool->page_size);
    int pages_written = 0;
    for (int frame = 0; frame <= safe_frame; frame++) {
        // Read frame header to get page_id
        off_t frame_offset = WAL_HEADER_SIZE +
            (off_t)frame * (WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE + pool->page_size);
        uint8_t frame_hdr[WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE];
        pread(wal->fd, frame_hdr, WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE, frame_offset);
        uint32_t page_id = read_be32(frame_hdr);
        // Read page data from WAL
        pread(wal->fd, page_buf, pool->page_size,
              frame_offset + WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE);
        // Write page data to database file at the correct offset
        off_t db_offset = (off_t)page_id * pool->page_size;
        pwrite(pool->fd, page_buf, pool->page_size, db_offset);
        pages_written++;
        // Invalidate this page in the buffer pool cache
        // (it now has a stale version — the database file is newer)
        int bp_frame = page_table_lookup(pool, page_id);
        if (bp_frame >= 0 && pool->frames[bp_frame].pin_count == 0) {
            page_table_remove(pool, page_id);
            lru_remove(pool, bp_frame);
            pool->frames[bp_frame].page_id = INVALID_PAGE_ID;
        }
    }
    free(page_buf);
    // Step 3: fsync the database file
    fsync(pool->fd);
    // Step 4: If all frames were checkpointed, reset the WAL
    if (safe_frame >= wal->frame_count - 1) {
        // All frames copied — truncate and reset the WAL
        ftruncate(wal->fd, 0);
        // Write fresh header with new salts
        wal->header.checkpoint_seq++;
        wal->header.salt1 = (uint32_t)time(NULL) ^ wal->header.checkpoint_seq;
        wal->header.salt2 = (uint32_t)getpid() ^ (uint32_t)time(NULL);
        wal->checksum1 = 0;
        wal->checksum2 = 0;
        uint8_t hdr_buf[WAL_HEADER_SIZE];
        write_wal_header(hdr_buf, &wal->header);
        wal_checksum(1, hdr_buf, WAL_HEADER_SIZE,
                     &wal->checksum1, &wal->checksum2);
        wal->header.checksum1 = wal->checksum1;
        wal->header.checksum2 = wal->checksum2;
        write(wal->fd, hdr_buf, WAL_HEADER_SIZE);
        fsync(wal->fd);
        // Clear WAL index
        for (int i = 0; i < wal->wal_index_size; i++) {
            wal->wal_index[i] = -1;
        }
        wal->frame_count = 0;
        printf("Checkpoint complete: WAL reset (%d pages written)\n",
               pages_written);
    } else {
        // Partial checkpoint — WAL still contains frames > safe_frame
        // Remove checkpointed entries from WAL index
        // (Frames > safe_frame retain their index entries)
        printf("Partial checkpoint: %d pages written, %d frames remain\n",
               pages_written, wal->frame_count - safe_frame - 1);
    }
    return pages_written;
}
```
### Why Partial Checkpoints Happen
When Reader A holds `max_frame = 49` and the WAL has 200 frames, the checkpoint can only copy frames 0-49 to the database. Frames 50-199 must remain in the WAL because they might contain pages that Reader A needs in their pre-frame-50 versions.
This is the **long-running reader problem**. A reader that starts and then takes a long time to complete (perhaps iterating a large result set, or the user walks away from an open transaction) pins WAL frames in place. The WAL cannot be truncated past that reader's snapshot boundary. If the system continues writing new frames while the old reader holds its snapshot, the WAL grows without bound.
This is not a theoretical concern. In production, a forgotten `BEGIN` in a database client (without a matching `COMMIT` or `ROLLBACK`) is one of the most common causes of WAL bloat. The reader appears to be doing nothing, but its open snapshot prevents WAL cleanup.
```c
// Detect stale readers (for diagnostics or forced cleanup)
void wal_report_stale_readers(SnapshotManager *sm, int current_frame) {
    for (int i = 0; i < MAX_READERS; i++) {
        if (sm->readers[i].is_active) {
            int frames_behind = current_frame - sm->readers[i].max_frame;
            if (frames_behind > 10000) {
                fprintf(stderr,
                    "WARNING: Reader %d is %d frames behind "
                    "(snapshot at frame %d, current frame %d). "
                    "This prevents WAL truncation.\n",
                    i, frames_behind,
                    sm->readers[i].max_frame, current_frame);
            }
        }
    }
}
```
### Auto-Checkpoint
To prevent unbounded WAL growth in normal operation, the engine triggers a checkpoint automatically when the WAL exceeds a configurable threshold:
```c
// In the Wal struct:
int auto_checkpoint_threshold;  // default: 1000 frames
// Called after each commit:
static void maybe_auto_checkpoint(Wal *wal, BufferPool *pool,
                                    SnapshotManager *sm) {
    if (wal->frame_count >= wal->auto_checkpoint_threshold) {
        wal_checkpoint(wal, pool, sm);
    }
}
```
SQLite's default is 1000 pages (~4MB with 4KB pages). You can configure this via `PRAGMA wal_autocheckpoint = N` where N is the number of frames. Setting N to 0 disables auto-checkpoint entirely — useful for batch imports where you want to defer checkpoint to the end.
The auto-checkpoint runs synchronously within the connection that triggers it. This means the commit that pushes the WAL past the threshold also pays the checkpoint cost. If the checkpoint writes 1000 pages to the database file, that's ~4MB of random I/O happening inside what the user thought was a simple INSERT. This bursty I/O characteristic is one of WAL's less-obvious tradeoffs compared to rollback journal mode, where write I/O is spread across the transaction.
---
## Mode Switching: PRAGMA journal_mode
The user switches between journal modes with:
```sql
PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;    -- switch to WAL mode
PRAGMA journal_mode = DELETE;  -- switch back to rollback journal (default)
```
Switching to WAL mode requires:
1. Ensure no active transactions.
2. Create the WAL file and shared-memory file.
3. Redirect the buffer pool's write path from database file to WAL file.
4. Redirect the buffer pool's read path to check WAL before database file.
Switching back to journal mode requires:
1. Run a full checkpoint (copy all WAL frames to database).
2. Delete the WAL file and shared-memory file.
3. Restore the buffer pool's original read/write paths.
```c
int pragma_journal_mode(Database *db, const char *mode) {
    if (strcasecmp(mode, "WAL") == 0 && !db->wal_enabled) {
        // Switch to WAL mode
        if (db->txn.state != TXN_NONE) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Cannot switch to WAL mode during a transaction\n");
            return -1;
        }
        Wal *wal = calloc(1, sizeof(Wal));
        int rc = wal_open(wal, db->path, db->pool->page_size);
        if (rc != 0) {
            free(wal);
            return -1;
        }
        wal->auto_checkpoint_threshold = DEFAULT_AUTO_CHECKPOINT_THRESHOLD;
        db->wal = wal;
        db->wal_enabled = true;
        db->pool->wal = wal;  // connect buffer pool to WAL
        printf("Journal mode set to WAL\n");
        return 0;
    } else if (strcasecmp(mode, "DELETE") == 0 && db->wal_enabled) {
        // Switch back to rollback journal mode
        // Step 1: Full checkpoint
        wal_checkpoint(db->wal, db->pool, &db->snapshot_mgr);
        // Step 2: Close and delete WAL file
        close(db->wal->fd);
        unlink(db->wal->path);
        // Step 3: Delete shared-memory file
        char shm_path[256];
        snprintf(shm_path, sizeof(shm_path), "%s-shm", db->path);
        unlink(shm_path);
        free(db->wal->wal_index);
        free(db->wal);
        db->wal = NULL;
        db->wal_enabled = false;
        db->pool->wal = NULL;
        printf("Journal mode set to DELETE (rollback journal)\n");
        return 0;
    }
    return 0;  // already in requested mode
}
```
---
## Integrating WAL with the Buffer Pool
The buffer pool needs to know whether it's operating in WAL mode or journal mode. The cleanest approach: a function pointer for the write-back strategy.
```c
// In BufferPool struct, add:
typedef struct BufferPool {
    // ... existing fields ...
    Wal  *wal;             // non-NULL when in WAL mode
    SnapshotManager *sm;   // snapshot manager for WAL mode
    int   reader_max_frame;// per-connection snapshot boundary
} BufferPool;
// Modified evict_frame for WAL mode
```
