```c
static void evict_frame_wal(BufferPool *pool, int frame_idx) {
    Frame *f = &pool->frames[frame_idx];
    if (f->is_dirty) {
        // In WAL mode, dirty pages are written to the WAL on commit,
        // NOT during eviction. If a dirty page is evicted mid-transaction,
        // we must write it to the WAL now.
        if (pool->wal) {
            wal_write_frame(pool->wal, f->page_id, f->data,
                            pool->page_size, false, 0);
            f->is_dirty = false;
        }
    }
    page_table_remove(pool, f->page_id);
    lru_remove(pool, frame_idx);
    f->page_id = INVALID_PAGE_ID;
    pool->evictions++;
}
```
Note the difference: in journal mode, eviction writes to the database file (after journaling). In WAL mode, eviction writes to the WAL file. The database file is never touched during normal operations.
---
## Testing
### Test: WAL File Created on Mode Switch
```c
void test_wal_mode_switch(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_wal.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val TEXT);",
                NULL, NULL);
    // No WAL file yet
    assert(access("test_wal.db-wal", F_OK) != 0);
    // Switch to WAL mode
    execute_sql(db, "PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;", NULL, NULL);
    assert(db->wal_enabled == true);
    assert(access("test_wal.db-wal", F_OK) == 0);
    // Switch back
    execute_sql(db, "PRAGMA journal_mode = DELETE;", NULL, NULL);
    assert(db->wal_enabled == false);
    assert(access("test_wal.db-wal", F_OK) != 0);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_wal.db");
    printf("PASS: WAL mode switch creates and removes WAL file\n");
}
```
### Test: Writes Append to WAL, Not Database
```c
void test_wal_writes_append(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_wal_append.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val INTEGER);",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;", NULL, NULL);
    // Record database file size before writes
    struct stat st_before;
    fstat(db->pool->fd, &st_before);
    // Insert data
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 100);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 200);", NULL, NULL);
    // Database file size should NOT have grown
    // (writes went to WAL, not database)
    struct stat st_after;
    fstat(db->pool->fd, &st_after);
    // Note: the database file size stays the same or only changes
    // if CREATE TABLE wrote before WAL was enabled
    // The key check: WAL file has grown
    struct stat st_wal;
    stat("test_wal_append.db-wal", &st_wal);
    assert(st_wal.st_size > WAL_HEADER_SIZE);  // WAL has frames
    // Data should still be readable
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT val FROM t ORDER BY id;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 2);
    assert(rc.rows[0][0].i == 100);
    assert(rc.rows[1][0].i == 200);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_wal_append.db");
    unlink("test_wal_append.db-wal");
    printf("PASS: writes append to WAL, data readable\n");
}
```
### Test: Concurrent Readers See Consistent Snapshots
```c
void test_snapshot_isolation(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_snapshot.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val INTEGER);",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;", NULL, NULL);
    // Insert initial data
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 100);", NULL, NULL);
    // Simulate Reader A starting (captures snapshot)
    int reader_a_id;
    int reader_a_max = snapshot_begin_read(&db->snapshot_mgr, &reader_a_id);
    // Writer inserts more data AFTER Reader A's snapshot
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 200);", NULL, NULL);
    // Reader A should see only 1 row (its snapshot predates the insert)
    db->pool->reader_max_frame = reader_a_max;
    ResultCollector rc_a = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t;", collect_results, &rc_a);
    // With snapshot isolation: Reader A sees 1 row
    // (Implementation note: requires the query execution to use
    //  reader_max_frame in all page lookups)
    // Simulate Reader B starting (captures newer snapshot)
    int reader_b_id;
    int reader_b_max = snapshot_begin_read(&db->snapshot_mgr, &reader_b_id);
    // Reader B should see 2 rows
    db->pool->reader_max_frame = reader_b_max;
    ResultCollector rc_b = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t;", collect_results, &rc_b);
    // Reader B sees 2 rows
    snapshot_end_read(&db->snapshot_mgr, reader_a_id);
    snapshot_end_read(&db->snapshot_mgr, reader_b_id);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_snapshot.db");
    unlink("test_snapshot.db-wal");
    printf("PASS: snapshot isolation — readers see consistent views\n");
}
```
### Test: Checkpoint Copies WAL to Database
```c
void test_checkpoint(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_ckpt.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val INTEGER);",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;", NULL, NULL);
    // Insert data (goes to WAL)
    for (int i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
        char sql[128];
        snprintf(sql, sizeof(sql), "INSERT INTO t VALUES (%d, %d);", i, i * 10);
        execute_sql(db, sql, NULL, NULL);
    }
    // WAL should have frames
    assert(db->wal->frame_count > 0);
    int frames_before = db->wal->frame_count;
    // Run checkpoint
    int pages_written = wal_checkpoint(db->wal, db->pool, &db->snapshot_mgr);
    assert(pages_written > 0);
    // WAL should be reset (no active readers to prevent full checkpoint)
    assert(db->wal->frame_count == 0);
    // Data should still be readable (now from database file, not WAL)
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT val FROM t WHERE id = 50;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 1);
    assert(rc.rows[0][0].i == 500);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_ckpt.db");
    unlink("test_ckpt.db-wal");
    printf("PASS: checkpoint copies WAL to database (%d frames → %d pages)\n",
           frames_before, pages_written);
}
```
### Test: Auto-Checkpoint Triggers
```c
void test_auto_checkpoint(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_autocp.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val INTEGER);",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;", NULL, NULL);
    // Set a low threshold for testing
    db->wal->auto_checkpoint_threshold = 50;
    // Insert enough to exceed threshold
    for (int i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
        char sql[128];
        snprintf(sql, sizeof(sql), "INSERT INTO t VALUES (%d, %d);", i, i);
        execute_sql(db, sql, NULL, NULL);
    }
    // Auto-checkpoint should have fired, keeping WAL size bounded
    // (exact frame count depends on when checkpoint triggered and how many
    //  frames were added after)
    assert(db->wal->frame_count < 100);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_autocp.db");
    unlink("test_autocp.db-wal");
    printf("PASS: auto-checkpoint triggers at threshold\n");
}
```
### Test: WAL Checksum Detects Corruption
```c
void test_wal_checksum_corruption(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_walcrc.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val INTEGER);",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 100);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 200);", NULL, NULL);
    // Close database
    int wal_fd = db->wal->fd;
    // Corrupt a byte in the WAL (simulate disk error)
    uint8_t garbage = 0xFF;
    pwrite(wal_fd, &garbage, 1,
           WAL_HEADER_SIZE + WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE + 10);
    db_close(db);
    // Reopen — recovery should detect corruption and stop before it
    db = db_open("test_walcrc.db");
    // The corrupted frame and everything after it should be discarded
    // At most, only data from valid frames should be visible
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_walcrc.db");
    unlink("test_walcrc.db-wal");
    printf("PASS: WAL checksum detects corruption during recovery\n");
}
```
### Test: Readers Don't Block Writers
```c
void test_concurrent_read_write(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_concurrent.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val INTEGER);",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 100);", NULL, NULL);
    // Start a reader
    int reader_id;
    int reader_max = snapshot_begin_read(&db->snapshot_mgr, &reader_id);
    // Writer should still be able to write (no blocking)
    int rc = execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 200);", NULL, NULL);
    assert(rc == 0);  // write succeeded while reader is active
    // Reader can still read its snapshot
    db->pool->reader_max_frame = reader_max;
    ResultCollector rc_read = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t;", collect_results, &rc_read);
    // Reader sees 1 row (its snapshot)
    // Clean up reader
    snapshot_end_read(&db->snapshot_mgr, reader_id);
    // Now a new reader sees 2 rows
    int reader2_id;
    snapshot_begin_read(&db->snapshot_mgr, &reader2_id);
    db->pool->reader_max_frame = db->snapshot_mgr.writer_max_frame;
    ResultCollector rc_read2 = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t;", collect_results, &rc_read2);
    snapshot_end_read(&db->snapshot_mgr, reader2_id);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_concurrent.db");
    unlink("test_concurrent.db-wal");
    printf("PASS: readers don't block writers in WAL mode\n");
}
```
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens During a WAL-Mode Write
**Level 1 — Public API**: The user executes `INSERT INTO users VALUES (42, 'Alice', 30)` in WAL mode. The insert succeeds, and subsequent SELECTs find Alice. If the process crashes after the insert, Alice is still there on restart.
**Level 2 — WAL Engine**: The VM executes `OP_INSERT`, which modifies a B-tree leaf page in the buffer pool. The page is marked dirty. On commit, `wal_commit` iterates all dirty pages in the buffer pool and for each one, serializes the page data into a WAL frame: 24-byte frame header (page_id, salts, cumulative checksum) followed by 4096 bytes of page data, appended sequentially to the WAL file. The final frame carries `db_size > 0`, marking it as the commit frame. `fsync` is called on the WAL file — the transaction is now durable. The database file was never touched. The WAL index is updated so that future reads of this page find the WAL frame instead of the (stale) database file version. If the WAL frame count exceeds the auto-checkpoint threshold (1000 by default), a checkpoint runs: it reads each WAL frame and writes the page data to the correct offset in the database file, then fsyncs the database file, then resets the WAL.
**Level 3 — OS and Hardware**: The WAL `write()` calls append data to the end of the WAL file. The OS allocates new file blocks sequentially — on a modern filesystem (ext4, APFS), these blocks are typically physically contiguous on the storage device. The `fsync()` flushes these blocks to the storage controller. On NVMe SSD, the sequential write pattern achieves near-peak throughput (~3GB/s) because the SSD controller can parallelize writes across multiple NAND channels. Compare this to rollback journal mode, where `pwrite()` calls scatter across the database file — the SSD controller must resolve these to different NAND pages, reducing effective bandwidth. During checkpoint, the `pwrite()` calls to the database file *are* random — checkpoint inherits the random I/O cost that WAL mode deferred. The total I/O is the same; WAL mode changes *when* and *how* the cost is paid.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What WAL Mode Unlocks
**→ Snapshot Isolation as MVCC-lite — From Databases to Version Control.** The snapshot mechanism you just built — readers see a frozen view of the database as of their start time — is the core concept behind **Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC)**, the concurrency strategy used by PostgreSQL, Oracle, MySQL/InnoDB, and CockroachDB. In PostgreSQL, every row has `xmin` and `xmax` fields recording which transaction created and deleted it; a reader's snapshot determines which row versions are "visible." Your `max_frame` plays the same role as PostgreSQL's transaction snapshot. But the same pattern extends far beyond databases: **Git** is essentially a WAL. Each commit appends new objects to the object store; branches are named snapshots (like your `reader_max_frame`). Running `git log` scans the commit chain the way your reader scans WAL frames. A Git checkout is like a database reader starting a snapshot — you see the repository as of that commit, regardless of what others are committing. **Event sourcing** in application architecture is the same idea: append events to a log, derive current state by replaying events. The "current state" is just the latest snapshot. Every time you encounter a system where "readers see a frozen view while writers append," you're seeing the WAL/MVCC/snapshot pattern.
**→ Append-Only as a Design Pattern — From Databases to Finance.** The WAL file is append-only: you never modify existing frames, only add new ones at the end. This constraint, which sounds limiting, is actually a **powerful architectural choice** that appears across domains. **Apache Kafka** is a distributed commit log — producers append messages, consumers read from offsets (analogous to your `max_frame`). Kafka's entire architecture is a distributed WAL. **Event sourcing** stores application state as an append-only sequence of events; the current state is derived by replaying the log. **Double-entry accounting** works the same way: you never modify a journal entry; you append a correcting entry. An audit trail is inherently append-only. Even **blockchain** is an append-only data structure — each block references the previous, forming a chain that can only grow, never shrink. The power of append-only comes from two properties: (1) historical data is immutable, enabling audit trails and point-in-time queries, and (2) append operations have no read-write conflicts, enabling high concurrency. Your WAL exploits both.
**→ Checkpoint as Compaction — The Universal Pattern of Deferred Cleanup.** Your checkpoint operation — merging WAL frames into the database file — is structurally identical to **compaction** in LSM-trees (used by RocksDB, LevelDB, Cassandra). An LSM-tree writes new key-value pairs to an in-memory buffer (memtable), which is periodically flushed to disk as a sorted run (SSTable). Compaction merges multiple SSTables into fewer, larger ones — reducing read amplification at the cost of write I/O. Your checkpoint merges WAL frames into the database file — reducing WAL size and read path complexity at the cost of random I/O. The tradeoff is identical: defer expensive reorganization work and do it in bulk, trading bursty I/O for amortized throughput. The same pattern appears in **garbage collection** (minor GC collects young objects frequently; major GC compacts the entire heap rarely), **memory defragmentation** (defer compaction until fragmentation exceeds a threshold), and **log-structured merge** filesystems like F2FS. Once you see the pattern — "append-only log + periodic compaction into organized storage" — you recognize it everywhere.
**→ Log-Structured vs In-Place Update — The Fundamental Storage Dichotomy.** Your database now supports both strategies. The rollback journal modifies the database file in-place (with undo protection). WAL writes to a log and defers in-place updates to checkpoint. This is the fundamental dichotomy in storage engine design:
| | In-Place Update (B-tree + journal) | Log-Structured (WAL, LSM-tree) |
|---|---|---|
| Write pattern | Random | Sequential |
| Read pattern | Single location (fast) | Multiple locations (check log first) |
| Write amplification | Low (1× per page) | Can be high (compaction rewrites) |
| Read amplification | Low (1 seek) | Can be high (check log + database) |
| Space amplification | Low | Medium (log + database both exist) |
| Concurrency | Limited (locks needed) | High (append doesn't conflict) |
Most modern storage engines are hybrids. Your WAL-mode database is a hybrid: B-tree for storage structure, append-only log for writes, periodic checkpoint to merge. PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, and Oracle all use this same hybrid. RocksDB and Cassandra go further — they're fully log-structured (no in-place updates at all; compaction is the only way data reaches its final position). Understanding both ends of this spectrum, and where your database sits on it, is what separates someone who can *use* a database from someone who can *build* one.
> **🔭 Deep Dive**: The formal treatment of WAL and ARIES recovery is in *ARIES: A Transaction Recovery Method Supporting Fine-Granularity Locking and Partial Rollbacks Using Write-Ahead Logging* (Mohan et al., 1992). For the practical LSM-tree perspective, see *The Log-Structured Merge-Tree* (O'Neil et al., 1996). For a modern comparison of B-tree vs LSM approaches, see *Designing Data-Intensive Applications* by Martin Kleppmann, Chapter 3.
---
## System Awareness
You've built WAL mode — an alternative transaction mechanism that inverts the rollback journal's strategy. Instead of writing old data to a journal and modifying the database in-place, WAL writes new data to an append-only log and leaves the database untouched. This inversion enables concurrent readers during writes, eliminates the reader-writer lock that was rollback journal mode's bottleneck, and converts random write I/O into sequential I/O.

![EXPLAIN Output Anatomy](./diagrams/diag-explain-output.svg)

The WAL connects to every layer of the system. The **buffer pool** (Milestone 4) now has a dual read path: check WAL index first, fall back to database file. The **B-tree** (Milestone 5) operates identically — it doesn't know whether its pages come from the WAL or the database file. The **VM** (Milestone 3) executes the same bytecode regardless of journal mode. The **query planner** (Milestone 8) is unaffected. The abstraction boundaries you built in earlier milestones mean that a fundamental change to the durability strategy — from undo logging to redo logging — requires changes only in the buffer pool and transaction layers. Everything above (SQL frontend, VM) and everything at the storage layer (B-tree, page format) is unchanged.
Without WAL mode, your database forces all readers to wait for any active writer — acceptable for a single-user tool, unacceptable for concurrent workloads. With it, readers and writers coexist peacefully: readers see consistent snapshots, writers append without interference, and checkpoint merges changes into the database at a time of the system's choosing.
In the next milestone (Aggregate Functions & JOIN), you'll return to the SQL frontend — extending the bytecode compiler and VM to handle GROUP BY, HAVING, COUNT/SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX, and nested-loop joins. The storage engine beneath is now complete: crash-safe, concurrent, and persistent. Everything from here is building richer query capabilities on top of this foundation.
---
<!-- END_MS -->
<!-- END_MS -->


<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m11 -->
<!-- MS_ID: build-sqlite-m11 -->
# Milestone 11: Aggregate Functions & JOIN
## Where We Are in the System
Your database engine is complete from SQL text to crash-recoverable, concurrent persistent storage. The tokenizer, parser, and bytecode compiler form the frontend. The VDBE executes bytecode through cursor operations. The buffer pool caches pages with LRU eviction. B-trees store table rows; B+trees store secondary indexes. The query planner chooses access paths based on statistics. Transactions are ACID via either rollback journal or WAL. Every layer works. Every layer is tested.

![SQLite Engine — Full System Architecture Map](./diagrams/diag-satellite-system-map.svg)

But your SQL dialect is crippled. You can filter rows (`WHERE`), project columns (`SELECT col1, col2`), and look up individual rows via indexes. You cannot answer the question every business user asks first: *"How many?"* You cannot compute `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status = 'shipped'`. You cannot compute `SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department`. You cannot combine data from two tables with `SELECT orders.id, customers.name FROM orders JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.id`.

![Data Flow: SQL Statement to Disk Page](./diagrams/diag-data-flow-sql-to-disk.svg)

This milestone adds the two most important query capabilities: **aggregate functions** (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX with GROUP BY and HAVING) and **JOIN execution** (nested loop INNER JOIN). These are not separate features — they interact deeply. A query like `SELECT c.country, COUNT(*), AVG(o.total) FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.cust_id = c.id GROUP BY c.country HAVING COUNT(*) > 10` requires both JOIN and aggregation, and the execution order matters: join first, then group, then filter groups.
---
## The Revelation: GROUP BY Is Not Sorting
You probably think GROUP BY works like this: sort the table by the group column, then walk through the sorted result, accumulating aggregates for each run of identical keys. When the key changes, emit the accumulated result and start fresh. This is the "sort-then-scan" mental model, and it's what most people picture when they hear "grouping."
This model is wrong in two ways — one about mechanism, one about semantics.
**The mechanism problem:** Sorting is O(n log n). For a million-row table, that's ~20 million comparisons. But if you only have 50 distinct departments, you're doing 20 million comparisons to produce 50 output rows. A **hash table** mapping department names to accumulator structs does the same work in O(n) — one hash lookup per row, one accumulator update, done. No sorting needed. No O(n log n) overhead. The hash table uses O(k) memory where k is the number of distinct groups, which is typically tiny compared to n.

![Aggregate Execution with GROUP BY](./diagrams/diag-aggregate-execution.svg)

**The semantics problem:** The edge cases around NULL and empty sets are where every naive aggregate implementation silently produces wrong answers. Consider:
```sql
SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(bonus), SUM(bonus), AVG(bonus)
FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Research';
```
If the Research department has 5 employees, 3 of whom have a non-NULL bonus (100, 200, 300) and 2 with NULL:
| Function | Result | Why |
|----------|--------|-----|
| `COUNT(*)` | **5** | Counts all rows, including those with NULL bonus |
| `COUNT(bonus)` | **3** | Counts only non-NULL values of the `bonus` column |
| `SUM(bonus)` | **600** | Sums non-NULL values: 100 + 200 + 300 |
| `AVG(bonus)` | **200.0** (not 120.0) | 600 / 3, not 600 / 5 — AVG ignores NULLs in *both* numerator and denominator |

![NULL Handling in Aggregate Functions](./diagrams/diag-aggregate-null-handling.svg)

If the Research department has **zero** employees (the WHERE clause matches nothing):
| Function | Result | Why |
|----------|--------|-----|
| `COUNT(*)` | **0** | Zero rows → count is 0 |
| `COUNT(bonus)` | **0** | Zero rows → count is 0 |
| `SUM(bonus)` | **NULL** | No values to sum → result is unknown, not 0 |
| `AVG(bonus)` | **NULL** | No values → unknown, not 0 |
`COUNT` is the *only* aggregate that returns 0 for empty input. Every other aggregate returns NULL. This asymmetry trips up almost every implementation. If you initialize `SUM` to 0 and never update it (because no rows match), you return 0 — wrong. The correct behavior: initialize `SUM` to "no value seen yet" (a flag separate from the running total), and if no value was ever seen, return NULL.
AVG must return a `REAL` (floating-point) value even if the input column is `INTEGER`. `AVG(salary)` where all salaries are integers like 50000, 60000, 70000 must return `60000.0`, not the integer `60000`. If your implementation uses integer division internally, `AVG` of (1, 2) returns 1 instead of 1.5 — a silent precision bug.
These aren't edge cases. They're the *specification*. Every SQL database in existence implements these exact rules (they're defined in the SQL standard, ISO/IEC 9075). Get any of them wrong and your database produces incorrect answers that no error message will flag.
---
## The Accumulator Pattern
[[EXPLAIN:accumulator-pattern|The accumulator pattern: maintaining running state across a stream of values]]
Every aggregate function follows the same pattern: **initialize, accumulate, finalize**. You create an accumulator struct, feed it one row at a time, and when you're done, extract the result. This pattern is the foundation of all stream processing — Apache Flink, Kafka Streams, and even financial running totals use the exact same structure.
```c
typedef enum {
    AGG_COUNT_STAR,   // COUNT(*)
    AGG_COUNT,        // COUNT(column)
    AGG_SUM,
    AGG_AVG,
    AGG_MIN,
    AGG_MAX,
} AggType;
typedef struct {
    AggType type;
    int64_t count;       // number of non-NULL values seen (for COUNT, AVG)
    double  sum;         // running sum (for SUM, AVG)
    Value   min_val;     // current minimum
    Value   max_val;     // current maximum
    bool    has_value;   // true if at least one non-NULL value was accumulated
} Accumulator;
```
### Initialize
```c
void accumulator_init(Accumulator *acc, AggType type) {
    acc->type = type;
    acc->count = 0;
    acc->sum = 0.0;
    acc->min_val.type = VAL_NULL;
    acc->max_val.type = VAL_NULL;
    acc->has_value = false;
}
```
The `has_value` flag is the key to correct empty-set behavior. When the scan completes and `has_value` is still false, SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX return NULL, not their default values.
### Accumulate
```c
void accumulator_step(Accumulator *acc, Value *val) {
    switch (acc->type) {
        case AGG_COUNT_STAR:
            // COUNT(*) counts every row, including NULLs
            acc->count++;
            acc->has_value = true;
            break;
        case AGG_COUNT:
            // COUNT(col) counts only non-NULL values
            if (val->type != VAL_NULL) {
                acc->count++;
                acc->has_value = true;
            }
            break;
        case AGG_SUM:
            if (val->type == VAL_NULL) break;  // skip NULLs
            acc->has_value = true;
            if (val->type == VAL_INTEGER) {
                acc->sum += (double)val->i;
            } else if (val->type == VAL_REAL) {
                acc->sum += val->r;
            }
            break;
        case AGG_AVG:
            if (val->type == VAL_NULL) break;  // skip NULLs
            acc->has_value = true;
            acc->count++;
            if (val->type == VAL_INTEGER) {
                acc->sum += (double)val->i;
            } else if (val->type == VAL_REAL) {
                acc->sum += val->r;
            }
            break;
        case AGG_MIN:
            if (val->type == VAL_NULL) break;
            if (!acc->has_value) {
                acc->min_val = *val;
                acc->has_value = true;
            } else {
                // Use the comparison logic from Milestone 6
                if (value_compare(val, &acc->min_val) < 0) {
                    acc->min_val = *val;
                }
            }
            break;
        case AGG_MAX:
            if (val->type == VAL_NULL) break;
            if (!acc->has_value) {
                acc->max_val = *val;
                acc->has_value = true;
            } else {
                if (value_compare(val, &acc->max_val) > 0) {
                    acc->max_val = *val;
                }
            }
            break;
    }
}
```
### Finalize
```c
void accumulator_finalize(Accumulator *acc, Value *result) {
    switch (acc->type) {
        case AGG_COUNT_STAR:
        case AGG_COUNT:
            // COUNT always returns an integer, even for empty sets
            result->type = VAL_INTEGER;
            result->i = acc->count;
            break;
        case AGG_SUM:
            if (!acc->has_value) {
                result->type = VAL_NULL;  // SUM of no rows = NULL, not 0
            } else {
                result->type = VAL_REAL;
                result->r = acc->sum;
            }
            break;
        case AGG_AVG:
            if (!acc->has_value || acc->count == 0) {
                result->type = VAL_NULL;  // AVG of no rows = NULL
            } else {
                // ALWAYS return REAL, even if inputs were INTEGER
                result->type = VAL_REAL;
                result->r = acc->sum / (double)acc->count;
            }
            break;
        case AGG_MIN:
            if (!acc->has_value) {
                result->type = VAL_NULL;
            } else {
                *result = acc->min_val;
            }
            break;
        case AGG_MAX:
            if (!acc->has_value) {
                result->type = VAL_NULL;
            } else {
                *result = acc->max_val;
            }
            break;
    }
}
```
Study the `AGG_AVG` finalize. The division `acc->sum / (double)acc->count` uses `(double)` to force floating-point division. Without it, if both are stored as integers, C performs integer division: `3 / 2 = 1` instead of `1.5`. This is the silent precision bug that trips up nearly every first implementation.
Also note: `COUNT` returns 0 for empty input, but SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX return NULL. This is the SQL standard's distinction between "the answer is zero" and "there is no answer." A COUNT of zero items is definitively zero. A SUM of zero items is not zero — it's undefined. This connects to functional programming's `Maybe`/`Option` type: `Some(0)` vs `None` are different values, even though they might look the same to careless code.
---
## GROUP BY: Hash-Based Grouping
GROUP BY partitions the input rows into groups (one per distinct combination of group-key column values), maintains a separate set of accumulators for each group, and produces one output row per group.

![GROUP BY → HAVING → Output Pipeline](./diagrams/diag-groupby-having-pipeline.svg)

### The Group Hash Table
The data structure at the core of GROUP BY is a hash table mapping group keys to accumulator arrays:
```c
typedef struct {
    Value  *key_values;     // array of group-key column values
    int     key_count;      // number of group-key columns
    Accumulator *accumulators; // one per aggregate function in the query
    int     agg_count;      // number of aggregate functions
} GroupEntry;
typedef struct {
    GroupEntry *entries;     // array of groups
    int         count;       // number of groups
    int         capacity;    // allocated capacity
    // Simple hash table for O(1) group lookup
    int        *buckets;     // hash buckets → index into entries array
    int         bucket_count;
} GroupHashTable;
```
```c
GroupHashTable* group_table_create(int key_count, int agg_count,
                                     AggType *agg_types) {
    GroupHashTable *ht = calloc(1, sizeof(GroupHashTable));
    ht->capacity = 64;
    ht->entries = calloc(ht->capacity, sizeof(GroupEntry));
    ht->count = 0;
    ht->bucket_count = 127;  // prime for good hash distribution
    ht->buckets = malloc(ht->bucket_count * sizeof(int));
    for (int i = 0; i < ht->bucket_count; i++) {
        ht->buckets[i] = -1;  // empty
    }
    return ht;
}
```
### Finding or Creating a Group
For each input row, compute the hash of the group-key values, look up the group in the hash table, and either find an existing group or create a new one:
```c
static uint32_t hash_group_key(Value *keys, int key_count) {
    uint32_t hash = 0x811c9dc5;  // FNV-1a offset basis
    for (int i = 0; i < key_count; i++) {
        switch (keys[i].type) {
            case VAL_NULL:
                hash ^= 0;
                break;
            case VAL_INTEGER:
                hash ^= (uint32_t)(keys[i].i & 0xFFFFFFFF);
                hash *= 0x01000193;
                hash ^= (uint32_t)(keys[i].i >> 32);
                break;
            case VAL_REAL: {
                uint64_t bits;
                memcpy(&bits, &keys[i].r, 8);
                hash ^= (uint32_t)(bits & 0xFFFFFFFF);
                hash *= 0x01000193;
                hash ^= (uint32_t)(bits >> 32);
                break;
            }
            case VAL_STRING:
                for (int j = 0; j < keys[i].s.length; j++) {
                    hash ^= (uint8_t)keys[i].s.data[j];
                    hash *= 0x01000193;
                }
                break;
            default:
                break;
        }
        hash *= 0x01000193;
    }
    return hash;
}
static bool keys_equal(Value *a, Value *b, int count) {
    for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
        if (a[i].type != b[i].type) return false;
        // NULL == NULL for grouping purposes (unlike WHERE comparisons!)
        if (a[i].type == VAL_NULL && b[i].type == VAL_NULL) continue;
        if (a[i].type == VAL_NULL || b[i].type == VAL_NULL) return false;
        switch (a[i].type) {
            case VAL_INTEGER:
                if (a[i].i != b[i].i) return false;
                break;
            case VAL_REAL:
                if (a[i].r != b[i].r) return false;
                break;
            case VAL_STRING:
                if (a[i].s.length != b[i].s.length) return false;
                if (memcmp(a[i].s.data, b[i].s.data, a[i].s.length) != 0)
                    return false;
                break;
            default:
                break;
        }
    }
    return true;
}
```
**Important nuance:** In GROUP BY, `NULL = NULL` is treated as `true` — all NULL keys are grouped together into one group. This differs from WHERE clause behavior (Milestone 6), where `NULL = NULL` evaluates to `NULL` (falsy). The SQL standard explicitly specifies this dual behavior: grouping treats NULLs as equal, but comparison operators do not.
```c
// Find existing group or create new one. Returns pointer to the group's
// accumulator array.
GroupEntry* group_table_find_or_create(GroupHashTable *ht,
                                        Value *keys, int key_count,
                                        int agg_count, AggType *agg_types) {
    uint32_t hash = hash_group_key(keys, key_count);
    int bucket = hash % ht->bucket_count;
    // Linear probe for matching group
    int idx = ht->buckets[bucket];
    while (idx >= 0) {
        GroupEntry *entry = &ht->entries[idx];
        if (keys_equal(entry->key_values, keys, key_count)) {
            return entry;  // found existing group
        }
        // Linear probing: check next bucket
        bucket = (bucket + 1) % ht->bucket_count;
        idx = ht->buckets[bucket];
    }
    // Group not found — create a new one
    if (ht->count >= ht->capacity) {
        ht->capacity *= 2;
        ht->entries = realloc(ht->entries,
                               ht->capacity * sizeof(GroupEntry));
    }
    GroupEntry *new_entry = &ht->entries[ht->count];
    // Copy key values (deep copy for strings)
    new_entry->key_values = malloc(key_count * sizeof(Value));
    new_entry->key_count = key_count;
    for (int i = 0; i < key_count; i++) {
        new_entry->key_values[i] = keys[i];
        if (keys[i].type == VAL_STRING) {
            new_entry->key_values[i].s.data = malloc(keys[i].s.length + 1);
            memcpy(new_entry->key_values[i].s.data,
                   keys[i].s.data, keys[i].s.length);
            new_entry->key_values[i].s.data[keys[i].s.length] = '\0';
        }
    }
    // Initialize accumulators
    new_entry->agg_count = agg_count;
    new_entry->accumulators = malloc(agg_count * sizeof(Accumulator));
    for (int i = 0; i < agg_count; i++) {
        accumulator_init(&new_entry->accumulators[i], agg_types[i]);
    }
    ht->buckets[bucket] = ht->count;
    ht->count++;
    return new_entry;
}
```
### Design Decision: Hash vs Sort Aggregation
| Strategy | Time | Memory | Ordering | Used By |
|----------|------|--------|----------|---------|
| **Hash aggregation ✓** | O(n) | O(k) where k = distinct groups | Output order is arbitrary | PostgreSQL (HashAggregate), MySQL |
| Sort aggregation | O(n log n) | O(1) extra (in-place sort) | Output is sorted by group key | SQLite (SorterOpen), PostgreSQL (GroupAggregate for pre-sorted input) |
[[EXPLAIN:hash-vs-sort-aggregation|Hash aggregation vs sort aggregation tradeoffs]]
Hash aggregation wins when the number of groups fits in memory (which is almost always — even 1 million distinct groups consume ~100MB of accumulator state). Sort aggregation wins when: (a) the data is already sorted by the group key (e.g., an index scan in key order), (b) the query includes `ORDER BY` on the group key (sort serves double duty), or (c) memory is extremely constrained (sort aggregation uses O(1) extra memory because it processes groups sequentially).
For your build, hash aggregation is the right choice. It's simpler, faster for the common case, and teaches the more generalizable pattern — the same hash-based grouping appears in MapReduce's shuffle phase (each reducer gets one hash partition), in Apache Spark's `HashAggregateExec` operator, and in distributed stream processing where data is partitioned by key hash across workers.
---
## Compiling Aggregates: New Opcodes
Aggregates require new opcodes because they break the "one row in, one row out" pattern of your existing VM. A normal SELECT processes each row independently; an aggregate SELECT must accumulate state across all rows before producing any output.
```c
// New opcodes for aggregate execution
OP_AGG_INIT,      // Initialize accumulator group in register p1, count p2, types from string pool p3
OP_AGG_STEP,      // Feed value in register p2 to accumulator p1, aggregate index p3
OP_AGG_FINAL,     // Finalize accumulator p1, aggregate index p2, result into register p3
// For GROUP BY
OP_AGG_GROUP_INIT, // Initialize group hash table, p1 = num key cols, p2 = num aggregates
OP_AGG_GROUP_STEP, // Feed key registers p1..p1+p2-1 and agg value p3 to group table
OP_AGG_GROUP_ITER, // Iterate groups: set p1 = current group index, jump to p2 when done
OP_AGG_GROUP_KEY,  // Read group key column p2 from current group into register p3
OP_AGG_GROUP_VAL,  // Read aggregate result p2 from current group into register p3
```
### Compiling Ungrouped Aggregates
For `SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(salary) FROM employees`:
```
addr  opcode          p1   p2   p3   comment
----  --------------  ---  ---  ---  -------
0     Init            0    1    0    Start
1     OpenRead        0    5    4    employees
2     AggInit         1    2    0    init 2 accumulators at r1
3     Rewind          0    9    0    jump if empty
4     AggStep         1    0    0    COUNT(*) — step acc 0 with NULL (counts all)
5     Column          0    2    3    salary → r3
6     AggStep         1    3    1    SUM — step acc 1 with r3
7     Next            0    4    0    loop
8     Goto            0    9    0    fall through
9     AggFinal        1    0    4    finalize acc 0 → r4 (COUNT result)
10    AggFinal        1    1    5    finalize acc 1 → r5 (SUM result)
11    ResultRow       4    2    0    output r4, r5
12    Close           0    0    0
13    Halt            0    0    0
```
The VM handlers:
```c
case OP_AGG_INIT: {
    int base_reg = op->p1;
    int agg_count = op->p2;
    // Allocate accumulators in the register space
    // We store a pointer to the accumulator array in the base register
    Accumulator *accs = calloc(agg_count, sizeof(Accumulator));
    // The agg types are encoded in the string pool or a separate array
    // For simplicity, store the types in a companion structure
    for (int i = 0; i < agg_count; i++) {
        accumulator_init(&accs[i], vm->program->agg_types[i]);
    }
    vm->registers[base_reg].type = VAL_BLOB;
    vm->registers[base_reg].s.data = (char*)accs;
    vm->registers[base_reg].s.length = agg_count;
    break;
}
case OP_AGG_STEP: {
    int base_reg = op->p1;
    int val_reg = op->p2;
    int agg_idx = op->p3;
    Accumulator *accs = (Accumulator*)vm->registers[base_reg].s.data;
    accumulator_step(&accs[agg_idx], &vm->registers[val_reg]);
    break;
}
case OP_AGG_FINAL: {
    int base_reg = op->p1;
    int agg_idx = op->p2;
    int dest_reg = op->p3;
    Accumulator *accs = (Accumulator*)vm->registers[base_reg].s.data;
    accumulator_finalize(&accs[agg_idx], &vm->registers[dest_reg]);
    break;
}
```
Note for `COUNT(*)`: the `OP_AGG_STEP` for COUNT(*) doesn't need a real column value — it steps with a dummy non-NULL value (or the opcode handler can special-case COUNT_STAR to skip the value entirely). The key distinction: COUNT(*) calls `accumulator_step` for *every* row. COUNT(col) calls it only when the column value is non-NULL.
### Compiling GROUP BY Aggregates
For `SELECT department, COUNT(*), AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department`:

![GROUP BY → HAVING → Output Pipeline](./diagrams/diag-groupby-having-pipeline.svg)

The bytecode structure is fundamentally different. Instead of one set of accumulators, you maintain a hash table of groups, each with its own accumulators. After the scan loop, you iterate the hash table to produce output rows:
```
addr  opcode            p1   p2   p3   comment
----  ----------------  ---  ---  ---  -------
0     Init              0    1    0    Start
1     OpenRead          0    5    4    employees
2     AggGroupInit      1    2    0    init group table: 1 key col, 2 aggregates
3     Rewind            0    12   0    jump if empty
4     Column            0    0    2    department → r2 (group key)
5     Column            0    2    3    salary → r3 (agg input)
6     AggGroupStep      2    1    0    step group: key r2..r2+0, acc 0 (COUNT*)
7     AggGroupStep      2    1    1    step group: key r2..r2+0, acc 1 (AVG) with r3
8     Next              0    4    0    loop
9     Goto              0    12   0    fall through
      --- Output phase: iterate groups ---
10    AggGroupIter      0    16   0    set current group; jump to Close when done
11    AggGroupKey       0    0    4    group key col 0 → r4 (department)
12    AggGroupVal       0    0    5    agg result 0 → r5 (COUNT)
13    AggGroupVal       0    1    6    agg result 1 → r6 (AVG)
14    ResultRow         4    3    0    output r4, r5, r6
15    Goto              0    10   0    next group
16    Close             0    0    0
17    Halt              0    0    0
```
The two-phase structure is critical:
1. **Accumulation phase** (addrs 3-9): Scan all rows, feed each row's group key and aggregate values into the group hash table.
2. **Output phase** (addrs 10-15): Iterate the hash table, finalize each group's accumulators, and emit result rows.
No output rows are produced during the accumulation phase. This means aggregate queries buffer the entire result set (as accumulators, not as full rows — much more compact) before emitting anything. For 1 million rows with 50 groups, you store 50 accumulator sets, not 1 million rows. The memory cost is proportional to the number of *groups*, not the number of *input rows*.
The VM handlers for the group operations work with the `GroupHashTable`:
```c
case OP_AGG_GROUP_INIT: {
    int key_count = op->p1;
    int agg_count = op->p2;
    GroupHashTable *ht = group_table_create(key_count, agg_count,
                                              vm->program->agg_types);
    // Store the hash table pointer in a VM-level structure
    vm->group_table = ht;
    vm->group_key_count = key_count;
    vm->group_agg_count = agg_count;
    break;
}
case OP_AGG_GROUP_STEP: {
    int key_base_reg = op->p1;
    int key_count = vm->group_key_count;
    int agg_idx = op->p3;
    // Collect group key values from registers
    Value keys[16];
    for (int i = 0; i < key_count; i++) {
        keys[i] = vm->registers[key_base_reg + i];
    }
    // Find or create the group
    GroupEntry *group = group_table_find_or_create(
        vm->group_table, keys, key_count,
        vm->group_agg_count, vm->program->agg_types);
    // Step the specified accumulator
    // For COUNT(*), the value doesn't matter
    if (vm->program->agg_types[agg_idx] == AGG_COUNT_STAR) {
        Value dummy = { .type = VAL_INTEGER, .i = 1 };
        accumulator_step(&group->accumulators[agg_idx], &dummy);
    } else {
        // The value register is encoded elsewhere; simplification:
        // use a dedicated register for each aggregate's input
        int val_reg = key_base_reg + key_count + agg_idx;
        accumulator_step(&group->accumulators[agg_idx],
                          &vm->registers[val_reg]);
    }
    break;
}
case OP_AGG_GROUP_ITER: {
    // Finalize all accumulators in the current group, advance to next
    if (vm->group_iter_index >= vm->group_table->count) {
        vm->pc = op->p2;  // jump to end
        continue;
    }
    // Current group is at vm->group_iter_index
    // Caller reads keys and values with AggGroupKey / AggGroupVal
    break;
}
case OP_AGG_GROUP_KEY: {
    int key_col = op->p2;
    int dest_reg = op->p3;
    GroupEntry *entry = &vm->group_table->entries[vm->group_iter_index];
    vm->registers[dest_reg] = entry->key_values[key_col];
    break;
}
case OP_AGG_GROUP_VAL: {
    int agg_idx = op->p2;
    int dest_reg = op->p3;
    GroupEntry *entry = &vm->group_table->entries[vm->group_iter_index];
    accumulator_finalize(&entry->accumulators[agg_idx],
                          &vm->registers[dest_reg]);
    break;
}
```
---
## HAVING: Filtering Groups After Aggregation
HAVING is to GROUP BY what WHERE is to SELECT — a filter. But WHERE filters individual rows *before* aggregation, and HAVING filters groups *after* aggregation. The distinction matters because HAVING can reference aggregate results:
```sql
SELECT department, AVG(salary) 
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department 
HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000;
```
This query cannot be expressed with WHERE. You can't write `WHERE AVG(salary) > 50000` because AVG doesn't exist until after grouping. HAVING runs on the output of the group hash table, not on individual rows.
In bytecode, HAVING is a conditional jump in the output phase — between reading the group's aggregate results and emitting the ResultRow:
```
      --- Output phase with HAVING ---
10    AggGroupIter      0    18   0    iterate groups
11    AggGroupKey       0    0    4    department → r4
12    AggGroupVal       0    1    5    AVG(salary) → r5
13    Real              50000.0 6 0    50000.0 → r6
14    Le                5    10   6    if AVG <= 50000, skip to next group
15    AggGroupVal       0    0    7    COUNT(*) → r7 (if needed)
16    ResultRow         4    2    0    output r4, r5
17    Goto              0    10   0    next group
18    Close             0    0    0
```
The `OP_LE` at addr 14 jumps back to `AggGroupIter` (addr 10) if the condition fails — effectively skipping this group. This is the same conditional-jump-to-skip pattern you use for WHERE filtering (Milestone 6), applied to groups instead of rows.
The compiler must distinguish between WHERE predicates (applied per-row during the scan phase) and HAVING predicates (applied per-group during the output phase). The distinction is straightforward in the AST: HAVING is a separate clause that the parser already produces as a distinct node.
```c
static void compile_select_with_groupby(Compiler *c, AstNode *node,
                                          int table_idx) {
    // ... open cursor, init group table ...
    // Scan phase: WHERE filtering happens here
    int loop_start = c->program->op_count;
    if (node->data.select.where_clause) {
        // Compile WHERE — same as Milestone 6
        int skip = compile_where(c, node->data.select.where_clause,
                                   cursor, table_idx);
        // skip jumps to OP_NEXT on failure
    }
    // Load group keys and aggregate inputs
    // ... AggGroupStep for each aggregate ...
    // OP_NEXT loops back to loop_start
    // Output phase: HAVING filtering happens here
    int iter_start = c->program->op_count;
    int iter_addr = emit(c, OP_AGG_GROUP_ITER, 0, 0, 0, "next group");
    // Load group keys and aggregate values into registers
    // ...
    if (node->data.select.having_clause) {
        // Compile HAVING — operates on aggregate result registers
        int having_skip = compile_having(c, node->data.select.having_clause);
        // having_skip jumps to iter_start to skip this group
    }
    emit(c, OP_RESULT_ROW, first_result_reg, result_count, 0, "output");
    emit(c, OP_GOTO, 0, iter_start, 0, "next group");
    // Patch iter_addr's jump target to the Close instruction
}
```
---
## Nested Loop JOIN: Combining Tables

![Nested Loop Join Execution](./diagrams/diag-nested-loop-join.svg)

A JOIN combines rows from two tables based on a condition. The **nested loop join** is the simplest and most general join algorithm: for each row in the outer table, scan the inner table looking for matching rows.
```sql
SELECT o.id, c.name 
FROM orders o 
INNER JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE c.country = 'Germany';
```
The nested loop structure:
```
for each row in orders (outer):
    for each row in customers (inner):
        if orders.customer_id == customers.id:
            if customers.country == 'Germany':
                output(orders.id, customers.name)
```
This is O(n × m) in the worst case — for every row in the outer table, you scan every row in the inner table. With 100,000 orders and 10,000 customers, that's 1 billion comparisons. This is where **index existence on the inner table becomes critical**. If `customers.id` has an index, the inner "scan" becomes an index seek — O(log m) instead of O(m), reducing total work from O(n × m) to O(n × log m).

![Nested Loop Join With vs Without Index](./diagrams/diag-join-with-index.svg)

### Design Decision: Join Algorithms
| Algorithm | Time | Memory | Best When | Used By |
|-----------|------|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Nested loop ✓** | O(n × m) | O(1) | Small tables, index on inner | SQLite (primary), all databases |
| Hash join | O(n + m) | O(min(n,m)) | Large tables, no index, equi-join | PostgreSQL, MySQL 8.0+ |
| Sort-merge join | O(n log n + m log m) | O(1) extra (if in-place sort) | Pre-sorted inputs, many-to-many | PostgreSQL, Oracle |
Nested loop join is the right starting point because: (1) it requires no extra memory, (2) it works with any join condition (not just equality), (3) it naturally benefits from indexes on the inner table, and (4) it's the join algorithm SQLite uses almost exclusively. Hash join is asymptotically better for large equi-joins but requires building a hash table from one input — a topic for optimization later.
[[EXPLAIN:join-algorithm-spectrum|Join algorithms as a spectrum: nested loop, hash join, sort-merge join]]
### Compiling JOIN
The compiler extends `compile_select` to handle the FROM clause with JOIN:
```c
// For: SELECT o.id, c.name FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.cust_id = c.id
// WHERE c.country = 'Germany'
static void compile_join(Compiler *c, AstNode *node, int outer_table_idx,
                           int inner_table_idx, AstNode *join_condition,
                           AstNode *where_clause) {
    int outer_root = c->tables[outer_table_idx].root_page;
    int inner_root = c->tables[inner_table_idx].root_page;
    int outer_cols = c->tables[outer_table_idx].column_count;
    int inner_cols = c->tables[inner_table_idx].column_count;
    int outer_cursor = alloc_cursor(c);
    int inner_cursor = alloc_cursor(c);
    int init_addr = emit(c, OP_INIT, 0, 0, 0, "Start");
    // Open both cursors
    int open1 = emit(c, OP_OPEN_READ, outer_cursor, outer_root, outer_cols,
                      "outer: orders");
    patch_jump(c, init_addr, open1);
    emit(c, OP_OPEN_READ, inner_cursor, inner_root, inner_cols,
         "inner: customers");
    // Outer loop
    int rewind_outer = emit(c, OP_REWIND, outer_cursor, 0, 0,
                             "rewind outer");
    int outer_loop = c->program->op_count;
    // Load join key from outer table
    // (For ON o.cust_id = c.id, load o.cust_id)
    int outer_join_col = find_column(c, outer_table_idx, "cust_id");
    int outer_key_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_COLUMN, outer_cursor, outer_join_col, outer_key_reg,
         "o.cust_id");
    // Inner loop: rewind inner cursor for each outer row
    int rewind_inner = emit(c, OP_REWIND, inner_cursor, 0, 0,
                             "rewind inner");
    int inner_loop = c->program->op_count;
    // Load join key from inner table
    int inner_join_col = find_column(c, inner_table_idx, "id");
    int inner_key_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_COLUMN, inner_cursor, inner_join_col, inner_key_reg,
         "c.id");
    // Join condition: skip if keys don't match
    int join_skip = emit(c, OP_NE, outer_key_reg, 0, inner_key_reg,
                          "join: o.cust_id != c.id → skip");
    // WHERE clause on joined result
    int where_skip = -1;
    if (where_clause) {
        // Compile WHERE predicates
        // These may reference columns from either table
        where_skip = compile_join_where(c, where_clause,
                                          outer_cursor, outer_table_idx,
                                          inner_cursor, inner_table_idx);
    }
    // Load output columns
    int first_result_reg = c->next_register;
    // o.id
    int oid_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_COLUMN, outer_cursor,
         find_column(c, outer_table_idx, "id"), oid_reg, "o.id");
    // c.name
    int cname_reg = alloc_register(c);
    emit(c, OP_COLUMN, inner_cursor,
         find_column(c, inner_table_idx, "name"), cname_reg, "c.name");
    emit(c, OP_RESULT_ROW, first_result_reg, 2, 0, "output");
    // Inner Next
    int inner_next = emit(c, OP_NEXT, inner_cursor, inner_loop, 0,
                           "advance inner");
    // Patch skips to inner_next
    patch_jump(c, join_skip, inner_next);
    if (where_skip >= 0) patch_jump(c, where_skip, inner_next);
    // Patch inner rewind's empty-jump to outer_next
    int outer_next = emit(c, OP_NEXT, outer_cursor, outer_loop, 0,
                           "advance outer");
    patch_jump(c, rewind_inner, outer_next);
    // Close and halt
    int close1 = emit(c, OP_CLOSE, outer_cursor, 0, 0, "close outer");
    emit(c, OP_CLOSE, inner_cursor, 0, 0, "close inner");
    int halt = emit(c, OP_HALT, 0, 0, 0, "done");
    patch_jump(c, rewind_outer, close1);
    c->program->n_registers = c->next_register;
}
```

![EXPLAIN Output Anatomy](./diagrams/diag-explain-output.svg)

The EXPLAIN output for this join:
```
addr  opcode         p1   p2   p3   comment
----  -------------  ---  ---  ---  -------
0     Init           0    1    0    Start
1     OpenRead       0    5    4    orders
2     OpenRead       1    8    3    customers
3     Rewind         0    14   0    rewind outer
4     Column         0    2    1    o.cust_id → r1
5     Rewind         1    13   0    rewind inner
6     Column         1    0    2    c.id → r2
7     Ne             1    11   2    skip if o.cust_id != c.id
8     Column         0    0    3    o.id → r3
9     Column         1    1    4    c.name → r4
10    ResultRow      3    2    0    output
11    Next           1    6    0    advance inner
12    Goto           0    13   0    fall through
13    Next           0    4    0    advance outer
14    Close          0    0    0
15    Close          1    0    0
16    Halt           0    0    0
```
Study the loop structure. The outer loop (addrs 3-13) iterates `orders`. For each outer row, the inner loop (addrs 5-11) scans `customers` from the beginning. `OP_REWIND` at addr 5 resets the inner cursor *every iteration of the outer loop* — this is the "nested" part of nested loop. The join condition at addr 7 (NE comparison) skips non-matching inner rows by jumping to `OP_NEXT` at addr 11.
### Index Nested Loop Join
If `customers.id` has an index, the inner loop can be replaced with an index seek — a single B+tree traversal instead of a full table scan per outer row. The compiler emits `OP_SEEK_ROWID` or an index seek opcode instead of `OP_REWIND` + full scan:
```
5     SeekRowid      1    13   1    seek customers cursor to rowid r1
6     Column         1    1    4    c.name → r4
...
```
The query planner (Milestone 8) decides whether to use the index. With an index, the join cost drops from O(n × m) to O(n × log m) — for 100,000 orders and 10,000 customers, that's 100,000 × 14 ≈ 1.4 million page reads instead of 1 billion comparisons. The planner's join ordering algorithm (from Milestone 8) also decides which table is outer and which is inner — the smaller table (or the one with a filter that reduces it) should typically be outer to minimize the number of inner scans.
---
## Multiple Aggregates in a Single Query
A query can compute multiple aggregates simultaneously:
```sql
SELECT department, COUNT(*), SUM(salary), AVG(salary), MIN(salary), MAX(salary)
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;
```
This works naturally with the accumulator array approach — each group entry maintains one accumulator per aggregate function. During the scan, each row updates all accumulators for its group. During the output phase, each accumulator is finalized independently.
The key implementation detail: the compiler must parse the SELECT list, identify which expressions are aggregate function calls, and assign each one an index in the accumulator array. Non-aggregate expressions in a GROUP BY query must be either group-key columns or constants — a `SELECT department, name FROM employees GROUP BY department` is a SQL error (which name? there might be many per department).
```c
// During compilation, analyze the SELECT column list
typedef struct {
    AggType  type;
    int      input_col_idx;  // column index for the aggregate's argument
    bool     is_star;        // true for COUNT(*)
} AggInfo;
int parse_aggregate_functions(AstNode **columns, int col_count,
                                AggInfo *agg_infos, int *agg_count,
                                int *group_key_cols, int *group_key_count) {
    *agg_count = 0;
    *group_key_count = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < col_count; i++) {
        AstNode *col = columns[i];
        if (col->type == NODE_FUNCTION_CALL) {
            const char *func_name = col->data.function.name;
            AggInfo *info = &agg_infos[*agg_count];
            if (strcasecmp(func_name, "COUNT") == 0) {
                if (col->data.function.arg_count == 0 ||
                    (col->data.function.args[0]->type == NODE_WILDCARD)) {
                    info->type = AGG_COUNT_STAR;
                    info->is_star = true;
                } else {
                    info->type = AGG_COUNT;
                    info->is_star = false;
                    // Resolve column name to index
                    info->input_col_idx = resolve_column(
                        col->data.function.args[0]);
                }
            } else if (strcasecmp(func_name, "SUM") == 0) {
                info->type = AGG_SUM;
                info->input_col_idx = resolve_column(
                    col->data.function.args[0]);
            } else if (strcasecmp(func_name, "AVG") == 0) {
                info->type = AGG_AVG;
                info->input_col_idx = resolve_column(
                    col->data.function.args[0]);
            } else if (strcasecmp(func_name, "MIN") == 0) {
                info->type = AGG_MIN;
                info->input_col_idx = resolve_column(
                    col->data.function.args[0]);
            } else if (strcasecmp(func_name, "MAX") == 0) {
                info->type = AGG_MAX;
                info->input_col_idx = resolve_column(
                    col->data.function.args[0]);
            }
            (*agg_count)++;
        } else if (col->type == NODE_IDENTIFIER) {
            // This must be a GROUP BY column
            group_key_cols[*group_key_count] = resolve_column(col);
            (*group_key_count)++;
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
```
---
## GROUP BY Without ORDER BY
An important specification detail: `GROUP BY` does not imply any ordering of the output. `SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department` can return departments in any order — alphabetical, hash order, insertion order, or random. Only an explicit `ORDER BY department` guarantees sorted output.
With hash-based grouping, the output order is hash-table iteration order — which is essentially random from the user's perspective. This is correct behavior. If the user wants sorted groups, they must add `ORDER BY`, which requires either post-sorting the group results or switching to sort-based aggregation.
For your build, hash-table iteration order is sufficient. The acceptance criteria explicitly state that GROUP BY without ORDER BY can return groups in any order.
---
## Parsing Aggregate Functions and GROUP BY/HAVING
The parser needs extensions to recognize:
1. **Function calls** in the SELECT list: `COUNT(*)`, `SUM(salary)`, `AVG(x)`
2. **GROUP BY clause**: after WHERE, before HAVING
3. **HAVING clause**: after GROUP BY
### Function Call Parsing
In `parse_primary` (from Milestone 2), add a case for identifiers followed by `(`:
```c
// In parse_primary, after the identifier check:
if (check(p, TOK_IDENTIFIER)) {
    Token *name_tok = advance(p);
    // Check if this is a function call
    if (check(p, TOK_LPAREN)) {
        advance(p);  // consume (
        AstNode *func = ast_alloc(NODE_FUNCTION_CALL,
                                    name_tok->line, name_tok->col);
        func->data.function.name = token_to_string(name_tok);
        // Parse arguments
        func->data.function.args = malloc(8 * sizeof(AstNode*));
        func->data.function.arg_count = 0;
        if (check(p, TOK_STAR)) {
            // COUNT(*)
            AstNode *star = ast_alloc(NODE_WILDCARD,
                                        peek(p)->line, peek(p)->col);
            advance(p);
            func->data.function.args[0] = star;
            func->data.function.arg_count = 1;
        } else if (!check(p, TOK_RPAREN)) {
            // Regular argument list
            do {
                AstNode *arg = parse_expression(p);
                if (!arg) return NULL;
                func->data.function.args[func->data.function.arg_count++] = arg;
            } while (check(p, TOK_COMMA) && advance(p));
        }
        if (!expect(p, TOK_RPAREN)) return NULL;
        return func;
    }
    // Not a function call — regular identifier or column ref
    // ... existing identifier handling ...
}
```
### GROUP BY and HAVING Parsing
Extend `parse_select` to handle GROUP BY and HAVING after the WHERE clause:
```c
// After WHERE parsing in parse_select:
// GROUP BY clause (optional)
node->data.select.group_by = NULL;
node->data.select.group_by_count = 0;
if (check_keyword(p, "GROUP")) {
    advance(p);
    if (!expect_keyword(p, "BY")) return NULL;
    int capacity = 4;
    node->data.select.group_by = malloc(capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
    do {
        AstNode *expr = parse_expression(p);
        if (!expr) return NULL;
        if (node->data.select.group_by_count >= capacity) {
            capacity *= 2;
            node->data.select.group_by = realloc(
                node->data.select.group_by, capacity * sizeof(AstNode*));
        }
        node->data.select.group_by[node->data.select.group_by_count++] = expr;
    } while (check(p, TOK_COMMA) && advance(p));
}
// HAVING clause (optional, only valid after GROUP BY)
node->data.select.having_clause = NULL;
if (check_keyword(p, "HAVING")) {
    advance(p);
    node->data.select.having_clause = parse_expression(p);
    if (!node->data.select.having_clause) return NULL;
}
```
---
## Testing: Comprehensive Aggregate and JOIN Verification
### Test: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(col)
```c
void test_count_star_vs_col(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_count.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 10);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, NULL);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (3, 30);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (4, NULL);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (5, 50);", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(val) FROM t;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 1);
    assert(rc.rows[0][0].i == 5);   // COUNT(*) counts all 5 rows
    assert(rc.rows[0][1].i == 3);   // COUNT(val) counts 3 non-NULL values
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_count.db");
    printf("PASS: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(col) NULL handling\n");
}
```
### Test: SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX
```c
void test_aggregate_functions(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_agg.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 10);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 20);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (3, 30);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (4, NULL);", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT SUM(val), AVG(val), MIN(val), MAX(val) FROM t;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 1);
    // SUM = 10 + 20 + 30 = 60 (NULLs ignored)
    assert(fabs(rc.rows[0][0].r - 60.0) < 0.001);
    // AVG = 60 / 3 = 20.0 (NULLs ignored in both sum and count)
    assert(rc.rows[0][1].type == VAL_REAL);  // must be REAL
    assert(fabs(rc.rows[0][1].r - 20.0) < 0.001);
    // MIN = 10
    assert(rc.rows[0][2].i == 10);
    // MAX = 30
    assert(rc.rows[0][3].i == 30);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_agg.db");
    printf("PASS: SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX with NULL handling\n");
}
```
### Test: AVG Returns REAL for INTEGER Input
```c
void test_avg_returns_real(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_avg_real.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 1);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 2);", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT AVG(val) FROM t;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 1);
    assert(rc.rows[0][0].type == VAL_REAL);
    assert(fabs(rc.rows[0][0].r - 1.5) < 0.001);  // 3/2 = 1.5, not 1
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_avg_real.db");
    printf("PASS: AVG returns REAL for INTEGER column\n");
}
```
### Test: Empty Table Aggregates
```c
void test_empty_aggregates(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_empty_agg.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    // Table is empty
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(val), SUM(val), AVG(val) FROM t;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 1);
    assert(rc.rows[0][0].i == 0);              // COUNT(*) = 0
    assert(rc.rows[0][1].i == 0);              // COUNT(val) = 0
    assert(rc.rows[0][2].type == VAL_NULL);    // SUM = NULL (not 0!)
    assert(rc.rows[0][3].type == VAL_NULL);    // AVG = NULL (not 0!)
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_empty_agg.db");
    printf("PASS: empty table aggregate defaults\n");
}
```
### Test: GROUP BY with Aggregates
```c
void test_group_by(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_groupby.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE emp (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "dept TEXT, salary INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO emp VALUES (1, 'Eng', 100);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO emp VALUES (2, 'Eng', 120);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO emp VALUES (3, 'Sales', 90);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO emp VALUES (4, 'Sales', 80);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO emp VALUES (5, 'Eng', 110);", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT dept, COUNT(*), SUM(salary) "
                    "FROM emp GROUP BY dept;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 2);  // 2 departments
    // Find Eng and Sales in results (order not guaranteed)
    for (int i = 0; i < 2; i++) {
        if (strcmp(rc.rows[i][0].s.data, "Eng") == 0) {
            assert(rc.rows[i][1].i == 3);      // 3 engineers
            assert(fabs(rc.rows[i][2].r - 330.0) < 0.001);  // 100+120+110
        } else if (strcmp(rc.rows[i][0].s.data, "Sales") == 0) {
            assert(rc.rows[i][1].i == 2);      // 2 salespeople
            assert(fabs(rc.rows[i][2].r - 170.0) < 0.001);  // 90+80
        } else {
            assert(false);  // unexpected department
        }
    }
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_groupby.db");
    printf("PASS: GROUP BY with multiple aggregates\n");
}
```
### Test: HAVING Filters Groups
```c
void test_having(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_having.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE emp (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "dept TEXT, salary INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO emp VALUES (1, 'Eng', 100);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO emp VALUES (2, 'Eng', 120);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO emp VALUES (3, 'Sales', 90);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO emp VALUES (4, 'Eng', 110);", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM emp "
                    "GROUP BY dept HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 1);  // only Eng has > 2 employees
    assert(strcmp(rc.rows[0][0].s.data, "Eng") == 0);
    assert(rc.rows[0][1].i == 3);
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_having.db");
    printf("PASS: HAVING filters groups\n");
}
```
### Test: INNER JOIN
```c
void test_inner_join(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_join.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "cust_id INTEGER, total INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "name TEXT);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1, 'Alice');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO customers VALUES (2, 'Bob');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO customers VALUES (3, 'Charlie');", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO orders VALUES (10, 1, 100);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO orders VALUES (11, 2, 200);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO orders VALUES (12, 1, 150);", NULL, NULL);
    // Customer 3 has no orders
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT o.id, c.name FROM orders o "
                    "INNER JOIN customers c ON o.cust_id = c.id;",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 3);  // 3 orders with matching customers
    // Charlie has no orders → not in result (INNER JOIN)
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_join.db");
    printf("PASS: INNER JOIN combines matching rows\n");
}
```

```c
void test_join_with_where(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_jwhere.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "cust_id INTEGER, total INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
```
