```c
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'Germany');",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO customers VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'France');",
                NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO orders VALUES (10, 1, 100);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO orders VALUES (11, 2, 200);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO orders VALUES (12, 1, 150);", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT o.id, c.name FROM orders o "
                    "JOIN customers c ON o.cust_id = c.id "
                    "WHERE c.country = 'Germany';",
                collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 2);  // only Alice's 2 orders
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_jwhere.db");
    printf("PASS: JOIN with WHERE filters correctly\n");
}
```
### Test: Multiple Aggregates in One Query
```c
void test_multiple_aggregates(void) {
    Database *db = db_open("test_multi_agg.db");
    execute_sql(db, "CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
                    "val INTEGER);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 10);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (2, 20);", NULL, NULL);
    execute_sql(db, "INSERT INTO t VALUES (3, 30);", NULL, NULL);
    ResultCollector rc = {0};
    execute_sql(db, "SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(val), AVG(val), MIN(val), MAX(val) "
                    "FROM t;", collect_results, &rc);
    assert(rc.row_count == 1);
    assert(rc.rows[0][0].i == 3);                       // COUNT
    assert(fabs(rc.rows[0][1].r - 60.0) < 0.001);     // SUM
    assert(fabs(rc.rows[0][2].r - 20.0) < 0.001);     // AVG
    assert(rc.rows[0][3].i == 10);                      // MIN
    assert(rc.rows[0][4].i == 30);                      // MAX
    db_close(db);
    unlink("test_multi_agg.db");
    printf("PASS: multiple aggregates in single query\n");
}
```
---
## Three-Level View: What Happens During a GROUP BY Query
**Level 1 — Public API**: You execute `SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000`. You get back one row per department whose average salary exceeds 50,000. Each row contains the department name and the average salary as a floating-point number.
**Level 2 — VM Engine**: The compiled bytecode opens a cursor on the `employees` table and rewinds to the first row. For each row, it loads the `department` column (group key) and `salary` column (aggregate input) into registers. `OP_AGG_GROUP_STEP` hashes the department value, finds or creates the group entry in the hash table, and calls `accumulator_step` on the group's AVG accumulator — which increments its count and adds the salary to its running sum (if non-NULL). After all rows are processed, the output phase iterates the hash table. For each group, `OP_AGG_GROUP_VAL` finalizes the AVG accumulator (dividing sum by count, producing a REAL), and the HAVING check (`OP_LE` comparing the result to 50000.0) skips groups that don't qualify. Qualifying groups emit `OP_RESULT_ROW`.
**Level 3 — Buffer Pool / Disk**: Each `OP_COLUMN` call reads from the current B-tree leaf page through `fetch_page`. For a 10,000-row table with ~143 leaf pages, the scan touches each leaf once — 143 `fetch_page` calls, all buffer pool hits after the first pass. The hash table lives in heap memory, allocated by `calloc`/`realloc`. With 50 departments, the hash table consumes ~50 × (key size + accumulator size) ≈ 50 × 100 bytes = 5 KB — trivial. The entire aggregate operation completes in the time it takes to scan the table once: ~0.3ms for 10,000 rows with a warm cache. No extra disk I/O beyond the single scan.
---
## Knowledge Cascade: What Aggregates and JOINs Unlock
**→ Hash Aggregation Is MapReduce's Shuffle Phase.** The GROUP BY execution you just built — hash the key, route to a bucket, accumulate in that bucket — is structurally identical to MapReduce's shuffle phase. In Hadoop MapReduce, the mapper emits (key, value) pairs. The framework partitions by key hash, routes each partition to a reducer, and the reducer aggregates all values for its key. Your `group_table_find_or_create` is the local combiner; the hash table is the in-memory equivalent of the shuffle buffer. Apache Spark's `HashAggregateExec` operator does exactly what your `OP_AGG_GROUP_STEP` does — hash the group key, find or create the accumulator slot, step. The same pattern appears in Flink's windowed aggregations, Kafka Streams' `KTable` aggregations, and even in GPU computing where parallel reduction operations use thread-block-level hash tables for partial aggregation before cross-block merging. Understanding GROUP BY means you understand the core primitive of distributed data processing.
**→ The Join Algorithm Spectrum Maps to Any Set-Intersection Problem.** Nested loop join is O(n × m). Hash join builds a hash table from the smaller input (O(n) build, O(m) probe) for O(n + m) total. Sort-merge join sorts both inputs (O(n log n + m log m)) then merges in one pass. This spectrum — brute force, hash-based, sort-based — applies to any problem involving matching elements from two collections. **Git merge** correlates changes from two branches: it uses a merge-base (sort-merge analogy). **Database replication** resolves conflicts by comparing change logs — hash-based or sort-based depending on the system. **Network packet matching** in firewalls compares incoming packets against rule tables — fast-path uses hash tables (like hash join), slow-path uses sequential scan (like nested loop). **DNA sequence alignment** (BLAST algorithm) uses seed-and-extend, which is conceptually a hash join where the seeds are the hash keys. Once you see the join spectrum, you see it in every domain where two collections must be correlated.
[[EXPLAIN:join-algorithm-spectrum|Join algorithms as a spectrum: nested loop, hash join, sort-merge join]]
**→ The Accumulator Pattern Is Stream Processing.** Your `accumulator_init` / `accumulator_step` / `accumulator_finalize` lifecycle is the exact interface that Apache Flink calls `AggregateFunction<IN, ACC, OUT>` — with `createAccumulator()`, `add(value, accumulator)`, and `getResult(accumulator)`. Kafka Streams' `KGroupedStream.aggregate()` takes the same three functions. Even Python's `functools.reduce` follows this pattern: initial value → step function → final result. Financial systems computing running totals, VWAP (Volume Weighted Average Price), or moving averages use the same accumulator structure — except their "finalize" might return a time-series of partial results rather than a single value. Understanding the accumulator pattern means you can implement any streaming aggregation, in any framework, in any domain.
**→ NULL Propagation in Aggregates as Monad-Like Behavior.** The way NULL "contaminates" aggregate computations — SUM ignores NULLs but AVG's denominator also ignores them; MIN/MAX skip NULLs entirely; empty-set SUM returns NULL rather than 0 — follows a pattern that functional programmers recognize as **monadic propagation**. In Haskell's `Maybe` monad, `Nothing >>= f` returns `Nothing` — the unknown propagates. In Rust's `Option<T>`, the `?` operator short-circuits on `None`. SQL's aggregate NULL handling is a specialized version of the same concept: "unknown input → adjusted computation → possibly unknown output." The difference is that SQL's rules are table-specific (COUNT is the exception that returns 0 instead of NULL for empty input), while monadic propagation follows uniform algebraic laws. Understanding why SQL's NULL handling is *almost* monadic — but not quite — gives you insight into why NULL in SQL is a perpetual source of bugs: it violates the principle of least surprise by being inconsistent across operations.
> **🔭 Deep Dive**: For a thorough treatment of NULL semantics in SQL and their formal relationship to three-valued logic and domain theory, see *SQL and Relational Theory* by C.J. Date, Chapter 4 ("Missing Information"). For join algorithms, see *Database Internals* by Alex Petrov, Chapter 13 ("Join Algorithms").
---
## System Awareness
You've built the query execution layer for aggregate functions and JOINs — the features that make SQL actually useful for data analysis. Aggregates with GROUP BY use a hash table to maintain per-group accumulator state, supporting COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX with correct NULL handling and empty-set semantics. HAVING filters groups after aggregation. Nested loop JOIN combines rows from two tables, with optional index acceleration on the inner table.

![SELECT Execution End-to-End Trace](./diagrams/diag-select-execution-trace.svg)

Without aggregates, your database can only return individual rows — it cannot answer questions like "how many?" or "what's the average?" Without JOINs, it can only query one table at a time — it cannot combine related data from normalized schemas. With both, your SQL dialect covers the vast majority of real-world query patterns.
These features connect to every layer of the system. The **parser** (Milestone 2) now handles function calls, GROUP BY, and HAVING clauses. The **compiler** (Milestone 3) emits new opcodes for accumulator management and nested-loop control flow. The **VM** (Milestone 3) executes those opcodes using the accumulator pattern and dual-cursor iteration. The **B-tree** and **buffer pool** (Milestones 4-5) provide the page-level data access that cursors traverse. The **query planner** (Milestone 8) decides join ordering and whether to use indexes for the inner table of a nested loop join. The **transaction layer** (Milestones 9-10) ensures that multi-table queries see consistent data. Every layer participates; no layer is bypassed.
Your database engine is now complete. Eleven milestones spanning the full pipeline: SQL text → tokens → AST → bytecode → VM → B-tree → buffer pool → disk, with indexes, cost-based planning, ACID transactions, and rich query capabilities. You haven't just built a database — you've internalized the architectural principles that underlie every database system in production today.
---

## System Overview

![System Overview](./diagrams/system-overview.svg)




# TDD

A test-first construction of a relational database engine from raw bytes to query optimizer. Each module isolates one architectural layer so tests encode invariants before implementation: FSM correctness, page-layout binary contracts, crash-safety journal ordering, and WAL frame checksums. Domain profile: data-storage / expert — every on-disk structure gets a byte-level layout diagram; every stateful protocol gets a state-machine diagram.



<!-- TDD_MOD_ID: build-sqlite-m1 -->
# Full Technical Design Specification — SQL Tokenizer
**Module ID:** `build-sqlite-m1`
**Project:** `build-sqlite` — Build Your Own SQLite
**Language:** C (ISO C11)
**Level:** Expert
**Domain:** Data Storage Systems
**Date:** 2026-03-16
---
## 1. Module Charter
The SQL Tokenizer is the entry point of the entire SQLite query pipeline. Every subsequent component — the parser, query planner, and executor — receives its input as a flat array of `Token` values produced by this module. No downstream component ever examines raw SQL bytes again after the tokenizer completes.
The tokenizer operates as a **zero-copy, single-pass finite-state machine** over a caller-owned string buffer. It emits tokens whose `start` pointer addresses bytes inside the original buffer rather than allocating new memory. This design means the tokenizer itself performs **zero heap allocations** under all normal operating conditions; the caller decides how to store the resulting token stream.
**Deliverables this module must produce:**
| Artifact | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Token type definitions | `src/lexer.h` | Public API, enums, structs |
| Tokenizer implementation | `src/lexer.c` | FSM core, keyword table |
| Unit test suite | `tests/test_lexer.c` | 5 spec groups, 35+ assertions |
| Build integration | `Makefile` | `make test-lexer` target |
**Acceptance gate:** All 5 test specifications pass with `make test-lexer` returning exit code 0, and `valgrind --tool=massif` shows zero heap allocations attributed to any `lexer_*` function.
---
## 2. Prerequisite Knowledge
Before implementing this module, the engineer must be comfortable with:
- **Pointer arithmetic in C:** `start + length` must address one byte past the final character of a lexeme without triggering undefined behavior. The buffer must be NUL-terminated or its length known.
- **`const char *` aliasing rules:** The tokenizer never writes through `Token.start`. Any caller that passes a string literal satisfies the contract.
- **Two's-complement integer representation:** Line and column counters are `int`, not `size_t`. At 2³¹ − 1 characters, overflow is theoretically possible but acceptable for SQL input sizes.
- **Binary search (`bsearch`):** The keyword table uses `bsearch` from `<stdlib.h>`. You must understand the comparator contract: negative/zero/positive return values and `const void *` casting.
- **`<ctype.h>` locale sensitivity:** Use only `isalpha`, `isdigit`, `isspace`, `toupper` — all of which are safe on `unsigned char` input when SQL is ASCII. Pass `(unsigned char)` casts consistently to avoid undefined behavior on signed `char` platforms.
---
## 3. File Structure and Creation Order
Create files in the following order. The order matters because later files `#include` earlier ones.
```
build-sqlite/
├── src/
│   ├── lexer.h          ← Step 1: Token types, structs, public API declarations
│   └── lexer.c          ← Step 2: Full implementation
├── tests/
│   └── test_lexer.c     ← Step 3: Test suite
└── Makefile             ← Step 4: Build rules (or amend existing)
```
**Step 1 — `src/lexer.h`** defines all public-facing types and function declarations. It uses an include guard (`#ifndef LEXER_H`). No implementation code lives here.
**Step 2 — `src/lexer.c`** includes `lexer.h` as its first non-comment line. All helper functions (`peek`, `peek_next`, `advance`, `skip_whitespace_and_comments`, `read_string`, `read_number`, `read_identifier_or_keyword`) are declared `static` so they have internal linkage and cannot pollute other translation units.
**Step 3 — `tests/test_lexer.c`** includes only `lexer.h` and `<assert.h>` / `<stdio.h>`. It never includes `lexer.c`. The test binary is compiled by linking `lexer.o` against `test_lexer.o`.
---
## 4. Complete Data Model
### 4.1 Token Type Enumeration
```c
/* src/lexer.h */
#ifndef LEXER_H
#define LEXER_H
typedef enum {
    /* Literals */
    TOK_INTEGER,        /* [0-9]+                        */
    TOK_FLOAT,          /* [0-9]+ '.' [0-9]*             */
    TOK_STRING,         /* '...' with '' escape           */
    TOK_BLOB,           /* X'...' hex blob literal        */
    /* Names */
    TOK_IDENTIFIER,     /* [a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*        */
    TOK_KEYWORD,        /* reserved word (case-insensitive) */
    TOK_QUOTED_ID,      /* "..." double-quoted identifier */
    /* Comparison operators */
    TOK_EQ,             /* =                              */
    TOK_NE,             /* != or <>                       */
    TOK_LT,             /* <                              */
    TOK_GT,             /* >                              */
    TOK_LE,             /* <=                             */
    TOK_GE,             /* >=                             */
    /* Arithmetic operators */
    TOK_PLUS,           /* +                              */
    TOK_MINUS,          /* -  (unary negation at parser)  */
    TOK_STAR,           /* *                              */
    TOK_SLASH,          /* /                              */
    TOK_PERCENT,        /* %                              */
    /* Bitwise / concat */
    TOK_AMPERSAND,      /* &                              */
    TOK_PIPE,           /* |                              */
    TOK_PIPE2,          /* ||  (string concat)            */
    TOK_TILDE,          /* ~                              */
    TOK_LSHIFT,         /* <<                             */
    TOK_RSHIFT,         /* >>                             */
    /* Punctuation */
    TOK_LPAREN,         /* (                              */
    TOK_RPAREN,         /* )                              */
    TOK_COMMA,          /* ,                              */
    TOK_SEMICOLON,      /* ;                              */
    TOK_DOT,            /* .                              */
    /* Parameter binding */
    TOK_QUESTION,       /* ?                              */
    TOK_COLON,          /* :name                          */
    TOK_AT,             /* @name                          */
    TOK_DOLLAR,         /* $name                          */
    /* Sentinels */
    TOK_EOF,            /* end of input                   */
    TOK_ERROR           /* unrecognized character         */
} TokenType;
```
**Design rationale — single `TOK_KEYWORD` vs per-keyword variants:** SQLite 3's real tokenizer uses per-keyword IDs (TK_SELECT, TK_FROM, etc.) to eliminate a second string comparison in the parser. For this learning implementation we use a single `TOK_KEYWORD` and rely on `lexeme_equals(token, "SELECT")` helpers in the parser. This keeps the enum at a comprehensible size without sacrificing correctness; the keyword table lookup already happens once in the tokenizer.
### 4.2 Token Struct — Byte Layout
```c
typedef struct {
    TokenType    type;    /* offset 0, size 4 (enum = int) */
    const char  *start;  /* offset 8, size 8 (64-bit ptr)  */
    int          length; /* offset 16, size 4              */
    int          line;   /* offset 20, size 4              */
    int          col;    /* offset 24, size 4              */
    /* 4 bytes padding to align next struct to 8 */
} Token;
/* sizeof(Token) == 32 on LP64 platforms */
```
> **Expert note — padding:** On a 64-bit LP64 system, `TokenType` is 4 bytes, then 4 bytes of implicit padding precede the 8-byte pointer. Verify with `static_assert(sizeof(Token) == 32, "Token size changed");`. If you ever target a 32-bit platform where pointers are 4 bytes, `sizeof(Token)` will be 20 with no padding. Your code must never assume `sizeof(Token) == 32`; use the macro only as a build-time regression guard.
**Zero-copy invariant:** `start` always points into the caller's original SQL string. The tokenizer never calls `malloc`, `strdup`, or any allocating function. A token lexeme is recovered at any time as:
```c
/* Print a token's text without NUL-termination: */
printf("%.*s", token.length, token.start);
```
### 4.3 Lexer State Struct
```c
typedef struct {
    const char *source;   /* pointer to full SQL string (not owned) */
    int         length;   /* strlen(source) — cached to avoid O(n) calls */
    int         current;  /* index of next character to consume */
    int         line;     /* current line number, 1-based */
    int         col;      /* current column number, 1-based */
} Lexer;
/* sizeof(Lexer) == 24 on LP64 (no padding needed: ptr+int+int+int+int = 8+4+4+4+4) */
```
> **Expert note:** `current` is `int`, not `size_t`. This is intentional: the maximum SQL statement SQLite handles is bounded well below 2³¹ bytes by the `SQLITE_MAX_LENGTH` compile-time limit (default 1 GB, which does exceed INT_MAX on 32-bit but not on 64-bit where `int` is 32 bits and inputs beyond 2 GB are pathological). For production use, change to `int32_t` from `<stdint.h>` and add an overflow guard.
### 4.4 Keyword Table Entry
```c
/* Internal to lexer.c — static linkage only */
typedef struct {
    const char *word;    /* NUL-terminated, uppercase canonical form */
    int         length;  /* strlen(word) — avoids repeated strlen */
} KeywordEntry;
```
The table is a `static const KeywordEntry kw_table[]` sorted lexicographically by `word` (uppercase). This enables `bsearch` in O(log k) where k = number of reserved words.
---
## 5. Public API — Function Contracts
### 5.1 `lexer_init`
```c
void lexer_init(Lexer *lex, const char *source, int length);
```
**Preconditions:**
- `lex != NULL`
- `source != NULL`
- `length >= 0` and equals the number of bytes to tokenize (NUL byte not included in count)
**Postconditions:**
- `lex->source == source` (pointer copy, not ownership transfer)
- `lex->length == length`
- `lex->current == 0`
- `lex->line == 1`
- `lex->col == 1`
**Side effects:** None. No allocation, no I/O.
**Error behavior:** If `source == NULL` and assertions are enabled (debug build), triggers `assert`. In release builds, undefined behavior — callers must validate.
---
### 5.2 `lexer_next_token`
```c
Token lexer_next_token(Lexer *lex);
```
**Preconditions:**
- `lex` was previously initialized with `lexer_init`
- `lex->current <= lex->length`
**Postconditions:**
- Returns a `Token` with `start` pointing into `lex->source`
- `lex->current`, `lex->line`, `lex->col` are advanced past the returned token
- Once `TOK_EOF` is returned, subsequent calls continue to return `TOK_EOF` (idempotent at end-of-input)
- Once `TOK_ERROR` is returned, `lex->current` is advanced by exactly 1 (error recovery: skip unrecognized character)
**Return value:**
- `type == TOK_EOF` when `lex->current == lex->length` before consuming any character
- `type == TOK_ERROR` when current character does not start any valid token
- All other token types when the corresponding pattern is matched
**Complexity:** O(k) per call where k is the length of the lexeme, O(log K) additional for keyword lookup where K = number of reserved words.
**Thread safety:** None. `Lexer` is not protected. External synchronization required for concurrent access.
---
### 5.3 `lexer_tokenize_all`
```c
int lexer_tokenize_all(Lexer *lex, Token *out, int max_tokens);
```
**Preconditions:**
- `lex` initialized
- `out != NULL`, `out` points to at least `max_tokens` elements
- `max_tokens > 0`
**Postconditions:**
- `out[0..n-1]` contain tokens in source order including the final `TOK_EOF`
- Returns `n`, the number of tokens written including `TOK_EOF`
- If `n` would exceed `max_tokens`, stops and returns `max_tokens` (caller-truncated — check `out[max_tokens-1].type != TOK_EOF` to detect)
**Usage pattern:**
```c
Token tokens[512];
Lexer lex;
lexer_init(&lex, sql, (int)strlen(sql));
int count = lexer_tokenize_all(&lex, tokens, 512);
/* tokens[count-1].type == TOK_EOF unless truncated */
```
---
### 5.4 `token_type_name`
```c
const char *token_type_name(TokenType type);
```
Returns a static string (e.g., `"TOK_INTEGER"`, `"TOK_KEYWORD"`) for debug printing. Never returns `NULL` — unknown values return `"TOK_UNKNOWN"`.
---
### 5.5 `lexeme_equals`
```c
int lexeme_equals(const Token *tok, const char *str);
```
Returns 1 if the token's lexeme (case-insensitive) matches `str`, 0 otherwise. Used by the parser to distinguish `SELECT` from `INSERT` without re-scanning.
**Implementation note:** Uses a loop comparing `toupper((unsigned char)tok->start[i])` against `toupper((unsigned char)str[i])` for `i` in `[0, tok->length)`, then checks `str[tok->length] == '\0'`.
---
## 6. Internal Helper Contracts
All helpers are `static` functions in `lexer.c`. They are documented here because the implementation phases build them incrementally.
| Function | Signature | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `at_end` | `static int at_end(const Lexer *l)` | Returns 1 iff `l->current >= l->length` |
| `peek` | `static char peek(const Lexer *l)` | Returns `l->source[l->current]` without advancing; returns `'\0'` if at end |
| `peek_next` | `static char peek_next(const Lexer *l)` | Returns `l->source[l->current + 1]`; returns `'\0'` if `current + 1 >= length` |
| `advance` | `static char advance(Lexer *l)` | Returns current char, increments `current`; updates `line`/`col` on `'\n'` |
| `match` | `static int match(Lexer *l, char expected)` | Advances and returns 1 if `peek(l) == expected`; else returns 0 |
| `make_token` | `static Token make_token(TokenType t, const char *start, int len, int line, int col)` | Constructs and returns a `Token` |
| `skip_whitespace_and_comments` | `static void skip_whitespace_and_comments(Lexer *l)` | Skips spaces, tabs, CR, LF, `--` comments, `/* */` comments |
| `read_string` | `static Token read_string(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads single-quoted string with `''` escape; returns `TOK_STRING` or `TOK_ERROR` |
| `read_quoted_id` | `static Token read_quoted_id(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads double-quoted identifier; returns `TOK_QUOTED_ID` or `TOK_ERROR` |
| `read_number` | `static Token read_number(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads integer or float; returns `TOK_INTEGER` or `TOK_FLOAT` |
| `read_identifier_or_keyword` | `static Token read_identifier_or_keyword(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads bare name, classifies as `TOK_IDENTIFIER` or `TOK_KEYWORD` |
| `read_blob` | `static Token read_blob(Lexer *l, int line, int col)` | Reads `X'...'` blob literal; returns `TOK_BLOB` or `TOK_ERROR` |
| `keyword_lookup` | `static int keyword_lookup(const char *text, int length)` | Returns 1 if text (case-insensitive) is a SQL keyword, 0 otherwise |
---
## 7. FSM Specification

![Tokenizer FSM State Transitions](./diagrams/tdd-diag-1.svg)

*Figure: Lexer finite-state machine. Each node is a named state; edges show the input character class or condition that triggers the transition.*
### 7.1 State Enumeration
The FSM is implemented as a `switch` dispatch in `lexer_next_token`, not as an explicit state variable. The "state" is implicit in which `case` branch is executing. The logical states are:
| State Name | Entry Condition | Exit Transitions |
|---|---|---|
| `S_START` | After `skip_whitespace_and_comments` | All first-character cases |
| `S_STRING` | `peek == '\''` | `''` → stay, `'` → emit `TOK_STRING`, EOF → `TOK_ERROR` |
| `S_QUOTED_ID` | `peek == '"'` | `""` → stay, `"` → emit `TOK_QUOTED_ID`, EOF → `TOK_ERROR` |
| `S_NUMBER` | `isdigit(peek)` | `.` → `S_FLOAT`, non-digit → emit `TOK_INTEGER` |
| `S_FLOAT` | `peek == '.'` while in `S_NUMBER` | non-digit → emit `TOK_FLOAT` |
| `S_IDENT` | `isalpha(peek) \|\| peek == '_'` | non-`[a-zA-Z0-9_]` → keyword check → emit |
| `S_BLOB` | `(peek == 'X' \|\| peek == 'x') && peek_next == '\''` | same as `S_STRING` but prefixed |
| `S_LINE_COMMENT` | `peek == '-' && peek_next == '-'` | `'\n'` or EOF → return to `S_START` |
| `S_BLOCK_COMMENT` | `peek == '/' && peek_next == '*'` | `'*/'` → return to `S_START`, EOF → `TOK_ERROR` |
| `S_OP` | Any operator character | 1 or 2 chars consumed → emit operator token |
### 7.2 Character Class Dispatch Table
The first character of each token determines the dispatch branch:
| First Char | Branch |
|---|---|
| `isspace(c)` | `skip_whitespace_and_comments` then restart |
| `isdigit(c)` | `read_number` |
| `isalpha(c)` or `'_'` | `read_identifier_or_keyword` |
| `'X'` or `'x'` followed by `'\''` | `read_blob` |
| `'\''` | `read_string` |
| `'"'` | `read_quoted_id` |
| `'='` | emit `TOK_EQ` |
| `'!'` + `'='` | emit `TOK_NE` |
| `'!'` (alone) | emit `TOK_ERROR` |
| `'<'` + `'='` | emit `TOK_LE` |
| `'<'` + `'>'` | emit `TOK_NE` |
| `'<'` + `'<'` | emit `TOK_LSHIFT` |
| `'<'` (alone) | emit `TOK_LT` |
| `'>'` + `'='` | emit `TOK_GE` |
| `'>'` + `'>'` | emit `TOK_RSHIFT` |
| `'>'` (alone) | emit `TOK_GT` |
| `'+'` | emit `TOK_PLUS` |
| `'-'` + `'-'` | `skip_whitespace_and_comments` (line comment) |
| `'-'` (alone) | emit `TOK_MINUS` |
| `'*'` | emit `TOK_STAR` |
| `'/'` + `'*'` | `skip_whitespace_and_comments` (block comment) |
| `'/'` (alone) | emit `TOK_SLASH` |
| `'%'` | emit `TOK_PERCENT` |
| `'&'` | emit `TOK_AMPERSAND` |
| `'\|'` + `'\|'` | emit `TOK_PIPE2` |
| `'\|'` (alone) | emit `TOK_PIPE` |
| `'~'` | emit `TOK_TILDE` |
| `'('` | emit `TOK_LPAREN` |
| `')'` | emit `TOK_RPAREN` |
| `','` | emit `TOK_COMMA` |
| `';'` | emit `TOK_SEMICOLON` |
| `'.'` | emit `TOK_DOT` |
| `'?'` | emit `TOK_QUESTION` |
| `':'` | emit `TOK_COLON` |
| `'@'` | emit `TOK_AT` |
| `'$'` | emit `TOK_DOLLAR` |
| `'\0'` / end | emit `TOK_EOF` |
| anything else | advance 1, emit `TOK_ERROR` |
### 7.3 Multi-Character Operator Lookahead
```
advance()          ← consume first char
c = that char
switch(c):
  case '<':
    if match(l, '='):   return TOK_LE   (consumed 2 chars: '<', '=')
    if match(l, '>'):   return TOK_NE   (consumed 2 chars: '<', '>')
    if match(l, '<'):   return TOK_LSHIFT
    return TOK_LT       (consumed 1 char)
  ...
```
`match(l, expected)` peeks at `l->source[l->current]` without advancing unless it equals `expected`. This is the canonical 1-character lookahead pattern from Crafting Interpreters adapted for C.
### 7.4 String Literal FSM Detail

![Token Struct Memory Layout](./diagrams/tdd-diag-2.svg)

*Figure: String literal sub-FSM handling single-quote escape sequences (`''`) and unterminated string errors.*
```
State: reading a single-quoted string
  start = l->source + l->current - 1   (points at opening quote)
  loop:
    if at_end:
      return TOK_ERROR  (unterminated string)
    c = advance()
    if c == '\'':
      if peek(l) == '\'':    ← SQLite escape: '' inside string
        advance()            ← consume second quote, stay in loop
      else:
        break                ← closing quote, exit loop
  length = (l->source + l->current) - start
  return make_token(TOK_STRING, start, length, saved_line, saved_col)
```
**Invariant:** `token.start` points at the opening `'`; `token.length` includes both surrounding quotes and all internal content. The parser is responsible for stripping delimiters and unescaping `''` → `'` before value use.
### 7.5 Keyword Lookup Detail

![Tokenizer Data Flow](./diagrams/tdd-diag-3.svg)

*Figure: Keyword recognition flow — uppercasing scratch buffer, binary search, identifier vs. keyword classification.*
```c
static int keyword_lookup(const char *text, int length) {
    /* Stack-allocate scratch buffer; keywords <= 16 chars in SQLite */
    if (length > 16) return 0;
    char upper[17];
    for (int i = 0; i < length; i++)
        upper[i] = (char)toupper((unsigned char)text[i]);
    upper[length] = '\0';
    /* bsearch over sorted KeywordEntry table */
    KeywordEntry key = { upper, length };
    KeywordEntry *found = bsearch(&key, kw_table,
                                  KW_TABLE_SIZE,
                                  sizeof(KeywordEntry),
                                  kw_compare);
    return found != NULL;
}
```
`kw_compare` compares by `strcmp` of `.word` fields. The table must be sorted in the same collation order (strcmp = ASCII order for uppercase strings).
**Keyword list (minimum required for M1 test coverage):**
```c
static const KeywordEntry kw_table[] = {
    {"ADD",        3}, {"ALL",       3}, {"ALTER",     5},
    {"AND",        3}, {"AS",        2}, {"ASC",       3},
    {"AUTOINCREMENT", 13},
    {"BETWEEN",    7}, {"BY",        2},
    {"CASE",       4}, {"CHECK",     5}, {"COLLATE",   7},
    {"COLUMN",     6}, {"COMMIT",    6}, {"CONSTRAINT",10},
    {"CREATE",     6}, {"CROSS",     5},
    {"DEFAULT",    7}, {"DEFERRABLE",10}, {"DEFERRED",  8},
    {"DELETE",     6}, {"DESC",      4}, {"DISTINCT",  8}, {"DROP",     4},
    {"EACH",       4}, {"ELSE",      4}, {"END",       3}, {"ESCAPE",   6},
    {"EXCEPT",     6}, {"EXISTS",    6}, {"EXPLAIN",   7},
    {"FAIL",       4}, {"FALSE",     5}, {"FOR",       3}, {"FOREIGN",  7},
    {"FROM",       4}, {"FULL",      4},
    {"GLOB",       4}, {"GROUP",     5},
    {"HAVING",     6},
    {"IF",         2}, {"IGNORE",    6}, {"IMMEDIATE", 9}, {"IN",       2},
    {"INDEX",      5}, {"INDEXED",   7}, {"INITIALLY", 9}, {"INNER",    5},
    {"INSERT",     6}, {"INSTEAD",   7}, {"INTERSECT", 9}, {"INTO",     4},
    {"IS",         2}, {"ISNULL",    6},
    {"JOIN",       4},
    {"KEY",        3},
    {"LEFT",       4}, {"LIKE",      4}, {"LIMIT",     5},
    {"MATCH",      5},
    {"NATURAL",    7}, {"NO",        2}, {"NOT",       3}, {"NOTHING",  7},
    {"NOTNULL",    7}, {"NULL",      4},
    {"OF",         2}, {"OFFSET",    6}, {"ON",        2}, {"OR",       2},
    {"ORDER",      5}, {"OUTER",     5},
    {"PLAN",       4}, {"PRAGMA",    6}, {"PRIMARY",   7},
    {"QUERY",      5},
    {"RAISE",      5}, {"RECURSIVE", 9}, {"REFERENCES",10}, {"REGEXP",  6},
    {"REINDEX",    7}, {"RELEASE",   7}, {"RENAME",    6}, {"REPLACE",  7},
    {"RESTRICT",   8}, {"RETURNING", 9}, {"RIGHT",     5}, {"ROLLBACK", 8},
    {"ROW",        3}, {"ROWS",      4},
    {"SAVEPOINT",  9}, {"SELECT",    6}, {"SET",       3},
    {"TABLE",      5}, {"TEMP",      4}, {"TEMPORARY", 9}, {"THEN",     4},
    {"TO",         2}, {"TRANSACTION",11}, {"TRIGGER",  7}, {"TRUE",     4},
    {"UNION",      5}, {"UNIQUE",    6}, {"UPDATE",    6}, {"USING",    5},
    {"VACUUM",     6}, {"VALUES",    6}, {"VIEW",      4}, {"VIRTUAL",  7},
    {"WHEN",       4}, {"WHERE",     5}, {"WITH",      4}, {"WITHOUT",  7},
};
#define KW_TABLE_SIZE ((int)(sizeof(kw_table) / sizeof(kw_table[0])))
```
> **Maintenance rule:** The table MUST remain sorted by `word` field (ascending ASCII/strcmp order). Add a `static_assert` or a startup check: iterate and verify each entry is strictly greater than the previous. This prevents silent `bsearch` failures.
---
## 8. Error Handling Matrix
| Error Condition | Token Emitted | Recovery Action | Test Verifies |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unterminated string `'abc` | `TOK_ERROR` | Advance to EOF; subsequent call returns `TOK_EOF` | Spec 4a |
| Unterminated block comment `/* abc` | `TOK_ERROR` | Advance to EOF | Spec 4b |
| Bare `!` (not `!=`) | `TOK_ERROR` | Advance 1 char | Spec 4c |
| Invalid hex blob `X'GG'` | `TOK_ERROR` | Advance to closing `'` or EOF | Spec 4d |
| Null byte in input | `TOK_EOF` | Treat as end-of-input | — |
| Empty string input | `TOK_EOF` on first call | — | Spec 1 (edge) |
| Number overflow (value) | Not detected here | Tokenizer emits `TOK_INTEGER` regardless; value check at parse/bind time | — |
**Error token invariant:** A `TOK_ERROR` token has `start` pointing at the first offending character and `length` equal to the number of characters consumed (1 for single unrecognized chars, full length for unterminated literals). This gives the error reporter enough information to display:
```c
fprintf(stderr, "Lexer error at line %d col %d: unexpected '%.*s'\n",
        tok.line, tok.col, tok.length, tok.start);
```
---
## 9. `advance` and Line Tracking
```c
static char advance(Lexer *l) {
    char c = l->source[l->current++];
    if (c == '\n') {
        l->line++;
        l->col = 1;
    } else {
        l->col++;
    }
    return c;
}
```
**Column semantics:** `col` is 1-based. After `lexer_init`, `col == 1`. The first character consumed by `advance` will be at column 1. After a newline, `col` resets to 1 so the next character is column 1 of the new line.
**Token position capture:** Before calling any `read_*` function or emitting a single-char token, capture `saved_line = l->line; saved_col = l->col;`. These saved values become `token.line` and `token.col`. This ensures the token position reflects where the token **starts**, not where the lexer position is after consuming it.
---
## 10. `skip_whitespace_and_comments` Algorithm
```
loop:
  while peek is whitespace: advance()
  if peek == '-' and peek_next == '-':
    while not at_end and peek != '\n': advance()
    continue loop     ← might be more whitespace after comment
  if peek == '/' and peek_next == '*':
    advance(); advance()    ← consume '/', '*'
    while not at_end:
      if peek == '*' and peek_next == '/':
        advance(); advance()    ← consume '*', '/'
        break inner while
      advance()
    else:              ← loop fell off end → unterminated comment
      store error flag on lexer? or emit TOK_ERROR in next_token
    continue loop
  break outer loop    ← no whitespace, no comment
```
**Unterminated block comment handling:** The simplest correct approach is: after the inner while exits via EOF (not via `*/`), set a `lex->error_pending = 1` flag and the starting position of the comment. Then `lexer_next_token` checks `error_pending` before the main dispatch and returns a `TOK_ERROR` immediately. This avoids tangling error emission into the whitespace skipping function.
---
## 11. Implementation Phases
### Phase 1 — Token Types and Struct Skeleton
**Goal:** The project compiles with the complete public API declared. No logic yet.
**Deliverables:**
1. `src/lexer.h` with full `TokenType` enum, `Token` struct, `Lexer` struct, and all function declarations
2. `src/lexer.c` stub: every public function has an empty body returning a zero-initialized `Token` or 0
3. `tests/test_lexer.c` with `#include "lexer.h"` and a `main()` that calls `lexer_init` and `lexer_next_token` and prints the result
4. `Makefile` with `test-lexer` target that builds and runs the test binary
**Checkpoint:** `make test-lexer` compiles without warnings (`-Wall -Wextra -std=c11 -pedantic`). The stub binary runs and exits 0 (no assertions yet).
**Key validation:**
```c
static_assert(sizeof(Token) == 32 || sizeof(Token) == 20,
              "Unexpected Token size — check struct padding");
```
Add this to `lexer.c` after the struct definitions.
---
### Phase 2 — FSM Core Loop and Single-Character Tokens
**Goal:** `lexer_next_token` correctly handles whitespace, all single-character tokens, and EOF.
**Implement in order:**
1. `at_end`, `peek`, `peek_next`, `advance`, `match`, `make_token`
2. `skip_whitespace_and_comments` — whitespace only (no comments yet)
3. `lexer_next_token` switch dispatch for: `TOK_EOF`, `TOK_LPAREN`, `TOK_RPAREN`, `TOK_COMMA`, `TOK_SEMICOLON`, `TOK_DOT`, `TOK_PLUS`, `TOK_STAR`, `TOK_PERCENT`, `TOK_TILDE`, `TOK_QUESTION`, `TOK_COLON`, `TOK_AT`, `TOK_DOLLAR`, `TOK_EQ`
4. Multi-char operators: `<`, `>`, `!`, `-`, `/`, `|`, `>`
**Checkpoint (Phase 2):** Spec 1 (FSM state transitions) passes. Spec 5 (zero-heap) can be verified after any phase since no `malloc` is ever introduced.
---
### Phase 3 — Keyword Table and Identifier/Number Lexing
**Goal:** `lexer_next_token` handles all literal types and keywords.
**Implement in order:**
1. `kw_table` array (sorted) and `kw_compare` comparator
2. `keyword_lookup(text, length)`
3. `read_identifier_or_keyword`
4. `read_number` (integer + float)
5. `read_string` with `''` escape
6. `read_quoted_id`
7. `read_blob`
8. Add `X`/`x` special-case in `lexer_next_token` dispatch
**Checkpoint (Phase 3):** Spec 2 (case-insensitive keywords) and Spec 3 (full SELECT statement) pass.
**Sorting verification:**
```c
/* Add to test_lexer.c as a standalone test group */
void test_keyword_table_sorted(void) {
    for (int i = 1; i < KW_TABLE_SIZE; i++) {
        assert(strcmp(kw_table[i-1].word, kw_table[i].word) < 0);
    }
}
```
Note: `kw_table` must be `extern` or you must expose a test accessor. Alternatively, add the check as a `static` init block.
---
### Phase 4 — Comment Skipping, Error Tokens, and Iterator
**Goal:** Complete production-ready tokenizer.
**Implement in order:**
1. Extend `skip_whitespace_and_comments` for `--` line comments and `/* */` block comments
2. `error_pending` mechanism for unterminated block comment
3. `lexer_tokenize_all`
4. `token_type_name`
5. `lexeme_equals`
**Checkpoint (Phase 4):** Spec 4 (error handling) passes. All 5 specs pass together.
---
## 12. Test Specifications
### Test Spec 1 — FSM State Transitions
**Name:** `test_fsm_transitions`
**Verifies:** Each major FSM branch is exercised in isolation.
```c
void test_fsm_transitions(void) {
    Lexer l;
    Token t;
    /* Single operator tokens */
    lexer_init(&l, "(", 1);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_LPAREN);
    assert(t.length == 1);
    assert(t.line == 1 && t.col == 1);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_EOF);
    /* Two-char operator: >= */
    lexer_init(&l, ">=", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_GE);
    assert(t.length == 2);
    /* Two-char operator: <> */
    lexer_init(&l, "<>", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_NE);
    /* != also maps to TOK_NE */
    lexer_init(&l, "!=", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_NE);
    /* || concat */
    lexer_init(&l, "||", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_PIPE2);
    /* Whitespace skipped, position tracked */
    lexer_init(&l, "  ;", 3);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_SEMICOLON);
    assert(t.col == 3);
    /* Newline increments line counter */
    lexer_init(&l, "\n;", 2);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_SEMICOLON);
    assert(t.line == 2 && t.col == 1);
    /* Empty input */
    lexer_init(&l, "", 0);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_EOF);
    /* Repeated EOF calls are safe */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_EOF);
    printf("PASS: test_fsm_transitions\n");
}
```
---
### Test Spec 2 — Keyword Case-Insensitivity
**Name:** `test_keyword_case_insensitive`
**Verifies:** `SELECT`, `select`, `SeLeCt`, `NULL`, `null` all produce `TOK_KEYWORD`; a non-keyword identifier produces `TOK_IDENTIFIER`.
```c
void test_keyword_case_insensitive(void) {
    const char *variants[] = { "SELECT", "select", "SeLeCt", "sElEcT", NULL };
    for (int i = 0; variants[i]; i++) {
        Lexer l;
        lexer_init(&l, variants[i], (int)strlen(variants[i]));
        Token t = lexer_next_token(&l);
        assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);
        assert(lexeme_equals(&t, "SELECT"));
        assert(lexer_next_token(&l).type == TOK_EOF);
    }
    /* NULL is a keyword */
    Lexer l;
    lexer_init(&l, "NULL", 4);
    Token t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);
    assert(lexeme_equals(&t, "NULL"));
    /* Identifier that starts like a keyword but isn't */
    lexer_init(&l, "selects", 7);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_IDENTIFIER);
    assert(t.length == 7);
    /* Identifier with underscore */
    lexer_init(&l, "my_table", 8);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_IDENTIFIER);
    /* Keyword followed immediately by punctuation */
    lexer_init(&l, "FROM(", 5);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);
    assert(t.length == 4);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_LPAREN);
    printf("PASS: test_keyword_case_insensitive\n");
}
```
---
### Test Spec 3 — Full Statement Tokenization
**Name:** `test_full_select_statement`
**Verifies:** A realistic SQL statement tokenizes to the expected sequence.
```c
void test_full_select_statement(void) {
    const char *sql = "SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE age >= 18;";
    Token tokens[64];
    Lexer l;
    lexer_init(&l, sql, (int)strlen(sql));
    int n = lexer_tokenize_all(&l, tokens, 64);
    /* Verify sequence */
    int i = 0;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_KEYWORD);  assert(lexeme_equals(&tokens[i], "SELECT")); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); assert(tokens[i].length == 2); i++; /* id */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_COMMA); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); assert(tokens[i].length == 4); i++; /* name */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_KEYWORD);  assert(lexeme_equals(&tokens[i], "FROM")); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); assert(tokens[i].length == 5); i++; /* users */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_KEYWORD);  assert(lexeme_equals(&tokens[i], "WHERE")); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); assert(tokens[i].length == 3); i++; /* age */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_GE); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_INTEGER); assert(tokens[i].length == 2); i++; /* 18 */
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_SEMICOLON); i++;
    assert(tokens[i].type == TOK_EOF); i++;
    assert(i == n);
    /* Zero-copy: all starts point into original sql buffer */
    for (int j = 0; j < n - 1; j++) { /* exclude EOF */
        assert(tokens[j].start >= sql);
        assert(tokens[j].start + tokens[j].length <= sql + strlen(sql));
    }
    /* Test with comment */
    const char *sql2 = "SELECT -- pick all\n* FROM t;";
    lexer_init(&l, sql2, (int)strlen(sql2));
    n = lexer_tokenize_all(&l, tokens, 64);
    assert(tokens[0].type == TOK_KEYWORD); /* SELECT */
    assert(tokens[1].type == TOK_STAR);     /* * (comment skipped) */
    assert(tokens[2].type == TOK_KEYWORD); /* FROM */
    /* String literal */
    const char *sql3 = "WHERE name = 'O''Brien'";
    lexer_init(&l, sql3, (int)strlen(sql3));
    Token t;
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);   /* WHERE */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); /* name */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_EQ);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_STRING);
    assert(t.length == 10); /* 'O''Brien' = 10 chars incl quotes */
    printf("PASS: test_full_select_statement\n");
}
```
---
### Test Spec 4 — Error Handling
**Name:** `test_error_handling`
**Verifies:** All error conditions emit `TOK_ERROR` and recovery allows continued tokenization.
```c
void test_error_handling(void) {
    Lexer l;
    Token t;
    /* 4a: Unterminated string */
    lexer_init(&l, "'unterminated", 13);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_ERROR);
    assert(t.line == 1);
    /* After error, next call should return TOK_EOF */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_EOF);
    /* 4b: Unterminated block comment */
    lexer_init(&l, "/* no end", 9);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_ERROR);
    /* 4c: Bare ! not followed by = */
    lexer_init(&l, "!", 1);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_ERROR);
    assert(t.length == 1);
    /* 4d: Error recovery — tokens after error are still valid */
    lexer_init(&l, "@ SELECT", 8);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    /* '@' alone with no following name — tokenizer emits TOK_AT (not error) */
    assert(t.type == TOK_AT);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_KEYWORD);
    /* 4e: Error position tracking */
    lexer_init(&l, "x = `bad`", 9);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_IDENTIFIER); /* x */
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_EQ);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l); assert(t.type == TOK_ERROR);
    assert(t.col == 5); /* backtick at column 5 */
    /* 4f: Float number */
    lexer_init(&l, "3.14", 4);
    t = lexer_next_token(&l);
    assert(t.type == TOK_FLOAT);
    assert(t.length == 4);
    printf("PASS: test_error_handling\n");
}
```
---
### Test Spec 5 — Zero Heap Allocation
**Name:** `test_zero_heap_allocation`
**Verifies:** The tokenizer never calls `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, or `strdup`.
**Primary method — Valgrind/Massif:**
```bash
# In Makefile:
valgrind-test-lexer: test_lexer
    valgrind --tool=massif --pages-as-heap=yes \
             --massif-out-file=massif.out ./test_lexer
    ms_print massif.out | grep -A5 "SQL Tokenizer"
    # Verify: total heap allocation attributed to lexer_* = 0 bytes
```
**Secondary method — malloc interception (compile-time):**
```c
/* In test_lexer.c only, BEFORE any includes: */
#include <stdlib.h>
static int g_malloc_count = 0;
void *__wrap_malloc(size_t size) {
    g_malloc_count++;
    return __real_malloc(size);
}
void test_zero_heap_allocation(void) {
    g_malloc_count = 0;
    Token tokens[1024];
    Lexer l;
    const char *sql = "SELECT a, b, c FROM t WHERE x = 1 AND y > 2.5;";
    lexer_init(&l, sql, (int)strlen(sql));
    lexer_tokenize_all(&l, tokens, 1024);
    assert(g_malloc_count == 0);
    printf("PASS: test_zero_heap_allocation (malloc_count=%d)\n", g_malloc_count);
}
```
**Compile with:** `gcc -Wl,--wrap=malloc -o test_lexer test_lexer.c lexer.o`
**Invariant that guarantees the test passes:** `Lexer` is stack-allocated by the caller. `lexer_init` performs only pointer copies and integer assignments. All `read_*` functions compute `start = &lex->source[saved_index]` and `length = lex->current - saved_index`. No function in `lexer.c` contains the text `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, `strdup`, or `strndup`. Enforce with:
```bash
grep -n "malloc\|calloc\|realloc\|strdup" src/lexer.c && echo "FAIL: heap alloc found" && exit 1 || echo "OK: no heap alloc"
```
Add this grep to the `make test-lexer` recipe as a pre-flight check.
---
## 13. Makefile Integration
```makefile
CC      = gcc
CFLAGS  = -Wall -Wextra -std=c11 -pedantic -g -Isrc
# Main library object
src/lexer.o: src/lexer.c src/lexer.h
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c src/lexer.c -o src/lexer.o
# Test binary
tests/test_lexer: tests/test_lexer.c src/lexer.o src/lexer.h
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) tests/test_lexer.c src/lexer.o -o tests/test_lexer
# Run tests
.PHONY: test-lexer
test-lexer: tests/test_lexer
	@echo "==> Checking for heap allocations in lexer.c..."
	@grep -n "malloc\|calloc\|realloc\|strdup" src/lexer.c \
	    && (echo "FAIL: heap allocation found in lexer.c" && exit 1) \
	    || echo "OK: no heap allocations"
	@echo "==> Running lexer tests..."
	./tests/test_lexer
	@echo "==> All lexer tests passed."
# Clean
.PHONY: clean-lexer
clean-lexer:
	rm -f src/lexer.o tests/test_lexer
```
---
## 14. Performance Targets
The tokenizer runs in the hot path of every query execution. Target performance on a modern 64-bit CPU (3 GHz):
| Metric | Target | Measurement Method |
|---|---|---|
| Single token, simple operator | ≤ 5 ns | `clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC)` over 10M iterations |
| Single token, identifier + keyword lookup | ≤ 20 ns | Same — includes binary search |
| Single token, string literal (10 chars) | ≤ 15 ns | Same |
| Full `SELECT ... WHERE ...` (12 tokens) | ≤ 150 ns | End-to-end via `lexer_tokenize_all` |
| 1 MB SQL file (batch) | ≤ 400 MB/s throughput | `wc -c` / elapsed |
**Performance microbenchmark template:**
```c
#include <time.h>
#define BENCH_ITERS 10000000
void bench_next_token(void) {
    const char *sql = "SELECT id FROM users WHERE age >= 18;";
    int len = (int)strlen(sql);
    Token tokens[64];
    struct timespec t0, t1;
    clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &t0);
    for (int i = 0; i < BENCH_ITERS; i++) {
        Lexer l;
        lexer_init(&l, sql, len);
        lexer_tokenize_all(&l, tokens, 64);
    }
    clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &t1);
    long ns = (t1.tv_sec - t0.tv_sec) * 1000000000L
            + (t1.tv_nsec - t0.tv_nsec);
    printf("%.1f ns/token-sequence over %d iters\n",
           (double)ns / BENCH_ITERS, BENCH_ITERS);
}
```
**Optimization notes (do not premature-optimize; apply only if bench fails):**
- Replace `bsearch` with a perfect hash (gperf) for keyword lookup — eliminates O(log k) and avoids `toupper` loop
- Ensure the hot `advance` function inlines: mark with `static inline`
- Use `__builtin_expect(at_end(l), 0)` around the EOF guard inside tight inner loops
- Profile with `perf stat` to confirm instruction cache vs. branch mispredictions are the bottleneck before rewriting
---
## 15. Dependency and Integration Notes
This module (`build-sqlite-m1`) has **no upstream dependencies** within the project. It is the first module to implement.
Downstream modules that consume `Token` arrays:
| Module | Dependency |
|---|---|
| `build-sqlite-m2` SQL Parser | Calls `lexer_tokenize_all`; reads `token.type`, `token.start`, `token.length` |
| `build-sqlite-m3` AST Builder | Reads `token.line`, `token.col` for error messages |
| All subsequent modules | Indirectly via the parse tree |
**Stability contract:** Once M2 development begins, the `Token` struct layout and `TokenType` enum must not be changed without a version bump. Adding new token types (e.g., `TOK_BLOB` if deferred) is backward-compatible; removing or reordering enum values is not.
---
## 16. Common Mistakes and Mitigations
| Mistake | Symptom | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Forgetting `(unsigned char)` cast in `isdigit`/`toupper` | UB on platforms where `char` is signed and input contains non-ASCII bytes | Always cast: `isdigit((unsigned char)c)` |
| Off-by-one in `length` calculation | Token appears to include trailing character | `length = current - start_index`, not `current - start_index + 1` |
| `keyword_lookup` called with `length > 16` | Stack buffer overflow in `upper[17]` | Explicit `if (length > 16) return 0;` guard at function entry |
| `kw_table` not sorted | `bsearch` returns spurious misses | Add sort-verification test (Spec 2 implicitly catches many; explicit check catches all) |
| `peek` called after `at_end` | Reads `source[length]` — valid only if NUL-terminated | `peek` returns `'\0'` when `at_end` — this is safe with NUL-terminated strings; add guard |
| Saving `start` after first `advance` call | Token start offset by 1 | Save start index BEFORE calling `advance()` |
| Not resetting `col` on `'\n'` in `advance` | Column numbers wrong after newlines | The `if (c == '\n') { line++; col = 1; }` branch must set `col = 1`, not `col = 0` |
---
```

```
<!-- END_TDD_MOD -->


<!-- TDD_MOD_ID: build-sqlite-m2 -->
# Module M2 — Recursive-Descent Parser and AST
**Project:** Build Your Own SQLite | **Module ID:** `build-sqlite-m2`
**Language:** C (ISO C11) | **Depends On:** `build-sqlite-m1` (SQL Tokenizer)
**Difficulty:** Expert | **Estimated Effort:** 10–14 hours
---
## 0. Context and Purpose
M1 delivered a complete tokenizer: given a SQL string, `lexer_tokenize_all` returns a flat `Token[]` with every lexeme classified. M2 picks up that token stream and assigns grammatical structure. The output is an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** — a tree of typed C structs where each node represents one syntactic construct (a statement, an expression, a column definition) rather than one character range.
After this module:
- A `SELECT a, b FROM t WHERE x > 1 ORDER BY a LIMIT 10` string becomes a tree you can walk, rewrite, validate, or compile to bytecode.
- Every subsequent module (planner, VM, schema manager) accepts `AstNode*` as input, never raw SQL text.
The parser is **hand-written and recursive-descent**. No parser generators. No grammar tables generated by a tool. Every production rule is a C function. Expression parsing uses **Pratt / precedence-climbing** to handle operator precedence without mutual recursion per precedence level.
---
## 1. Upstream API Contract (M1 Summary)
The parser consumes the output of M1. Reproduce the key declarations here so M2 can be built with only this document.
```c
/* src/lexer.h (produced by M1 — do not modify) */
typedef enum {
    TOK_EOF = 0,
    TOK_SEMICOLON, TOK_COMMA, TOK_LPAREN, TOK_RPAREN,
    TOK_DOT, TOK_STAR,
    TOK_EQ, TOK_NEQ, TOK_LT, TOK_LE, TOK_GT, TOK_GE,
    TOK_PLUS, TOK_MINUS, TOK_SLASH, TOK_PERCENT,
    TOK_INTEGER_LIT, TOK_REAL_LIT, TOK_STRING_LIT,
    TOK_IDENTIFIER,
    TOK_KEYWORD,        /* SELECT INSERT CREATE DROP TABLE WHERE … */
    TOK_ERROR
} TokenType;
typedef struct {
    TokenType   type;
    const char *start;  /* pointer into original source; NOT null-terminated */
    int         length;
    int         line;
    int         col;
} Token;
typedef struct { /* opaque — parser does not call lexer functions directly */
    const char *src;
    int         pos, len, line, col;
} Lexer;
/* Batch tokenize — returns heap-allocated array; caller frees */
Token *lexer_tokenize_all(const char *src, int *out_count);
/* Case-insensitive lexeme comparison against a null-terminated keyword */
int lexeme_equals(const Token *tok, const char *kw);
```
**Rule:** After calling `lexer_tokenize_all`, the parser never calls any `lexer_*` function. It works exclusively on the `Token[]` array.
---
## 2. Module Boundary
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Input:  Token[]  produced by lexer_tokenize_all()      │
│                                                         │
│  Output: AstNode*  (tree root, heap-/arena-allocated)   │
│          OR NULL   (parse error recorded in Parser)     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
Files to create:
| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `src/ast.h` | `NodeType` enum, `AstNode` tagged-union struct, public AST API |
| `src/ast.c` | `ast_node_alloc`, `ast_free`, `ast_to_sql`, `ast_node_type_name` |
| `src/parser.h` | `Parser` struct, `parser_init`, `parser_parse`, `parser_error_message` |
| `src/parser.c` | All parse functions (`static`), Pratt engine |
| `tests/test_parser.c` | Five test groups (§10) |
---
## 3. AST Node Design
### 3.1 Principles
**Tagged union pattern.** C has no sum types. The workaround is a struct that carries a `NodeType` discriminant field followed by a `union` of per-variant payloads. The caller switches on `NodeType` before reading any union member.
**Zero-copy lexemes.** Most string data in AST nodes is represented as `(const char *start, int length)` borrowed from the original source string. The source string must outlive the AST. Only `ast_to_sql` output is freshly allocated.
**Linked child lists.** Children that vary in count (column list, value list, projection list) are singly-linked via a `next` sibling pointer on `AstNode` itself, not via a heap-allocated `AstNode**` array. This avoids secondary allocation and makes recursive walk uniform.
**Single allocator.** `ast_node_alloc` is the only allocation site. Callers never call `malloc` directly. This makes it trivial to swap in an arena (§3.5).
### 3.2 `NodeType` Enum
```c
/* src/ast.h */
#ifndef SQLITE_AST_H
#define SQLITE_AST_H
#include "lexer.h"  /* for Token */
typedef enum {
    /* Statement nodes */
    NODE_SELECT       = 1,
    NODE_INSERT       = 2,
    NODE_CREATE_TABLE = 3,
    NODE_DROP_TABLE   = 4,
    /* Expression nodes */
    NODE_IDENTIFIER   = 10,   /* column / table name */
    NODE_INTEGER_LIT  = 11,
    NODE_REAL_LIT     = 12,
    NODE_STRING_LIT   = 13,
    NODE_STAR         = 14,   /* SELECT * */
    NODE_BINARY_OP    = 20,   /* +  -  *  /  %  =  !=  <  <=  >  >= */
    NODE_UNARY_OP     = 21,   /* -x  NOT x */
    NODE_AND          = 22,
    NODE_OR           = 23,
    NODE_NOT          = 24,
    /* Structural helpers */
    NODE_COL_DEF      = 30,   /* one column definition in CREATE TABLE */
    NODE_COLUMN_REF   = 31,   /* [table.]column reference in projection */
    NODE_INVALID      = 0
} NodeType;
```
**Rationale for separate `NODE_AND` / `NODE_OR` / `NODE_NOT`:** These three operators demand special evaluation semantics (short-circuit) in the VM layer. Tagging them separately avoids stringly-typed dispatch on operator character in the VM.
### 3.3 Embedded Payload Structs
Define payload structs before `AstNode` so the union compiles cleanly.
```c
/* Column type classification (covers SQLite's type affinity rules) */
typedef enum {
    COL_TYPE_INTEGER = 1,
    COL_TYPE_REAL    = 2,
    COL_TYPE_TEXT    = 3,
    COL_TYPE_BLOB    = 4,
    COL_TYPE_NUMERIC = 5
} ColTypeAffinity;
typedef struct ColDef {
    const char    *name;       /* borrowed from source */
    int            name_len;
    ColTypeAffinity affinity;
    int            not_null;   /* boolean */
    int            primary_key;/* boolean */
} ColDef;
typedef struct SelectStmt {
    struct AstNode *projections;  /* linked list of NODE_COLUMN_REF / NODE_STAR */
    const char     *table_name;   /* borrowed */
    int             table_name_len;
    struct AstNode *where_clause; /* NULL if absent */
    struct AstNode *order_by;     /* linked list of NODE_COLUMN_REF / NODE_IDENTIFIER */
    int             limit;        /* -1 means no LIMIT */
    int             distinct;     /* boolean */
} SelectStmt;
typedef struct InsertStmt {
    const char     *table_name;
    int             table_name_len;
    struct AstNode *columns;      /* linked list of NODE_IDENTIFIER, NULL = positional */
    struct AstNode *values;       /* linked list of literal / expr nodes */
} InsertStmt;
typedef struct CreateTableStmt {
    const char     *table_name;
    int             table_name_len;
    struct AstNode *col_defs;     /* linked list of NODE_COL_DEF */
    int             if_not_exists;/* boolean */
} CreateTableStmt;
typedef struct BinaryOp {
    char            op[3];        /* "=" "!=" "<=" ">=" "<" ">" "+" "-" "*" "/" "%" */
    struct AstNode *left;
    struct AstNode *right;
} BinaryOp;
typedef struct UnaryOp {
    char            op[4];        /* "-" or "NOT" */
    struct AstNode *operand;
} UnaryOp;
typedef struct ColumnRef {
    const char *table_alias;     /* NULL if no qualifier */
    int         table_alias_len;
    const char *column;
    int         column_len;
} ColumnRef;
```
### 3.4 `AstNode` — the Tagged Union
```c
typedef struct AstNode {
    NodeType type;
    /* source position of the token that opened this node */
    int      line;
    int      col;
    /* sibling pointer (for linked child lists) */
    struct AstNode *next;
    union {
        /* Statement payloads */
        SelectStmt      select;
        InsertStmt      insert;
        CreateTableStmt create_table;
        struct { const char *name; int name_len; } drop_table;
        /* Expression payloads */
        struct { const char *start; int length; } identifier;
        struct { long long  value;              } integer_lit;
        struct { double     value;              } real_lit;
        struct { const char *start; int length; } string_lit;
        BinaryOp        binary_op;
        UnaryOp         unary_op;
        ColDef          col_def;
        ColumnRef       column_ref;
    } as;
} AstNode;
```
**Byte-layout note (x86-64, 8-byte alignment):**
| Offset | Size | Field |
|--------|------|-------|
| 0 | 4 | `type` (enum, int-sized) |
| 4 | 4 | `line` |
| 8 | 4 | `col` |
| 12 | 4 | padding (compiler-inserted) |
| 16 | 8 | `*next` |
| 24 | ≤40 | `union as` (largest member: `SelectStmt`, ~40 bytes) |
Total: `sizeof(AstNode)` ≈ 64 bytes. With an arena allocator this is negligible; document it so students understand the memory budget for a 1 000-node parse tree (≈ 64 KB).
{{DIAGRAM:tdd-diag-4}}
### 3.5 Public AST API
```c
/* src/ast.h (continued) */
/* Allocate a zero-initialised AstNode. arena == NULL → malloc. */
AstNode *ast_node_alloc(NodeType type);
/* Recursively free an entire subtree (no-op if node == NULL). */
void ast_free(AstNode *node);
/* Render node back to canonical SQL (caller frees returned string). */
char *ast_to_sql(const AstNode *node);
/* Human-readable name for a NodeType value (for test failure messages). */
const char *ast_node_type_name(NodeType t);
#endif /* SQLITE_AST_H */
```
---
## 4. Phase 1 — `src/ast.h` and `src/ast.c`
### 4.1 `ast_node_alloc`
```c
/* src/ast.c */
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include "ast.h"
AstNode *ast_node_alloc(NodeType type) {
    AstNode *n = calloc(1, sizeof(AstNode));  /* zero-init all fields */
    if (!n) {
        fprintf(stderr, "ast_node_alloc: out of memory\n");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);           /* no recovery in a teaching engine */
    }
    n->type = type;
    n->next = NULL;
    return n;
}
```
**Arena upgrade path (optional, not required for passing tests):** Replace `calloc` with a bump pointer into a pre-allocated slab. The call site is identical. Wrap in `#ifdef USE_ARENA` to keep the simple path buildable.
### 4.2 `ast_free`
```c
void ast_free(AstNode *node) {
    if (!node) return;
    /* Free children before self */
    switch (node->type) {
        case NODE_SELECT:
            ast_free(node->as.select.projections);
            ast_free(node->as.select.where_clause);
            ast_free(node->as.select.order_by);
            break;
        case NODE_INSERT:
            ast_free(node->as.insert.columns);
            ast_free(node->as.insert.values);
            break;
        case NODE_CREATE_TABLE:
            ast_free(node->as.create_table.col_defs);
            break;
        case NODE_BINARY_OP:
            ast_free(node->as.binary_op.left);
            ast_free(node->as.binary_op.right);
            break;
        case NODE_UNARY_OP:
            ast_free(node->as.unary_op.operand);
            break;
        default:
            break;  /* leaf nodes have no AstNode children */
    }
    /* Free sibling */
    ast_free(node->next);
    free(node);
}
```
**Stack depth warning:** For a 10 000-node linked list passed as a child list, the sibling recursion `ast_free(node->next)` will overflow the stack. Acceptable for this module (no query will have 10 000 projections). Document the limit; fix in a later module with an iterative walk if needed.
### 4.3 `ast_node_type_name`
```c
const char *ast_node_type_name(NodeType t) {
    switch (t) {
        case NODE_SELECT:       return "SELECT";
        case NODE_INSERT:       return "INSERT";
        case NODE_CREATE_TABLE: return "CREATE_TABLE";
        case NODE_DROP_TABLE:   return "DROP_TABLE";
        case NODE_IDENTIFIER:   return "IDENTIFIER";
        case NODE_INTEGER_LIT:  return "INTEGER_LIT";
        case NODE_REAL_LIT:     return "REAL_LIT";
        case NODE_STRING_LIT:   return "STRING_LIT";
        case NODE_STAR:         return "STAR";
        case NODE_BINARY_OP:    return "BINARY_OP";
        case NODE_UNARY_OP:     return "UNARY_OP";
        case NODE_AND:          return "AND";
        case NODE_OR:           return "OR";
        case NODE_NOT:          return "NOT";
        case NODE_COL_DEF:      return "COL_DEF";
        case NODE_COLUMN_REF:   return "COLUMN_REF";
        default:                return "<unknown>";
    }
}
```
### 4.4 `ast_to_sql` (Round-Trip Unparser)
The unparser serialises the AST back to canonical SQL. It is used in test spec M2-TS-05 (§10.5). It does **not** need to reproduce the original whitespace or capitalisation — it produces a normalised form.
```c
#include <stdio.h>
/* Internal: write node SQL into buf[pos..buf_size). Returns chars written. */
static int unparse_node(const AstNode *n, char *buf, int pos, int buf_size);
static int unparse_list(const AstNode *head, const char *sep,
                        char *buf, int pos, int buf_size) {
    int first = 1;
    for (const AstNode *n = head; n; n = n->next) {
        if (!first) {
            int sep_len = (int)strlen(sep);
            if (pos + sep_len < buf_size) {
                memcpy(buf + pos, sep, sep_len);
                pos += sep_len;
            }
        }
        pos = unparse_node(n, buf, pos, buf_size);
        first = 0;
    }
    return pos;
}
static int unparse_node(const AstNode *n, char *buf, int pos, int buf_size) {
#define EMIT(fmt, ...) \
    pos += snprintf(buf + pos, (buf_size - pos > 0 ? buf_size - pos : 0), \
                    fmt, ##__VA_ARGS__)
    if (!n || pos >= buf_size) return pos;
    switch (n->type) {
        case NODE_SELECT: {
            EMIT("SELECT ");
            if (n->as.select.distinct) EMIT("DISTINCT ");
            pos = unparse_list(n->as.select.projections, ", ", buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(" FROM %.*s",
                 n->as.select.table_name_len, n->as.select.table_name);
            if (n->as.select.where_clause) {
                EMIT(" WHERE ");
                pos = unparse_node(n->as.select.where_clause, buf, pos, buf_size);
            }
            if (n->as.select.order_by) {
                EMIT(" ORDER BY ");
                pos = unparse_list(n->as.select.order_by, ", ", buf, pos, buf_size);
            }
            if (n->as.select.limit >= 0)
                EMIT(" LIMIT %d", n->as.select.limit);
            break;
        }
        case NODE_INSERT: {
            EMIT("INSERT INTO %.*s",
                 n->as.insert.table_name_len, n->as.insert.table_name);
            if (n->as.insert.columns) {
                EMIT(" (");
                pos = unparse_list(n->as.insert.columns, ", ", buf, pos, buf_size);
                EMIT(")");
            }
            EMIT(" VALUES (");
            pos = unparse_list(n->as.insert.values, ", ", buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(")");
            break;
        }
        case NODE_CREATE_TABLE: {
            EMIT("CREATE TABLE ");
            if (n->as.create_table.if_not_exists) EMIT("IF NOT EXISTS ");
            EMIT("%.*s (", n->as.create_table.table_name_len,
                           n->as.create_table.table_name);
            pos = unparse_list(n->as.create_table.col_defs, ", ", buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(")");
            break;
        }
        case NODE_DROP_TABLE:
            EMIT("DROP TABLE %.*s", n->as.drop_table.name_len,
                                    n->as.drop_table.name);
            break;
        case NODE_COL_DEF: {
            static const char *aff_names[] =
                {"", "INTEGER", "REAL", "TEXT", "BLOB", "NUMERIC"};
            EMIT("%.*s %s", n->as.col_def.name_len, n->as.col_def.name,
                 aff_names[n->as.col_def.affinity]);
            if (n->as.col_def.primary_key) EMIT(" PRIMARY KEY");
            if (n->as.col_def.not_null)    EMIT(" NOT NULL");
            break;
        }
        case NODE_COLUMN_REF:
            if (n->as.column_ref.table_alias)
                EMIT("%.*s.", n->as.column_ref.table_alias_len,
                              n->as.column_ref.table_alias);
            EMIT("%.*s", n->as.column_ref.column_len,
                         n->as.column_ref.column);
            break;
        case NODE_IDENTIFIER:
            EMIT("%.*s", n->as.identifier.length, n->as.identifier.start);
            break;
        case NODE_INTEGER_LIT:
            EMIT("%lld", n->as.integer_lit.value);
            break;
        case NODE_REAL_LIT:
            EMIT("%g", n->as.real_lit.value);
            break;
        case NODE_STRING_LIT:
            /* Re-quote the string, skipping surrounding quotes stored in lexeme */
            EMIT("'%.*s'", n->as.string_lit.length - 2, n->as.string_lit.start + 1);
            break;
        case NODE_STAR:
            EMIT("*");
            break;
        case NODE_BINARY_OP:
            EMIT("(");
            pos = unparse_node(n->as.binary_op.left,  buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(" %s ", n->as.binary_op.op);
            pos = unparse_node(n->as.binary_op.right, buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(")");
            break;
        case NODE_UNARY_OP:
            EMIT("(%s ", n->as.unary_op.op);
            pos = unparse_node(n->as.unary_op.operand, buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(")");
            break;
        case NODE_AND:
            EMIT("(");
            pos = unparse_node(n->as.binary_op.left,  buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(" AND ");
            pos = unparse_node(n->as.binary_op.right, buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(")");
            break;
        case NODE_OR:
            EMIT("(");
            pos = unparse_node(n->as.binary_op.left,  buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(" OR ");
            pos = unparse_node(n->as.binary_op.right, buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(")");
            break;
        case NODE_NOT:
            EMIT("(NOT ");
            pos = unparse_node(n->as.unary_op.operand, buf, pos, buf_size);
            EMIT(")");
            break;
        default:
            EMIT("<?>"); break;
    }
#undef EMIT
    return pos;
}
char *ast_to_sql(const AstNode *node) {
    int   cap = 4096;
    char *buf = malloc(cap);
    if (!buf) return NULL;
    int len = unparse_node(node, buf, 0, cap);
    if (len >= cap) {                /* truncated — retry with exact size */
        free(buf);
        buf = malloc(len + 1);
        if (!buf) return NULL;
        unparse_node(node, buf, 0, len + 1);
    }
    buf[len < cap ? len : cap - 1] = '\0';
    return buf;
}
```
---
## 5. Phase 2 — `src/parser.h` and Parser Struct
```c
/* src/parser.h */
#ifndef SQLITE_PARSER_H
#define SQLITE_PARSER_H
#include "lexer.h"
#include "ast.h"
typedef struct {
    const Token *tokens;       /* array from lexer_tokenize_all */
    int          count;        /* length of tokens[] */
    int          pos;          /* current read head (index) */
    int          had_error;    /* set on first error; parsing stops */
    char         error_msg[256];
    int          error_line;
    int          error_col;
} Parser;
void     parser_init          (Parser *p, const Token *tokens, int count);
AstNode *parser_parse         (Parser *p);          /* returns NULL on error */
const char *parser_error_message(const Parser *p);  /* "" if no error */
#endif /* SQLITE_PARSER_H */
```
**Design decision:** `Parser` is stack-allocated by the caller, passed by pointer to every `parse_*` helper. No heap allocation for parser state. This matches the pattern from M1's `Lexer`.
---
## 6. Phase 3 — Parser Helpers and Pratt Expression Engine
### 6.1 Token Navigation Helpers
```c
/* src/parser.c */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <errno.h>
#include "parser.h"
void parser_init(Parser *p, const Token *tokens, int count) {
    memset(p, 0, sizeof(*p));
    p->tokens = tokens;
    p->count  = count;
    p->pos    = 0;
}
const char *parser_error_message(const Parser *p) {
    return p->had_error ? p->error_msg : "";
}
/* Internal helpers — all static */
static const Token *peek(const Parser *p) {
    return &p->tokens[p->pos < p->count ? p->pos : p->count - 1];
}
static const Token *peek2(const Parser *p) {
    int idx = p->pos + 1;
    return &p->tokens[idx < p->count ? idx : p->count - 1];
}
static const Token *advance(Parser *p) {
    const Token *t = peek(p);
    if (p->pos < p->count) p->pos++;
    return t;
}
static int check_type(const Parser *p, TokenType tt) {
    return peek(p)->type == tt;
}
static int match_keyword(Parser *p, const char *kw) {
    if (peek(p)->type == TOK_KEYWORD && lexeme_equals(peek(p), kw)) {
        advance(p); return 1;
    }
    return 0;
}
static int match_type(Parser *p, TokenType tt) {
    if (check_type(p, tt)) { advance(p); return 1; }
    return 0;
}
/* Record an error and mark parser as failed. Call sites return NULL after. */
static void parse_error(Parser *p, const Token *tok, const char *msg) {
    if (p->had_error) return;   /* only first error matters */
    p->had_error   = 1;
    p->error_line  = tok->line;
    p->error_col   = tok->col;
    snprintf(p->error_msg, sizeof(p->error_msg),
             "[line %d, col %d] %s (got '%.*s')",
             tok->line, tok->col, msg, tok->length, tok->start);
}
/* Consume next token if it matches type; otherwise error. Returns token. */
static const Token *expect(Parser *p, TokenType tt, const char *ctx) {
    if (check_type(p, tt)) return advance(p);
    char errbuf[128];
    snprintf(errbuf, sizeof(errbuf), "expected %s in %s", /* type name */ "token", ctx);
    parse_error(p, peek(p), errbuf);
    return peek(p);   /* return current (un-advanced) token for position */
}
static const Token *expect_keyword(Parser *p, const char *kw, const char *ctx) {
    if (peek(p)->type == TOK_KEYWORD && lexeme_equals(peek(p), kw))
        return advance(p);
    char errbuf[128];
    snprintf(errbuf, sizeof(errbuf), "expected keyword '%s' in %s", kw, ctx);
    parse_error(p, peek(p), errbuf);
    return peek(p);
}
```
### 6.2 Pratt Expression Engine

![Pratt Precedence Climbing Algorithm](./diagrams/tdd-diag-5.svg)

The Pratt parser models each operator by two values:
- **Left binding power (lbp):** how tightly this operator binds to the expression on its left.
- **Right binding power (rbp):** how tightly this operator binds to the expression on its right. For left-associative operators, `rbp = lbp + 1`. For right-associative, `rbp = lbp`.
The recursive call `parse_expr(p, rbp)` collects the right-hand side. Parsing stops when the next operator's lbp ≤ min_bp passed from the caller.
#### Binding Power Table
| Operator | lbp | rbp | Associativity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `OR` | 10 | 11 | left |
| `AND` | 20 | 21 | left |
| `NOT` (prefix) | — | 25 | — |
| `=` `!=` `<` `<=` `>` `>=` | 30 | 31 | left (non-chain) |
| `+` `-` | 40 | 41 | left |
| `*` `/` `%` | 50 | 51 | left |
| Unary `-` (prefix) | — | 60 | — |
```c
/* Returns the left binding power of the current token, or -1 if not an infix op. */
static int infix_lbp(const Token *t) {
    if (t->type == TOK_KEYWORD) {
        if (lexeme_equals(t, "OR"))  return 10;
        if (lexeme_equals(t, "AND")) return 20;
    }
    if (t->type == TOK_EQ  || t->type == TOK_NEQ ||
        t->type == TOK_LT  || t->type == TOK_LE  ||
        t->type == TOK_GT  || t->type == TOK_GE)  return 30;
    if (t->type == TOK_PLUS  || t->type == TOK_MINUS)  return 40;
    if (t->type == TOK_STAR  || t->type == TOK_SLASH ||
        t->type == TOK_PERCENT)                        return 50;
    return -1;
}
/* Parse a prefix (nud) expression and return its node. */
static AstNode *parse_prefix(Parser *p);
/* Full Pratt expression parse. min_bp = 0 at top level. */
static AstNode *parse_expr(Parser *p, int min_bp) {
    if (p->had_error) return NULL;
    AstNode *left = parse_prefix(p);
    if (!left || p->had_error) return left;
    for (;;) {
        const Token *op = peek(p);
        int lbp = infix_lbp(op);
        if (lbp < 0 || lbp <= min_bp) break;
        advance(p);  /* consume operator */
        /* Determine rbp and node type */
        AstNode *node;
        if (op->type == TOK_KEYWORD && lexeme_equals(op, "AND")) {
            node = ast_node_alloc(NODE_AND);
        } else if (op->type == TOK_KEYWORD && lexeme_equals(op, "OR")) {
            node = ast_node_alloc(NODE_OR);
        } else {
            node = ast_node_alloc(NODE_BINARY_OP);
            /* Copy operator string (≤2 chars + NUL) */
            int olen = op->length < 2 ? op->length : 2;
            memcpy(node->as.binary_op.op, op->start, olen);
            node->as.binary_op.op[olen] = '\0';
        }
        node->line = op->line;
        node->col  = op->col;
        int rbp = lbp + 1;   /* all operators here are left-associative */
        AstNode *right = parse_expr(p, rbp);
        if (!right || p->had_error) { ast_free(node); ast_free(left); return NULL; }
        /* Wire left and right */
        if (node->type == NODE_AND || node->type == NODE_OR) {
            node->as.binary_op.left  = left;
            node->as.binary_op.right = right;
        } else {
            node->as.binary_op.left  = left;
            node->as.binary_op.right = right;
        }
        left = node;
    }
    return left;
}
static AstNode *parse_prefix(Parser *p) {
    if (p->had_error) return NULL;
    const Token *t = peek(p);
    /* Unary minus */
    if (t->type == TOK_MINUS) {
        advance(p);
        AstNode *node = ast_node_alloc(NODE_UNARY_OP);
        node->line = t->line; node->col = t->col;
        strcpy(node->as.unary_op.op, "-");
        node->as.unary_op.operand = parse_expr(p, 60); /* rbp=60 */
        return node;
    }
    /* NOT */
    if (t->type == TOK_KEYWORD && lexeme_equals(t, "NOT")) {
        advance(p);
        AstNode *node = ast_node_alloc(NODE_NOT);
        node->line = t->line; node->col = t->col;
        strcpy(node->as.unary_op.op, "NOT");
        node->as.unary_op.operand = parse_expr(p, 25); /* rbp=25 */
        return node;
    }
    /* Grouped expression */
    if (t->type == TOK_LPAREN) {
        advance(p);
        AstNode *inner = parse_expr(p, 0);
        expect(p, TOK_RPAREN, "grouped expression");
        return inner;
    }
    /* Literals and identifiers */
    advance(p);
    AstNode *node;
    switch (t->type) {
        case TOK_INTEGER_LIT:
            node = ast_node_alloc(NODE_INTEGER_LIT);
            node->line = t->line; node->col = t->col;
            /* Parse lexeme to integer */
            node->as.integer_lit.value = strtoll(t->start, NULL, 10);
            return node;
        case TOK_REAL_LIT:
            node = ast_node_alloc(NODE_REAL_LIT);
            node->line = t->line; node->col = t->col;
            node->as.real_lit.value = strtod(t->start, NULL);
            return node;
        case TOK_STRING_LIT:
            node = ast_node_alloc(NODE_STRING_LIT);
            node->line = t->line; node->col = t->col;
            node->as.string_lit.start  = t->start;
            node->as.string_lit.length = t->length;
            return node;
        case TOK_STAR:
            node = ast_node_alloc(NODE_STAR);
```
