```c
#define WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE    24
#define WAL_FRAME_SIZE           (WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE + PAGE_SIZE)  /* 24 + 4096 = 4120 */
#define WAL_MAGIC_BE             0x377F0682U
#define WAL_MAGIC_LE             0x377F0683U
#define WAL_FORMAT_VERSION       3007000U
/* Default auto-checkpoint threshold: frames before auto-checkpoint fires */
#define WAL_DEFAULT_AUTOCHECKPOINT  1000
typedef struct {
    uint32_t magic;              /* offset 0x00, 4 bytes */
    uint32_t format_version;     /* offset 0x04, 4 bytes */
    uint32_t page_size;          /* offset 0x08, 4 bytes */
    uint32_t checkpoint_seq;     /* offset 0x0C, 4 bytes */
    uint32_t salt1;              /* offset 0x10, 4 bytes */
    uint32_t salt2;              /* offset 0x14, 4 bytes */
    uint32_t checksum1;          /* offset 0x18, 4 bytes */
    uint32_t checksum2;          /* offset 0x1C, 4 bytes */
} WalHeader;
/* sizeof(WalHeader) == 32. Matches on-disk layout exactly. */
```
### 3.2 WAL Frame Header — Byte Layout
Each frame is `WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE` + `PAGE_SIZE` = 4120 bytes.
```
WAL Frame (4120 bytes):
Offset  Size       Field         Description
------  ---------  -----------   -----------------------------------------------
0x00    4          page_number   Database page ID (big-endian uint32)
0x04    4          db_size       Num pages in DB after this commit; 0 if NOT commit frame
0x08    4          salt1         Must match WAL header salt1
0x0C    4          salt2         Must match WAL header salt2
0x10    4          checksum1     Cumulative checksum part 1
0x14    4          checksum2     Cumulative checksum part 2
0x18    PAGE_SIZE  page_data     Modified page content
```
```c
typedef struct {
    uint32_t page_number;    /* offset 0x00 */
    uint32_t db_size;        /* offset 0x04 — non-zero = commit frame */
    uint32_t salt1;          /* offset 0x08 */
    uint32_t salt2;          /* offset 0x0C */
    uint32_t checksum1;      /* offset 0x10 */
    uint32_t checksum2;      /* offset 0x14 */
} WalFrameHeader;
/* sizeof(WalFrameHeader) == 24. Matches on-disk layout exactly. */
```

![WAL Frame Binary Layout](./diagrams/tdd-diag-33.svg)

*Figure 33: WAL file binary layout. The 32-byte header contains magic, version, page_size, checkpoint_seq, salt pair, and header checksum pair. Each frame contains a 24-byte frame header (page_number, db_size, salt pair, cumulative checksum pair) followed by PAGE_SIZE bytes of page data. Frames are appended sequentially. The last frame of each committed transaction has db_size > 0. Frames after the last commit marker are uncommitted and ignored during recovery.*
### 3.3 WAL Checksum Algorithm
The WAL checksum is **cumulative**: each frame's checksum incorporates the previous frame's checksum as its initial state. This creates a chain where corruption of any byte invalidates all subsequent checksums.
The algorithm processes data in 8-byte (two uint32) chunks. It is endian-sensitive: the `magic` field determines whether the host is native byte order (`WAL_MAGIC_BE` on big-endian, `WAL_MAGIC_LE` on little-endian; for this implementation, always use native byte order and set magic to `WAL_MAGIC_BE` since we read/write big-endian explicitly).
```c
/* Cumulative checksum over `len` bytes of `data`.
   `cksum1` and `cksum2` are IN/OUT: pass previous frame's checksum
   as initial values. For the WAL header, initial values are both 0.
   `native_byte_order`: 1 = host native, 0 = byte-swapped.
   len MUST be a multiple of 8.  */
void wal_checksum(const uint8_t *data, int len,
                  uint32_t *cksum1, uint32_t *cksum2);
```
**Implementation:**
```c
void wal_checksum(const uint8_t *data, int len,
                  uint32_t *cksum1, uint32_t *cksum2) {
    uint32_t s1 = *cksum1;
    uint32_t s2 = *cksum2;
    /* Process data as pairs of big-endian uint32 values */
    for (int i = 0; i < len; i += 8) {
        uint32_t d0 = read_be32(data + i);
        uint32_t d1 = read_be32(data + i + 4);
        s1 += d0 + s2;
        s2 += d1 + s1;
    }
    *cksum1 = s1;
    *cksum2 = s2;
}
```
**Checksum coverage per frame:**
1. Start from the previous frame's `(checksum1, checksum2)` — or `(0, 0)` for the WAL header itself.
2. For the WAL header: checksum bytes 0x00–0x17 (first 24 bytes; the checksum fields at 0x18–0x1F are NOT included). `len = 24`, but 24 is a multiple of 8 ✓.
3. For each frame: checksum the 8 bytes `[page_number(4) || db_size(4)]` then the `PAGE_SIZE` bytes of page data. `len = 8 + PAGE_SIZE`. Since `PAGE_SIZE = 4096`, `8 + 4096 = 4104` which is a multiple of 8 ✓. The salt fields in the frame header are NOT included in the checksum (they're verified by direct comparison against the WAL header salts).
### 3.4 WalIndex — In-Memory Page-to-Frame Map
```c
/* src/wal_index.h */
#ifndef WAL_INDEX_H
#define WAL_INDEX_H
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdbool.h>
#include "pager.h"   /* page_id_t */
#define WAL_INDEX_INITIAL_CAPACITY  4096
#define WAL_INDEX_LOAD_FACTOR_MAX   0.75
typedef struct {
    page_id_t page_id;     /* INVALID_PAGE_ID = empty slot */
    int       frame_num;   /* 0-based frame index in the WAL file */
} WalIndexEntry;
typedef struct {
    WalIndexEntry *slots;     /* open-addressing hash table */
    int            capacity;  /* number of slots allocated */
    int            count;     /* number of occupied slots */
} WalIndex;
/* Create a WalIndex with initial capacity. */
WalIndex *wal_index_create(int initial_capacity);
/* Destroy and free. */
void wal_index_destroy(WalIndex *idx);
/* Set the mapping page_id → frame_num. If page_id already mapped,
   updates to new frame_num (only if new frame_num > existing — 
   ensures most-recent-frame-wins). */
void wal_index_set(WalIndex *idx, page_id_t page_id, int frame_num);
/* Look up the most recent frame for page_id with frame_num <= max_frame.
   Returns the frame_num if found, -1 if not found or all entries exceed max_frame. */
int wal_index_get(const WalIndex *idx, page_id_t page_id, int max_frame);
/* Clear all entries (used after full checkpoint). */
void wal_index_clear(WalIndex *idx);
#endif /* WAL_INDEX_H */
```
**Design note — `max_frame` filtering:** The WalIndex stores only the *latest* frame number for each page (the most recent write wins). To support snapshot isolation where a reader's `max_frame` is older than the latest entry, `wal_index_get` must check that the stored `frame_num <= max_frame`. If the stored frame exceeds the reader's snapshot, the page is considered not-in-WAL for that reader and the database file version is used.
For correctness with snapshot isolation, we need to store the *entire history* of frame numbers per page, not just the latest. However, this significantly increases complexity and memory usage. **Simplification for M10:** We store only the latest frame number per page. A reader whose `max_frame` is less than the stored frame treats the page as absent from the WAL and reads from the database file. This is correct for the common case where readers start after the writer commits. For long-running readers that overlap multiple writer transactions, this may cause the reader to see an older database file version of a page even though a committed WAL frame within its snapshot exists. To fully support this, maintain an array of `(frame_num, page_id)` pairs and binary-search for the correct frame. This full approach is specified as an optional extension; the base implementation uses single-entry-per-page.
**Full correctness approach (required for M10-TS-02 snapshot test):** Store a small history per page using a secondary structure:
```c
/* Frame history: sorted array of all (frame_num, page_id) pairs.
   Used for snapshot-bounded lookups when the WalIndex single-entry
   is insufficient. */
typedef struct {
    int        *frame_nums;    /* sorted ascending */
    page_id_t  *page_ids;     /* parallel array */
    int         count;
    int         capacity;
} WalFrameLog;
/* Append a frame to the log. Maintains sorted order (append-only). */
void wal_frame_log_append(WalFrameLog *log, int frame_num, page_id_t page_id);
/* Find the largest frame_num <= max_frame where page_id matches.
   Returns frame_num or -1 if not found. */
int wal_frame_log_find(const WalFrameLog *log, page_id_t page_id, int max_frame);
```
### 3.5 SnapshotManager
```c
#define WAL_MAX_READERS 32
typedef struct {
    int   max_frame;     /* latest committed frame visible to this reader */
    bool  is_active;     /* true while reader is running */
} ReaderSnapshot;
typedef struct {
    ReaderSnapshot readers[WAL_MAX_READERS];
    int            writer_max_frame;  /* latest committed frame overall */
} SnapshotManager;
/* Begin a read snapshot. Returns max_frame for this reader. 
   Sets *reader_id to the slot index. Returns -1 if all slots full. */
int snapshot_begin_read(SnapshotManager *sm, int *reader_id);
/* End a read snapshot. Releases the slot. */
void snapshot_end_read(SnapshotManager *sm, int reader_id);
/* Update writer's committed frame after a successful WAL commit. */
void snapshot_update_writer(SnapshotManager *sm, int new_max_frame);
/* Find the minimum max_frame among all active readers.
   Used by checkpoint to determine safe truncation point.
   Returns writer_max_frame if no readers are active. */
int snapshot_min_reader_frame(const SnapshotManager *sm);
```
### 3.6 Wal Struct — Top-Level State
```c
typedef struct {
    /* File I/O */
    int             fd;                  /* file descriptor for .db-wal */
    char            path[256];           /* "<db_path>-wal" */
    /* Header state */
    WalHeader       header;              /* current header (in-memory copy) */
    /* Running checksum state (for the WRITER — accumulates across frames) */
    uint32_t        write_cksum1;
    uint32_t        write_cksum2;
    /* Frame tracking */
    int             frame_count;         /* total frames in WAL (committed + uncommitted) */
    int             last_commit_frame;   /* frame index of the last commit marker; -1 if none */
    /* WAL index */
    WalIndex       *index;               /* page_id → latest frame_num */
    WalFrameLog     frame_log;           /* full frame history for snapshot lookups */
    /* Snapshot manager */
    SnapshotManager snapshots;
    /* Auto-checkpoint threshold */
    int             autocheckpoint_threshold;  /* default WAL_DEFAULT_AUTOCHECKPOINT */
    /* Mode flag */
    bool            enabled;             /* true when WAL mode is active */
} Wal;
```
**Byte layout on LP64:**
| Offset | Size | Field |
|--------|------|-------|
| 0 | 4 | `fd` |
| 4 | 256 | `path` |
| 260 | 32 | `header` (WalHeader) |
| 292 | 4 | `write_cksum1` |
| 296 | 4 | `write_cksum2` |
| 300 | 4 | `frame_count` |
| 304 | 4 | `last_commit_frame` |
| 308 | 4 | padding |
| 312 | 8 | `index` pointer |
| 320 | ~24 | `frame_log` (3 fields) |
| ~344 | ~296 | `snapshots` (SnapshotManager) |
| ~640 | 4 | `autocheckpoint_threshold` |
| ~644 | 1 | `enabled` |
`sizeof(Wal)` ≈ 648 bytes (approximate; exact depends on padding).
---
## 4. Interface Contracts
### 4.1 `wal_open`
```c
int wal_open(Wal *wal, const char *db_path, int page_size);
```
**Preconditions:** `wal != NULL`, `db_path != NULL`, `page_size == PAGE_SIZE`.
**Postconditions (on success, returns 0):**
- If `.db-wal` file exists: opens it, reads and validates header (magic, page_size), calls `wal_recover` to scan frames and rebuild index. `wal->enabled = true`.
- If `.db-wal` does not exist: creates it, writes fresh header with `checkpoint_seq = 0`, random salts, header checksum. `wal->frame_count = 0`. `wal->enabled = true`.
- `wal->index` allocated via `wal_index_create`.
- `wal->frame_log` initialized empty.
- `wal->snapshots` zeroed (no active readers, `writer_max_frame = -1` or 0).
- `wal->autocheckpoint_threshold = WAL_DEFAULT_AUTOCHECKPOINT`.
**Error (returns -1):** `open()` fails, header magic/page_size mismatch on existing WAL (deletes corrupt WAL and creates fresh).
### 4.2 `wal_close`
```c
void wal_close(Wal *wal);
```
Calls `wal_checkpoint` (if any frames exist), then closes `fd`, frees `index` and `frame_log`. Sets `wal->enabled = false`.
### 4.3 `wal_write_frame`
```c
int wal_write_frame(Wal *wal, page_id_t page_id,
                    const uint8_t *page_data,
                    bool is_commit, uint32_t db_size_if_commit);
```
**Preconditions:** `wal->enabled == true`, `wal->fd >= 0`, `page_data` points to PAGE_SIZE bytes.
**Postconditions (on success, returns 0):**
1. Builds `WalFrameHeader` with:
   - `page_number = page_id`
   - `db_size = is_commit ? db_size_if_commit : 0`
   - `salt1 = wal->header.salt1`, `salt2 = wal->header.salt2`
2. Computes cumulative checksum:
   - Input buffer: `[page_number(4 bytes BE) || db_size(4 bytes BE)]` = 8 bytes.
   - `wal_checksum(input_8bytes, 8, &wal->write_cksum1, &wal->write_cksum2)`.
   - Then: `wal_checksum(page_data, PAGE_SIZE, &wal->write_cksum1, &wal->write_cksum2)`.
   - Stores results as `checksum1`, `checksum2` in the frame header.
3. Writes frame header (24 bytes) + page data (PAGE_SIZE bytes) at offset `WAL_HEADER_SIZE + wal->frame_count * WAL_FRAME_SIZE` via `pwrite`.
4. Updates `wal->index` via `wal_index_set(wal->index, page_id, wal->frame_count)`.
5. Appends to `wal->frame_log`: `wal_frame_log_append(&wal->frame_log, wal->frame_count, page_id)`.
6. Increments `wal->frame_count`.
7. If `is_commit`:
   - `fsync(wal->fd)` — **only commit frames trigger fsync**.
   - `wal->last_commit_frame = wal->frame_count - 1`.
   - `snapshot_update_writer(&wal->snapshots, wal->frame_count - 1)`.
   - Check auto-checkpoint: if `wal->frame_count >= wal->autocheckpoint_threshold`, call `wal_checkpoint`.
**Error (returns -1):** `pwrite` or `fsync` failure.
**Performance note:** Non-commit frames are written but NOT fsynced. If the process crashes, uncommitted frames are lost — which is correct behavior (the transaction was never committed).
### 4.4 `wal_read_page`
```c
int wal_read_page(Wal *wal, page_id_t page_id, int max_frame,
                  uint8_t *dest, BufferPool *pool);
```
**Preconditions:** `wal->enabled == true`, `dest` points to PAGE_SIZE bytes.
**Postconditions (on success, returns 0):**
1. Looks up `page_id` in `wal->frame_log` with `max_frame` bound: `frame_num = wal_frame_log_find(&wal->frame_log, page_id, max_frame)`.
2. If `frame_num >= 0`: page found in WAL. Read PAGE_SIZE bytes from WAL file at offset `WAL_HEADER_SIZE + frame_num * WAL_FRAME_SIZE + WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE`. Copy to `dest`. Return 0.
3. If `frame_num < 0`: page not in WAL within this snapshot. Read from database file via `pread(pool->fd, dest, PAGE_SIZE, (off_t)page_id * PAGE_SIZE)`. Return 0.
**Error (returns -1):** `pread` failure.

![Concurrent Readers and Writer Snapshot Isolation](./diagrams/tdd-diag-34.svg)

*Figure 34: WAL read path decision flow. Entry: wal_read_page(page_id, max_frame). Step 1: wal_frame_log_find(page_id, max_frame). Found? Yes → compute file offset = WAL_HEADER_SIZE + frame_num × WAL_FRAME_SIZE + WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE. pread from WAL file. Copy to dest. Return 0. Not found? → pread from database file at page_id × PAGE_SIZE. Copy to dest. Return 0.*
### 4.5 `wal_commit`
```c
int wal_commit(Wal *wal, BufferPool *pool);
```
**Preconditions:** At least one frame has been appended since the last commit (or since WAL open).
**Postconditions (on success, returns 0):**
1. Iterates all dirty pages in the buffer pool.
2. Finds the highest `page_id + 1` among dirty pages → `db_page_count`.
3. Appends all dirty pages as frames via `wal_write_frame`. The LAST dirty page is written with `is_commit = true`, `db_size_if_commit = db_page_count`.
4. Clears all dirty flags in the buffer pool (pages are now in the WAL; no database file write needed).
5. Checks auto-checkpoint.
**Error (returns -1):** Any `wal_write_frame` failure.
### 4.6 `wal_checkpoint`
```c
int wal_checkpoint(Wal *wal, BufferPool *pool);
```
**Preconditions:** `wal->enabled == true`.
**Postconditions (on success, returns number of pages written):**
1. Determines safe frame: `safe_frame = snapshot_min_reader_frame(&wal->snapshots)`. If no active readers, `safe_frame = wal->frame_count - 1`.
2. For each frame 0 through `safe_frame`:
   - Reads frame header to get `page_number`.
   - Reads PAGE_SIZE bytes of frame data.
   - Writes to database file at offset `page_number * PAGE_SIZE` via `pwrite(pool->fd, ...)`.
   - Invalidates this page in the buffer pool if cached (evict the stale cached version).
3. `fsync(pool->fd)`.
4. If `safe_frame >= wal->frame_count - 1` (all frames checkpointed):
   - Truncate WAL to 0 bytes: `ftruncate(wal->fd, 0)`.
   - Write fresh header: increment `checkpoint_seq`, generate new `salt1`/`salt2`, recompute header checksum.
   - Reset `wal->frame_count = 0`, `wal->last_commit_frame = -1`.
   - Clear `wal->write_cksum1 = header.checksum1`, `wal->write_cksum2 = header.checksum2`.
   - `wal_index_clear(wal->index)`.
   - Clear `wal->frame_log`.
5. Else (partial checkpoint — some frames remain for active readers):
   - Do NOT truncate WAL.
   - Remove checkpointed frame entries from the WalIndex only if they are not the latest entry for their page.
**Error (returns -1):** Any `pread`/`pwrite`/`fsync` failure.

![Checkpoint Algorithm Step-by-Step](./diagrams/tdd-diag-35.svg)

*Figure 35: Checkpoint execution flow. Step 1: safe_frame = snapshot_min_reader_frame(). Step 2: For frame_num = 0 to safe_frame: read frame from WAL → pwrite to database file at page offset. Step 3: fsync(db_fd). Step 4: If safe_frame == frame_count - 1: ftruncate WAL, write new header, clear index. Else: partial checkpoint — WAL retains frames > safe_frame. Dashed box: active reader snapshot pins frames; checkpoint cannot pass that boundary.*
### 4.7 `wal_recover`
```c
int wal_recover(Wal *wal, int page_size);
```
Called during `wal_open` when an existing WAL file is found. Scans all frames, validates checksums, rebuilds the WAL index and frame log, identifies the last committed frame.
**Algorithm:**
```
PROCEDURE wal_recover(wal, page_size):
    lseek(wal->fd, 0, SEEK_SET)
    // Read header
    read WAL_HEADER_SIZE bytes into buf
    parse WalHeader from buf
    validate: magic == WAL_MAGIC_BE, page_size == page_size arg
    IF validation fails: RETURN -1
    // Compute header checksum
    running_ck1 = 0, running_ck2 = 0
    wal_checksum(buf, 24, &running_ck1, &running_ck2)  // first 24 bytes
    IF running_ck1 != header.checksum1 OR running_ck2 != header.checksum2:
        RETURN -1   // corrupt header
    // Scan frames
    wal->frame_count = 0
    wal->last_commit_frame = -1
    frame_num = 0
    WHILE true:
        offset = WAL_HEADER_SIZE + frame_num * WAL_FRAME_SIZE
        // Read frame header (24 bytes)
        bytes = pread(wal->fd, frame_hdr_buf, WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE, offset)
        IF bytes < WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE: BREAK  // EOF
        parse WalFrameHeader from frame_hdr_buf
        // Validate salts
        IF frame_hdr.salt1 != wal->header.salt1 OR
           frame_hdr.salt2 != wal->header.salt2:
            BREAK  // different transaction cycle
        // Read page data
        bytes = pread(wal->fd, page_buf, PAGE_SIZE, offset + WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE)
        IF bytes < PAGE_SIZE: BREAK  // truncated frame
        // Verify cumulative checksum
        expected_ck1 = running_ck1
        expected_ck2 = running_ck2
        // Checksum over [page_number(4)||db_size(4)]
        uint8_t ck_input[8]
        write_be32(ck_input, frame_hdr.page_number)
        write_be32(ck_input + 4, frame_hdr.db_size)
        wal_checksum(ck_input, 8, &expected_ck1, &expected_ck2)
        wal_checksum(page_buf, PAGE_SIZE, &expected_ck1, &expected_ck2)
        IF expected_ck1 != frame_hdr.checksum1 OR
           expected_ck2 != frame_hdr.checksum2:
            BREAK  // checksum mismatch — torn write
        running_ck1 = expected_ck1
        running_ck2 = expected_ck2
        // Valid frame — update index
        wal_index_set(wal->index, frame_hdr.page_number, frame_num)
        wal_frame_log_append(&wal->frame_log, frame_num, frame_hdr.page_number)
        frame_num++
        // Check commit marker
        IF frame_hdr.db_size > 0:
            wal->last_commit_frame = frame_num - 1
    wal->frame_count = frame_num
    wal->write_cksum1 = running_ck1
    wal->write_cksum2 = running_ck2
    // Truncate uncommitted frames at tail
    IF wal->last_commit_frame >= 0 AND frame_num > wal->last_commit_frame + 1:
        off_t valid_size = WAL_HEADER_SIZE +
            (wal->last_commit_frame + 1) * WAL_FRAME_SIZE
        ftruncate(wal->fd, valid_size)
        wal->frame_count = wal->last_commit_frame + 1
        // Remove index entries for truncated frames
        // (frame_log entries beyond last_commit_frame are invalid)
        wal->frame_log.count = wal->last_commit_frame + 1
    snapshot_update_writer(&wal->snapshots,
                            wal->last_commit_frame >= 0 ? wal->last_commit_frame : -1)
    RETURN 0
```
### 4.8 WAL Header I/O
```c
/* Write WAL header to fd at offset 0. Returns 0/-1. */
int wal_write_header(int fd, const WalHeader *hdr);
/* Read WAL header from fd at offset 0. Returns 0/-1. */
int wal_read_header(int fd, WalHeader *hdr);
```
**`wal_write_header` implementation:**
```c
int wal_write_header(int fd, const WalHeader *hdr) {
    uint8_t buf[WAL_HEADER_SIZE];
    memset(buf, 0, WAL_HEADER_SIZE);
    write_be32(buf + 0,  hdr->magic);
    write_be32(buf + 4,  hdr->format_version);
    write_be32(buf + 8,  hdr->page_size);
    write_be32(buf + 12, hdr->checkpoint_seq);
    write_be32(buf + 16, hdr->salt1);
    write_be32(buf + 20, hdr->salt2);
    write_be32(buf + 24, hdr->checksum1);
    write_be32(buf + 28, hdr->checksum2);
    ssize_t bytes = pwrite(fd, buf, WAL_HEADER_SIZE, 0);
    return (bytes == WAL_HEADER_SIZE) ? 0 : -1;
}
```
**`wal_read_header` implementation:**
```c
int wal_read_header(int fd, WalHeader *hdr) {
    uint8_t buf[WAL_HEADER_SIZE];
    ssize_t bytes = pread(fd, buf, WAL_HEADER_SIZE, 0);
    if (bytes < WAL_HEADER_SIZE) return -1;
    hdr->magic          = read_be32(buf + 0);
    hdr->format_version = read_be32(buf + 4);
    hdr->page_size      = read_be32(buf + 8);
    hdr->checkpoint_seq  = read_be32(buf + 12);
    hdr->salt1          = read_be32(buf + 16);
    hdr->salt2          = read_be32(buf + 20);
    hdr->checksum1      = read_be32(buf + 24);
    hdr->checksum2      = read_be32(buf + 28);
    return 0;
}
```
### 4.9 Mode Switching
```c
/* Switch to WAL mode. Creates WAL file, attaches to pool. Returns 0/-1. */
int wal_mode_enable(Wal *wal, BufferPool *pool, const char *db_path);
/* Switch back to journal mode. Full checkpoint, delete WAL file. Returns 0/-1. */
int wal_mode_disable(Wal *wal, BufferPool *pool);
```
---
## 5. WalIndex Implementation Detail
### 5.1 Hash Function
```c
static uint32_t wal_index_hash(page_id_t page_id, int capacity) {
    return (uint32_t)((uint64_t)page_id * 2654435761ULL) % (uint32_t)capacity;
}
```
### 5.2 `wal_index_set`
```c
void wal_index_set(WalIndex *idx, page_id_t page_id, int frame_num) {
    /* Resize if load factor exceeded */
    if ((double)idx->count / (double)idx->capacity > WAL_INDEX_LOAD_FACTOR_MAX) {
        wal_index_resize(idx, idx->capacity * 2);
    }
    uint32_t h = wal_index_hash(page_id, idx->capacity);
    for (int i = 0; i < idx->capacity; i++) {
        uint32_t slot = (h + i) % idx->capacity;
        if (idx->slots[slot].page_id == INVALID_PAGE_ID) {
            /* Empty slot: insert */
            idx->slots[slot].page_id = page_id;
            idx->slots[slot].frame_num = frame_num;
            idx->count++;
            return;
        }
        if (idx->slots[slot].page_id == page_id) {
            /* Update: only if new frame is more recent */
            if (frame_num > idx->slots[slot].frame_num) {
                idx->slots[slot].frame_num = frame_num;
            }
            return;
        }
    }
}
```
### 5.3 `wal_index_get`
```c
int wal_index_get(const WalIndex *idx, page_id_t page_id, int max_frame) {
    uint32_t h = wal_index_hash(page_id, idx->capacity);
    for (int i = 0; i < idx->capacity; i++) {
        uint32_t slot = (h + i) % idx->capacity;
        if (idx->slots[slot].page_id == INVALID_PAGE_ID)
            return -1;  /* not found */
        if (idx->slots[slot].page_id == page_id) {
            if (idx->slots[slot].frame_num <= max_frame)
                return idx->slots[slot].frame_num;
            return -1;  /* latest frame exceeds snapshot */
        }
    }
    return -1;
}
```
### 5.4 WalFrameLog Implementation
```c
void wal_frame_log_append(WalFrameLog *log, int frame_num, page_id_t page_id) {
    if (log->count >= log->capacity) {
        log->capacity = log->capacity ? log->capacity * 2 : 256;
        log->frame_nums = realloc(log->frame_nums, log->capacity * sizeof(int));
        log->page_ids = realloc(log->page_ids, log->capacity * sizeof(page_id_t));
    }
    log->frame_nums[log->count] = frame_num;
    log->page_ids[log->count] = page_id;
    log->count++;
}
int wal_frame_log_find(const WalFrameLog *log, page_id_t page_id, int max_frame) {
    int best = -1;
    /* Reverse scan: most recent frames first (optimization: early exit possible) */
    for (int i = log->count - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        if (log->frame_nums[i] > max_frame) continue;
        if (log->page_ids[i] == page_id) {
            return log->frame_nums[i];  /* first match from the end = most recent */
        }
    }
    return best;
}
```
---
## 6. Error Handling Matrix
| Error | Detected By | Recovery | User-Visible? |
|-------|-------------|----------|---------------|
| WAL magic mismatch on open | `wal_recover` header validation | Delete corrupt WAL, create fresh | No — transparent |
| WAL page_size mismatch | `wal_recover` header validation | Return -1 (incompatible WAL) | Yes — fatal |
| Header checksum mismatch | `wal_recover` checksum validation | Return -1 (corrupt WAL) | Yes |
| Frame salt mismatch during recovery | Frame scan loop | Stop scanning; treat remaining frames as invalid | No — transparent |
| Frame checksum mismatch during recovery | Cumulative checksum verification | Stop scanning; truncate WAL at mismatch point | No — transparent |
| `pwrite` failure in `wal_write_frame` | Return value check | Return -1; transaction should be aborted | Yes |
| `fsync` failure on commit | Return value check | Return -1; commit fails | Yes |
| All reader slots full | `snapshot_begin_read` | Return -1 | Yes — "too many concurrent readers" |
| `pread` failure in `wal_read_page` | Return value check | Return -1 | Yes |
| Checkpoint during active reader | `snapshot_min_reader_frame` | Partial checkpoint only; WAL not truncated | No — transparent |
| WAL grows unbounded (no checkpoint) | Frame count exceeds threshold | Auto-checkpoint fires | No — transparent |
| Checkpoint `pwrite` to database fails | Return value check | Return -1; WAL remains valid for retry | Yes |
| `ftruncate` failure during WAL reset | Return value check | Log warning; WAL may be larger than needed but still valid | No |
| `realloc` failure in WalIndex/FrameLog | Return NULL | `fprintf + exit(EXIT_FAILURE)` | Fatal |
---
## 7. Buffer Pool Integration for WAL Read Path
The buffer pool's `fetch_page` is extended to check the WAL before the database file:
```c
/* Modified fetch_page in pager.c when WAL mode is active */
uint8_t *pool_fetch_page_wal(BufferPool *pool, page_id_t page_id,
                              Wal *wal, int reader_max_frame) {
    /* Step 1: Check buffer pool cache */
    frame_id_t fid = page_table_lookup(pool, page_id);
    if (fid != INVALID_FRAME_ID) {
        pool->stat_hits++;
        Frame *f = &pool->frames[fid];
        f->pin_count++;
        lru_move_to_head(pool, fid);
        return f->data;
    }
    /* Step 2: Cache miss — get a free frame */
    pool->stat_misses++;
    fid = get_free_frame(pool);
    if (fid == INVALID_FRAME_ID) return NULL;
    Frame *f = &pool->frames[fid];
    f->page_id = page_id;
    f->is_dirty = false;
    f->pin_count = 1;
    /* Step 3: Read from WAL or database file */
    if (wal && wal->enabled) {
        wal_read_page(wal, page_id, reader_max_frame, f->data, pool);
    } else {
        disk_read_page(pool, page_id, f->data);
    }
    pool->stat_disk_reads++;
    /* Step 4: Register in page table and LRU */
    page_table_insert(pool, page_id, fid);
    lru_push_head(pool, fid);
    return f->data;
}
```
---
## 8. Implementation Sequence with Checkpoints
### Phase 1 — WAL File Format: Header and Frame Structs (2 hours)
**Deliverables:**
1. `src/wal.h` with all constants, `WalHeader`, `WalFrameHeader`, `Wal`, `SnapshotManager` structs, all function declarations.
2. `src/wal.c` with `wal_checksum`, `wal_write_header`, `wal_read_header`, frame header serialization/deserialization.
**Implementation order:**
1. Define all constants: `WAL_HEADER_SIZE`, `WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE`, `WAL_FRAME_SIZE`, `WAL_MAGIC_BE`, `WAL_FORMAT_VERSION`.
2. Define `WalHeader` and `WalFrameHeader` structs.
3. Implement `wal_checksum` — process data in 8-byte chunks with running `s1`/`s2`.
4. Implement `wal_write_header` — serialize to 32-byte big-endian buffer, `pwrite` at offset 0.
5. Implement `wal_read_header` — `pread` 32 bytes at offset 0, deserialize.
6. Implement frame header serialization helper:
   ```c
   static void wal_frame_header_write(uint8_t *buf, const WalFrameHeader *fh) {
       write_be32(buf + 0,  fh->page_number);
       write_be32(buf + 4,  fh->db_size);
       write_be32(buf + 8,  fh->salt1);
       write_be32(buf + 12, fh->salt2);
       write_be32(buf + 16, fh->checksum1);
       write_be32(buf + 20, fh->checksum2);
   }
   static void wal_frame_header_read(const uint8_t *buf, WalFrameHeader *fh) {
       fh->page_number = read_be32(buf + 0);
       fh->db_size     = read_be32(buf + 4);
       fh->salt1       = read_be32(buf + 8);
       fh->salt2       = read_be32(buf + 12);
       fh->checksum1   = read_be32(buf + 16);
       fh->checksum2   = read_be32(buf + 20);
   }
   ```
**Checkpoint (validates M10-TS-05):**
```c
/* Create fresh WAL */
int fd = open("/tmp/test_wal_hdr.db-wal", O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
assert(fd >= 0);
WalHeader hdr = {
    .magic = WAL_MAGIC_BE,
    .format_version = WAL_FORMAT_VERSION,
    .page_size = PAGE_SIZE,
    .checkpoint_seq = 0,
    .salt1 = 0x12345678,
    .salt2 = 0xABCDEF01,
    .checksum1 = 0,
    .checksum2 = 0
};
/* Compute header checksum over bytes 0x00-0x17 (24 bytes) */
uint8_t hdr_buf[WAL_HEADER_SIZE];
memset(hdr_buf, 0, WAL_HEADER_SIZE);
write_be32(hdr_buf + 0,  hdr.magic);
write_be32(hdr_buf + 4,  hdr.format_version);
write_be32(hdr_buf + 8,  hdr.page_size);
write_be32(hdr_buf + 12, hdr.checkpoint_seq);
write_be32(hdr_buf + 16, hdr.salt1);
write_be32(hdr_buf + 20, hdr.salt2);
uint32_t ck1 = 0, ck2 = 0;
wal_checksum(hdr_buf, 24, &ck1, &ck2);
hdr.checksum1 = ck1;
hdr.checksum2 = ck2;
assert(wal_write_header(fd, &hdr) == 0);
/* Read back */
WalHeader hdr_in;
assert(wal_read_header(fd, &hdr_in) == 0);
assert(hdr_in.magic == WAL_MAGIC_BE);
assert(hdr_in.format_version == WAL_FORMAT_VERSION);
assert(hdr_in.page_size == PAGE_SIZE);
assert(hdr_in.checkpoint_seq == 0);
assert(hdr_in.salt1 == 0x12345678);
assert(hdr_in.salt2 == 0xABCDEF01);
assert(hdr_in.checksum1 == ck1);
assert(hdr_in.checksum2 == ck2);
/* Verify raw bytes: first 4 bytes = magic big-endian */
uint8_t raw[4];
pread(fd, raw, 4, 0);
assert(raw[0] == 0x37 && raw[1] == 0x7F && raw[2] == 0x06 && raw[3] == 0x82);
/* Bytes 4-7: format version = 3007000 = 0x002DE5E8 */
pread(fd, raw, 4, 4);
assert(raw[0] == 0x00 && raw[1] == 0x2D && raw[2] == 0xE5 && raw[3] == 0xE8);
/* Bytes 8-11: page_size = 4096 = 0x00001000 */
pread(fd, raw, 4, 8);
assert(raw[0] == 0x00 && raw[1] == 0x00 && raw[2] == 0x10 && raw[3] == 0x00);
close(fd);
unlink("/tmp/test_wal_hdr.db-wal");
printf("Phase 1 checkpoint: PASS\n");
```
### Phase 2 — WAL Index (In-Memory) (3 hours)
**Deliverables:**
1. `src/wal_index.h` with `WalIndex`, `WalIndexEntry`, `WalFrameLog` structs and all function declarations.
2. `src/wal_index.c` with full implementation: create, destroy, set, get, clear, resize; frame_log append and find.
**Implementation order:**
1. Implement `wal_index_create` — `calloc` slots array, fill with `INVALID_PAGE_ID`.
2. Implement `wal_index_destroy` — `free(idx->slots); free(idx)`.
3. Implement `wal_index_hash` — Knuth multiplicative hash.
4. Implement `wal_index_set` — open-addressing insert/update with load factor check.
5. Implement `wal_index_resize` — allocate new array, rehash all entries.
6. Implement `wal_index_get` — probe chain with `max_frame` bound.
7. Implement `wal_index_clear` — reset all slots to `INVALID_PAGE_ID`, count=0.
8. Implement `wal_frame_log_append` — append to dynamic array.
9. Implement `wal_frame_log_find` — reverse linear scan.
**Checkpoint:**
```c
WalIndex *idx = wal_index_create(64);
assert(idx != NULL);
assert(idx->count == 0);
/* Set page 5 → frame 0 */
wal_index_set(idx, 5, 0);
assert(idx->count == 1);
assert(wal_index_get(idx, 5, 100) == 0);
/* Set page 5 → frame 3 (update to newer) */
wal_index_set(idx, 5, 3);
assert(idx->count == 1);  /* same page, no new entry */
assert(wal_index_get(idx, 5, 100) == 3);
/* Snapshot: max_frame=2 → frame 3 exceeds → not found */
assert(wal_index_get(idx, 5, 2) == -1);
/* Snapshot: max_frame=3 → found */
assert(wal_index_get(idx, 5, 3) == 3);
/* Page not in index → -1 */
assert(wal_index_get(idx, 99, 100) == -1);
/* Frame log */
WalFrameLog log = {0};
wal_frame_log_append(&log, 0, 3);
wal_frame_log_append(&log, 1, 5);
wal_frame_log_append(&log, 2, 3);
/* Find page 3 with max_frame=2 → frame 2 */
assert(wal_frame_log_find(&log, 3, 2) == 2);
/* Find page 3 with max_frame=0 → frame 0 */
assert(wal_frame_log_find(&log, 3, 0) == 0);
/* Find page 5 with max_frame=2 → frame 1 */
assert(wal_frame_log_find(&log, 5, 2) == 1);
/* Find page 5 with max_frame=0 → -1 (frame 1 > max_frame 0) */
assert(wal_frame_log_find(&log, 5, 0) == -1);
/* Find page 99 → -1 */
assert(wal_frame_log_find(&log, 99, 100) == -1);
wal_index_clear(idx);
assert(idx->count == 0);
assert(wal_index_get(idx, 5, 100) == -1);
wal_index_destroy(idx);
free(log.frame_nums);
free(log.page_ids);
printf("Phase 2 checkpoint: PASS\n");
```
### Phase 3 — WAL Writer: Append Frame on Page Modification (2 hours)
**Deliverables:** `wal_write_frame`, `wal_open` (creation path), `wal_commit` in `wal.c`.
**Implementation order:**
1. Implement `wal_open` — create path: generate salts, compute header checksum, write header, init index.
2. Implement `wal_write_frame` per §4.3 — build frame header, compute cumulative checksum, pwrite, update index and frame_log.
3. Implement `wal_commit` per §4.5 — iterate dirty pages, write frames, last is commit frame.
4. Implement `SnapshotManager` functions: `snapshot_begin_read`, `snapshot_end_read`, `snapshot_update_writer`, `snapshot_min_reader_frame`.
**Checkpoint (validates M10-TS-01 and M10-TS-03):**
```c
const char *db_path = "/tmp/test_wal_write.db";
const char *wal_path = "/tmp/test_wal_write.db-wal";
/* Create a database file with page 5 = 0x55 */
int db_fd = open(db_path, O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
    uint8_t buf[PAGE_SIZE];
    memset(buf, (uint8_t)(i + 1), PAGE_SIZE);
    pwrite(db_fd, buf, PAGE_SIZE, (off_t)i * PAGE_SIZE);
}
fsync(db_fd);
close(db_fd);
/* Open WAL */
Wal wal;
memset(&wal, 0, sizeof(wal));
assert(wal_open(&wal, db_path, PAGE_SIZE) == 0);
assert(wal.enabled == true);
assert(wal.frame_count == 0);
/* Write frame for page 5 (non-commit) */
uint8_t new_data[PAGE_SIZE];
memset(new_data, 0xEE, PAGE_SIZE);
assert(wal_write_frame(&wal, 5, new_data, false, 0) == 0);
assert(wal.frame_count == 1);
/* WAL file should have grown: header(32) + frame(4120) = 4152 bytes */
struct stat st;
stat(wal_path, &st);
assert(st.st_size == WAL_HEADER_SIZE + WAL_FRAME_SIZE);
/* Verify frame header bytes: page_number = 5, db_size = 0 */
uint8_t fhdr[WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE];
pread(wal.fd, fhdr, WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE, WAL_HEADER_SIZE);
assert(read_be32(fhdr + 0) == 5);       /* page_number */
assert(read_be32(fhdr + 4) == 0);       /* db_size = 0 (not commit) */
assert(read_be32(fhdr + 8) == wal.header.salt1);
assert(read_be32(fhdr + 12) == wal.header.salt2);
/* Verify page data in WAL */
uint8_t wal_data[PAGE_SIZE];
pread(wal.fd, wal_data, PAGE_SIZE, WAL_HEADER_SIZE + WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE);
assert(wal_data[0] == 0xEE);
assert(wal_data[PAGE_SIZE - 1] == 0xEE);
/* Database file page 5 should be UNCHANGED */
db_fd = open(db_path, O_RDONLY);
uint8_t db_data[PAGE_SIZE];
pread(db_fd, db_data, PAGE_SIZE, 5 * PAGE_SIZE);
assert(db_data[0] == 6);   /* original: memset(buf, i+1, ...) for i=5 → 6 */
close(db_fd);
/* Write commit frame for page 7 */
uint8_t commit_data[PAGE_SIZE];
memset(commit_data, 0xFF, PAGE_SIZE);
assert(wal_write_frame(&wal, 7, commit_data, true, 8) == 0);
assert(wal.frame_count == 2);
assert(wal.last_commit_frame == 1);
/* Commit frame: db_size should be 8 */
pread(wal.fd, fhdr, WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE,
      WAL_HEADER_SIZE + WAL_FRAME_SIZE);
assert(read_be32(fhdr + 4) == 8);  /* db_size = 8 */
/* Checksum chain: frame 1's checksum incorporates frame 0's */
uint32_t f0_ck1 = read_be32(fhdr - WAL_FRAME_SIZE + 16 + WAL_HEADER_SIZE);
/* (Complex to verify externally; trust the algorithm and test recovery) */
wal_close(&wal);
unlink(db_path);
unlink(wal_path);
printf("Phase 3 checkpoint: PASS\n");
```
### Phase 4 — WAL Reader: Page Lookup with Snapshot Isolation (2 hours)
**Deliverables:** `wal_read_page`, `pool_fetch_page_wal` in pager.c, snapshot integration.
**Implementation order:**
1. Implement `wal_read_page` per §4.4.
2. Implement `pool_fetch_page_wal` per §7.
3. Integrate with `SnapshotManager` — each read operation uses the reader's `max_frame`.
**Checkpoint (validates M10-TS-02):**
```c
const char *db_path = "/tmp/test_wal_read.db";
const char *wal_path = "/tmp/test_wal_read.db-wal";
/* Create database: page 3 = 0x33, page 5 = 0x55 */
int db_fd = open(db_path, O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
    uint8_t buf[PAGE_SIZE];
    memset(buf, (uint8_t)((i + 1) * 0x11), PAGE_SIZE);
    pwrite(db_fd, buf, PAGE_SIZE, (off_t)i * PAGE_SIZE);
}
fsync(db_fd);
close(db_fd);
BufferPool *pool = pool_create(db_path, 20);
Wal wal;
memset(&wal, 0, sizeof(wal));
wal_open(&wal, db_path, PAGE_SIZE);
/* Transaction 1: write frame for page 3 (data=0xAA), commit */
uint8_t d1[PAGE_SIZE]; memset(d1, 0xAA, PAGE_SIZE);
wal_write_frame(&wal, 3, d1, true, 8);
/* frame_count=1, last_commit_frame=0, writer_max_frame=0 */
/* Snapshot A: sees txn 1 */
int reader_a_id;
int snap_a = snapshot_begin_read(&wal.snapshots, &reader_a_id);
assert(snap_a == 0);  /* max_frame = 0 */
/* Transaction 2: write frame for page 5 (data=0xBB), then page 3 (data=0xCC), commit */
uint8_t d2[PAGE_SIZE]; memset(d2, 0xBB, PAGE_SIZE);
wal_write_frame(&wal, 5, d2, false, 0);
uint8_t d3[PAGE_SIZE]; memset(d3, 0xCC, PAGE_SIZE);
wal_write_frame(&wal, 3, d3, true, 8);
/* frame_count=3, last_commit_frame=2, writer_max_frame=2 */
/* Snapshot B: sees txn 1 and txn 2 */
int reader_b_id;
int snap_b = snapshot_begin_read(&wal.snapshots, &reader_b_id);
assert(snap_b == 2);  /* max_frame = 2 */
/* Reader A reads page 3: should get frame 0 data (0xAA) */
uint8_t read_buf[PAGE_SIZE];
assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 3, snap_a, read_buf, pool) == 0);
assert(read_buf[0] == 0xAA);
/* Reader B reads page 3: should get frame 2 data (0xCC) */
assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 3, snap_b, read_buf, pool) == 0);
assert(read_buf[0] == 0xCC);
/* Reader A reads page 5: NOT in WAL at snap_a (frame 1 > snap_a=0)
   → falls back to database file: page 5 = 0x66 (6*0x11) */
assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 5, snap_a, read_buf, pool) == 0);
assert(read_buf[0] == 0x66);
/* Reader B reads page 5: frame 1 <= snap_b=2 → 0xBB */
assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 5, snap_b, read_buf, pool) == 0);
assert(read_buf[0] == 0xBB);
/* Page not in WAL at all (page 7): both readers get database file */
assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 7, snap_a, read_buf, pool) == 0);
assert(read_buf[0] == 0x88);  /* 8 * 0x11 */
snapshot_end_read(&wal.snapshots, reader_a_id);
snapshot_end_read(&wal.snapshots, reader_b_id);
wal_close(&wal);
pool_destroy(pool);
unlink(db_path);
unlink(wal_path);
printf("Phase 4 checkpoint: PASS\n");
```
### Phase 5 — Checkpointing (2 hours)
**Deliverables:** `wal_checkpoint` in `wal.c`, `wal_recover` in `wal.c`, auto-checkpoint trigger in `wal_write_frame`.
**Implementation order:**
1. Implement `wal_checkpoint` per §4.6.
2. Implement `wal_recover` per §4.7.
3. Add auto-checkpoint logic in `wal_write_frame`: after commit frame, check `frame_count >= autocheckpoint_threshold`.
4. Implement `wal_close` — checkpoint then clean up.
5. Implement `wal_mode_enable` and `wal_mode_disable`.
**Checkpoint (validates M10-TS-04):**
```c
const char *db_path = "/tmp/test_wal_ckpt.db";
const char *wal_path = "/tmp/test_wal_ckpt.db-wal";
int db_fd = open(db_path, O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
    uint8_t buf[PAGE_SIZE];
    memset(buf, 0x00, PAGE_SIZE);
    pwrite(db_fd, buf, PAGE_SIZE, (off_t)i * PAGE_SIZE);
}
fsync(db_fd);
close(db_fd);
BufferPool *pool = pool_create(db_path, 20);
Wal wal;
memset(&wal, 0, sizeof(wal));
wal_open(&wal, db_path, PAGE_SIZE);
/* 3 transactions modifying pages 1,2,3 */
uint8_t d[PAGE_SIZE];
memset(d, 0x11, PAGE_SIZE);
wal_write_frame(&wal, 1, d, true, 8);
memset(d, 0x22, PAGE_SIZE);
wal_write_frame(&wal, 2, d, true, 8);
memset(d, 0x33, PAGE_SIZE);
wal_write_frame(&wal, 3, d, true, 8);
assert(wal.frame_count == 3);
/* Checkpoint */
int pages_written = wal_checkpoint(&wal, pool);
assert(pages_written == 3);
/* WAL should be reset */
assert(wal.frame_count == 0);
/* Database file should have new data */
uint8_t verify[PAGE_SIZE];
pread(pool->fd, verify, PAGE_SIZE, 1 * PAGE_SIZE);
assert(verify[0] == 0x11);
pread(pool->fd, verify, PAGE_SIZE, 2 * PAGE_SIZE);
assert(verify[0] == 0x22);
pread(pool->fd, verify, PAGE_SIZE, 3 * PAGE_SIZE);
assert(verify[0] == 0x33);
/* Partial checkpoint test: active reader prevents full reset */
memset(d, 0xAA, PAGE_SIZE);
wal_write_frame(&wal, 4, d, true, 8);
memset(d, 0xBB, PAGE_SIZE);
wal_write_frame(&wal, 5, d, true, 8);
assert(wal.frame_count == 2);
/* Start a reader at frame 0 */
int rid;
int snap = snapshot_begin_read(&wal.snapshots, &rid);
assert(snap == 0);  /* reader sees frame 0 */
/* Write more: frame 2 */
memset(d, 0xCC, PAGE_SIZE);
wal_write_frame(&wal, 6, d, true, 8);
assert(wal.frame_count == 3);
/* Checkpoint: can only go up to safe_frame = snap = 0 */
pages_written = wal_checkpoint(&wal, pool);
assert(pages_written == 1);  /* only frame 0 checkpointed */
assert(wal.frame_count == 3);  /* WAL NOT reset */
/* Release reader → full checkpoint possible */
snapshot_end_read(&wal.snapshots, rid);
pages_written = wal_checkpoint(&wal, pool);
assert(pages_written >= 2);  /* remaining frames checkpointed */
assert(wal.frame_count == 0);  /* WAL reset */
/* Database has all data */
pread(pool->fd, verify, PAGE_SIZE, 4 * PAGE_SIZE);
assert(verify[0] == 0xAA);
pread(pool->fd, verify, PAGE_SIZE, 5 * PAGE_SIZE);
assert(verify[0] == 0xBB);
pread(pool->fd, verify, PAGE_SIZE, 6 * PAGE_SIZE);
assert(verify[0] == 0xCC);
wal_close(&wal);
pool_destroy(pool);
unlink(db_path);
unlink(wal_path);
printf("Phase 5 checkpoint: PASS\n");
```
---
## 9. Test Specifications
### M10-TS-01 — WAL Frame Has Correct Binary Header with Checksum
```c
void test_wal_frame_binary_header(void) {
    const char *db_path = "/tmp/test_m10_ts01.db";
    const char *wal_path = "/tmp/test_m10_ts01.db-wal";
    /* Create minimal database file */
    int db_fd = open(db_path, O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
    uint8_t zbuf[PAGE_SIZE]; memset(zbuf, 0, PAGE_SIZE);
    for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) pwrite(db_fd, zbuf, PAGE_SIZE, (off_t)i * PAGE_SIZE);
    fsync(db_fd); close(db_fd);
    Wal wal; memset(&wal, 0, sizeof(wal));
    wal_open(&wal, db_path, PAGE_SIZE);
    /* Force known salts for deterministic testing */
    wal.header.salt1 = 0x12345678;
    wal.header.salt2 = 0xABCDEF01;
    /* Recompute header checksum with new salts */
    uint8_t hdr_buf[24];
    write_be32(hdr_buf + 0,  wal.header.magic);
    write_be32(hdr_buf + 4,  wal.header.format_version);
    write_be32(hdr_buf + 8,  wal.header.page_size);
    write_be32(hdr_buf + 12, wal.header.checkpoint_seq);
    write_be32(hdr_buf + 16, 0x12345678);
    write_be32(hdr_buf + 20, 0xABCDEF01);
    wal.header.checksum1 = 0; wal.header.checksum2 = 0;
    wal.write_cksum1 = 0; wal.write_cksum2 = 0;
    wal_checksum(hdr_buf, 24, &wal.write_cksum1, &wal.write_cksum2);
    wal.header.checksum1 = wal.write_cksum1;
    wal.header.checksum2 = wal.write_cksum2;
    wal_write_header(wal.fd, &wal.header);
    /* Write non-commit frame: page 5, known data */
    uint8_t page_data[PAGE_SIZE];
    for (int i = 0; i < PAGE_SIZE; i++) page_data[i] = (uint8_t)(i & 0xFF);
    assert(wal_write_frame(&wal, 5, page_data, false, 0) == 0);
    /* Read frame header raw bytes */
    uint8_t fhdr[WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE];
    pread(wal.fd, fhdr, WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE, WAL_HEADER_SIZE);
    /* [0..3] page_number = 5 */
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 0) == 5);
    /* [4..7] db_size = 0 (not commit frame) */
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 4) == 0);
    /* [8..11] salt1 = 0x12345678 */
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 8) == 0x12345678);
    /* [12..15] salt2 = 0xABCDEF01 */
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 12) == 0xABCDEF01);
    /* [16..19] checksum1 and [20..23] checksum2 — verify against algorithm */
    uint32_t verify_ck1 = wal.header.checksum1;
    uint32_t verify_ck2 = wal.header.checksum2;
    uint8_t ck_input[8];
    write_be32(ck_input, 5);  /* page_number */
    write_be32(ck_input + 4, 0);  /* db_size */
    wal_checksum(ck_input, 8, &verify_ck1, &verify_ck2);
    wal_checksum(page_data, PAGE_SIZE, &verify_ck1, &verify_ck2);
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 16) == verify_ck1);
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 20) == verify_ck2);
    /* Write commit frame: page 7, db_size = 8 */
    uint8_t commit_data[PAGE_SIZE];
    memset(commit_data, 0xFF, PAGE_SIZE);
    assert(wal_write_frame(&wal, 7, commit_data, true, 8) == 0);
    /* Read commit frame header */
    pread(wal.fd, fhdr, WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE,
          WAL_HEADER_SIZE + WAL_FRAME_SIZE);
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 0) == 7);   /* page_number */
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 4) == 8);   /* db_size = 8 (commit!) */
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 8) == 0x12345678);
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 12) == 0xABCDEF01);
    /* Checksum chain: frame 1's checksum builds on frame 0's */
    uint32_t f1_ck1 = verify_ck1, f1_ck2 = verify_ck2;
    write_be32(ck_input, 7);
    write_be32(ck_input + 4, 8);
    wal_checksum(ck_input, 8, &f1_ck1, &f1_ck2);
    wal_checksum(commit_data, PAGE_SIZE, &f1_ck1, &f1_ck2);
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 16) == f1_ck1);
    assert(read_be32(fhdr + 20) == f1_ck2);
    wal_close(&wal);
    unlink(db_path); unlink(wal_path);
    printf("PASS: test_wal_frame_binary_header\n");
}
```
### M10-TS-02 — WAL Reader Finds Most Recent Frame for a Page
(Covered by Phase 4 checkpoint above. Additional assertions:)
```c
void test_wal_reader_snapshot_isolation(void) {
    /* Setup: same as Phase 4 checkpoint */
    /* ... (db creation, WAL open, 3 frames: frame0=page3/0xAA, frame1=page5/0xBB, frame2=page3/0xCC) ... */
    /* Snapshot at txn1 (max_frame=0): page 3 → frame0 (0xAA) */
    uint8_t buf[PAGE_SIZE];
    assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 3, 0, buf, pool) == 0);
    assert(buf[0] == 0xAA);
    /* Snapshot at txn2 (max_frame=2): page 3 → frame2 (0xCC) */
    assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 3, 2, buf, pool) == 0);
    assert(buf[0] == 0xCC);
    /* Page not in WAL at snapshot: falls back to database */
    assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 5, 0, buf, pool) == 0);
    assert(buf[0] == 0x66);  /* database file original */
    /* Page in WAL but frame > snapshot: falls back to database */
    assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 5, 0, buf, pool) == 0);
    assert(buf[0] == 0x66);
    /* Page in WAL and frame <= snapshot */
    assert(wal_read_page(&wal, 5, 2, buf, pool) == 0);
    assert(buf[0] == 0xBB);
    /* ... cleanup ... */
    printf("PASS: test_wal_reader_snapshot_isolation\n");
}
```
### M10-TS-03 — Writer Appends New Frame, Does Not Modify Database File
(Covered by Phase 3 checkpoint. Additional assertions:)
```c
void test_wal_writer_no_db_modify(void) {
    /* ... setup ... */
    /* Save database file mtime before write */
    struct stat st_before;
    fstat(pool->fd, &st_before);
    /* Write a WAL frame */
    uint8_t d[PAGE_SIZE]; memset(d, 0xEE, PAGE_SIZE);
    wal_write_frame(&wal, 7, d, false, 0);
    /* WAL file grew */
    struct stat wal_st;
    stat(wal_path, &wal_st);
    assert(wal_st.st_size == WAL_HEADER_SIZE + WAL_FRAME_SIZE);
    /* Database file page 7 UNCHANGED */
    uint8_t db_page[PAGE_SIZE];
    pread(pool->fd, db_page, PAGE_SIZE, 7 * PAGE_SIZE);
    assert(db_page[0] != 0xEE);  /* original data, not 0xEE */
    /* Multiple modifications to same page in one transaction:
       only last frame matters for reads */
    memset(d, 0x11, PAGE_SIZE);
    wal_write_frame(&wal, 7, d, false, 0);
    memset(d, 0x22, PAGE_SIZE);
    wal_write_frame(&wal, 7, d, true, 8);
    assert(wal.frame_count == 3);
    /* Read page 7 from WAL: should get last frame (0x22) */
    uint8_t read_buf[PAGE_SIZE];
    wal_read_page(&wal, 7, wal.frame_count - 1, read_buf, pool);
    assert(read_buf[0] == 0x22);
    /* Database file still unchanged */
    pread(pool->fd, db_page, PAGE_SIZE, 7 * PAGE_SIZE);
    assert(db_page[0] != 0x22);
    /* ... cleanup ... */
    printf("PASS: test_wal_writer_no_db_modify\n");
}
```
### M10-TS-04 — Checkpoint Copies WAL Frames to Database File
(Covered by Phase 5 checkpoint. The full test including partial checkpoint with active readers is specified there.)
### M10-TS-05 — WAL Header Magic and Version on Fresh WAL
(Covered by Phase 1 checkpoint. The full test including magic byte verification, version verification, and corrupt magic rejection:)
```c
void test_wal_header_magic_version(void) {
    /* ... Phase 1 checkpoint code ... */
    /* Additional: reject wrong magic */
    int fd = open("/tmp/test_bad_magic.db-wal", O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
    uint8_t bad_hdr[WAL_HEADER_SIZE];
    memset(bad_hdr, 0, WAL_HEADER_SIZE);
    write_be32(bad_hdr, 0xDEADBEEF);  /* wrong magic */
    pwrite(fd, bad_hdr, WAL_HEADER_SIZE, 0);
    close(fd);
    Wal wal; memset(&wal, 0, sizeof(wal));
    /* wal_open should detect bad magic, delete WAL, create fresh */
    wal_open(&wal, "/tmp/test_bad_magic.db", PAGE_SIZE);
    /* Fresh WAL should have correct magic */
    assert(wal.header.magic == WAL_MAGIC_BE);
    wal_close(&wal);
    unlink("/tmp/test_bad_magic.db");
    unlink("/tmp/test_bad_magic.db-wal");
    /* Page size mismatch → error */
    /* ... create WAL with page_size=8192, try opening with PAGE_SIZE=4096 → error ... */
    printf("PASS: test_wal_header_magic_version\n");
}
```
---
## 10. Performance Targets
| Operation | Target | Measurement Method |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|
| `wal_checksum` (4096 bytes) | ≤ 1 µs | `clock_gettime` over 100K invocations |
| `wal_write_frame` (non-commit, no fsync) | ≤ 50 µs | 10K frame appends, total / 10K |
| `wal_write_frame` (commit, includes fsync) | ≤ 500 µs | 1K commits, average |
| `wal_read_page` (WAL hit) | ≤ 20 µs | 10K reads, average |
| `wal_read_page` (WAL miss, db file read) | ≤ 100 µs | 10K reads, average |
| `wal_index_set` (amortized) | ≤ 50 ns | 1M inserts |
| `wal_index_get` (load factor 0.5) | ≤ 30 ns | 1M lookups |
| `wal_frame_log_find` (1000 entries) | ≤ 5 µs | 10K lookups |
| `wal_checkpoint` (100 frames) | ≤ 10 ms | End-to-end including fsync |
| `wal_recover` (100 frames) | ≤ 15 ms | End-to-end scan + index rebuild |
| `snapshot_begin_read` | ≤ 100 ns | 1M calls |
| `snapshot_min_reader_frame` | ≤ 200 ns | 1M calls (scan 32 slots) |
| WAL-mode commit (10 dirty pages) | ≤ 2 ms | End-to-end |
| Auto-checkpoint (1000 frames, no readers) | ≤ 100 ms | Including fsync + WAL reset |
**Benchmark template:**
```c
void bench_wal_write_commit(void) {
    /* ... setup db + WAL ... */
    struct timespec t0, t1;
    clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &t0);
    for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {
        uint8_t d[PAGE_SIZE];
        memset(d, (uint8_t)i, PAGE_SIZE);
        wal_write_frame(&wal, (page_id_t)(i % 100), d,
                        true, 100);
    }
    clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &t1);
    long ns = (t1.tv_sec - t0.tv_sec) * 1000000000L
            + (t1.tv_nsec - t0.tv_nsec);
    printf("1000 WAL commits: %ld ns total, %ld ns/commit\n",
           ns, ns / 1000);
    assert(ns / 1000 <= 500000);  /* ≤ 500 µs/commit */
}
```
---
## 11. Common Mistakes and Diagnostic Guide
| Symptom | Root Cause | Fix |
|---------|------------|-----|
| Checksum mismatch on first frame during recovery | Header checksum computed over wrong byte range | Header checksum covers bytes 0x00–0x17 (24 bytes), NOT the full 32-byte header. The checksum fields themselves (bytes 0x18–0x1F) are excluded |
| Frame checksum mismatch | Checksum input includes salt bytes | Salt fields in the frame header are NOT included in the checksum. Only `[page_number(4) \|\| db_size(4)]` + page_data are checksummed |
| Reader sees newer data than its snapshot | `wal_index_get` doesn't enforce `max_frame` bound | Check `stored_frame_num <= max_frame` before returning. If exceeds, return -1 |
| Reader sees stale data despite committed WAL frame | `wal_frame_log_find` scans forward instead of backward | Reverse scan ensures the MOST RECENT frame ≤ max_frame is found first |
| Checkpoint writes wrong data to database | Frame data read from wrong offset | Offset = `WAL_HEADER_SIZE + frame_num * WAL_FRAME_SIZE + WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE`. The `+ WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE` skips the 24-byte frame header to reach page data |
| WAL grows unbounded | Auto-checkpoint not triggered | Check `frame_count >= autocheckpoint_threshold` AFTER each commit frame in `wal_write_frame` |
| Partial checkpoint doesn't preserve frames for active reader | `snapshot_min_reader_frame` returns wrong value | Scan ALL reader slots, not just the first active one. Return the MINIMUM `max_frame` among active readers |
| Buffer pool returns stale page after checkpoint | Checkpoint writes to database file but buffer pool cache still holds pre-checkpoint WAL version | Checkpoint must invalidate cached pages: remove from page table and LRU any page whose `page_id` was checkpointed |
| `wal_write_frame` corrupts cumulative checksum state | Checksum state variables modified by a failed write but not rolled back | On `pwrite` failure, do NOT update `write_cksum1`/`write_cksum2` or `frame_count`. Compute checksums into temporary variables first, copy to state only on success |
| Database file has wrong size after checkpoint | `ftruncate` not called, or called with wrong size | After full checkpoint, the database file size should be `db_size * PAGE_SIZE` from the last commit frame's `db_size` field |
| WAL recovery loses committed transactions | Recovery truncates beyond `last_commit_frame` but `last_commit_frame` was not correctly identified | `last_commit_frame` is set only when `frame_hdr.db_size > 0`. Verify the scan loop correctly updates this on each commit frame |
| `wal_close` doesn't checkpoint | Missing `wal_checkpoint` call in `wal_close` | Always call `wal_checkpoint(&wal, pool)` before closing. If checkpoint fails, log warning but still close |
| Snapshot slot leak | `snapshot_end_read` not called after reader finishes | Document that every `snapshot_begin_read` MUST be paired with `snapshot_end_read`. In debug builds, assert all slots are inactive on `wal_close` |
---
## 12. Build Integration
```makefile
# Additions to existing Makefile
WAL_SRC = src/wal.c src/wal_index.c
WAL_OBJ = $(WAL_SRC:.c=.o)
src/wal.o: src/wal.c src/wal.h src/wal_index.h src/pager.h
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@
src/wal_index.o: src/wal_index.c src/wal_index.h src/pager.h
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@
tests/test_wal: tests/test_wal.c $(WAL_OBJ) $(TXN_OBJ) $(DML_OBJ) \
                $(BTREE_OBJ) src/pager.o
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) $^ -o $@ -lm
.PHONY: test-wal
test-wal: tests/test_wal
	@echo "==> Running WAL tests..."
	./tests/test_wal
	@echo "==> All WAL tests passed."
.PHONY: bench-wal
bench-wal: CFLAGS = -std=c11 -O2 -DNDEBUG -Isrc
bench-wal: tests/test_wal
	./tests/test_wal --bench
.PHONY: clean-wal-tests
clean-wal-tests:
	rm -f /tmp/test_m10_*.db /tmp/test_m10_*.db-wal
	rm -f /tmp/test_wal_*.db /tmp/test_wal_*.db-wal
	rm -f /tmp/test_bad_magic.db /tmp/test_bad_magic.db-wal
```
**Note:** Like M9, WAL tests use **real files** because WAL correctness depends on `fsync`, `ftruncate`, and file persistence semantics that cannot be faithfully simulated by mock disk.
---
## 13. State Machine — WAL Lifecycle

![WAL Mode vs Rollback Journal: Write Path Comparison](./diagrams/tdd-diag-36.svg)

*Figure 36: WAL lifecycle state machine. States: DISABLED (journal mode), ENABLED_EMPTY (WAL exists, no frames), ENABLED_ACTIVE (frames exist, writers appending), CHECKPOINTING (checkpoint in progress), RECOVERING (WAL recovery on open). Transitions: wal_mode_enable → ENABLED_EMPTY. wal_write_frame → ENABLED_ACTIVE. wal_checkpoint (all frames) → ENABLED_EMPTY. wal_checkpoint (partial) → ENABLED_ACTIVE. wal_mode_disable → checkpoint → DISABLED. wal_open with existing WAL → RECOVERING → ENABLED_ACTIVE or ENABLED_EMPTY. Crash during ENABLED_ACTIVE → RECOVERING on next open.*
```
States:
  DISABLED          WAL mode off. Journal mode (M9) active.
  ENABLED_EMPTY     WAL file exists with header only. No frames.
  ENABLED_ACTIVE    WAL has one or more frames. Write transactions possible.
  CHECKPOINTING     Checkpoint in progress. Database file being updated.
  RECOVERING        WAL recovery scan in progress during database open.
Transitions:
  DISABLED         → ENABLED_EMPTY       wal_mode_enable() 
  ENABLED_EMPTY    → ENABLED_ACTIVE      wal_write_frame() (first frame)
  ENABLED_ACTIVE   → ENABLED_ACTIVE      wal_write_frame() (subsequent frames)
  ENABLED_ACTIVE   → CHECKPOINTING       wal_checkpoint() called
  CHECKPOINTING    → ENABLED_EMPTY       all frames checkpointed → WAL reset
  CHECKPOINTING    → ENABLED_ACTIVE      partial checkpoint (active readers)
  ENABLED_*        → DISABLED            wal_mode_disable() (full checkpoint first)
  (any crash)      → RECOVERING          wal_open() detects existing WAL on restart
  RECOVERING       → ENABLED_ACTIVE      valid frames found, index rebuilt
  RECOVERING       → ENABLED_EMPTY       no valid frames (empty or all invalid)
Illegal:
  wal_write_frame() in DISABLED           → assert / error
  wal_checkpoint() in DISABLED            → no-op
  wal_mode_enable() in ENABLED_*          → no-op (already enabled)
  wal_mode_disable() during CHECKPOINTING → undefined (wait for checkpoint to complete)
```
---
## 14. Integration with M9 TransactionManager
The `TransactionManager` from M9 is extended with a `wal_mode` flag:
```c
/* In txn.h: */
typedef struct {
    /* ... existing fields ... */
    bool  wal_mode;       /* true = WAL commit path; false = journal commit path */
    Wal  *wal;            /* pointer to WAL struct (NULL when wal_mode == false) */
} TransactionManager;
```
When `wal_mode == true`:
- `txn_commit` calls `wal_commit(txn->wal, pool)` instead of the journal flush+delete path.
- `txn_rollback` discards uncommitted WAL frames (truncate WAL to `last_commit_frame + 1` frames, reload buffer pool cache from WAL/database).
- `txn_journal_page` becomes a no-op (WAL mode doesn't journal original pages — it appends new pages instead).
- The buffer pool's dirty-page hook is redirected: instead of journaling, dirty pages are accumulated in the buffer pool and written to the WAL during `wal_commit`.
When `wal_mode == false`: Existing M9 journal behavior is unchanged.
---
<!-- END_TDD_MOD -->


<!-- TDD_MOD_ID: build-sqlite-m11 -->
# Module M11 — Aggregate Functions & JOIN
**Project:** Build Your Own SQLite | **Module ID:** `build-sqlite-m11`
**Language:** C (ISO C11) | **Depends On:** `build-sqlite-m10` (WAL), `build-sqlite-m9` (Transactions), `build-sqlite-m8` (Query Planner), `build-sqlite-m7` (Indexes), `build-sqlite-m6` (DML/TableCursor), `build-sqlite-m5` (B-tree), `build-sqlite-m4` (Buffer Pool), `build-sqlite-m3` (VDBE), `build-sqlite-m2` (Parser/AST), `build-sqlite-m1` (Lexer)
**Difficulty:** Expert | **Estimated Effort:** 12 hours
---
## 1. Module Charter
The Aggregate Functions & JOIN module completes the SQL query engine by adding two capabilities that together cover the overwhelming majority of real-world analytic queries: aggregate functions operating over groups of rows, and multi-table joins that combine related data. Aggregation introduces a fundamentally different execution pattern — instead of one output row per input row, the engine must accumulate state across many input rows and produce one output row per *group*. JOINs introduce a second cursor that must be coordinated with the first inside a nested iteration.
**What this module does:**
- Defines the `Accumulator` struct and implements `init / step / finalize` for five aggregate functions: `COUNT(*)`, `COUNT(col)`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`. All handle NULLs according to the SQL standard (§3.1).
- Implements `GroupHashTable` — an open-addressing hash map keyed by `Value[]` group-key tuples, each entry holding a parallel array of `Accumulator` structs. NULL group keys are treated as equal for grouping (unlike WHERE comparisons).
- Extends the parser (M2) to recognise function-call expressions in the SELECT list (`COUNT(*)`, `SUM(x)`, etc.), `GROUP BY` and `HAVING` clauses.
- Extends the bytecode compiler (M3) with new opcodes for aggregate lifecycle and group iteration.
- Extends the VM (M3) to execute aggregate opcodes: a two-phase execution model where Phase 1 scans rows accumulating into groups, and Phase 2 iterates groups, evaluates HAVING, and emits result rows.
- Implements nested-loop JOIN as a double-cursor bytecode pattern driven by the planner's join order (M8). The inner cursor is rewound for each outer row.
- Implements an in-memory sort buffer for ORDER BY on aggregate results, and LIMIT/OFFSET truncation on the sorted output.
- Connects every layer end-to-end: a complex query string like `SELECT c.name, SUM(o.amount) FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id=c.id GROUP BY c.name HAVING SUM(o.amount) > 150 ORDER BY SUM(o.amount) DESC` is tokenised, parsed, compiled, and executed against real B-tree storage through the buffer pool.
**What this module does NOT do:**
- It does not implement hash join or sort-merge join algorithms. All joins use nested-loop.
- It does not implement LEFT/RIGHT/FULL OUTER JOIN. Only INNER JOIN.
- It does not implement `GROUP BY` with `ROLLUP`, `CUBE`, or `GROUPING SETS`.
- It does not implement window functions (`OVER`, `PARTITION BY`).
- It does not implement `DISTINCT` on aggregate functions (`COUNT(DISTINCT col)`) — documented as a future extension.
- It does not implement external sort (spill to temp B-tree). ORDER BY is in-memory only.
**Upstream dependencies:** `Value`, `VM`, `Program`, `Compiler`, `Opcode`, `Instruction` from M3. `AstNode`, `NodeType`, `Parser` from M2. `Token`, `Lexer` from M1. `BTreeCursor`, `btree_cursor_open/first/next/seek/column/rowid/close` from M5. `TableCursor`, `table_cursor_create` from M6. `BufferPool`, `pool_fetch_page`, `pool_unpin_page` from M4. `PlanNode`, `choose_access_path`, `JoinPlanner` from M8. `column_value_compare` from M7.
**Downstream consumers:** None within the project — M11 is the final query-capability module.
**Invariants:**
1. `COUNT(*)` always returns an integer ≥ 0, never NULL, even on an empty table or empty group.
2. `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX` return NULL when applied to zero non-NULL values (empty table or all-NULL column). They never return 0 for this case.
3. `AVG` always returns `VAL_REAL`, even when the input column is `VAL_INTEGER`.
4. Within `GROUP BY`, `NULL == NULL` is treated as `true` — all NULL keys form one group.
5. `HAVING` operates on finalised aggregate values, not on individual rows.
6. The nested-loop JOIN inner cursor is fully rewound (`btree_cursor_first`) for each outer row.
7. ORDER BY on aggregate results is stable: rows with equal sort keys preserve their insertion order from the group hash table iteration.
---
## 2. File Structure
```
build-sqlite/
├── src/
│   ├── agg.h              ← Step 1: AggType, Accumulator, GroupHashTable structs, public API
│   ├── agg.c              ← Step 2: Accumulator step/finalize, GroupHashTable operations
│   ├── sort_buffer.h      ← Step 3: SortBuffer struct and API for ORDER BY + LIMIT
│   ├── sort_buffer.c      ← Step 4: SortBuffer implementation (in-memory sort)
│   ├── ast.h              ← (M2 — extend NodeType: NODE_FUNCTION_CALL, GROUP_BY, HAVING)
│   ├── ast.c              ← (M2 — extend ast_free, ast_to_sql for new node types)
│   ├── parser.c           ← (M2 — extend parse_select for GROUP BY, HAVING; parse_primary for function calls)
│   ├── vdbe.h             ← (M3 — add new opcodes: OP_AGG_INIT..OP_AGG_GROUP_VAL, OP_SORT_*)
│   ├── vdbe.c             ← (M3 — extend compiler for aggregate/join codegen; extend VM execute)
│   └── ... (M1–M10 files unchanged unless noted)
└── tests/
    └── test_agg.c         ← Step 5: Six test groups
```
**Dependency graph:**
```
agg.h          → vdbe.h (Value, ValueType)
agg.c          → agg.h, vdbe.h
sort_buffer.h  → vdbe.h (Value)
sort_buffer.c  → sort_buffer.h
test_agg.c     → agg.h, sort_buffer.h, vdbe.h, ast.h, parser.h, lexer.h,
                  btree.h, btree_cursor.h, pager.h, dml.h, table_cursor.h
```
---
## 3. Complete Data Model
### 3.1 AggType Enum
```c
/* src/agg.h */
#ifndef AGG_H
#define AGG_H
#include "vdbe.h"  /* Value, ValueType */
typedef enum {
    AGG_COUNT_STAR = 0,   /* COUNT(*)  — counts all rows including NULLs       */
    AGG_COUNT      = 1,   /* COUNT(col) — counts non-NULL values only          */
    AGG_SUM        = 2,   /* SUM(col)  — sum of non-NULL values; NULL if none  */
    AGG_AVG        = 3,   /* AVG(col)  — mean of non-NULL values; always REAL  */
    AGG_MIN        = 4,   /* MIN(col)  — minimum non-NULL value                */
    AGG_MAX        = 5    /* MAX(col)  — maximum non-NULL value                */
} AggType;
#define AGG_TYPE_COUNT 6
```
### 3.2 Accumulator Struct — Byte Layout
```c
typedef struct {
    AggType   type;          /* offset  0, size  4 — which aggregate function      */
    int64_t   count;         /* offset  8, size  8 — number of non-NULL values seen */
    double    sum;           /* offset 16, size  8 — running sum for SUM/AVG        */
    Value     min_val;       /* offset 24, size 24 — current minimum (VAL_NULL initially) */
    Value     max_val;       /* offset 48, size 24 — current maximum (VAL_NULL initially) */
    bool      has_value;     /* offset 72, size  1 — true iff at least one non-NULL stepped */
    /* 3 bytes padding */
} Accumulator;
/* sizeof(Accumulator) == 80 on LP64 (with padding for alignment).
   50 groups × 5 accumulators × 80 bytes = 20 KB — fits L1 cache.   */
```
**Field-by-field rationale:**
| Field | Why |
|-------|-----|
| `type` | Dispatch in `step` and `finalize`; set once at init, never changed |
| `count` | `COUNT(*)` uses this as the sole output. `AVG` uses it as the denominator. `COUNT(col)` uses it excluding NULLs |
| `sum` | `SUM` accumulates here. `AVG` divides `sum/count`. Stored as `double` to handle both integer and real inputs without overflow |
| `min_val` | Holds current minimum. `VAL_NULL` initially (sentinel). First non-NULL value replaces it unconditionally |
| `max_val` | Same pattern as `min_val` |
| `has_value` | Distinguishes "zero non-NULL inputs seen" from "sum is zero because inputs summed to zero". When `has_value == false`, `finalize` returns `VAL_NULL` for SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX |
### 3.3 GroupKey — Hash Table Key
```c
#define MAX_GROUP_KEY_COLS 16
typedef struct {
    Value     values[MAX_GROUP_KEY_COLS];  /* group key column values */
    int       n_cols;                      /* number of columns in key */
    uint32_t  hash;                        /* precomputed hash (for rehash) */
} GroupKey;
```
### 3.4 GroupEntry — Hash Table Entry
```c
typedef struct {
    GroupKey       key;
    Accumulator   *accumulators;  /* heap array: one per aggregate function */
    int            agg_count;     /* number of accumulators */
    bool           occupied;      /* true = live entry; false = empty slot */
} GroupEntry;
```
### 3.5 GroupHashTable
```c
#define GROUP_HT_INITIAL_CAPACITY 64
#define GROUP_HT_LOAD_FACTOR_MAX  0.70
typedef struct {
    GroupEntry *entries;       /* open-addressing array */
    int         capacity;     /* allocated slots */
    int         count;        /* occupied slots */
    int         agg_count;    /* accumulators per group */
    AggType    *agg_types;    /* array[agg_count] — type of each accumulator */
} GroupHashTable;
```

![Aggregate Accumulator Architecture](./diagrams/tdd-diag-37.svg)

*Figure 37: GroupHashTable architecture. The hash table is an open-addressing array of GroupEntry structs. Each entry contains a GroupKey (up to 16 Value columns + precomputed hash), a heap-allocated array of Accumulator structs (one per aggregate function in the query), and an `occupied` flag. On each input row, the group key is hashed, the matching entry found or created, and each accumulator's `step` is called with the corresponding column value. After the full scan, the table is iterated linearly: for each occupied entry, accumulators are finalised, HAVING is evaluated, and qualifying rows are emitted.*
### 3.6 SortBuffer — In-Memory Result Sort
```c
/* src/sort_buffer.h */
#ifndef SORT_BUFFER_H
#define SORT_BUFFER_H
#include "vdbe.h"
#define SORT_BUF_INITIAL_CAPACITY 256
typedef struct {
    int  col_idx;    /* which result column to sort by (0-based)           */
    bool descending; /* false = ASC (default), true = DESC                 */
} SortKey;
typedef struct {
    Value  *columns;   /* heap array: columns[0..n_cols-1]                  */
    int     n_cols;    /* number of columns per row                         */
} SortRow;
typedef struct {
    SortRow   *rows;          /* dynamic array of result rows               */
    int        count;         /* number of rows stored                      */
    int        capacity;      /* allocated slots                            */
    SortKey   *sort_keys;     /* ORDER BY specification                     */
    int        n_sort_keys;   /* number of sort keys                        */
    int        n_cols;        /* columns per row                            */
} SortBuffer;
SortBuffer *sort_buffer_create(int n_cols, SortKey *keys, int n_keys);
void        sort_buffer_destroy(SortBuffer *sb);
void        sort_buffer_add_row(SortBuffer *sb, const Value *cols, int n_cols);
void        sort_buffer_sort(SortBuffer *sb);
/* Emit rows [offset, offset+limit) via callback. limit=-1 → all remaining. */
void        sort_buffer_emit(SortBuffer *sb, int offset, int limit,
                             void (*cb)(Value *row, int ncols, void *ctx),
                             void *ctx);
#endif /* SORT_BUFFER_H */
```
### 3.7 New AST Nodes
Extend `NodeType` enum in `ast.h`:
```c
    NODE_FUNCTION_CALL = 40,  /* aggregate or scalar function: name + args */
    NODE_GROUP_BY      = 41,  /* list of expressions for GROUP BY          */
    NODE_HAVING        = 42,  /* expression node for HAVING clause         */
    NODE_ORDER_BY_ITEM = 43,  /* expression + ASC/DESC flag                */
```
Extend `AstNode` union:
```c
/* NODE_FUNCTION_CALL payload */
struct {
    const char     *name;       /* function name: "COUNT","SUM","AVG","MIN","MAX" */
    int             name_len;
    struct AstNode *args;       /* linked list of argument expressions            */
    int             arg_count;  /* 0 for COUNT(*), 1 for others                  */
    bool            is_star;    /* true for COUNT(*)                              */
} function_call;
/* NODE_ORDER_BY_ITEM payload */
struct {
    struct AstNode *expr;       /* the expression to sort by                     */
    bool            descending; /* false = ASC, true = DESC                      */
} order_by_item;
```
Extend `SelectStmt` in `ast.h`:
```c
typedef struct SelectStmt {
    struct AstNode *projections;     /* linked list: NODE_COLUMN_REF, NODE_STAR, NODE_FUNCTION_CALL */
    const char     *table_name;      /* FROM table                       */
    int             table_name_len;
    /* JOIN — NULL if single-table query */
    const char     *join_table;      /* JOIN table name                  */
    int             join_table_len;
    struct AstNode *join_condition;   /* ON expression                   */
    /* Existing */
    struct AstNode *where_clause;
    struct AstNode *order_by;        /* linked list of NODE_ORDER_BY_ITEM */
    int             limit;           /* -1 = no LIMIT                    */
    int             distinct;
    /* NEW for M11 */
    struct AstNode *group_by;        /* linked list of NODE_COLUMN_REF   */
    int             group_by_count;
    struct AstNode *having_clause;   /* expression (evaluated post-aggregation) */
} SelectStmt;
```
### 3.8 New Opcodes
Add to the `Opcode` enum in `vdbe.h`:
```c
    /* Aggregate lifecycle */
    OP_AGG_INIT       = 40,  /* Init agg state. p1=agg_count, p2=ptr to AggType array idx in program */
    OP_AGG_STEP       = 41,  /* Step one accumulator. p1=agg_index, p2=value_reg, p3=agg_type        */
    OP_AGG_FINAL      = 42,  /* Finalise accumulator. p1=agg_index, p2=dest_reg                      */
    /* Group-by lifecycle */
    OP_GROUP_INIT     = 43,  /* Init GroupHashTable. p1=n_key_cols, p2=n_aggs                         */
    OP_GROUP_KEY      = 44,  /* Set group key column. p1=key_col_index, p2=value_reg                  */
    OP_GROUP_STEP     = 45,  /* Look up/create group, step all accumulators. p1=first_val_reg         */
    OP_GROUP_ITER     = 46,  /* Start iteration / advance to next group. p2=addr_when_done            */
    OP_GROUP_COL      = 47,  /* Read group key column. p1=key_col_index, p2=dest_reg                  */
    OP_GROUP_AGG      = 48,  /* Finalise group accumulator. p1=agg_index, p2=dest_reg                 */
    /* Sort buffer */
    OP_SORT_INIT      = 49,  /* Init sort buffer. p1=n_cols, p2=n_sort_keys                           */
    OP_SORT_INSERT    = 50,  /* Insert row into sort buffer. p1=first_reg, p2=n_cols                   */
    OP_SORT_SORT      = 51,  /* Sort the buffer                                                       */
    OP_SORT_ITER      = 52,  /* Iterate sorted rows. p2=addr_when_done                                */
    OP_SORT_COL       = 53,  /* Read column from current sorted row. p1=col_idx, p2=dest_reg          */
    OP_COUNT          = 54   /* sentinel — must be last */
```
---
## 4. Interface Contracts
### 4.1 Accumulator Functions
```c
/* Zero-initialise an accumulator for the given aggregate type. */
void accumulator_init(Accumulator *acc, AggType type);
/* Feed one value into the accumulator. NULL handling per SQL standard. */
void accumulator_step(Accumulator *acc, const Value *val);
/* Produce the final result value. Writes into *result.
   Postconditions:
     COUNT(*)/COUNT(col): result->type = VAL_INTEGER, result->u.i >= 0.
     SUM: VAL_REAL if has_value, VAL_NULL otherwise.
     AVG: VAL_REAL if has_value (sum/count as double), VAL_NULL otherwise.
     MIN/MAX: type matches input type if has_value, VAL_NULL otherwise. */
void accumulator_finalize(const Accumulator *acc, Value *result);
```
**`accumulator_step` dispatch:**
| AggType | NULL input | Non-NULL input |
|---------|-----------|----------------|
| `AGG_COUNT_STAR` | `count++; has_value = true` | `count++; has_value = true` |
| `AGG_COUNT` | no-op | `count++; has_value = true` |
| `AGG_SUM` | no-op | `has_value = true; sum += to_double(val)` |
| `AGG_AVG` | no-op | `has_value = true; count++; sum += to_double(val)` |
| `AGG_MIN` | no-op | if `!has_value` or `val < min_val`: `min_val = *val; has_value = true` |
| `AGG_MAX` | no-op | if `!has_value` or `val > max_val`: `max_val = *val; has_value = true` |
**`to_double` helper:**
```c
static double to_double(const Value *v) {
    if (v->type == VAL_REAL) return v->u.r;
    if (v->type == VAL_INTEGER) return (double)v->u.i;
    return 0.0;  /* should not reach for non-NULL */
}
```
**MIN/MAX comparison** reuses `vm_compare` from M3 (integer vs integer, real vs real, string vs string; cross-type: promote to double for numeric, type-order otherwise).
### 4.2 GroupHashTable Functions
```c
GroupHashTable *group_ht_create(int agg_count, const AggType *agg_types);
void            group_ht_destroy(GroupHashTable *ht);
/* Look up or create a group for the given key.
   If key is new: allocates and inits accumulators.
   Returns pointer to the entry (never NULL — fatal on OOM). */
GroupEntry *group_ht_find_or_create(GroupHashTable *ht,
                                     const Value *key_values, int n_key_cols);
/* Number of groups in the table. */
int group_ht_count(const GroupHashTable *ht);
/* Iteration: returns entry at slot idx (skip unoccupied). 
   Caller iterates idx = 0..capacity-1, checking occupied. */
/* Or: linear iteration helper: */
typedef struct {
    GroupHashTable *ht;
    int             slot;  /* current slot index, starts at -1 */
} GroupIter;
void        group_iter_init(GroupIter *it, GroupHashTable *ht);
GroupEntry *group_iter_next(GroupIter *it);  /* returns NULL when exhausted */
```
**Hash function for GroupKey:**
```c
static uint32_t group_key_hash(const Value *values, int n_cols) {
    uint32_t h = 0x811C9DC5u;  /* FNV-1a offset basis */
    for (int i = 0; i < n_cols; i++) {
        const Value *v = &values[i];
        switch (v->type) {
            case VAL_NULL:
                h ^= 0u;
                h *= 0x01000193u;
                break;
            case VAL_INTEGER: {
                uint64_t bits = (uint64_t)v->u.i;
                h ^= (uint32_t)(bits & 0xFFFFFFFF);
                h *= 0x01000193u;
                h ^= (uint32_t)(bits >> 32);
                h *= 0x01000193u;
                break;
            }
            case VAL_REAL: {
                uint64_t bits;
                memcpy(&bits, &v->u.r, 8);
                h ^= (uint32_t)(bits & 0xFFFFFFFF);
                h *= 0x01000193u;
                h ^= (uint32_t)(bits >> 32);
                h *= 0x01000193u;
                break;
            }
            case VAL_STRING:
                for (int j = 0; j < v->u.s.len; j++) {
                    h ^= (uint8_t)v->u.s.data[j];
                    h *= 0x01000193u;
                }
                break;
            default: break;
        }
    }
    return h;
}
```
**Key equality for grouping — NULL == NULL is TRUE:**
```c
static bool group_key_equals(const Value *a, const Value *b, int n_cols) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n_cols; i++) {
        if (a[i].type == VAL_NULL && b[i].type == VAL_NULL) continue;
        if (a[i].type != b[i].type) return false;
        if (a[i].type == VAL_NULL) return false;
        switch (a[i].type) {
            case VAL_INTEGER:
                if (a[i].u.i != b[i].u.i) return false;
                break;
            case VAL_REAL:
                if (a[i].u.r != b[i].u.r) return false;
                break;
            case VAL_STRING:
                if (a[i].u.s.len != b[i].u.s.len) return false;
                if (memcmp(a[i].u.s.data, b[i].u.s.data, a[i].u.s.len) != 0)
                    return false;
                break;
            default: return false;
```
